❤️ Cardiology

ABIM Board Review — ACC/AHA/ESC Guidelines • Evidence-Based

🎯 Core Cardiology Principles

🚨Risk-stratify ACS early — time is myocardium
💊GDMT saves lives in HFrEF — use all four pillars
⚡Rate control is non-inferior to rhythm control in AFib
📊Use ASCVD risk score before starting statins
🩺Echo before, not instead of, clinical diagnosis
🔬Troponin sensitivity — apply 0/1h or 0/3h rule-out protocols
🚨 ACS 🫀 Stable CAD 💧 Heart Failure ⚡ AFib 📈 Arrhythmias 🔵 Valvular 🫁 Cardiomyopathy 🟡 Pericardial 📊 HTN 🧪 Lipids 🫁 Pulm HTN 🔴 Aortic 🦵 PAD 🩸 VTE ⚙️ Devices 📷 Imaging 🏥 Perioperative

🚨 Acute Coronary Syndromes

❤️

ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)

HIGH YIELD
🚨 TIME IS MYOCARDIUM
  • Door-to-balloon time: ≤90 minutes (primary PCI)
  • Door-to-needle time: ≤30 minutes (if fibrinolysis chosen)
  • First medical contact to device: ≤120 minutes if transfer for PCI
📈 STEMI ECG CRITERIA
  • ST elevation: ≥1mm in ≥2 contiguous leads
  • Anterior: V1-V4 (LAD)
  • Inferior: II, III, aVF (RCA or LCx)
  • Lateral: I, aVL, V5-V6 (LCx or diagonal)
  • Posterior: ST depression V1-V3 (get V7-V9; LCx or RCA)
  • Right ventricular: V4R ST elevation with inferior STEMI (proximal RCA)
STEMI Equivalents
  • New LBBB: With clinical suspicion (use modified Sgarbossa criteria)
  • De Winter T waves: Upsloping ST depression V1-V6 + tall symmetric T waves = proximal LAD occlusion
  • Wellens' syndrome: Biphasic or deeply inverted T waves V2-V3 in pain-free patient = critical LAD stenosis (avoid stress test → cath)
  • Posterior STEMI: Isolated ST depression V1-V3
  • Hyperacute T waves: Tall, symmetric, peaked T waves (earliest sign)
📘 ACC/AHA STEMI Guidelines (2013, focused update 2017)
Primary PCI is preferred reperfusion strategy when:
  • PCI-capable hospital (door-to-balloon ≤90 min)
  • Transfer possible with FMC-to-device ≤120 min
  • Contraindication to fibrinolysis
  • Cardiogenic shock
  • Diagnosis uncertain
Acute STEMI Medications
  • Aspirin: 162-325mg chewed immediately (continue 81mg daily indefinitely)
  • P2Y12 inhibitor: Ticagrelor 180mg or prasugrel 60mg (preferred for PCI); clopidogrel 600mg if lytic therapy or unable to take others
  • Anticoagulation: UFH, bivalirudin, or enoxaparin (per protocol)
  • High-intensity statin: Start in hospital (atorvastatin 80mg or rosuvastatin 40mg)
  • Beta-blocker: Within 24h if no contraindications (HF, low output, heart block, active bronchospasm)
  • ACEi/ARB: Within 24h, especially if anterior MI, HF, or EF ≤40%
⚡ BOARD PEARL: DAPT Duration
  • Post-STEMI/ACS with stent: DAPT × 12 months (minimum)
  • High bleeding risk: May shorten to 6 months
  • Extended DAPT: Consider beyond 12 months if low bleeding risk & tolerated well
  • Prasugrel: Avoid if prior stroke/TIA, age ≥75, weight <60kg
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: Right Ventricular MI

Scenario: Patient with inferior STEMI develops hypotension after nitroglycerin. Clear lung fields, JVD present.

Answer: Right ventricular infarction (proximal RCA). Avoid nitrates and diuretics. Treatment: IV fluids to maintain RV preload. Get V4R (ST elevation confirms RV involvement).

NSTEMI / Unstable Angina

HIGH YIELD
Definitions
  • NSTEMI: Elevated troponin + ischemic symptoms/ECG changes WITHOUT ST elevation
  • Unstable angina: Ischemic symptoms without troponin elevation (now rare with high-sensitivity troponin)
Risk Stratification: TIMI Risk Score
  • Age ≥65 (1 point)
  • ≥3 CAD risk factors (1 point)
  • Known CAD (≥50% stenosis) (1 point)
  • Aspirin use within 7 days (1 point)
  • Severe angina (≥2 episodes in 24h) (1 point)
  • ST deviation ≥0.5mm (1 point)
  • Positive cardiac biomarker (1 point)

0-2: Low risk; 3-4: Intermediate; 5-7: High risk (favor early invasive)

📘 ACC/AHA NSTE-ACS Guidelines (2014)
Invasive Strategy Timing:
  • Immediate (<2h): Refractory angina, hemodynamic/electrical instability, acute HF
  • Early (within 24h): GRACE score >140, troponin rise/fall, dynamic ST changes
  • Delayed invasive (25-72h): None of above but TIMI ≥2, diabetes, EF <40%, GFR <60, prior CABG
  • Ischemia-guided: Low-risk, prefer medical management (stress test, then cath if positive)
Medical Therapy
  • Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor: Same as STEMI
  • Anticoagulation: UFH, enoxaparin, fondaparinux, or bivalirudin
  • Beta-blocker: Oral within 24h (IV if ongoing ischemia, HTN, tachycardia)
  • Nitrates: For ongoing ischemia (avoid if RV infarct, severe AS, recent PDE5 inhibitor)
  • Statin: High-intensity, started in hospital
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Troponin Interpretation
  • High-sensitivity troponin: Detects smaller infarcts, earlier rise (1-3h)
  • Rise and/or fall pattern: Suggests acute MI (not chronic elevation)
  • Non-ACS causes of troponin elevation: HF, PE, myocarditis, sepsis, renal failure, takotsubo, arrhythmias, cardiac contusion

Post-MI Complications

HIGH YIELD
Complication Timing Presentation Management
Cardiogenic shock Any time Hypotension, cold extremities, pulmonary edema Revascularization, inotropes, mechanical support
Free wall rupture 3-5 days Sudden hemodynamic collapse, PEA Emergency surgery (usually fatal)
VSD (septal rupture) 3-5 days New harsh holosystolic murmur, HF IABP, vasodilators, surgery
Papillary muscle rupture 2-7 days Acute severe MR, pulmonary edema IABP, surgery
LV aneurysm Weeks-months Persistent ST elevation, HF, arrhythmias Medical (anticoagulation if thrombus); consider surgery
LV pseudoaneurysm Weeks Contained rupture, narrow neck on echo Surgery (high rupture risk)
Dressler syndrome 2-10 weeks Fever, pleuritic chest pain, pericardial friction rub NSAIDs, colchicine; avoid anticoagulation
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: Post-MI Murmur

Scenario: 5 days post-MI, patient develops sudden hypotension and new harsh holosystolic murmur with thrill at left lower sternal border.

Answer: Ventricular septal rupture. Echo will show VSD with left-to-right shunt. Management: IABP to reduce afterload, stabilize, and emergent surgical repair.

⚡ BOARD PEARL: VSD vs Papillary Muscle Rupture
  • Both: New holosystolic murmur, hemodynamic compromise
  • VSD: Murmur at LLSB, palpable thrill, oxygen step-up in RV on PA catheter
  • Papillary muscle rupture: Murmur at apex radiating to axilla, large V waves on PCWP tracing
  • Confirm with echo

🫀 Stable Ischemic Heart Disease

💊

Chronic Coronary Disease Management

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA Chronic Coronary Disease Guidelines (2023)
Optimal Medical Therapy (OMT) Components:
  • Antiplatelet: Aspirin 75-100mg daily (clopidogrel if aspirin intolerant)
  • Statin: High-intensity (atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg)
  • ACEi/ARB: If HF, DM, HTN, CKD, or EF ≤40%
  • Beta-blocker: If prior MI, LV dysfunction, or for symptom control
  • Anti-anginal: Beta-blocker and/or CCB first-line; add nitrate, ranolazine if needed
Anti-Anginal Medications
Agent Mechanism Notes
Beta-blockers ↓HR, ↓contractility, ↓O2 demand First-line; proven mortality benefit post-MI
CCB (non-DHP) Diltiazem, verapamil: ↓HR + vasodilation Good for vasospastic angina; avoid with BB (heart block)
CCB (DHP) Amlodipine, nifedipine: vasodilation Can combine with BB; good for HTN
Nitrates Venodilation → ↓preload Nitrate-free interval to prevent tolerance
Ranolazine Late sodium channel inhibitor Add-on; prolongs QT; no mortality benefit
⚡ BOARD PEARL: ISCHEMIA Trial (2020)
  • Question: Does routine invasive strategy improve outcomes in stable CAD with moderate-severe ischemia?
  • Answer: NO difference in death or MI at 5 years
  • Key takeaway: OMT is appropriate initial strategy for stable CAD. Revascularization for refractory symptoms despite OMT.
  • Exclusion: Left main >50%, EF <35%, NYHA III-IV, recent ACS

Stress Testing Selection

HIGH YIELD
Choosing the Right Test
Patient Characteristic Recommended Test Rationale
Can exercise + interpretable ECG Exercise ECG Most physiologic, prognostic value from exercise capacity
Uninterpretable ECG (LBBB, paced, LVH, digoxin, WPW) Exercise + imaging (echo or nuclear) ST changes unreliable
Cannot exercise Pharmacologic stress + imaging Dobutamine echo or vasodilator (adenosine/regadenoson) nuclear
LBBB or paced rhythm Vasodilator nuclear (avoid dobutamine) Dobutamine causes false positive septal defects
Bronchospasm/severe COPD Dobutamine stress Avoid adenosine/regadenoson (bronchoconstriction)
🚨 CONTRAINDICATIONS TO STRESS TESTING
  • Acute MI within 2 days
  • Unstable angina not stabilized on medical therapy
  • Uncontrolled arrhythmia with hemodynamic compromise
  • Symptomatic severe aortic stenosis
  • Decompensated HF
  • Acute PE, myocarditis, pericarditis, aortic dissection
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Duke Treadmill Score

Formula: Exercise time (min) – (5 × max ST deviation in mm) – (4 × angina index)

  • Angina index: 0 = none, 1 = non-limiting, 2 = exercise-limiting
  • ≥5: Low risk (annual mortality <1%)
  • -10 to +4: Moderate risk
  • <-10: High risk (annual mortality ≥5%)

Revascularization: PCI vs CABG

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA/SCAI Revascularization Guidelines
CABG generally preferred over PCI for:
  • Left main disease (especially with SYNTAX score >22)
  • Three-vessel disease, especially with diabetes
  • Complex CAD with high SYNTAX score (>32)
  • Reduced EF (<35%)
PCI reasonable for:
  • 1-2 vessel disease without proximal LAD
  • Left main with low SYNTAX (≤22) if surgery high risk
  • Patient factors favoring less invasive approach
💎 SYNTAX SCORE
  • Angiographic scoring system assessing CAD complexity
  • Low (0-22): PCI outcomes similar to CABG
  • Intermediate (23-32): Heart team discussion
  • High (>32): CABG preferred (better outcomes)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Diabetes and Revascularization (FREEDOM Trial)
  • FREEDOM (2012): Diabetics with multivessel CAD had better outcomes with CABG vs PCI
  • Lower death, MI, stroke at 5 years with CABG
  • Key: Use LIMA to LAD (survival benefit)

💔 Heart Failure

💊

Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (HFrEF)

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA Heart Failure Guidelines (2022 Update)
HF Classification by EF:
  • HFrEF: EF ≤40%
  • HFmrEF: EF 41-49% ("mildly reduced")
  • HFpEF: EF ≥50%
  • HFimpEF: Previously reduced, now ≥40% (keep GDMT!)
Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF

Four Pillars of HFrEF Therapy — Start all simultaneously when possible:

Drug Class Examples Mortality Benefit Key Consideration
ARNI or ACEi/ARB Sacubitril-valsartan; lisinopril, losartan Yes ARNI superior to ACEi (PARADIGM-HF); 36h washout from ACEi before ARNI
Beta-blocker Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol Yes Only these 3 BB proven beneficial in HFrEF
MRA Spironolactone, eplerenone Yes If EF ≤35% and NYHA II-IV; watch K+ and Cr
SGLT2 inhibitor Dapagliflozin, empagliflozin Yes Use regardless of diabetes status; reduces HF hospitalization
📝 FOUR PILLARS MEMORY AID
"ABMS" = ARNI, Beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Key HFrEF Trials
  • PARADIGM-HF: Sacubitril-valsartan superior to enalapril (20% ↓ CV death/HF hospitalization)
  • DAPA-HF/EMPEROR-Reduced: SGLT2i reduces HF hospitalization + CV death (regardless of DM)
  • RALES/EMPHASIS-HF: MRAs reduce mortality in HFrEF
  • COPERNICUS/MERIT-HF: Beta-blockers reduce mortality
Additional Therapies
  • Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate: Add if African American with NYHA III-IV on max GDMT (A-HeFT trial)
  • Ivabradine: If sinus rhythm, HR ≥70 despite max BB or unable to tolerate BB
  • Diuretics: For congestion (symptom relief, no mortality benefit)
  • Digoxin: May reduce HF hospitalizations (no mortality benefit; avoid toxicity)

Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF)

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • HF symptoms (dyspnea, edema, fatigue)
  • EF ≥50%
  • Evidence of diastolic dysfunction or elevated filling pressures
  • H2FPEF score: Predicts probability of HFpEF
📘 Treatment of HFpEF (2022 Guidelines)
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Class IIa recommendation (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER trials)
  • Diuretics: For congestion
  • Treat comorbidities: HTN, AF, CAD, obesity, sleep apnea
  • MRA: May be considered (TOPCAT trial — benefit in Americas subset)
  • ARNi: May be considered (PARAGON-HF — benefit near EF 50%)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: SGLT2i Now Evidence-Based for HFpEF
EMPEROR-Preserved (2021) and DELIVER (2022): SGLT2 inhibitors reduce HF hospitalization in HFpEF. First therapies with strong evidence in this population.

Acute Decompensated Heart Failure

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Profiles (Warm/Cold, Wet/Dry)
Profile Congestion (Wet) Perfusion (Warm) Treatment
Warm & Wet (most common) Yes Adequate Diuretics (IV loop), vasodilators if HTN
Cold & Wet Yes Poor Inotropes + diuretics; consider MCS
Cold & Dry No Poor Careful fluid challenge; may need inotropes
Warm & Dry No Adequate Optimize oral meds; investigate symptoms
Diuretic Management
  • IV loop diuretic: Furosemide, bumetanide, or torsemide
  • Dose: 1-2× home oral dose as IV (or 40mg if diuretic-naïve)
  • Goal: Net negative 1-2 L/day; relieve congestion
  • Diuretic resistance: Add thiazide (metolazone), increase dose, continuous infusion
🚨 CARDIOGENIC SHOCK
  • Definition: SBP <90 for >30 min (or need for vasopressors) + end-organ hypoperfusion + CI <2.2
  • Inotropes: Dobutamine (first-line), milrinone (caution in renal failure)
  • Vasopressors: Norepinephrine if hypotensive
  • Mechanical support: IABP, Impella, ECMO
💎 DISCHARGE CHECKLIST
  • Optimized GDMT (initiate before discharge if possible)
  • Stable on oral diuretics × 24h
  • Weight and symptom diary education
  • Follow-up within 7-14 days
  • Flu + pneumococcal vaccines

Device Therapy in Heart Failure

HIGH YIELD
ICD Indications (Primary Prevention)
  • EF ≤35% despite ≥3 months of GDMT
  • NYHA II-III
  • Expected survival >1 year
  • Ischemic: ≥40 days post-MI
  • Non-ischemic: ≥3 months of GDMT
CRT Indications
  • EF ≤35%
  • LBBB with QRS ≥150ms: Class I (strongest indication)
  • LBBB with QRS 120-149ms: Class IIa
  • Non-LBBB with QRS ≥150ms: Class IIa
  • QRS <120ms: No benefit (harm in LESSER-EARTH)
  • NYHA II-IV (ambulatory) on optimal medical therapy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: CRT Response Predictors
Best responders:
  • LBBB morphology
  • QRS ≥150ms
  • Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy
  • Female sex
Poor responders: RV pacing-induced LBBB, ischemic scar in LV lead position

💓 Atrial Fibrillation

⚡

Rate vs Rhythm Control

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA/HRS AF Guidelines (2023 Update)
Rate Control Target:
  • Lenient: Resting HR <110 bpm (RACE II trial — noninferior)
  • Strict: Resting HR <80 bpm (consider if symptomatic)
Rate Control Agents:
  • Beta-blockers: First-line (metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol)
  • Non-DHP CCB: Diltiazem, verapamil (avoid in HFrEF)
  • Digoxin: Add-on or if HF with hypotension
  • Amiodarone: If above fail; more for rhythm but slows rate
Rhythm Control Considerations
  • Preferred if: Symptomatic despite rate control, younger patients, first episode, tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy
  • EAST-AFNET 4 (2020): Early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) reduced CV outcomes vs rate control
  • Methods: Antiarrhythmic drugs, electrical cardioversion, catheter ablation
Antiarrhythmic Drug Selection
Clinical Scenario Preferred AAD Avoid
No structural heart disease Flecainide, propafenone, sotalol, dronedarone —
CAD Sotalol, dofetilide, amiodarone Flecainide, propafenone (proarrhythmic)
HFrEF Amiodarone, dofetilide Flecainide, propafenone, sotalol, dronedarone
LVH (significant) Amiodarone Flecainide, propafenone, sotalol
🚨 DRONEDARONE BLACK BOX
Contraindicated in:
  • Permanent AF (PALLAS trial — increased mortality)
  • NYHA III-IV HF or recent decompensation
  • Severe liver injury risk

Anticoagulation & Stroke Prevention

HIGH YIELD
CHA₂DS₂-VASc Score
  • CHF (1)
  • HTN (1)
  • Age ≥75 (2)
  • Diabetes (1)
  • Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism (2)
  • Vascular disease (MI, PAD, aortic plaque) (1)
  • Age 65-74 (1)
  • Sex category — female (1)
📘 Anticoagulation Recommendations
  • Score 0 (males) or 1 (females — sex alone): No anticoagulation
  • Score 1 (males): Consider anticoagulation
  • Score ≥2: Anticoagulation recommended
DOACs preferred over warfarin (unless mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis)
DOAC Dose (AF) Renal Adjustment Notes
Apixaban 5mg BID 2.5mg BID if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, Cr ≥1.5 Best studied in CKD; least renal excretion
Rivaroxaban 20mg daily with dinner 15mg daily if CrCl 15-50 Once daily; take with food
Dabigatran 150mg BID 75mg BID if CrCl 15-30 Most GI upset; reversible with idarucizumab
Edoxaban 60mg daily 30mg daily if CrCl 15-50 Less effective if CrCl >95
⚡ BOARD PEARL: When to Use Warfarin Instead of DOAC
  • Mechanical heart valve — DOACs contraindicated (RE-ALIGN trial)
  • Moderate-severe rheumatic mitral stenosis
  • Antiphospholipid syndrome (especially triple positive)
  • Severe CKD (CrCl <15) or dialysis — warfarin or apixaban per guidelines
Left Atrial Appendage Closure
  • WATCHMAN device: For patients with contraindication to long-term anticoagulation
  • Requires anticoagulation for 45 days post-procedure
  • Non-inferior to warfarin for stroke prevention (PROTECT AF, PREVAIL)

Cardioversion Considerations

HIGH YIELD
Anticoagulation for Cardioversion
  • AF <48 hours: Cardiovert; start anticoagulation and continue ≥4 weeks
  • AF ≥48 hours or unknown duration:
    • Option 1: Anticoagulate ≥3 weeks → cardiovert → continue ≥4 weeks
    • Option 2: TEE to exclude LAA thrombus → cardiovert → anticoagulate ≥4 weeks
  • Long-term anticoagulation: Based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc (not cardioversion status)
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Patient in AF for 2 days, hemodynamically stable. CHA₂DS₂-VASc = 4.

Answer: Can cardiovert now (AF <48h). Start anticoagulation (DOAC preferred). Continue anticoagulation indefinitely due to high stroke risk.

Catheter Ablation for AF

HIGH YIELD
📘 Indications for AF Ablation (2023)
  • Class I: Symptomatic paroxysmal AF refractory to ≥1 AAD
  • Class IIa: First-line therapy (before AAD) in paroxysmal AF
  • Class IIa: HFrEF with symptomatic AF (CASTLE-AF — mortality benefit)
  • Persistent AF: Less effective than paroxysmal; still reasonable
⚡ BOARD PEARL: CASTLE-AF Trial (2018)
Key finding: In HFrEF + AF, catheter ablation reduced death and HF hospitalization by 38% vs medical therapy. Ablation should be strongly considered in this population.

⚡ Arrhythmias

📈

Supraventricular Tachycardia

HIGH YIELD
SVT Classification
  • AVNRT: Most common (60%); slow-fast pathway in AV node
  • AVRT: Accessory pathway (WPW if preexcitation on baseline ECG)
  • Atrial tachycardia: Focal or macroreentrant (atrial flutter)
Acute Management
  • Vagal maneuvers: Carotid massage, Valsalva (modified — blow then lie flat with legs raised)
  • Adenosine: 6mg rapid IV push, then 12mg if needed (use central line if available)
  • If adenosine fails: Diltiazem IV, beta-blocker, or cardioversion
  • Unstable: Synchronized cardioversion
🚨 WPW + AFIB
DO NOT give AV nodal blockers (adenosine, diltiazem, digoxin, verapamil)
  • Blocks AV node → conduction via accessory pathway → rapid ventricular response → VF
  • Treatment: Procainamide or cardioversion
  • ECG clue: Irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology
📈 WPW ECG FEATURES (Sinus Rhythm)
  • Delta wave: Slurred upstroke of QRS
  • Short PR interval: <120ms
  • Wide QRS: Due to delta wave
  • Secondary ST-T changes

Ventricular Tachycardia

HIGH YIELD
VT vs SVT with Aberrancy

Assume VT until proven otherwise (especially if structural heart disease)

  • Favors VT: AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats, QRS >140ms, extreme axis deviation, precordial concordance
  • Favors SVT: Typical RBBB or LBBB pattern, preceding P waves
VT Management
  • Unstable: Synchronized cardioversion (100-200J biphasic)
  • Pulseless VT: Defibrillation + ACLS
  • Stable monomorphic VT: Amiodarone 150mg IV over 10 min; procainamide; cardioversion
  • Polymorphic VT (Torsades): IV magnesium 2g; correct QT-prolonging factors; isoproterenol/pacing if bradycardia-dependent
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Torsades de Pointes
  • Polymorphic VT + prolonged QT
  • Causes: Drugs (sotalol, amiodarone, antipsychotics, antibiotics), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, bradycardia, congenital LQTS
  • Treatment: IV magnesium, correct electrolytes, stop offending drugs, overdrive pacing if refractory
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Patient on sotalol develops syncope. ECG shows QTc 560ms with runs of polymorphic VT.

Answer: Drug-induced Torsades de Pointes. Stop sotalol, give IV magnesium 2g, correct K+ and Mg++, temporary pacing if needed.

Bradyarrhythmias

HIGH YIELD
AV Block Classification
  • 1st degree: PR >200ms, all P waves conducted
  • 2nd degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach): Progressive PR prolongation → dropped beat; usually AV nodal
  • 2nd degree Mobitz II: Constant PR → sudden dropped beat; infranodal, high risk of complete block
  • 3rd degree (complete): No relationship between P waves and QRS; requires pacing
📘 Pacemaker Indications
Class I (definite indications):
  • Symptomatic sinus node dysfunction
  • Third-degree AV block (regardless of symptoms)
  • Mobitz II second-degree AV block
  • Symptomatic chronotropic incompetence
  • Alternating bundle branch block
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Reversible Causes
Before permanent pacing, exclude reversible causes:
  • Drug-induced (beta-blockers, CCB, digoxin, amiodarone)
  • Lyme disease (especially if young, endemic area)
  • Electrolyte disturbances
  • Myocardial ischemia
  • Post-cardiac surgery (may resolve)

Sudden Cardiac Death Prevention

HIGH YIELD
ICD Indications — Secondary Prevention
  • Survivors of VF or hemodynamically unstable VT
  • Sustained VT with structural heart disease
  • Syncope with inducible VT on EPS
ICD Indications — Primary Prevention
  • Ischemic cardiomyopathy: EF ≤35% on GDMT, ≥40 days post-MI, NYHA II-III
  • Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy: EF ≤35% on GDMT ≥3 months, NYHA II-III
  • HCM: High-risk features (family hx SCD, syncope, LVH ≥30mm, NSVT, abnormal BP response)
  • ARVC: Based on risk stratification
  • Long QT, Brugada: Based on clinical risk
💎 WEARABLE DEFIBRILLATOR (LifeVest)
  • Bridge to ICD decision in high-risk patients
  • Post-MI with low EF (reassess after 40 days)
  • Newly diagnosed cardiomyopathy (reassess after 3 months GDMT)
  • Post-transplant while awaiting evaluation

🫀 Valvular Heart Disease

💊

Aortic Stenosis

HIGH YIELD
Severity Classification
Parameter Mild Moderate Severe
Aortic valve area (cm²) >1.5 1.0-1.5 <1.0
Mean gradient (mmHg) <20 20-40 >40
Peak velocity (m/s) <3.0 3.0-4.0 >4.0
📝 AS SYMPTOMS ("SAD")
Syncope (3yr survival) → Angina (5yr) → Dyspnea/HF (2yr)
📘 ACC/AHA VHD Guidelines (2020)
Intervention Indications:
  • Class I: Severe AS with symptoms
  • Class I: Severe AS + EF <50%
  • Class I: Severe AS undergoing other cardiac surgery
  • Class IIa: Asymptomatic severe AS with very severe AS (Vmax ≥5m/s)
  • Class IIa: Asymptomatic severe AS with abnormal exercise test
SAVR vs TAVR
  • Low surgical risk: SAVR preferred (durability); TAVR acceptable
  • Intermediate risk: Either SAVR or TAVR
  • High/prohibitive risk: TAVR preferred
  • Bicuspid valve: SAVR traditionally preferred; TAVR increasingly used
  • Age consideration: TAVR increasingly used in older patients (≥65-70)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Low-Flow, Low-Gradient AS
  • Definition: AVA <1.0 cm², mean gradient <40 mmHg, EF <50%
  • Question: Is it truly severe AS or pseudosevere (valve just not opening due to low flow)?
  • Answer: Dobutamine stress echo — if AVA stays <1.0 with increased flow → truly severe

Mitral Regurgitation

HIGH YIELD
Primary vs Secondary MR
  • Primary (degenerative): Intrinsic valve disease (prolapse, flail leaflet, rheumatic)
  • Secondary (functional): Normal valve with annular dilation or tethering from LV dysfunction
📘 Intervention for Primary MR
  • Class I: Severe MR with symptoms (NYHA II-IV)
  • Class I: Severe MR with EF 30-60% or LVESD ≥40mm
  • Class IIa: Severe MR, high likelihood of repair, EF >60%
Repair preferred over replacement when feasible (especially degenerative MR)
Secondary MR Management
  • First-line: GDMT for HF (may reduce MR severity)
  • CRT: If indicated for HF (may improve MR)
  • MitraClip: For select patients with HFrEF + severe MR despite GDMT (COAPT trial)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: COAPT vs MITRA-FR
  • COAPT (positive): MitraClip reduced HF hospitalization/death in HFrEF with severe MR
  • MITRA-FR (negative): No benefit
  • Key difference: COAPT selected "disproportionately severe MR" (MR out of proportion to LV size)

Aortic Regurgitation & Mitral Stenosis

CORE
Aortic Regurgitation
  • Causes: Bicuspid valve, endocarditis, aortic root dilation, rheumatic
  • Physical exam: Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, diastolic decrescendo murmur at LUSB
  • Surgery indications:
    • Symptomatic severe AR
    • Asymptomatic with EF ≤55%
    • Asymptomatic with LVESD >50mm or LVEDD >65mm
Mitral Stenosis
  • Most common cause: Rheumatic heart disease
  • Symptoms: Dyspnea, orthopnea, hemoptysis, AF, systemic emboli
  • Physical exam: Opening snap, low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex
  • Severe MS: MVA <1.5 cm², mean gradient >10 mmHg
  • Intervention: PBMV (if favorable anatomy) or surgery; anticoagulation if AF
🚨 MS + PREGNANCY
  • High-risk condition (increased blood volume → pulmonary edema)
  • Beta-blockers for rate control
  • PBMV can be done during pregnancy if needed
  • Screen and treat before conception if possible

Prosthetic Valve Management

HIGH YIELD
Anticoagulation for Mechanical Valves
  • Aortic mechanical: INR 2.0-3.0 (+ low-dose aspirin)
  • Mitral mechanical: INR 2.5-3.5 (+ low-dose aspirin)
  • DOACs are CONTRAINDICATED for mechanical valves
Bioprosthetic Valves
  • Anticoagulation: Aspirin alone (some centers use warfarin × 3-6 months post-SAVR)
  • Post-TAVR: DAPT or aspirin + anticoagulant × 3-6 months, then aspirin alone
  • Durability: ~10-15 years (less in younger patients)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Bridging Anticoagulation
  • High-risk (any mitral mechanical, older aortic mechanical, recent stroke/TIA): Bridge with heparin/LMWH
  • Low-risk (newer bileaflet aortic): May be able to hold briefly without bridging
  • Consult cardiology for periprocedural management

💔 Cardiomyopathies

🫀

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis & Features
  • Definition: LV wall thickness ≥15mm without loading condition explanation
  • Genetics: Autosomal dominant; sarcomere protein mutations
  • LVOT obstruction: Present in ~70% (dynamic; worsens with decreased preload/afterload)
  • Murmur: Harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo at LLSB; increases with Valsalva/standing
Medical Management
  • Beta-blockers: First-line for symptoms
  • Non-DHP CCB (verapamil): If beta-blocker intolerant
  • Disopyramide: Add for refractory symptoms with obstruction
  • Mavacamten: Cardiac myosin inhibitor (EXPLORER-HCM) — reduces LVOT gradient
  • Avoid: Vasodilators, diuretics, high-dose diuretics, digoxin
🚨 SCD RISK STRATIFICATION IN HCM
ICD if any major risk factor:
  • Prior cardiac arrest or sustained VT
  • Family history of SCD from HCM
  • Maximum LV wall thickness ≥30mm
  • Unexplained syncope
  • NSVT on monitoring
  • LV apical aneurysm
  • EF <50%
Interventions for Obstruction
  • Septal reduction therapy: If drug-refractory symptoms + LVOT gradient ≥50 mmHg
  • Surgical myectomy: Gold standard; mortality <1% at experienced centers
  • Alcohol septal ablation: Alternative if high surgical risk or patient preference

Dilated, Restrictive & Other Cardiomyopathies

HIGH YIELD
Dilated Cardiomyopathy
  • Causes: Idiopathic, familial (30-50%), ischemic, viral, alcohol, peripartum, toxins (doxorubicin), tachycardia-mediated
  • Workup: Echo, coronary evaluation (angiography or CTA), consider MRI, genetic testing if familial
  • Treatment: GDMT for HFrEF; treat underlying cause
Restrictive Cardiomyopathy
  • Features: Impaired filling, normal/small ventricles, preserved EF initially
  • Causes: Amyloidosis (most common), hemochromatosis, sarcoidosis, radiation, endomyocardial fibrosis
  • Distinguish from constrictive pericarditis: Similar hemodynamics; MRI, CT, cath helpful
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Cardiac Amyloidosis
  • AL amyloidosis: Light chain (plasma cell dyscrasia); poor prognosis; treat underlying disease
  • ATTR (transthyretin): Wild-type (elderly) or hereditary; better prognosis
  • Tafamidis: TTR stabilizer; improves outcomes in ATTR-CM (ATTR-ACT trial)
  • Diagnosis: Technetium pyrophosphate scan (positive in ATTR, negative in AL); biopsy if needed
  • Avoid digoxin: Binds amyloid fibrils → toxicity
Takotsubo (Stress) Cardiomyopathy
  • Trigger: Emotional or physical stress
  • Features: Apical ballooning, troponin elevation out of proportion to coronary disease
  • Angiography: Normal or non-obstructive coronaries
  • Prognosis: Usually resolves in weeks; supportive care
Arrhythmogenic RV Cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
  • Features: Fibrofatty replacement of RV; VT (often LBBB morphology)
  • ECG: Epsilon wave, T-wave inversions V1-V3
  • Diagnosis: Task Force criteria (MRI, echo, ECG, arrhythmias, family history)
  • Management: Avoid competitive sports; beta-blockers; ICD if high risk

💓 Pericardial Disease

🫀

Acute Pericarditis

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis (2 of 4 criteria)
  • Chest pain (sharp, pleuritic, positional — better leaning forward)
  • Pericardial friction rub
  • ECG changes (diffuse ST elevation, PR depression)
  • New or worsening pericardial effusion
📈 PERICARDITIS ECG
  • Diffuse ST elevation (concave up, "smiley face")
  • PR depression (most specific finding)
  • No reciprocal changes (unlike STEMI)
  • Spodick sign: Downsloping TP segment
📘 Treatment (ESC 2015)
  • First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600mg TID or aspirin 750-1000mg TID) × 1-2 weeks + colchicine × 3 months
  • Colchicine: Reduces recurrence by 50% (COPE, ICAP trials)
  • Post-MI pericarditis: Aspirin preferred; avoid high-dose NSAIDs (impair healing)
  • Corticosteroids: AVOID if possible (increase recurrence); use only if refractory or contraindication to NSAIDs/colchicine
⚡ BOARD PEARL: High-Risk Features
  • Fever >38°C
  • Subacute onset
  • Large effusion or tamponade
  • Failure to respond to NSAIDs within 1 week
  • Immunocompromised
  • Trauma
  • Anticoagulation use
Consider hospitalization and further workup for underlying cause.

Cardiac Tamponade

HIGH YIELD
🚨 CARDIAC TAMPONADE
Clinical triad (Beck's triad):
  • Hypotension
  • Jugular venous distension
  • Muffled heart sounds
Other findings: Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration), tachycardia
Echo Findings
  • RA systolic collapse
  • RV diastolic collapse (more specific)
  • IVC plethora (no respiratory variation)
  • Respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid inflow
Management
  • Unstable: Emergent pericardiocentesis
  • Stable: Urgent drainage (echo-guided or surgical)
  • Volume resuscitation: May temporize while preparing for drainage
  • Avoid: Diuretics, positive pressure ventilation (reduce preload)
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Post-cardiac surgery patient with hypotension, JVD, clear lungs, pulsus paradoxus. Echo shows moderate effusion with RV diastolic collapse.

Answer: Cardiac tamponade. Emergent pericardiocentesis or surgical drainage.

Constrictive Pericarditis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Right-sided HF symptoms (edema, ascites, JVD)
  • Kussmaul's sign: JVP increases (or fails to decrease) with inspiration
  • Pericardial knock: Early diastolic sound
Distinguishing from Restrictive Cardiomyopathy
Feature Constrictive Restrictive
Pericardial thickening Present Absent
Ventricular interdependence Present (septal bounce) Absent
Respiratory variation in E velocity ≥25% <15%
BNP Normal or mildly elevated Markedly elevated
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Treatment
  • Definitive: Pericardiectomy (surgical removal of pericardium)
  • Medical: Diuretics for congestion; anti-inflammatory therapy if active inflammation
  • Trial of anti-inflammatory: Consider in subacute/transient constriction (may resolve)

📊 Hypertension

💊

Diagnosis & Classification

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA HTN Guidelines (2017)
Category Systolic Diastolic
Normal <120 <80
Elevated 120-129 <80
Stage 1 HTN 130-139 80-89
Stage 2 HTN ≥140 ≥90
BP Targets
  • General: <130/80 (if 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% or known CVD, DM, CKD)
  • Lower risk: <130/80 reasonable; <140/90 minimum
  • SPRINT trial: Intensive (<120) reduced CV events vs standard (<140) in high-risk patients

Antihypertensive Selection

HIGH YIELD
First-Line Agents
  • Thiazides: Chlorthalidone, hydrochlorothiazide, indapamide
  • ACEi/ARB: Especially with DM, CKD (proteinuria), HF
  • CCB: Amlodipine, nifedipine; especially in African Americans
Comorbidity Preferred Agents
HFrEF ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, diuretic
CKD with proteinuria ACEi or ARB (renoprotective)
Post-MI ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker
Diabetes ACEi or ARB
African American (no CKD) Thiazide or CCB (ACEi/ARB less effective)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Resistant HTN
  • Definition: BP above goal despite 3 drugs (including diuretic) at optimal doses
  • Rule out: Non-adherence, white coat HTN, secondary causes
  • Add-on: Spironolactone (PATHWAY-2 trial — most effective 4th agent)

Hypertensive Emergency

HIGH YIELD
🚨 HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY
Definition: Severe HTN + acute end-organ damage (encephalopathy, AKI, pulmonary edema, aortic dissection, MI, stroke)
Treatment Principles
  • Goal: Reduce MAP by 20-25% in first hour (except aortic dissection — faster)
  • IV agents: Nicardipine, labetalol, nitroprusside, clevidipine, esmolol
  • Aortic dissection: Beta-blocker FIRST (reduce dP/dt), then vasodilator; target SBP <120 and HR <60
  • Avoid: Rapid reduction (risk of ischemia)

💊 Lipid Management

📊

Statin Therapy

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA Cholesterol Guidelines (2018)
High-Intensity Statin (↓LDL ≥50%):
  • Clinical ASCVD
  • LDL ≥190 mg/dL
  • Diabetes age 40-75 with ASCVD risk factors
Moderate-Intensity Statin (↓LDL 30-49%):
  • Diabetes age 40-75
  • 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5% (use risk calculator)
Intensity Options
High Atorvastatin 40-80mg, rosuvastatin 20-40mg
Moderate Atorvastatin 10-20mg, rosuvastatin 5-10mg, simvastatin 20-40mg, pravastatin 40mg
⚡ BOARD PEARL: LDL Goals (2018 Multisociety)
  • Very high-risk ASCVD: LDL goal <70 mg/dL
  • If on max statin and LDL ≥70: Add ezetimibe
  • If still ≥70 (or ≥100 for high-risk): Add PCSK9 inhibitor

Non-Statin Therapies

HIGH YIELD
Ezetimibe
  • Inhibits intestinal cholesterol absorption
  • ↓LDL 15-20% additional when added to statin
  • IMPROVE-IT: Reduced CV events post-ACS when added to statin
PCSK9 Inhibitors
  • Agents: Evolocumab, alirocumab (injectable q2 weeks or monthly)
  • ↓LDL 50-60% additional
  • FOURIER, ODYSSEY: Reduced CV events in ASCVD patients
  • Indications: ASCVD not at goal on max statin + ezetimibe; familial hypercholesterolemia
Bempedoic Acid
  • ACL inhibitor (upstream of HMG-CoA reductase)
  • Oral agent; can use with statin intolerance
  • ↓LDL 15-25%
Hypertriglyceridemia
  • TG 150-499: Lifestyle; optimize statin
  • TG 500-999: Consider fibrate or omega-3
  • TG ≥1000: Fibrate to prevent pancreatitis; very low fat diet
  • Icosapent ethyl (EPA): REDUCE-IT trial — reduced CV events in ASCVD + TG 150-499 on statin

🫁 Pulmonary Hypertension

💓

Classification & Diagnosis

HIGH YIELD
📘 WHO Classification
  • Group 1 (PAH): Idiopathic, heritable, drug/toxin, CTD, HIV, portal HTN
  • Group 2: Left heart disease (most common)
  • Group 3: Lung disease/hypoxia (COPD, ILD, OSA)
  • Group 4: CTEPH (chronic thromboembolic PH)
  • Group 5: Multifactorial/unclear (sarcoid, myeloproliferative)
Hemodynamic Definitions (RHC)
  • PH: Mean PAP >20 mmHg
  • Precapillary (PAH): mPAP >20, PCWP ≤15, PVR ≥3 WU
  • Postcapillary (LHD): mPAP >20, PCWP >15
  • Combined: mPAP >20, PCWP >15, PVR ≥3 WU
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Vasoreactivity Testing
  • Done in Group 1 PAH only
  • Positive response: ↓mPAP ≥10 mmHg to ≤40 mmHg with normal/increased CO
  • ~10% are responders: May use high-dose CCB (only in responders!)

PAH Treatment

HIGH YIELD
Pathway Agents Notes
Endothelin receptor antagonist Ambrisentan, bosentan, macitentan Bosentan: hepatotoxicity, drug interactions
PDE-5 inhibitor Sildenafil, tadalafil Also used in Group 2 (off-label)
sGC stimulator Riociguat Contraindicated with PDE-5i; approved for CTEPH
Prostacyclin pathway Epoprostenol (IV), treprostinil, iloprost, selexipag Epoprostenol = most potent; continuous IV
🚨 DO NOT USE PAH DRUGS FOR GROUP 2 PH
PAH-specific therapies are NOT beneficial (and may be harmful) in PH due to left heart disease. Treat underlying cardiac condition.
CTEPH (Group 4)
  • Surgical pulmonary thromboendarterectomy (PTE): Potentially curative; evaluate all patients
  • Balloon pulmonary angioplasty: If inoperable
  • Medical: Riociguat if inoperable or persistent PH post-surgery
  • Lifelong anticoagulation

🩺 Aortic Disease

❤️

Aortic Dissection

HIGH YIELD
🚨 SURGICAL EMERGENCY (TYPE A)
Type A (Stanford): Involves ascending aorta → surgery immediately
Type B: Descending only → medical management unless complicated
Clinical Features
  • Classic presentation: Sudden, severe, "tearing" chest/back pain
  • Pulse deficits: Unequal arm BPs
  • Aortic regurgitation: If dissection extends to aortic root
  • Complications: MI (RCA involvement), stroke, limb ischemia, tamponade
Medical Management
  • Beta-blocker FIRST: Reduce heart rate (<60) and dP/dt before vasodilators
  • Target SBP: <120 mmHg
  • IV esmolol or labetalol
  • Add vasodilator if needed: Nicardipine, nitroprusside (only after beta-blocker)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Type B Complications Requiring Intervention
  • Malperfusion (renal, mesenteric, limb ischemia)
  • Rupture or impending rupture
  • Refractory pain or hypertension
  • Rapid expansion
Treatment: TEVAR (thoracic endovascular repair) preferred

Aortic Aneurysm

CORE
Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm (TAA)
  • Screening: First-degree relatives of patients with TAA or bicuspid aortic valve
  • Surgical threshold: ≥5.5 cm (≥5.0 in Marfan or bicuspid with risk factors)
  • Medical: BP control (beta-blocker preferred), statin, lifestyle
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA)
  • Screening: One-time ultrasound for men 65-75 who ever smoked
  • Repair threshold: ≥5.5 cm (or ≥5.0 in women), or rapid expansion (>0.5 cm/6 months)
  • Symptomatic: Urgent repair regardless of size
  • EVAR vs open: Similar long-term survival; EVAR lower perioperative mortality but more reinterventions

🦵 Peripheral Arterial Disease

💊

PAD Diagnosis & Management

HIGH YIELD
Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI)
  • Normal: 1.0-1.4
  • Borderline: 0.91-0.99
  • Abnormal (PAD): ≤0.90
  • Severe PAD: <0.40
  • Non-compressible: >1.40 (calcified vessels; need toe pressures)
Management
  • Cardiovascular risk reduction: Smoking cessation, statin, antiplatelet, BP control
  • Claudication: Supervised exercise therapy (first-line), cilostazol
  • Critical limb ischemia: Revascularization (endovascular or surgical)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: COMPASS Trial
Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID + aspirin reduced MACE and major limb events in stable PAD/CAD compared to aspirin alone. Consider in high-risk PAD patients.

🩸 Venous Thromboembolism

💊

VTE Treatment

HIGH YIELD
📘 CHEST Guidelines / ASH 2020
Initial anticoagulation:
  • DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
  • Rivaroxaban or apixaban: Can start without parenteral lead-in
  • Dabigatran or edoxaban: Require 5-10 days parenteral first
  • Exceptions (use warfarin): Antiphospholipid syndrome, severe renal impairment
Duration of Anticoagulation
  • Provoked (surgery, immobilization): 3 months
  • Unprovoked: ≥3 months, then reassess for extended therapy
  • Active cancer: LMWH or DOAC (edoxaban, rivaroxaban) indefinitely while cancer active
  • Recurrent unprovoked VTE: Indefinite anticoagulation
⚡ BOARD PEARL: IVC Filter Indications
  • Absolute contraindication to anticoagulation with acute VTE
  • Failure of anticoagulation (recurrent PE despite therapeutic AC)
  • NOT indicated: Prophylaxis alone, chronic PE, to allow surgery
  • Retrievable filters: Should be removed when no longer needed

🔌 Cardiac Devices

💓

Pacemaker Modes & Indications

HIGH YIELD
Pacemaker Nomenclature (NBG Code)
  • 1st letter: Chamber paced (A, V, D=dual)
  • 2nd letter: Chamber sensed (A, V, D, O=none)
  • 3rd letter: Response to sensing (I=inhibit, T=trigger, D=both)
  • 4th letter: Rate modulation (R=rate responsive)
Mode Description Indications
VVI Ventricular pacing, sensing, inhibited Chronic AF with slow ventricular response
AAI Atrial pacing/sensing, inhibited Sinus node dysfunction with intact AV conduction
DDD Dual chamber pacing/sensing AV block, maintains AV synchrony
DDI Dual pacing, dual sensing, inhibited only Paroxysmal AF (prevents tracking fast atrial rates)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Pacemaker Syndrome
  • Definition: Symptoms from loss of AV synchrony (VVI mode pacing with intact atrial contraction)
  • Symptoms: Fatigue, dyspnea, pulsations in neck ("cannon A waves"), hypotension
  • Treatment: Upgrade to dual-chamber (DDD) pacing

ICD & CRT Summary

HIGH YIELD
ICD Primary Prevention Indications
  • Ischemic CMP: EF ≤35%, NYHA II-III, ≥40 days post-MI, on GDMT
  • Non-ischemic CMP: EF ≤35%, NYHA II-III, ≥3 months GDMT
  • HCM: High-risk features (see HCM section)
  • Expected survival >1 year with good functional status
CRT Indications
  • EF ≤35% + NYHA II-IV
  • LBBB + QRS ≥150ms: Class I (strongest indication)
  • LBBB + QRS 120-149ms: Class IIa
  • Non-LBBB + QRS ≥150ms: Class IIa
  • QRS <120ms: No benefit
🚨 DEVICE COMPLICATIONS
  • Lead dislodgement: Early post-implant; loss of capture/sensing
  • Pocket infection: Device removal required
  • Lead endocarditis: S. aureus most common; requires extraction
  • Inappropriate shocks: Common in ICDs; treat underlying cause (AF, lead fracture)
  • Twiddler syndrome: Patient manipulation of device causing lead displacement

📷 Cardiac Imaging

🔬

Echocardiography

CORE
TTE vs TEE
  • TEE preferred for: Endocarditis (vegetations, abscesses), LAA thrombus, prosthetic valve evaluation, aortic dissection, intracardiac shunts
  • TTE sufficient for: LV function, wall motion, pericardial effusion, most valve assessments
Key Hemodynamic Calculations
  • LVEF: (EDV - ESV) / EDV × 100
  • Continuity equation (AVA): AVA = (LVOT area × LVOT VTI) / AV VTI
  • PASP: 4(TR velocity)² + RAP
  • E/e' ratio: Estimates LV filling pressures (>14 = elevated)

Cardiac MRI

HIGH YIELD
Indications
  • Cardiomyopathy evaluation: Distinguish ischemic vs non-ischemic (scar pattern)
  • Myocarditis: Edema, late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) in non-coronary distribution
  • Infiltrative disease: Amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis
  • ARVC diagnosis: RV fat/fibrosis, wall motion abnormalities
  • Constrictive vs restrictive
  • Viability assessment: Transmural vs subendocardial scar
⚡ BOARD PEARL: LGE Patterns
  • Subendocardial/transmural (coronary distribution): Ischemic cardiomyopathy
  • Mid-wall (septal): Dilated cardiomyopathy, myocarditis
  • Epicardial/patchy: Myocarditis, sarcoidosis
  • Diffuse subendocardial: Amyloidosis
🚨 MRI CONTRAINDICATIONS
  • Absolute: Non-MRI compatible devices (older pacemakers/ICDs), metallic foreign body in eye
  • Gadolinium: Avoid if eGFR <30 (nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk)
  • MRI-conditional devices: May be scanned under specific conditions

Cardiac CT

CORE
Coronary CT Angiography (CCTA)
  • Excellent negative predictive value: Rules out significant CAD
  • Best for: Low-intermediate risk chest pain, acute chest pain in ED
  • Limitations: Heavy calcification, fast/irregular heart rate, obesity
Coronary Artery Calcium (CAC) Score
  • 0: Very low risk
  • 1-99: Mild plaque
  • 100-399: Moderate plaque
  • ≥400: Severe plaque; consider high-intensity statin

Use: Risk stratification in intermediate-risk patients (borderline indication for statin)

🏥 Perioperative Cardiac Evaluation

📋

Risk Stratification & Testing

HIGH YIELD
📘 ACC/AHA Perioperative Guidelines (2014)
Stepwise Approach:
  1. Emergency surgery: Proceed to OR; optimize postop
  2. Active cardiac conditions? (ACS, decompensated HF, significant arrhythmia, severe VHD) → Delay for evaluation/treatment
  3. Low-risk surgery: Proceed without testing (cataract, superficial)
  4. Functional capacity ≥4 METs? → Proceed without testing
  5. <4 METs or unknown: Calculate risk (RCRI); test if elevated risk AND results will change management
RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index)
  • High-risk surgery (intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, vascular)
  • History of ischemic heart disease
  • History of HF
  • History of cerebrovascular disease
  • Diabetes on insulin
  • Creatinine >2.0

0 points: 0.4% MACE; 1: 1%; 2: 2.4%; ≥3: 5.4%

⚡ BOARD PEARL: Perioperative Beta-Blockers
  • Continue if already on beta-blocker (Class I)
  • Do NOT start beta-blocker day of surgery (POISE trial — increased stroke/death)
  • May start days-weeks before surgery with titration if intermediate-high risk

Perioperative Medication Management

HIGH YIELD
Medication Recommendation
Beta-blockers Continue
Statins Continue (reduces periop MI)
ACEi/ARB Hold morning of surgery (risk of hypotension)
Aspirin (primary prevention) Hold 7 days before
Aspirin (stent <6 weeks) Continue (very high stent thrombosis risk)
P2Y12 inhibitor Hold 5-7 days (clopidogrel/ticagrelor) or 7 days (prasugrel)
Warfarin Hold 5 days; bridge per risk
DOACs Hold 24-48h (longer if renal impairment or high bleeding risk)
🚨 STENT THROMBOSIS RISK
Elective surgery timing after stent:
  • BMS: Delay ≥30 days (ideally 3 months)
  • DES (newer generation): Delay ≥3-6 months
  • High-risk (ACS, left main, bifurcation): Delay 12 months if possible

❤️ ABIM Cardiology Study Guide

Evidence-based content from ACC/AHA/ESC Guidelines

For educational purposes only. Verify all clinical decisions with current guidelines.

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide 🦠 ID Study Guide 💊 Drug Guide
↑ Back to top

🦠 Infectious Disease

ABIM Board Review — IDSA/ATS/SCCM Guidelines • Evidence-Based

🎯 Core ID Principles

🧫Culture before antibiotics whenever possible
⏱️Source control is as important as antibiotics in sepsis
💊Narrow spectrum early — de-escalate based on cultures
📅Shortest effective antibiotic duration reduces resistance
🔄IV-to-oral switch early when gut is functional
🚫Do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria (except pregnancy/pre-procedure)
📋 Principles 💊 Antibiotics 🫁 Respiratory 🧠 CNS 🫀 Cardiovascular 🟠 GI/Hepatobiliary 💧 GU 🩹 Skin/Soft Tissue 🦴 Bone/Joint ⚠️ Sepsis 🔴 HIV/AIDS 🍄 Fungal 🦠 Viral 🪱 Parasites ✈️ Travel 🏥 HAIs Immunocomprmzd 💉 Prophylaxis

📋 Antimicrobial Principles

🧬

Antimicrobial Stewardship

HIGH YIELD
📘 IDSA/SHEA Guidelines (2016, updated 2023)
  • Core elements: Leadership commitment, accountability, pharmacy expertise, action (interventions), tracking, reporting, education
  • Goal: Optimize clinical outcomes while minimizing unintended consequences (resistance, C. diff, adverse effects)
Key Stewardship Interventions
  • Prospective audit with feedback: ID/pharmacy review with recommendations (most effective)
  • Formulary restriction/preauthorization: Requires approval for restricted agents
  • IV-to-PO conversion: When patient stable, tolerating PO, GI tract functioning
  • De-escalation: Narrow spectrum based on culture data
  • Dose optimization: PK/PD-based dosing (extended infusions for β-lactams)
⚡ BOARD PEARL
Procalcitonin-guided therapy: Can safely reduce antibiotic duration in respiratory infections and sepsis. PCT <0.25 ng/mL suggests low likelihood of bacterial infection; PCT <0.5 or 80% decrease from peak supports stopping antibiotics.

Pharmacokinetics/Pharmacodynamics (PK/PD)

HIGH YIELD
Pattern Parameter Drug Classes Optimization Strategy
Time-dependent fT > MIC β-lactams, carbapenems Extended/continuous infusion, frequent dosing
Concentration-dependent Cmax/MIC Aminoglycosides, daptomycin, metronidazole High-dose, extended-interval (once daily)
AUC-dependent AUC/MIC Fluoroquinolones, vancomycin, azithromycin Optimize total daily dose
💎 CLINICAL PEARL
Extended infusion piperacillin-tazobactam: 4-hour infusion (vs 30-min) improves fT>MIC, shown to reduce mortality in critically ill patients with Pseudomonas infections (BLING III trial, Lancet 2022).
Vancomycin Dosing (IDSA 2020)
  • Target: AUC/MIC 400-600 (replaces trough-based dosing)
  • Loading dose: 25-30 mg/kg (actual body weight) for serious infections
  • Maintenance: 15-20 mg/kg q8-12h, adjust based on AUC
  • AUC monitoring preferred: Two-level (Bayesian) approach most accurate
  • Nephrotoxicity risk: AUC >600 or trough >20 increases risk

Antimicrobial Resistance Mechanisms

HIGH YIELD
Mechanism Examples Clinical Relevance
β-lactamase production ESBL, AmpC, KPC, NDM ESBLs: use carbapenems; CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam
Altered PBPs MRSA (mecA → PBP2a) Resistant to all β-lactams except ceftaroline
Efflux pumps Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter Multi-drug resistance, intrinsic resistance
Target modification VRE (vanA/vanB), FQ resistance (gyrA) VanA: high-level resistance; VanB: susceptible to teicoplanin
Decreased permeability Porin mutations (Pseudomonas OprD) Carbapenem resistance in Pseudomonas
🚨 CDC URGENT THREATS (2019)
  • Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter (CRAB)
  • Candida auris — often pan-resistant, healthcare transmission
  • Clostridioides difficile
  • Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
  • Drug-resistant Neisseria gonorrhoeae
⚡ BOARD PEARL: ESBL vs AmpC
  • ESBLs (CTX-M, TEM, SHV): Plasmid-mediated, inhibited by clavulanate in vitro (but don't use clinically), use carbapenems
  • AmpC (SPACE organisms): Chromosomal, NOT inhibited by clavulanate, inducible with cephalosporins → use carbapenems or cefepime
  • SPACE: Serratia, Pseudomonas/Providencia, Acinetobacter, Citrobacter, Enterobacter

Culture Interpretation & Common Contaminants

HIGH YIELD
Blood Culture Interpretation
  • Always pathogens: S. aureus, S. pneumoniae, β-hemolytic strep, Enterobacterales, Pseudomonas, Candida, Listeria
  • Usually contaminants: Coagulase-negative staph (CoNS), Corynebacterium, Bacillus (non-anthracis), Propionibacterium
  • CoNS interpretation: Likely pathogen if ≥2 positive bottles, same species, patient with intravascular device, or immunocompromised
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Post-op patient with fever, 1 of 2 blood cultures positive for coagulase-negative staphylococcus at 36 hours.

Answer: Most likely contaminant. Do not treat. Repeat cultures if clinically concerned. True CoNS bacteremia typically has ≥2 positive cultures with identical species.

Time to Positivity (TTP)
  • Earlier positivity (<12-14 hours) suggests higher bacterial burden
  • S. aureus bacteremia: TTP <12h associated with endocarditis, metastatic infection
  • Contaminants often have longer TTP (>24-48 hours)

💊 Antibiotic Classes & Spectrum

💉

Penicillins

CORE
Class Agents Spectrum Key Uses
Natural Penicillin G/V Strep, syphilis, Listeria Strep pharyngitis, syphilis, endocarditis (viridans)
Anti-staphylococcal Nafcillin, oxacillin, dicloxacillin MSSA only MSSA bacteremia, endocarditis (preferred)
Aminopenicillins Ampicillin, amoxicillin + Enterococcus, Listeria, H. flu Listeria meningitis, enterococcal infections
+ β-lactamase inhibitor Amox-clav, amp-sulbactam + anaerobes, β-lactamase producers Bite wounds, aspiration PNA, diabetic foot
Anti-pseudomonal Piperacillin-tazobactam Broad including Pseudomonas HAP/VAP, neutropenic fever, intra-abdominal
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Anti-staphylococcal Penicillins
Nafcillin/oxacillin remain FIRST-LINE for MSSA bacteremia and endocarditis — superior outcomes vs vancomycin for MSSA. Cefazolin is acceptable alternative (better for renal dosing). Never use vancomycin for MSSA if alternatives available.
🚨 NAFCILLIN TOXICITY
  • Interstitial nephritis: More common than with other β-lactams
  • Neutropenia: With prolonged use (>2 weeks)
  • Hepatotoxicity: Transaminase elevation
  • Hypokalemia: High sodium load

Cephalosporins

CORE
Generation IV Agent PO Agent Spectrum Notes
1st Cefazolin Cephalexin MSSA, strep; surgical prophylaxis
2nd Cefuroxime, cefoxitin Cefuroxime axetil Better GN; cefoxitin covers anaerobes
3rd Ceftriaxone, ceftazidime Cefixime, cefpodoxime Excellent GN; ceftazidime for Pseudomonas
4th Cefepime — + Pseudomonas, stable to AmpC
5th Ceftaroline — + MRSA (only β-lactam!)
📝 MEMORY AID
"1st → Skin, 2nd → Sinuses, 3rd → Serious (meningitis), 4th → Fierce (Pseudomonas), 5th → MRSA Fighter"
💎 CEFTRIAXONE PEARLS
  • Long half-life: Once-daily dosing (even for meningitis: 2g q12h)
  • Biliary excretion: No renal adjustment needed
  • Caution: Biliary sludge (especially with TPN), avoid with calcium-containing infusions in neonates
  • NOT for Pseudomonas: Use ceftazidime or cefepime
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Cefepime Neurotoxicity
Cefepime-induced encephalopathy: Particularly in renal impairment. Presents with altered mental status, myoclonus, seizures. EEG shows triphasic waves. Stop cefepime; hemodialysis can remove drug.

Carbapenems

HIGH YIELD
Agent Spectrum Key Differences
Meropenem Broadest; GP, GN, anaerobes, Pseudomonas CNS penetration (meningitis); less seizure risk
Imipenem-cilastatin Similar to meropenem Higher seizure risk; cilastatin prevents renal metabolism
Ertapenem NO Pseudomonas, NO Acinetobacter Once daily; OPAT-friendly; good for ESBL
🚨 CARBAPENEM CAUTIONS
  • Seizures: Imipenem > meropenem; risk factors: CNS disease, renal failure, high doses
  • C. difficile: High risk; use judiciously
  • Drug interaction: Valproic acid levels ↓ 90% — contraindicated together
⚡ BOARD PEARL: When to Use Carbapenems
  • ESBL infections: Carbapenems are drug of choice
  • Severe intra-abdominal infections: Especially if prior antibiotics
  • Hospital-acquired infections with resistance risk
  • NOT first-line: Avoid if narrower spectrum will work (stewardship)

Fluoroquinolones

HIGH YIELD
Agent Spectrum Highlights Key Uses
Ciprofloxacin Best Pseudomonas; poor GP coverage UTI, Pseudomonas, GI infections
Levofloxacin + Strep pneumoniae ("respiratory FQ") CAP, UTI, prostatitis
Moxifloxacin Best GP; + anaerobes; NO Pseudomonas CAP, AECB; NO urine (not renally excreted)
🚨 FDA BLACK BOX WARNINGS
  • Tendinitis/rupture: Risk ↑ with age >60, steroids, transplant recipients; Achilles most common
  • Peripheral neuropathy: May be irreversible
  • CNS effects: Seizures, psychosis, confusion
  • QT prolongation: Especially moxifloxacin
  • Aortic aneurysm/dissection: Avoid if known aneurysm or risk factors
  • Hypoglycemia: Especially in diabetics on sulfonylureas
⚡ BOARD PEARL: FQ Resistance
Rising E. coli resistance: 20-30% in many areas. Check local antibiogram before empiric use for UTI. Do NOT use FQ for empiric gonorrhea (high resistance).
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 68-year-old on prednisone for COPD presents with sudden Achilles pain/swelling after starting levofloxacin.

Answer: Stop fluoroquinolone immediately. Diagnose tendinopathy (may need MRI). Conservative management; avoid rupture with rest and immobilization.

Vancomycin

HIGH YIELD
Spectrum
Gram-positive ONLY MRSA
  • MRSA, MSSA (but inferior to nafcillin for MSSA)
  • Coagulase-negative staph
  • Streptococci (including penicillin-resistant pneumococcus)
  • Enterococcus faecalis (not VRE)
  • C. difficile (oral only for C. diff colitis)
📘 IDSA 2020 Vancomycin Dosing Guidelines
  • Target: AUC/MIC 400-600 (preferred over trough)
  • Loading: 25-30 mg/kg (actual body weight) for serious infections
  • Maintenance: 15-20 mg/kg q8-12h based on renal function
  • Monitoring: AUC-guided (Bayesian) preferred; if trough-based, target 15-20 for serious infections
🚨 VANCOMYCIN TOXICITY
  • Nephrotoxicity: Risk increases with AUC >600, trough >20, concurrent nephrotoxins (pip-tazo controversial)
  • Red man syndrome: Histamine release from rapid infusion (NOT allergy); slow infusion rate
  • Ototoxicity: Rare; may be irreversible
  • DRESS/drug rash: Can be severe
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Vancomycin MIC Creep
  • MIC ≤1: Standard dosing adequate
  • MIC = 2: Borderline; may have treatment failures
  • MIC ≥4: Resistant; use alternative (daptomycin, linezolid, ceftaroline)
  • hVISA: Heteroresistant VISA; standard susceptibility testing may miss; suspect if slow clinical response

Aminoglycosides

CORE
Key Agents
  • Gentamicin: Synergy for enterococcal/streptococcal endocarditis
  • Tobramycin: Best anti-pseudomonal; inhaled for CF
  • Amikacin: Broadest; often active against gentamicin-resistant organisms
  • Streptomycin: TB, tularemia, plague
PK/PD & Dosing
  • Concentration-dependent killing: High Cmax/MIC ratio desired
  • Post-antibiotic effect: Continued killing after drug cleared
  • Extended-interval dosing (once daily): Higher peak, lower toxicity
  • Traditional dosing: Still used for synergy (e.g., endocarditis: gentamicin 1 mg/kg q8h)
🚨 TOXICITY
  • Nephrotoxicity: Usually reversible; risk ↑ with duration, other nephrotoxins
  • Ototoxicity: Vestibular (gentamicin, streptomycin) or cochlear (amikacin); may be irreversible
  • Neuromuscular blockade: Rare; risk in myasthenia gravis

Other Important Antibiotics

HIGH YIELD
Daptomycin
  • Spectrum: Gram-positive only; excellent MRSA, VRE activity
  • Mechanism: Calcium-dependent membrane depolarization
  • Key uses: MRSA bacteremia, right-sided endocarditis, complicated SSTI
  • Contraindicated in pneumonia: Inactivated by pulmonary surfactant
  • Toxicity: CPK elevation (check weekly); eosinophilic pneumonia
  • Dosing: 6 mg/kg for bacteremia; 8-10 mg/kg may improve outcomes
Linezolid
  • Spectrum: Gram-positive including MRSA, VRE
  • Mechanism: Oxazolidinone; inhibits 50S ribosome initiation
  • Key uses: MRSA pneumonia (penetrates lung well), VRE infections
  • 100% oral bioavailability: IV = PO dosing
  • Toxicity: Myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia > anemia), peripheral/optic neuropathy, serotonin syndrome with SSRIs, lactic acidosis
  • Duration limit: Use <2 weeks if possible (myelosuppression)
Metronidazole
  • Spectrum: Anaerobes (NOT aerobic bacteria), protozoa
  • Key uses: C. difficile, intra-abdominal infections, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, amebiasis, giardiasis
  • Excellent oral bioavailability
  • Toxicity: Disulfiram reaction with alcohol, metallic taste, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use)
  • CNS penetration: Excellent; used for brain abscesses (anaerobic component)
Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)
  • Spectrum: CA-MRSA, PCP, Nocardia, Stenotrophomonas, Listeria
  • Key uses: PCP prophylaxis/treatment, CA-MRSA SSTI, UTI
  • Toxicity: Hyperkalemia, myelosuppression (especially with methotrexate), rash/SJS, crystalluria
  • Drug interactions: Warfarin (↑ INR), methotrexate toxicity, ACE-I (hyperkalemia)
Doxycycline
  • Spectrum: Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella), MRSA, Rickettsia, spirochetes
  • Key uses: CAP (atypical coverage), MRSA SSTI, STIs, Lyme disease, RMSF, malaria prophylaxis
  • Safe in renal failure: No adjustment needed
  • Toxicity: Photosensitivity, esophageal ulceration (take with water, upright), teeth discoloration (avoid in children <8)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Daptomycin vs Linezolid for MRSA
Feature Daptomycin Linezolid
Pneumonia ❌ NO (inactivated by surfactant) ✅ YES (excellent lung penetration)
Bacteremia ✅ Preferred ✅ Alternative
Oral option ❌ IV only ✅ 100% bioavailability
Key toxicity CPK elevation Myelosuppression

🫁 Respiratory Infections

🦠

Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)

HIGH YIELD
📘 ATS/IDSA CAP Guidelines (2019)
Major changes from prior guidelines:
  • Eliminated "HCAP" category — treat based on risk factors, not healthcare exposure
  • Recommend against routine procalcitonin to determine need for antibiotics in CAP
  • Recommend against corticosteroids routinely (except if refractory shock)
Common Pathogens
  • Most common overall: S. pneumoniae
  • Atypicals: Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella
  • Viral: Influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2, rhinovirus
  • Aspiration: Anaerobes (especially elderly, altered mental status)
  • No pathogen identified: Up to 50% of cases
Empiric Treatment (ATS/IDSA 2019)
Setting Preferred Regimen Alternative
Outpatient, healthy Amoxicillin 1g TID OR doxycycline OR macrolide (if resistance <25%) —
Outpatient, comorbidities Amox-clav + macrolide OR respiratory FQ monotherapy Cephalosporin + macrolide
Inpatient, non-severe β-lactam (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam) + macrolide Respiratory FQ monotherapy
Inpatient, severe (ICU) β-lactam + macrolide β-lactam + respiratory FQ
⚡ BOARD PEARL: MRSA/Pseudomonas Coverage in CAP
Only add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage if:
  • Prior respiratory isolation of MRSA or Pseudomonas
  • Recent hospitalization AND parenteral antibiotics (both required)
Do NOT routinely add based on: Nursing home residence alone, recent hospitalization without antibiotics
Severity Assessment: CURB-65
  • Confusion
  • Uremia (BUN >20 mg/dL)
  • Respiratory rate ≥30
  • Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)
  • 65 years or older

Score interpretation: 0-1: outpatient; 2: short inpatient or close outpatient; ≥3: inpatient/ICU

📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: Legionella

Scenario: 55-year-old smoker returning from cruise ship with fever, dry cough, diarrhea, confusion, hyponatremia (Na 128).

Clues: Travel (water source), GI symptoms, hyponatremia, confusion out of proportion to pneumonia severity

Diagnosis: Legionella pneumophila — urine antigen (detects L. pneumophila serogroup 1)

Treatment: Azithromycin or fluoroquinolone (preferred over β-lactams)

Duration of Therapy
  • Minimum 5 days for most CAP
  • Should be afebrile for 48-72 hours before stopping
  • Clinically stable (HR, RR, BP, O2, mental status)
  • Longer duration (7-14 days): Legionella, Pseudomonas, MRSA, lung abscess

Hospital-Acquired & Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia

HIGH YIELD
Definitions
  • HAP: Pneumonia occurring ≥48 hours after hospital admission (not incubating at admission)
  • VAP: Pneumonia occurring >48 hours after endotracheal intubation
📘 ATS/IDSA HAP/VAP Guidelines (2016)
Key risk factors for MDR pathogens:
  • IV antibiotics in prior 90 days
  • Septic shock at time of VAP
  • ARDS preceding VAP
  • ≥5 days hospitalization prior to VAP
  • Acute renal replacement therapy prior to VAP
  • Most important: Local antibiogram data (>10-20% resistance = add coverage)
Common Pathogens
  • Gram-negative: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella, E. coli, Enterobacter, Acinetobacter
  • MRSA: Especially if prior colonization, hemodialysis, prior antibiotics
  • Polymicrobial: Common
Empiric Treatment
Risk Category Regimen
No MDR risk factors Single agent: Pip-tazo, cefepime, levofloxacin, or meropenem
MDR risk factors OR high mortality risk Two anti-pseudomonal agents (different classes) + MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: De-escalation
  • De-escalate based on culture results at 48-72 hours
  • If cultures negative and patient improving, consider stopping MRSA coverage
  • Narrowest effective spectrum reduces resistance selection and C. diff risk
  • Duration: 7 days for most VAP (not 14-21 days as previously)

Aspiration Pneumonia & Lung Abscess

HIGH YIELD
Key Concepts
  • Aspiration pneumonitis: Chemical injury from gastric acid; does NOT require antibiotics unless secondary infection develops
  • Aspiration pneumonia: Infection from aspirated oropharyngeal bacteria; requires antibiotics
Aspiration Pneumonia
  • Risk factors: Dysphagia, altered consciousness, GERD, NG tube, poor dentition
  • Pathogens: Streptococci, anaerobes (if poor dentition/periodontal disease), gram-negatives (if hospitalized)
  • Location: Right lower lobe (when upright), posterior segments of upper lobes (when supine)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Anaerobic Coverage
Anaerobic coverage NOT routinely needed for aspiration pneumonia unless:
  • Severe periodontal disease
  • Putrid sputum
  • Lung abscess or empyema present
  • Evidence of necrotizing pneumonia
If needed: Ampicillin-sulbactam, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or add metronidazole
Lung Abscess
  • Etiology: Aspiration (most common), post-obstruction (cancer), hematogenous (Staph, septic emboli)
  • Microbiology: Polymicrobial; anaerobes + streptococci; consider MRSA if risk factors
  • Treatment: Antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam, clindamycin, or metronidazole + β-lactam)
  • Duration: Until cavity resolves or minimal stable residual (often 4-8 weeks)
  • Drainage: Consider if >6 cm, not responding to antibiotics, or empyema
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 62-year-old alcoholic with poor dentition presents with 3 weeks of fever, productive cough with foul-smelling sputum. CXR shows cavitary lesion in right lower lobe.

Diagnosis: Lung abscess (aspiration, anaerobes)

Treatment: Ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin × weeks until cavity resolves

Tuberculosis

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • TST (PPD): Positive based on risk category (≥5mm, ≥10mm, ≥15mm)
  • IGRA (QuantiFERON, T-SPOT): More specific, single visit, not affected by BCG
  • Sputum AFB smear: 3 specimens; smear-positive = higher infectivity
  • Sputum culture: Gold standard; takes 2-6 weeks
  • NAAT (GeneXpert): Rapid (within hours); can detect rifampin resistance
📝 TST INTERPRETATION
≥5mm: HIV, close contacts, CXR fibrotic changes, immunosuppressed
≥10mm: High-risk groups (immigrants, IVDU, healthcare workers)
≥15mm: Low-risk individuals
Treatment of Active TB
Drug Mechanism Key Toxicity
Isoniazid (INH) Inhibits mycolic acid synthesis Hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy (give pyridoxine)
Rifampin (RIF) Inhibits RNA polymerase Hepatotoxicity, orange secretions, CYP450 inducer
Pyrazinamide (PZA) Disrupts membrane function Hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia, arthralgias
Ethambutol (EMB) Inhibits arabinosyl transferase Optic neuritis (dose-related, reversible)
📘 ATS/CDC/IDSA TB Guidelines
Standard regimen for drug-susceptible TB:
  • Intensive phase: RIPE × 2 months
  • Continuation phase: INH + RIF × 4 months (total 6 months)
  • Directly observed therapy (DOT): Strongly recommended
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Latent TB Treatment
  • 3HP: Weekly INH + rifapentine × 12 weeks (preferred for most)
  • 4R: Daily rifampin × 4 months
  • 9H: Daily INH × 9 months (traditional but long)
  • Always check for active TB before treating LTBI (symptoms, CXR)
🚨 TB DRUG INTERACTIONS
  • Rifampin is potent CYP450 inducer: Decreases levels of warfarin, OCPs, HIV meds, immunosuppressants, methadone
  • Rifabutin: Less CYP450 induction; use with HIV meds
  • INH: Inhibits monoamine oxidase; tyramine reaction possible

🧠 CNS Infections

🦠

Bacterial Meningitis

HIGH YIELD
🚨 MEDICAL EMERGENCY
Give antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation. Delays in antibiotic administration increase mortality. Do NOT wait for LP if will delay antibiotics.
Pathogens by Risk Group
Age/Risk Factor Common Pathogens Empiric Therapy
Adult (18-50) S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis Ceftriaxone + vancomycin
Age >50 + Listeria monocytogenes Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin
Immunocompromised + Listeria, gram-negatives Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (± meropenem)
Neurosurgery/trauma S. aureus, gram-negatives Vancomycin + cefepime (or meropenem)
📘 IDSA Meningitis Guidelines (2004, reaffirmed)
Dexamethasone:
  • Give 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days BEFORE or WITH first dose of antibiotics
  • Benefit proven for S. pneumoniae meningitis (↓ mortality, neurologic sequelae)
  • Continue only if pneumococcal meningitis confirmed (or suspected)
  • Less clear benefit for other pathogens
CSF Findings
Parameter Bacterial Viral TB/Fungal
Opening pressure ↑↑ (>25 cmH₂O) Normal-↑ ↑
WBC count >1000 (often >10,000) 10-500 50-500
Predominant cell Neutrophils (>80%) Lymphocytes Lymphocytes
Glucose <40 mg/dL Normal ↓
Protein >250 mg/dL <100 ↑ (100-500)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Listeria Coverage
Add ampicillin for Listeria coverage if:
  • Age ≥50 years
  • Immunocompromised (steroids, transplant, HIV, malignancy)
  • Alcoholism
  • Pregnancy
Ceftriaxone does NOT cover Listeria.
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 68-year-old with fever, headache, stiff neck, altered mental status. CSF shows 2500 WBC (90% PMNs), glucose 25, protein 320.

Management:

  1. Dexamethasone (before or with antibiotics)
  2. Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (age >50 = Listeria coverage)
  3. CT head if focal deficits or papilledema before LP

Encephalitis

HIGH YIELD
Key Features Distinguishing from Meningitis
  • Altered mental status/personality changes — hallmark
  • Seizures
  • Focal neurologic deficits
  • Movement disorders
  • Meningismus may be present or absent
Etiology
  • HSV-1: Most common cause of sporadic encephalitis (treat empirically!)
  • Arboviruses: West Nile, Eastern equine, La Crosse (seasonal, summer-fall)
  • VZV: Especially immunocompromised
  • Autoimmune: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis (young women, ovarian teratoma)
🚨 HSV ENCEPHALITIS
  • Start acyclovir empirically — do not wait for PCR results
  • MRI: Temporal lobe involvement (T2/FLAIR hyperintensity)
  • EEG: Periodic lateralizing epileptiform discharges (PLEDs)
  • CSF HSV PCR: May be negative early; repeat if high suspicion
  • Treatment: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 14-21 days
  • Mortality without treatment: 70%
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: HSV Encephalitis

Scenario: 35-year-old with 3 days of fever, headache, confusion, and new-onset seizures. MRI shows bilateral temporal lobe enhancement. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis.

Answer: HSV encephalitis. Start IV acyclovir immediately. Send CSF HSV PCR.

📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: West Nile

Scenario: 72-year-old in late summer with fever, weakness, flaccid paralysis, tremor. CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis.

Answer: West Nile virus neuroinvasive disease. Polio-like flaccid paralysis is characteristic. Diagnosis: serum/CSF WNV IgM. No specific treatment.

Brain Abscess

HIGH YIELD
Etiology & Sources
  • Contiguous spread (40-50%): Sinusitis, otitis, dental infection → frontal/temporal lobe
  • Hematogenous (25%): Endocarditis, pulmonary AVMs, lung abscess → multiple abscesses
  • Trauma/neurosurgery: S. aureus predominant
  • Cryptogenic (25%): No source identified
Microbiology
  • Polymicrobial common (30-60%)
  • Streptococci: Viridans group, S. anginosus (milleri) group
  • Anaerobes: Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella
  • S. aureus: Trauma, neurosurgery, endocarditis
  • Gram-negatives: Otogenic source, immunocompromised
  • Immunocompromised: Nocardia, Toxoplasma, Aspergillus, Cryptococcus
Treatment
  • Empiric antibiotics: Ceftriaxone + metronidazole (± vancomycin if MRSA risk)
  • Duration: 6-8 weeks IV
  • Surgical drainage: Abscesses >2.5 cm, mass effect, no improvement with antibiotics
  • Steroids: For significant mass effect (controversial)
⚡ BOARD PEARL
LP is contraindicated in brain abscess due to risk of herniation. Diagnosis is made by neuroimaging (MRI with contrast: ring-enhancing lesion with restricted diffusion).

❤️ Cardiovascular Infections

🦠

Infective Endocarditis

HIGH YIELD
📘 Modified Duke Criteria (AHA 2015)
Definite IE: 2 major, OR 1 major + 3 minor, OR 5 minor criteria

Major Criteria:
  1. Positive blood cultures (typical organism × 2, or persistently positive)
  2. Echo evidence (vegetation, abscess, new dehiscence, new valvular regurgitation)
Minor Criteria:
  • Predisposition (IVDU, structural heart disease)
  • Fever ≥38°C
  • Vascular phenomena (septic emboli, Janeway lesions, mycotic aneurysm)
  • Immunologic phenomena (Osler nodes, Roth spots, + RF)
  • Microbiologic evidence not meeting major criteria
Microbiology
Organism Patient Population Key Features
S. aureus IVDU, healthcare-associated, devices Most common overall; acute presentation; high mortality
Viridans streptococci Native valve, dental procedures Subacute; large vegetations
Enterococcus Elderly, GI/GU procedures Requires combination therapy (β-lactam + aminoglycoside or ampicillin + ceftriaxone)
HACEK organisms Native valve Fastidious; prolonged incubation; large vegetations
Coagulase-negative staph Prosthetic valves Presents <1 year after surgery; often need surgery
📝 HACEK ORGANISMS
Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella
Empiric Treatment (AHA 2015)
Scenario Regimen Duration
Native valve, empiric Vancomycin + ceftriaxone Until culture data
Prosthetic valve, empiric Vancomycin + cefepime + gentamicin + rifampin Until culture data
MSSA native valve Nafcillin or oxacillin 6 weeks
MRSA native valve Vancomycin or daptomycin 6 weeks
Viridans strep (pen-susceptible) Penicillin G or ceftriaxone 4 weeks (native), 6 weeks (prosthetic)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: S. aureus Bacteremia Workup
ALL S. aureus bacteremia requires:
  • Repeat blood cultures q48h until negative
  • Echocardiogram (TEE preferred, especially for prosthetic valves/devices)
  • Evaluation for metastatic infection
  • Minimum 2 weeks IV antibiotics (4-6 weeks if complicated)
"Uncomplicated" criteria (can consider 2 weeks): No device, TEE negative, no metastatic infection, defervescence within 72h, negative repeat cultures within 4 days
🚨 SURGICAL INDICATIONS FOR IE
  • Heart failure: Due to severe valve dysfunction
  • Uncontrolled infection: Persistent bacteremia/fever >5-7 days on appropriate therapy
  • Perivalvular extension: Abscess, fistula, heart block
  • Fungal endocarditis: Almost always requires surgery
  • Large vegetation (>10mm) with embolic events
  • Prosthetic valve endocarditis with relapse
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 32-year-old IVDU with fever, new murmur at left sternal border (holosystolic, increases with inspiration). Blood cultures grow S. aureus.

Diagnosis: Tricuspid valve endocarditis (right-sided IE in IVDU)

Management: IV nafcillin/oxacillin (if MSSA) × 4-6 weeks; TEE to assess for complications

CIED Infections (Pacemaker/ICD)

HIGH YIELD
Classification
  • Pocket infection: Localized to generator site (erythema, swelling, drainage, erosion)
  • Lead infection/CIED-IE: Vegetation on lead or valve
Microbiology
  • Most common: Coagulase-negative staphylococci (CoNS)
  • S. aureus: More virulent; higher complication rate
  • Gram-negatives: Less common; may indicate contamination
🚨 KEY PRINCIPLE
Complete device removal is required for all CIED infections (pocket or lead). Antibiotics alone are insufficient and associated with high failure rates.
⚡ BOARD PEARL
S. aureus bacteremia in patient with CIED = presume device infection until proven otherwise. Get TEE and plan for device extraction.

🦠 GI & Hepatobiliary Infections

💊

Clostridioides difficile Infection

HIGH YIELD
📘 IDSA/SHEA CDI Guidelines (2021 Update)
Major change: Fidaxomicin now preferred over oral vancomycin for initial episodes (reduces recurrence)
Diagnosis
  • Only test symptomatic patients (≥3 unformed stools/24h)
  • Preferred: NAAT (PCR) OR multistep algorithm (GDH + toxin EIA, then NAAT if discordant)
  • Do NOT test for cure: Can remain positive for weeks after treatment
Severity Classification & Treatment
Severity Criteria Treatment
Non-severe WBC <15,000, Cr <1.5 Fidaxomicin 200mg BID × 10d (preferred) OR vancomycin 125mg PO QID × 10d
Severe WBC ≥15,000 OR Cr ≥1.5 Fidaxomicin 200mg BID × 10d (preferred) OR vancomycin 125mg PO QID × 10d
Fulminant Hypotension, ileus, megacolon Vancomycin 500mg PO/NG QID + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h ± vancomycin enemas
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Recurrent CDI
  • 1st recurrence: Fidaxomicin (if vanc used initially) OR prolonged vancomycin taper-pulse
  • 2nd recurrence: Vancomycin taper-pulse, then rifaximin chaser; consider bezlotoxumab (anti-toxin B antibody)
  • ≥3 recurrences: Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) highly effective (85-90% cure)
🚨 DO NOT USE
  • Metronidazole for non-fulminant CDI: Inferior to vancomycin; no longer first-line
  • Antimotility agents: Can precipitate toxic megacolon
  • Probiotics for treatment: Not proven effective; may use for prevention only

Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • Ascitic fluid PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ = diagnostic (treat empirically)
  • Culture positive in ~40% — negative cultures don't exclude SBP
  • Most common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, S. pneumoniae
Treatment
  • Empiric: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily × 5 days
  • Alternative: Ciprofloxacin (if not on FQ prophylaxis)
  • Albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3 (if Cr >1, BUN >30, or bilirubin >4) — reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality
📘 AASLD Guidelines: SBP Prophylaxis
  • GI bleeding in cirrhosis: Ceftriaxone 1g daily × 7 days (all patients)
  • Prior SBP: Lifelong prophylaxis (ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX)
  • Low protein ascites (<1.5 g/dL) with renal dysfunction or liver failure: Consider prophylaxis
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Cirrhotic patient with fever, abdominal pain, altered mental status. Paracentesis shows 400 PMNs/mm³.

Answer: SBP. Start ceftriaxone + albumin. Do NOT wait for cultures.

Acute Cholangitis

HIGH YIELD
📝 CHARCOT'S TRIAD & REYNOLDS' PENTAD
Charcot's triad: Fever + RUQ pain + Jaundice
Reynolds' pentad: + Altered mental status + Hypotension (severe cholangitis)
Treatment Principles
  • Antibiotics: Cover gram-negatives and anaerobes (pip-tazo, ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or ampicillin-sulbactam)
  • Biliary drainage: Essential — ERCP within 24-48 hours (urgent if severe)
  • Duration: 4-7 days after adequate source control

🔬 Genitourinary Infections

💊

Urinary Tract Infections

HIGH YIELD
📘 IDSA UTI Guidelines (2010 Cystitis, 2022 Catheter-Associated)
Key principle: Asymptomatic bacteriuria should NOT be treated (except pregnant women and pre-urologic surgery)
Uncomplicated Cystitis (Women)
Agent Regimen Notes
Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID × 5 days Avoid if CrCl <30 or pyelonephritis suspected
TMP-SMX DS BID × 3 days If local resistance <20%
Fosfomycin 3g single dose Less effective than multi-day regimens; convenient
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Fluoroquinolones NOT First-Line for UTI
  • Reserve FQs for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI
  • Risk of serious adverse effects outweighs benefit for cystitis
  • Rising E. coli resistance (20-30% in many areas)
Pyelonephritis
  • Outpatient (mild): Fluoroquinolone × 5-7 days OR TMP-SMX × 14 days
  • Inpatient: Ceftriaxone, fluoroquinolone, or pip-tazo (if severe/septic)
  • Duration: 5-14 days depending on agent and response
  • Imaging: Consider CT if not improving by 48-72 hours (abscess? obstruction?)
🚨 CATHETER-ASSOCIATED UTI (CAUTI)
  • Only treat if symptomatic: Fever, suprapubic pain, CVA tenderness, altered mental status
  • Remove or replace catheter before obtaining cultures
  • Pyuria alone is NOT an indication for treatment
  • Duration: 7 days (14 days if delayed response)

Prostatitis

CORE
Acute Bacterial Prostatitis
  • Presentation: Fever, dysuria, perineal pain, exquisitely tender prostate
  • Pathogens: E. coli (most common), Pseudomonas, Enterococcus
  • Treatment: Fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX × 4-6 weeks (need prostate penetration)
  • Avoid prostatic massage: Risk of bacteremia
⚡ BOARD PEARL
Chronic bacterial prostatitis is a cause of recurrent UTI in men with the same organism. Requires prolonged therapy (6-12 weeks) with prostate-penetrating antibiotic (FQ or TMP-SMX).

🦠 Skin & Soft Tissue Infections

💊

Cellulitis & Erysipelas

HIGH YIELD
Key Distinctions
  • Erysipelas: Superficial (dermis), well-demarcated raised borders, "peau d'orange," face or lower extremity; β-hemolytic streptococci
  • Cellulitis: Deeper (dermis + subcutaneous), poorly demarcated; β-hemolytic strep or S. aureus
📘 IDSA SSTI Guidelines (2014)
Purulent vs Non-Purulent determines MRSA coverage:
  • Non-purulent (no abscess): β-hemolytic strep most likely; cephalexin or dicloxacillin
  • Purulent (abscess, pustule): S. aureus (often CA-MRSA); I&D ± TMP-SMX or doxycycline
Treatment
Type Outpatient Inpatient
Non-purulent Cephalexin, dicloxacillin Cefazolin, nafcillin
Purulent (MRSA coverage) TMP-SMX, doxycycline + I&D Vancomycin + I&D
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Cellulitis Mimics
  • Stasis dermatitis: Bilateral, chronic, scaling; no fever
  • DVT: Unilateral swelling, less erythema; check D-dimer/duplex
  • Gout: Joint involvement, tophi
