  • Contact dermatitis: Pruritus, vesicles, linear pattern

Necrotizing Fasciitis

HIGH YIELD
🚨 SURGICAL EMERGENCY
Mortality increases with every hour delay to debridement. Do NOT wait for imaging if clinical suspicion is high. Surgery is diagnostic and therapeutic.
Classification
  • Type I (polymicrobial): Mixed aerobes + anaerobes; occurs in DM, PVD, post-operative
  • Type II (monomicrobial): Group A Strep ("flesh-eating bacteria") or S. aureus; healthy individuals
  • Type III: Clostridial myonecrosis (gas gangrene); trauma/surgery
Clinical Features (vs Cellulitis)
  • Pain out of proportion to exam findings — key feature
  • Rapid progression despite antibiotics
  • Bullae, crepitus, skin necrosis
  • Systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension)
  • Wooden-hard induration on exam
Treatment
  • Surgical debridement: Urgent and often repeated
  • Antibiotics: Vancomycin + pip-tazo (or carbapenem) + clindamycin
  • Clindamycin: Inhibits toxin production (add for GAS or clostridial)
  • IVIG: May be considered for streptococcal toxic shock
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Diabetic patient with leg wound, fever, hypotension, crepitus on exam, pain out of proportion to findings.

Answer: Necrotizing fasciitis. Immediate surgical debridement + broad-spectrum antibiotics (vanc + pip-tazo + clindamycin). Do NOT wait for CT.

Diabetic Foot Infections

HIGH YIELD
📘 IDSA DFI Guidelines (2012)
Severity guides treatment setting and antibiotics:
Microbiology (Depends on Severity)
  • Mild (superficial): S. aureus, β-hemolytic strep
  • Moderate-severe/chronic: Polymicrobial: Staph, Strep, Enterococcus, gram-negatives, anaerobes
  • MRSA risk factors: Prior MRSA, recent hospitalization/antibiotics, high local prevalence
  • Pseudomonas: Not routinely covered unless macerated wound, water exposure, or failure
Key Management Principles
  • Wound care: Debridement, offloading
  • Assess for osteomyelitis: Probe-to-bone test, MRI
  • Vascular assessment: Many need revascularization
  • Antibiotics: Culture-directed when possible; duration 1-2 weeks for soft tissue, 4-6 weeks if osteomyelitis
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Probe-to-Bone Test
If sterile metal probe reaches bone through ulcer, probability of osteomyelitis is high (~85% positive predictive value). Order MRI for confirmation.

🦴 Bone & Joint Infections

🦠

Osteomyelitis

HIGH YIELD
Classification
  • Hematogenous: Children (metaphysis of long bones), adults (vertebral)
  • Contiguous (with or without vascular insufficiency): Diabetic foot, pressure ulcers, post-surgical
Microbiology
Setting Common Pathogens
Hematogenous (adults) S. aureus (#1), gram-negatives, streptococci
Vertebral S. aureus, gram-negatives, TB (Pott's disease), Brucella
Contiguous/diabetic Polymicrobial: Staph, strep, GNR, anaerobes
IVDU S. aureus, Pseudomonas (vertebral)
Sickle cell Salmonella (unique), S. aureus
Diagnosis
  • Gold standard: Bone biopsy with culture (obtain before antibiotics when possible)
  • MRI: Most sensitive imaging (T1 hypointense, T2/STIR hyperintense, enhancement)
  • ESR/CRP: Elevated; useful for monitoring response
📘 IDSA Vertebral Osteomyelitis Guidelines (2015)
  • Duration: 6 weeks of IV or highly bioavailable oral antibiotics
  • Oral antibiotics: May be used after initial IV therapy if good bioavailability and susceptibilities known (OVIVA trial)
  • Surgery: Neurologic deficit, spinal instability, failure of medical therapy
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Sickle cell patient with femoral pain, fever, x-ray shows periosteal elevation.

Answer: Osteomyelitis — consider Salmonella (most common in sickle cell) alongside S. aureus. MRI for confirmation; bone biopsy ideal before antibiotics.

Septic Arthritis

HIGH YIELD
🚨 ORTHOPAEDIC EMERGENCY
Delay in treatment leads to irreversible joint destruction. If suspected, aspirate joint immediately and start empiric antibiotics.
Diagnosis
  • Synovial fluid WBC >50,000 (often >100,000) with PMN predominance is highly suggestive
  • Gram stain positive in ~50%
  • Culture gold standard
  • Blood cultures: Positive in ~50%
Microbiology
  • S. aureus: Most common in adults
  • N. gonorrhoeae: Young, sexually active (disseminated gonococcal infection)
  • Streptococci, gram-negatives: Immunocompromised, IVDU
Treatment
  • Empiric: Vancomycin (MRSA coverage) + ceftriaxone (GN/gonococcal coverage)
  • Drainage: Serial aspiration or surgical drainage (especially hip, prosthetic joint)
  • Duration: Typically 2-4 weeks (longer for S. aureus)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Disseminated Gonococcal Infection
  • Two syndromes:
    1. Triad of migratory polyarthralgia + tenosynovitis + pustular skin lesions (arthritis-dermatitis syndrome)
    2. Purulent monoarthritis (usually knee)
  • Treatment: Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily × 7 days (switch to PO cefixime after 24-48h improvement)
  • Test and treat for chlamydia concurrently

Prosthetic Joint Infection

HIGH YIELD
Classification by Timing
  • Early (<3 months): S. aureus, gram-negatives; perioperative inoculation
  • Delayed (3-12 months): CoNS, Propionibacterium; low-virulence organisms
  • Late (>12 months): Hematogenous seeding; S. aureus, streptococci
Treatment Strategies
  • DAIR (debridement, antibiotics, implant retention): Early infection (<30 days), stable implant, no sinus tract
  • One-stage exchange: Remove and replace in single surgery (Europe)
  • Two-stage exchange: Remove → antibiotic spacer → weeks of antibiotics → reimplant (most common in US)
  • Chronic suppression: When surgery not feasible
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Rifampin in PJI
Rifampin is critical for staphylococcal PJI when hardware retained — penetrates biofilm. Always use with second agent (never monotherapy → rapid resistance). Typically add after bacteremia cleared.

🔴 Sepsis & Septic Shock

🚨

Sepsis Definitions & Diagnosis

HIGH YIELD
📘 Sepsis-3 Definitions (JAMA 2016)
  • Sepsis: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection (SOFA score ≥2 points above baseline)
  • Septic shock: Sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation
qSOFA (Quick SOFA) — Bedside Screening
  • Respiratory rate ≥22/min
  • Altered mental status (GCS <15)
  • Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg

≥2 criteria: High risk for poor outcome; evaluate for organ dysfunction with full SOFA

SOFA Score Components
  • Respiration: PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio
  • Coagulation: Platelet count
  • Liver: Bilirubin
  • Cardiovascular: MAP and vasopressor requirement
  • CNS: Glasgow Coma Scale
  • Renal: Creatinine and urine output
⚡ BOARD PEARL: SIRS vs Sepsis-3
  • SIRS criteria no longer define sepsis — too sensitive, not specific (many non-infectious causes)
  • Sepsis-3 emphasizes organ dysfunction (SOFA score)
  • However: SIRS still useful for early identification; CMS quality measures still reference it

Sepsis Management: Hour-1 Bundle

HIGH YIELD
🚨 TIME IS CRITICAL
Mortality increases 7-8% for each hour delay in antibiotic administration.
📘 Surviving Sepsis Campaign (2021 Update)
Hour-1 Bundle — Start immediately upon sepsis recognition:
  1. Measure lactate — remeasure if initial >2 mmol/L
  2. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics (but don't delay abx >45 min)
  3. Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics
  4. Begin fluid resuscitation: 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  5. Apply vasopressors if hypotensive during/after fluids (target MAP ≥65)
Fluid Resuscitation
  • Crystalloid preferred: Balanced solutions (LR, Plasmalyte) may be preferred over NS (less hyperchloremic acidosis)
  • Initial bolus: 30 mL/kg for hypotension or hypoperfusion
  • Reassess fluid responsiveness: Dynamic measures (pulse pressure variation, passive leg raise) preferred over static (CVP)
  • Avoid fluid overload: Associated with worse outcomes; reassess frequently
Vasopressor Selection
Agent Mechanism Role
Norepinephrine α1 > β1 agonist First-line; increases MAP with minimal chronotropy
Vasopressin V1 receptor agonist Second-line; add at 0.03-0.04 U/min to reduce norepinephrine dose
Epinephrine α + β agonist Third-line if refractory; increases lactate (confounds monitoring)
Dopamine D1, β1, α1 (dose-dependent) NOT preferred — increased arrhythmia risk
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Corticosteroids in Septic Shock
  • Consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg IV q6h or continuous infusion) if hemodynamically unstable despite fluids + vasopressors
  • ADRENAL/APROCCHSS trials: Faster shock reversal, possible small mortality benefit in severe shock
  • Do NOT use ACTH stimulation test to guide decision
  • Fludrocortisone: Not routinely added (ADRENAL used hydrocortisone alone)

Source Control

HIGH YIELD
🚨 SOURCE CONTROL IS ESSENTIAL
Identify and control source within 6-12 hours. Antibiotics alone cannot cure undrained infections.
Interventions
  • Drain abscesses: Percutaneous or surgical
  • Remove infected devices: Central lines, pacemakers, prosthetic joints
  • Debride necrotic tissue: Necrotizing fasciitis, gangrenous bowel
  • Relieve obstructions: Biliary stent, nephrostomy tube
  • Definitive surgery: Cholecystectomy, bowel resection if needed
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Patient with septic shock from cholangitis, not improving after 24 hours of antibiotics and fluids.

Answer: Needs emergent biliary drainage (ERCP or percutaneous). Source control is essential — antibiotics cannot sterilize obstructed biliary system.

Empiric Antibiotic Selection

HIGH YIELD
General Principles
  • Broad-spectrum initially: Cover likely pathogens based on suspected source
  • Consider local resistance patterns: Know your antibiogram
  • Consider patient risk factors: Recent antibiotics, healthcare exposure, immunocompromise
  • De-escalate at 48-72 hours: Based on culture results and clinical response
Suspected Source Empiric Regimen
Unknown source Vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (or cefepime + metronidazole)
Pulmonary (CAP) Ceftriaxone + azithromycin (± vancomycin if MRSA risk)
Pulmonary (HAP/VAP) Vancomycin + antipseudomonal β-lactam (± aminoglycoside if MDR risk)
Intra-abdominal Piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole
Urinary Ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone (+ ampicillin if Enterococcus risk)
Skin/soft tissue Vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (cover MRSA + GN + anaerobes)
Neutropenic fever Cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam (monotherapy usually adequate initially)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Duration
  • Most sepsis: 7-10 days typically adequate
  • Procalcitonin-guided: Can safely shorten duration; stop if PCT <0.5 or ↓80% from peak
  • Longer courses: S. aureus bacteremia (2+ weeks), endocarditis, osteomyelitis

🎗️ HIV/AIDS

🦠

HIV Diagnosis & Staging

HIGH YIELD
Diagnostic Testing (CDC 2014)
  • 4th generation Ag/Ab combo: Initial screening (detects p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies); window period ~18 days
  • HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation: If initial test positive
  • HIV-1 RNA (viral load): If antibody tests indeterminate or acute infection suspected
AIDS-Defining Conditions (CD4 <200 OR AIDS-defining illness)
  • Opportunistic infections: PCP, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcal meningitis, CMV retinitis, MAC
  • Malignancies: Kaposi sarcoma, primary CNS lymphoma, invasive cervical cancer
  • Other: HIV wasting, HIV encephalopathy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: CD4 Thresholds for OIs
CD4 Count Associated Infections
<500 Kaposi sarcoma, TB, bacterial pneumonia
<200 PCP, mucocutaneous candidiasis, Cryptococcus
<100 Toxoplasmosis, Cryptosporidium
<50 CMV, MAC, CNS lymphoma, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)

Opportunistic Infections

HIGH YIELD
Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PCP)
  • Presentation: Subacute dyspnea, dry cough, fever over days-weeks; bilateral interstitial infiltrates; elevated LDH
  • Diagnosis: Induced sputum or BAL with silver stain, DFA, or PCR; β-D-glucan elevated
  • Treatment: TMP-SMX (15-20 mg/kg/day TMP) × 21 days
  • Steroids if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient ≥35: Prednisone 40mg BID × 5 days, taper over 21 days
Toxoplasma Encephalitis
  • Presentation: Headache, confusion, focal deficits, seizures; CD4 <100
  • Imaging: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions with mass effect (basal ganglia common)
  • Diagnosis: + Toxoplasma IgG + typical imaging = empiric treatment
  • Treatment: Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin × 6 weeks (then maintenance)
  • Alternative: TMP-SMX (preferred for prophylaxis)
Cryptococcal Meningitis
  • Presentation: Subacute headache, fever, altered mental status; CD4 <100
  • Diagnosis: CSF cryptococcal antigen (very sensitive), India ink (less sensitive), culture
  • Treatment: Amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks (induction) → fluconazole (consolidation/maintenance)
  • Elevated ICP: Serial LPs for pressure management (goal: opening pressure <20 cmH2O)
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: HIV+ patient with CD4 50 presents with weeks of headache, fever, neck stiffness. CSF shows elevated opening pressure, low WBC, low glucose. India ink positive.

Answer: Cryptococcal meningitis. Treat with amphotericin B + flucytosine. Perform serial LPs to manage elevated ICP.

CMV Disease (CD4 <50)
  • CMV retinitis: "Pizza pie" appearance (hemorrhages + exudates); treat with ganciclovir or foscarnet; intravitreal + systemic
  • CMV colitis: Bloody diarrhea, ulcers on colonoscopy
  • CMV esophagitis: Large shallow ulcers (vs HSV: small deep ulcers)
Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC)
  • Presentation: Fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, hepatosplenomegaly; CD4 <50
  • Diagnosis: Blood cultures (isolator lysis-centrifugation), bone marrow biopsy
  • Treatment: Clarithromycin (or azithromycin) + ethambutol ± rifabutin
  • Prophylaxis: Azithromycin 1200mg weekly if CD4 <50

OI Prophylaxis

HIGH YIELD
Infection CD4 Threshold Prophylaxis Stop When
PCP <200 or oropharyngeal candidiasis TMP-SMX DS daily (preferred) CD4 >200 × 3 months
Toxoplasma <100 + IgG+ TMP-SMX DS daily CD4 >200 × 3 months
MAC <50 Azithromycin 1200mg weekly CD4 >100 × 3 months
Cryptococcus After treatment Fluconazole 200mg daily CD4 >100 × 3 months + ART × 1 year
⚡ BOARD PEARL: TMP-SMX is a "2-for-1"
TMP-SMX DS daily provides prophylaxis for BOTH PCP AND Toxoplasma. If patient is sulfa-allergic, need separate agents (dapsone for PCP, pyrimethamine + leucovorin for toxo).

Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS)

HIGH YIELD
Definition
  • Paradoxical clinical worsening after starting ART due to recovering immune response
  • Unmasking IRIS: Previously subclinical infection becomes symptomatic
  • Paradoxical IRIS: Worsening of known infection despite treatment
Management
  • Continue ART (unless life-threatening)
  • Treat underlying OI
  • NSAIDs or corticosteroids for severe inflammation
⚡ BOARD PEARL: When to Start ART with OI?
  • Most OIs: Start ART within 2 weeks
  • TB: Start ART within 2-8 weeks (may delay if CD4 >50 to reduce IRIS risk)
  • Cryptococcal meningitis: Delay ART 4-6 weeks (early ART increases mortality)

🍄 Fungal Infections

🦠

Candidiasis

HIGH YIELD
Candidemia
  • Risk factors: Central lines, TPN, broad-spectrum antibiotics, neutropenia, surgery
  • Always clinically significant: Never a contaminant
  • Treatment: Echinocandin preferred (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin); fluconazole if species known susceptible and patient stable
  • Remove central line if possible
  • Duration: 2 weeks after first negative culture (assuming uncomplicated)
📘 IDSA Candidiasis Guidelines (2016)
All patients with candidemia need:
  • Dilated fundoscopic exam (rule out endophthalmitis)
  • Repeat blood cultures q48h until negative
  • Echocardiogram if prolonged candidemia or prosthetic valve
  • Central line removal when feasible
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Candida Species
  • C. albicans: Most common; usually fluconazole-susceptible
  • C. glabrata: Often fluconazole-resistant; use echinocandin
  • C. krusei: Intrinsically fluconazole-resistant
  • C. parapsilosis: Associated with catheters; may have reduced echinocandin susceptibility
  • C. auris: MDR, healthcare-associated, infection control emergency

Aspergillosis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Syndromes
  • Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA): Immunocompromised (neutropenia, transplant); high mortality
  • Aspergilloma: Fungus ball in pre-existing cavity (TB, sarcoid); hemoptysis
  • Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA): Asthma/CF patients; eosinophilia, elevated IgE
  • Chronic pulmonary aspergillosis: Immunocompetent with structural lung disease
Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis
  • CT findings: Halo sign (early), air crescent sign (later, with neutrophil recovery)
  • Galactomannan: Serum antigen; sensitivity varies (lower with prophylaxis/treatment)
  • BAL galactomannan: More sensitive than serum
  • Treatment: Voriconazole (first-line); alternatives: isavuconazole, liposomal amphotericin B
🚨 VORICONAZOLE TOXICITY
  • Visual disturbances: Photophobia, altered color vision (transient)
  • Hepatotoxicity: Monitor LFTs
  • Skin cancer risk: With long-term use; photoprotection essential
  • Drug interactions: CYP450 substrate and inhibitor (many interactions)
  • Fluorosis: With prolonged use (painful periostitis)

Endemic Mycoses

HIGH YIELD
Organism Geography Key Features Treatment
Histoplasma Ohio/Mississippi River valleys; bat/bird droppings Cave exploration, chicken coop; mediastinal lymphadenopathy; "buckshot" calcifications Mild: itraconazole; Severe: amphotericin B → itraconazole
Blastomyces Great Lakes, Ohio/Mississippi valleys Lung + skin (verrucous lesions); bone Mild: itraconazole; Severe: amphotericin B → itraconazole
Coccidioides Southwest US, San Joaquin Valley Desert dust; erythema nodosum, arthralgias; "valley fever" Fluconazole (CNS disease); itraconazole; amphotericin B if severe
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE: Histoplasmosis

Scenario: Patient from Ohio River valley with subacute pneumonia after spelunking. CXR shows hilar lymphadenopathy. Urine Histoplasma antigen positive.

Answer: Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis. Mild cases may self-resolve; treat moderate-severe with itraconazole or amphotericin B.

⚡ BOARD PEARL: Urine Antigen Testing
Histoplasma and Blastomyces urine antigens cross-react. Interpret in clinical context. Histoplasma antigen also useful for monitoring treatment response.

🦠 Viral Infections

💊

Influenza

HIGH YIELD
📘 IDSA Influenza Guidelines (2018)
Treat with antivirals if:
  • Hospitalized patients
  • Severe/progressive illness (any setting)
  • High-risk for complications (age ≥65, immunocompromised, chronic disease, pregnant, BMI ≥40, nursing home)
  • Within 48 hours of symptom onset (any patient)
Antiviral Options
  • Oseltamivir: 75mg PO BID × 5 days (first-line)
  • Zanamivir: Inhaled; avoid in asthma/COPD
  • Peramivir: Single IV dose; useful if NPO
  • Baloxavir: Single PO dose; cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Influenza Complications
  • Primary viral pneumonia: Diffuse infiltrates, ARDS
  • Secondary bacterial pneumonia: S. aureus (including MRSA), S. pneumoniae; biphasic illness
  • Myocarditis
  • Encephalitis
  • Rhabdomyolysis

Herpes Simplex Virus

CORE
Clinical Syndromes
  • Mucocutaneous: Orolabial (HSV-1), genital (HSV-2 > HSV-1)
  • Encephalitis: HSV-1; temporal lobe involvement; high mortality without treatment
  • Esophagitis: Immunocompromised; small deep ulcers (vs CMV large shallow)
  • Keratitis: Dendritic ulcer on fluorescein stain
Treatment
  • Mucocutaneous: Valacyclovir, acyclovir, famciclovir (oral)
  • Encephalitis: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 14-21 days
  • Disseminated/visceral: IV acyclovir

Varicella-Zoster Virus

CORE
Herpes Zoster (Shingles)
  • Presentation: Painful vesicular rash in dermatomal distribution
  • Treatment: Valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days (within 72h of rash onset)
  • Complications: Postherpetic neuralgia, disseminated zoster (immunocompromised)
🚨 HERPES ZOSTER OPHTHALMICUS
  • V1 (ophthalmic) distribution
  • Hutchinson's sign: Vesicles on nose tip = nasociliary nerve involvement = high risk for eye complications
  • Urgent ophthalmology referral
  • Treatment: Valacyclovir + ophthalmology management

🪱 Parasitic Infections

🦠

Malaria

HIGH YIELD
🚨 MEDICAL EMERGENCY
Consider malaria in ANY febrile patient with travel to endemic area. P. falciparum can be fatal within 24-48 hours.
Species & Clinical Features
  • P. falciparum: Most severe; cerebral malaria, severe anemia, ARDS, hypoglycemia, renal failure
  • P. vivax/P. ovale: Hypnozoite stage (liver) → relapses; need primaquine for radical cure
  • P. malariae: Chronic infection; nephrotic syndrome
Diagnosis
  • Thick smear: Screening (higher sensitivity)
  • Thin smear: Species identification, parasitemia quantification
  • Rapid diagnostic test: Antigen detection; good for non-endemic areas
Treatment
  • Uncomplicated (chloroquine-sensitive): Chloroquine
  • Uncomplicated (chloroquine-resistant, most areas): Artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) OR atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone)
  • Severe malaria: IV artesunate (first-line); exchange transfusion if >10% parasitemia
  • P. vivax/ovale: Add primaquine (after checking G6PD status) to prevent relapse
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Chloroquine Resistance
Assume chloroquine resistance for P. falciparum from sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia. Only Central America (west of Panama Canal), Caribbean, and Middle East still have chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum.

Strongyloides

HIGH YIELD
🚨 HYPERINFECTION SYNDROME
Life-threatening in immunocompromised (especially steroids). Screen and treat BEFORE immunosuppression in patients from endemic areas.
Key Features
  • Autoinfection: Unique ability to complete life cycle within host → chronic infection for decades
  • Eosinophilia: May be only sign of chronic infection
  • Hyperinfection: Disseminated disease with gram-negative sepsis, meningitis, ARDS
Diagnosis & Treatment
  • Stool O&P: Low sensitivity; multiple samples or concentration techniques
  • Serology: More sensitive but may cross-react with other helminths
  • Treatment: Ivermectin 200 mcg/kg daily × 2 days (uncomplicated); longer for hyperinfection

✈️ Travel Medicine & Tropical Diseases

🌍

Traveler's Diarrhea

HIGH YIELD
Etiology
  • ETEC (Enterotoxigenic E. coli): Most common cause worldwide
  • Other bacteria: Campylobacter (Southeast Asia), Salmonella, Shigella
  • Parasites: Giardia, Cyclospora, Cryptosporidium
Treatment
  • Mild (tolerable): Hydration, loperamide
  • Moderate-severe: Azithromycin 1000mg × 1 (preferred) OR fluoroquinolone × 1-3 days
  • Avoid FQ in Southeast Asia: High Campylobacter FQ resistance
  • Bloody diarrhea: Avoid loperamide; antibiotics indicated

Dengue Fever

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • "Breakbone fever": High fever, severe myalgias/arthralgias, retro-orbital headache
  • Rash: Maculopapular, "islands of white in a sea of red"
  • Labs: Thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, elevated AST/ALT
🚨 SEVERE DENGUE (Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever)
  • Warning signs: Abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, pleural effusion, bleeding, lethargy
  • Plasma leak: Hemoconcentration, hypoalbuminemia
  • Treatment: Supportive; careful fluid management
  • Risk increases with secondary infection (different serotype)
⚡ BOARD PEARL
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin in dengue — increase bleeding risk. Use acetaminophen for fever/pain.

Typhoid Fever

CORE
Clinical Features
  • Stepwise fever over first week
  • Relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation)
  • Rose spots: Faint salmon-colored macules on trunk
  • Hepatosplenomegaly
  • Complications: Intestinal perforation, GI bleeding (week 3-4)
Diagnosis & Treatment
  • Blood culture: Best in first week
  • Stool culture: Better in weeks 2-3
  • Treatment: Fluoroquinolone or azithromycin (check resistance patterns); ceftriaxone if MDR

🏥 Healthcare-Associated Infections

🦠

Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection (CLABSI)

HIGH YIELD
Definition
  • BSI in patient with central line in place within 48 hours
  • No other apparent source of infection
Microbiology
  • Coagulase-negative staphylococci: Most common
  • S. aureus: High morbidity
  • Enterococcus
  • Gram-negatives: Especially in ICU, TPN
  • Candida: TPN, prolonged antibiotics
📘 IDSA Catheter-Related Infection Guidelines (2009)
Line removal indications:
  • S. aureus (always remove)
  • Candida (always remove)
  • P. aeruginosa, MDR organisms
  • Tunnel infection, port abscess
  • Septic shock
  • Persistent bacteremia (>72h on appropriate therapy)
Prevention Bundle
  • Hand hygiene
  • Maximal sterile barrier precautions on insertion
  • Chlorhexidine skin antisepsis
  • Optimal site selection (avoid femoral in adults)
  • Daily review of line necessity; prompt removal

Surgical Site Infection (SSI)

CORE
Classification
  • Superficial incisional: Skin and subcutaneous tissue only
  • Deep incisional: Fascia and muscle
  • Organ/space: Any part of anatomy opened during surgery
Prevention
  • Timing of prophylaxis: Within 60 minutes of incision (120 min for vancomycin, FQ)
  • Redosing: For long procedures or significant blood loss
  • Duration: ≤24 hours post-op (no benefit from prolonged prophylaxis)
  • Standard agent: Cefazolin for most procedures

🛡️ Infections in Immunocompromised Hosts

🦠

Neutropenic Fever

HIGH YIELD
🚨 ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY
Empiric antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation. Fever may be only sign of serious infection.
Definitions
  • Neutropenia: ANC <500/µL (or expected to fall <500)
  • Fever: Single temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) OR ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) sustained for 1 hour
📘 IDSA Neutropenic Fever Guidelines (2010)
Initial Empiric Therapy:
  • Monotherapy: Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (cefepime, pip-tazo, meropenem, or imipenem)
  • Add vancomycin if: Catheter infection, skin/soft tissue infection, pneumonia, hemodynamic instability, known MRSA colonization
  • Add antifungal if: Persistent fever after 4-7 days on antibiotics
Risk Stratification (MASCC Score)
  • High risk: Anticipated prolonged neutropenia (>7 days), significant comorbidities, MASCC <21
  • Low risk: Short duration neutropenia, no comorbidities, clinically stable — may consider outpatient oral therapy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Fungal Coverage
  • Persistent fever after 4-7 days: Add empiric antifungal (caspofungin, liposomal amphotericin B, or voriconazole)
  • CT chest for persistent fever: Look for IPA (halo sign)
  • Galactomannan/β-D-glucan: Useful adjuncts

Transplant-Related Infections Timeline

HIGH YIELD
Time Post-Transplant Predominant Infections
<1 month Nosocomial: SSI, line infections, HAP, C. diff; donor-derived infections
1-6 months Opportunistic: CMV, PCP, Aspergillus, reactivation (HBV, TB, endemic fungi)
>6 months Community-acquired (if immunosuppression reduced); late CMV; EBV-related PTLD
⚡ BOARD PEARL: CMV in Transplant
  • Highest risk: D+/R- (donor positive, recipient negative)
  • Prevention: Valganciclovir prophylaxis or preemptive monitoring
  • CMV syndrome: Fever, cytopenias, elevated LFTs
  • Tissue-invasive: Colitis, pneumonitis, retinitis
  • Treatment: IV ganciclovir or valganciclovir

💉 Prophylaxis & Prevention

🛡️

Surgical Prophylaxis

HIGH YIELD
Procedure Recommended Agent Duration
Most clean procedures Cefazolin Single dose (24h max)
CABG, cardiac valve Cefazolin (± vancomycin if MRSA risk) ≤48 hours
Colorectal Cefazolin + metronidazole (or ertapenem) Single dose
GU (involving intestine) Cefazolin + metronidazole Single dose
β-lactam allergy Vancomycin + aminoglycoside or aztreonam Single dose
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Timing
  • Within 60 minutes of incision for most agents
  • Within 120 minutes for vancomycin and fluoroquinolones (need infusion time)
  • Redose for long procedures (>2 half-lives) or major blood loss

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis

HIGH YIELD
Exposure Prophylaxis Notes
HIV (needlestick) 3-drug ART × 28 days Start within 72h (ideally <2h)
Meningococcal Ciprofloxacin × 1 OR rifampin × 2d OR ceftriaxone × 1 Close contacts within 7 days
Pertussis Azithromycin × 5 days Close contacts within 21 days
Influenza Oseltamivir × 7-10 days High-risk contacts; institutional outbreaks
Rabies Wound care + RIG + vaccine series Unprovoked animal bite, bat exposure

Endocarditis Prophylaxis

HIGH YIELD
📘 AHA 2021 Update
Prophylaxis only for highest-risk cardiac conditions:
  • Prosthetic heart valve or prosthetic material used for valve repair
  • Previous infective endocarditis
  • Congenital heart disease (unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired with prosthetic material <6 months, residual defect near prosthetic material)
  • Cardiac transplant with valvulopathy
Procedures Requiring Prophylaxis
  • Dental: Manipulation of gingival tissue, periapical region, or oral mucosa perforation
  • NOT routine: GI or GU procedures (unless established infection)
Regimen
  • Standard: Amoxicillin 2g PO 30-60 min before procedure
  • Penicillin allergy: Azithromycin 500mg or clindamycin 600mg

🦠 ABIM Infectious Disease Study Guide

Evidence-based content from IDSA, ATS, SCCM, CDC, and major society guidelines

For educational purposes only. Verify all clinical decisions with current guidelines and institutional protocols.

📚 Clinical Suite Home 🦠 ID Quick Reference 💊 Drug Guide
↑ Back to top

🟠 Gastroenterology

ABIM Board Review — ACG/AGA/AASLD Guidelines • Evidence-Based

🎯 Core GI Principles

🔍Alarm symptoms always warrant endoscopic evaluation
🦠Test-and-treat H. pylori before EGD in low-risk PUD
🩸Risk-stratify GI bleeds — Glasgow-Blatchford before scope
📊FIB-4/elastography before liver biopsy in MASLD
💊Re-evaluate PPI need annually — avoid indefinite use
🔬IBD diagnosis requires histology — not clinical alone
🔴 Esophagus 🟡 Stomach/PUD 🩸 GI Bleeding 🟠 Small Bowel 🔥 IBD 🟤 Colon 🔍 CRC Screening 🟢 Liver Disease ⚠️ Cirrhosis 🦠 Hepatitis 💛 Biliary 🫁 Pancreas 🔄 Motility 🥗 Nutrition

🔴 Esophageal Disorders

🫁

Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • Clinical diagnosis: Typical symptoms (heartburn, regurgitation) + response to PPI trial
  • EGD indications: Alarm symptoms, PPI non-response, long-standing symptoms (Barrett's screening)
  • pH monitoring: If diagnosis uncertain, refractory symptoms, pre-surgical evaluation
  • Alarm symptoms: Dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anemia, GI bleeding
📘 ACG GERD Guidelines (2022)
Treatment Approach:
  • Lifestyle: Weight loss (if overweight), avoid late meals, elevate head of bed
  • PPI therapy: Standard dose × 8 weeks; if responsive, attempt to step down
  • H2RA: For mild/intermittent symptoms or as add-on for nocturnal symptoms
  • Refractory GERD: Confirm diagnosis (pH testing), optimize PPI timing (30-60 min before meals)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: PPI Timing
PPIs work best when taken 30-60 minutes BEFORE meals (need active proton pumps to inhibit). Once-daily dosing should be before breakfast. Twice-daily = before breakfast and dinner.
Extraesophageal Manifestations
  • Chronic cough, asthma, laryngitis
  • Dental erosions
  • Note: Less responsive to PPI therapy; ensure adequate GERD diagnosis before attributing to reflux
🚨 LONG-TERM PPI RISKS
  • C. difficile infection: Modest increased risk
  • Bone fractures: Minimal risk; calcium/vitamin D not routinely needed
  • Hypomagnesemia: Rare; check if on diuretics or symptoms develop
  • B12 deficiency: Consider monitoring in long-term users
  • CKD/AIN: Association reported; causality uncertain

Bottom line: Use lowest effective dose; benefits outweigh risks when indicated

Barrett's Esophagus

HIGH YIELD
Definition & Diagnosis
  • Definition: Intestinal metaplasia of esophageal squamous epithelium (salmon-colored mucosa + goblet cells on biopsy)
  • Risk factors: Chronic GERD, male sex, Caucasian, obesity, smoking, family history
  • Screening: Consider in patients with chronic GERD (>5 years) AND multiple risk factors
📘 ACG Barrett's Guidelines (2022)
Surveillance Intervals:
Dysplasia Grade Interval Management
No dysplasia Every 3-5 years Continue PPI, surveillance
Low-grade dysplasia Every 6-12 months OR ablation Endoscopic eradication therapy preferred
High-grade dysplasia Endoscopic eradication RFA, EMR, or esophagectomy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Endoscopic Eradication
  • Radiofrequency ablation (RFA): First-line for flat dysplasia
  • Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR): For nodular/raised lesions
  • Goal: Complete eradication of intestinal metaplasia (CE-IM)
  • Continue surveillance after successful ablation (recurrence possible)
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 58-year-old man with 15 years of GERD. EGD shows salmon-colored mucosa extending 4 cm above GE junction. Biopsy confirms intestinal metaplasia without dysplasia.

Answer: Barrett's esophagus without dysplasia. Continue PPI therapy. Repeat surveillance EGD in 3-5 years.

Achalasia

HIGH YIELD
Pathophysiology & Presentation
  • Mechanism: Loss of inhibitory neurons in myenteric plexus → impaired LES relaxation + absent peristalsis
  • Symptoms: Progressive dysphagia to BOTH solids AND liquids, regurgitation of undigested food, chest pain, weight loss
  • Key clue: Dysphagia to liquids AND solids from onset suggests motility disorder (vs mechanical obstruction: solids → liquids)
Diagnosis
  • Barium swallow: "Bird's beak" appearance at GE junction, dilated esophagus
  • High-resolution manometry: Gold standard — absent peristalsis + impaired LES relaxation (IRP >15 mmHg)
  • EGD: To rule out pseudoachalasia (malignancy at GE junction)
Treatment
  • Pneumatic dilation: Effective; may require repeat; risk of perforation (2-5%)
  • Heller myotomy + fundoplication: Surgical; durable response; laparoscopic preferred
  • POEM (peroral endoscopic myotomy): Endoscopic; similar efficacy to surgery; higher GERD rates
  • Botox injection: Temporary effect (3-6 months); reserve for poor surgical candidates
🚨 PSEUDOACHALASIA
Always consider malignancy (especially gastric adenocarcinoma at GE junction) in:
  • Age >60 with new-onset symptoms
  • Rapid symptom progression (<1 year)
  • Significant weight loss
EGD with careful inspection/biopsy of GE junction mandatory

Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE)

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Demographics: Young adults, males > females, often with atopy (asthma, eczema, allergies)
  • Symptoms: Dysphagia (solids > liquids), food impaction, chest pain, heartburn
  • Key clue: Young patient with recurrent food impactions
Diagnosis
  • EGD findings: Rings ("trachealization"), furrows, white exudates, strictures
  • Histology: ≥15 eosinophils/HPF in esophageal biopsies
  • Must exclude GERD: PPI trial or pH monitoring (note: some EoE is PPI-responsive)
📘 ACG EoE Guidelines (2020)
Treatment Options:
  • PPI: First-line therapy (PPI-responsive EoE is still EoE)
  • Topical steroids: Swallowed fluticasone or budesonide (oral viscous)
  • Dietary elimination: 6-food elimination or targeted elimination
  • Dupilumab: FDA-approved for EoE (IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor)
  • Dilation: For strictures; symptom relief, not disease-modifying
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 28-year-old man with asthma presents with food impaction requiring emergent EGD. Scope shows esophageal rings and furrows. Biopsies show 45 eosinophils/HPF.

Answer: Eosinophilic esophagitis. Start PPI (first-line) or topical steroids.

Esophageal Cancer

CORE
Feature Squamous Cell Adenocarcinoma
Location Upper/middle esophagus Distal esophagus/GE junction
Risk factors Alcohol, tobacco, caustic injury, achalasia, Plummer-Vinson, hot beverages Barrett's esophagus, GERD, obesity, male sex
Trends Decreasing in US Increasing (now most common in US)
Presentation & Staging
  • Symptoms: Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids), weight loss, odynophagia
  • Staging: EUS (T and N staging), CT chest/abdomen, PET-CT
  • Treatment: Depends on stage; surgery, chemoradiation, or palliation

🔴 Stomach & Peptic Ulcer Disease

💊

Peptic Ulcer Disease

HIGH YIELD
Etiology
  • H. pylori: Most common cause globally; 70-90% of duodenal ulcers, 50-70% of gastric ulcers
  • NSAIDs: Second most common cause; direct mucosal injury + COX-1 inhibition
  • Stress ulcers: ICU patients (mechanical ventilation, coagulopathy)
  • Zollinger-Ellison: Gastrinoma → gastric acid hypersecretion
H. pylori Testing
Test Advantages Limitations
Urea breath test High accuracy, non-invasive, confirms active infection Hold PPI 2 weeks, antibiotics 4 weeks before
Stool antigen High accuracy, non-invasive Same hold requirements
Serology No hold required Cannot confirm active infection; remains positive after eradication
Biopsy (histology, rapid urease) Can assess for pathology Invasive; patchy infection may cause false negative
📘 ACG H. pylori Guidelines (2017)
First-Line Regimens (14 days preferred):
  • Bismuth quadruple: PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline (preferred if prior macrolide exposure)
  • Concomitant therapy: PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin + metronidazole
  • Triple therapy (if local clarithromycin resistance <15%): PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin
Confirm eradication: Urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after completing therapy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Who to Test for H. pylori
  • Active PUD or history of PUD
  • Low-grade gastric MALT lymphoma
  • After resection of early gastric cancer
  • Uninvestigated dyspepsia (test-and-treat strategy)
  • Long-term NSAID use (test and treat before starting)
  • Unexplained iron deficiency anemia, ITP
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Patient completes 14-day triple therapy for H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer. How do you confirm eradication?

Answer: Urea breath test or stool antigen at least 4 weeks after treatment completion and 2 weeks after stopping PPI. Do NOT use serology (remains positive).

Gastritis & Gastropathy

CORE
Autoimmune Gastritis
  • Mechanism: Antibodies against parietal cells and intrinsic factor → achlorhydria, B12 deficiency
  • Location: Body/fundus (parietal cell distribution)
  • Associations: Pernicious anemia, other autoimmune conditions (thyroiditis)
  • Cancer risk: Increased risk of gastric adenocarcinoma and carcinoid tumors
  • Labs: Elevated gastrin (due to loss of acid feedback), low B12, anti-parietal cell antibodies
H. pylori Gastritis
  • Location: Antrum predominantly (may progress to pangastritis)
  • Associations: PUD, gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma
  • Treatment: Eradication therapy
⚡ BOARD PEARL: MALT Lymphoma
  • Low-grade B-cell lymphoma associated with H. pylori
  • First-line treatment: H. pylori eradication (70-80% remission in localized disease)
  • Surveillance EGD after eradication
  • Chemo/radiation for persistent or advanced disease

Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Etiology: Gastrin-secreting tumor (gastrinoma), usually duodenum or pancreas
  • Presentation: Severe/refractory PUD, multiple ulcers, ulcers in unusual locations (distal duodenum, jejunum), diarrhea
  • Associations: MEN1 (20-30% of cases) — check for hyperparathyroidism, pituitary tumors
Diagnosis
  • Fasting serum gastrin: Markedly elevated (>1000 pg/mL highly suggestive; >10× ULN virtually diagnostic)
  • Must be off PPI (PPIs elevate gastrin; hold 1-2 weeks if possible)
  • Gastric pH: Must be <2 (excludes achlorhydria as cause of elevated gastrin)
  • Secretin stimulation test: Paradoxical rise in gastrin (>120 pg/mL increase)
  • Tumor localization: CT, MRI, EUS, somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Octreoscan) or Ga-68 DOTATATE PET
🚨 CAUSES OF ELEVATED GASTRIN
  • PPI use: Most common cause (stop before testing)
  • H. pylori infection
  • Atrophic gastritis / pernicious anemia: (gastric pH will be high)
  • Renal failure: Decreased gastrin clearance
  • Retained antrum post-surgery
  • Gastrinoma (ZES)

🩸 Gastrointestinal Bleeding

🚨

Upper GI Bleeding

HIGH YIELD
Common Causes
  • Peptic ulcer disease: Most common (30-50%)
  • Erosive gastritis/esophagitis
  • Esophageal/gastric varices: In cirrhosis
  • Mallory-Weiss tear: Mucosal tear at GE junction after vomiting
  • Malignancy
  • Dieulafoy lesion: Submucosal artery, can cause massive bleeding
📘 ACG UGIB Guidelines (2021)
Initial Management:
  • Resuscitation: IV access, fluids, type and crossmatch
  • Transfusion threshold: Hemoglobin <7 g/dL (restrictive strategy preferred); higher threshold if CAD or hemodynamic instability
  • PPI: IV PPI infusion (no benefit for pre-endoscopy bolus in all patients)
  • Prokinetic: Erythromycin 250mg IV 30-90 min before EGD may improve visualization
  • Timing of EGD: Within 24 hours; urgent (<12h) if hemodynamic instability or high-risk features
📊 GLASGOW-BLATCHFORD SCORE (GBS)
  • BUN, hemoglobin, systolic BP, heart rate, melena, syncope, hepatic disease, cardiac failure
  • Score 0-1: Low risk; may consider outpatient management
  • Higher scores: Need intervention or transfusion more likely
Forrest Classification (Ulcer Bleeding)
Class Description Rebleed Risk Endoscopic Therapy
Ia Spurting hemorrhage 90% Yes
Ib Oozing hemorrhage 50% Yes
IIa Visible vessel 40-50% Yes
IIb Adherent clot 20-30% Consider (remove clot, treat underlying)
IIc Flat pigmented spot 10% No
III Clean base 5% No
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Post-Endoscopy Management
  • High-risk stigmata (Ia, Ib, IIa, IIb): IV PPI infusion (80mg bolus then 8 mg/hr) × 72 hours
  • Low-risk stigmata (IIc, III): Oral PPI; can discharge early
  • Test and treat H. pylori in all PUD bleeding

Variceal Hemorrhage

HIGH YIELD
🚨 VARICEAL BLEEDING IS A MEDICAL EMERGENCY
Mortality 10-20% per episode
📘 AASLD Variceal Bleeding Guidelines
Acute Management:
  • Resuscitation: Restrictive transfusion (Hgb target 7-8 g/dL); avoid over-transfusion (increases portal pressure)
  • Vasoactive drugs: Octreotide 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hr infusion (or terlipressin where available) × 2-5 days
  • Antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily × 7 days (reduces mortality!)
  • EGD within 12 hours: Endoscopic band ligation (EBL) preferred over sclerotherapy
  • Balloon tamponade: Bridge to definitive therapy if uncontrolled bleeding
  • TIPS: For refractory bleeding or early TIPS in high-risk patients (Child C or B with active bleeding)
Secondary Prophylaxis
  • Non-selective beta-blocker: Propranolol or nadolol (target HR 55-60 or 25% reduction)
  • PLUS serial EBL until varices eradicated
  • Carvedilol: May be more effective than traditional NSBB
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Primary Prophylaxis
  • Screen for varices: All patients with cirrhosis at diagnosis
  • No varices: Repeat EGD in 2-3 years (or 1 year if decompensated)
  • Small varices: NSBB or surveillance
  • Medium/large varices: NSBB OR endoscopic band ligation

Lower GI Bleeding

HIGH YIELD
Common Causes
  • Diverticulosis: Most common cause of massive LGIB; painless, often right-sided
  • Angiodysplasia (AVMs): Especially in elderly, CKD, aortic stenosis
  • Hemorrhoids: Most common cause of minor LGIB
  • Colorectal cancer/polyps
  • Colitis: IBD, ischemic, infectious, radiation
  • Post-polypectomy bleeding
📘 ACG LGIB Guidelines (2016)
Evaluation:
  • Colonoscopy: Preferred first-line diagnostic and therapeutic modality
  • Timing: Within 24 hours after bowel prep (may be urgent if hemodynamically significant)
  • CTA: If ongoing bleeding and colonoscopy not feasible/non-diagnostic
  • Tagged RBC scan: Detects slower bleeds; localizes for angiography
  • Angiography: For ongoing brisk bleeding; allows embolization
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 72-year-old with painless, large-volume maroon stool. Hemodynamically stable after resuscitation. No prior colonoscopy.

Answer: Likely diverticular bleed. Colonoscopy after bowel prep within 24 hours for diagnosis and potential therapy.

💎 HEYDE SYNDROME
GI bleeding from angiodysplasia + aortic stenosis. Mechanism: Acquired von Willebrand syndrome (high shear stress across stenotic valve destroys vWF multimers). Treatment: AVR can resolve bleeding.

🔬 Small Bowel Disorders

💊

Celiac Disease

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Presentation
  • Classic: Diarrhea, steatorrhea, weight loss, abdominal bloating
  • Non-classic: Iron deficiency anemia, osteoporosis, elevated transaminases, dermatitis herpetiformis
  • Silent: Positive serology + villous atrophy but no symptoms
  • Associations: Type 1 DM, autoimmune thyroiditis, Down syndrome, IgA deficiency
Diagnosis
  • First-line test: IgA tissue transglutaminase (TTG-IgA) — high sensitivity and specificity
  • Check total IgA: IgA deficiency causes false negative TTG-IgA; use IgG-based tests if deficient
  • Must be on gluten-containing diet at time of testing
  • Confirmation: EGD with duodenal biopsies (≥4 samples from duodenum)
  • Histology: Villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytosis
📘 ACG Celiac Guidelines (2023)
Treatment:
  • Strict lifelong gluten-free diet (avoid wheat, barley, rye)
  • Dietitian referral essential
  • Screen for nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium)
  • Pneumococcal vaccination (functional hyposplenism)
  • DEXA scan for bone density
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Non-Responsive Celiac
If symptoms persist despite GFD:
  1. Ongoing gluten exposure (most common)
  2. Other diagnosis (IBS, lactose intolerance, microscopic colitis, SIBO)
  3. Refractory celiac disease
  4. Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) — rare but serious
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 35-year-old woman with iron deficiency anemia unresponsive to oral iron, mild diarrhea, and a pruritic, vesicular rash on elbows and knees.

Answer: Celiac disease with dermatitis herpetiformis. Test TTG-IgA, confirm with duodenal biopsies. Treat with gluten-free diet (rash also responds).

Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO)

HIGH YIELD
Risk Factors
  • Anatomic: Blind loops, strictures, surgical anastomoses, diverticula
  • Motility disorders: Scleroderma, diabetic gastroparesis, chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction
  • Achlorhydria: PPI use, atrophic gastritis
  • Immune deficiency
  • Cirrhosis
Diagnosis
  • Glucose or lactulose breath test: Measures hydrogen/methane (positive = early rise)
  • Jejunal aspirate and culture: Gold standard (>10³ CFU/mL) but invasive, rarely used
Treatment
  • Rifaximin 550mg TID × 14 days: Preferred; non-absorbed, well-tolerated
  • Alternatives: Ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, amoxicillin-clavulanate
  • Recurrence common: May need cyclical or long-term antibiotics
  • Address underlying cause when possible

Malabsorption Syndromes

CORE
Condition Key Features Diagnosis
Lactose intolerance Bloating, diarrhea, gas after dairy Lactose breath test or empiric trial
Pancreatic insufficiency Steatorrhea, weight loss; chronic pancreatitis, CF Low fecal elastase (<200); treat with PERT
Bile acid malabsorption Watery diarrhea; post-cholecystectomy, ileal disease/resection SeHCAT scan; empiric cholestyramine trial
Tropical sprue Travel to endemic area; megaloblastic anemia Duodenal biopsy; responds to antibiotics + folate
Whipple disease Diarrhea, weight loss, arthralgias, CNS symptoms PAS-positive macrophages on biopsy; PCR for T. whipplei
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Whipple Disease
  • Classic triad: Diarrhea, weight loss, arthralgias (arthritis may precede GI symptoms by years)
  • Other features: Cardiac involvement, CNS disease, lymphadenopathy
  • Treatment: Ceftriaxone × 2 weeks → TMP-SMX × 1 year

🔥 Inflammatory Bowel Disease

💊

Ulcerative Colitis

HIGH YIELD
Key Features
  • Distribution: Continuous inflammation, starts at rectum, extends proximally
  • Depth: Mucosal and submucosal only
  • Symptoms: Bloody diarrhea, urgency, tenesmus
  • Endoscopy: Erythema, friability, loss of vascular pattern, pseudopolyps
  • Histology: Crypt abscesses, crypt architectural distortion
📘 AGA/ACG UC Treatment Guidelines
Mild-Moderate (Distal):
  • Topical mesalamine (suppository for proctitis, enema for left-sided) — most effective
  • Oral + topical mesalamine for extensive disease
Moderate-Severe:
  • Oral corticosteroids for induction (not maintenance)
  • Thiopurines (azathioprine, 6-MP) for maintenance
  • Biologics: Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab), vedolizumab (gut-selective), ustekinumab, tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor)
Acute Severe UC (Fulminant):
  • IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 60mg/day)
  • If no response in 3-5 days: infliximab, cyclosporine, or colectomy
🚨 TOXIC MEGACOLON
  • Definition: Colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity
  • Management: NPO, NG decompression, IV steroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • Avoid: Opioids, anticholinergics, colonoscopy
  • Surgery if: Perforation, no improvement in 24-72 hours
⚡ BOARD PEARL: UC Cancer Surveillance
  • Start surveillance colonoscopy 8 years after diagnosis
  • Interval: Every 1-3 years depending on risk factors
  • Higher risk: Pancolitis, PSC, family history of CRC, prior dysplasia
  • Chromoendoscopy with targeted biopsies preferred over random biopsies

Crohn's Disease

HIGH YIELD
Key Features
  • Distribution: Any part of GI tract (mouth to anus); skip lesions; terminal ileum most common
  • Depth: Transmural inflammation
  • Symptoms: Abdominal pain, diarrhea (often non-bloody), weight loss
  • Endoscopy: Aphthous ulcers, cobblestoning, strictures
  • Histology: Non-caseating granulomas (present in ~30%)
Feature Ulcerative Colitis Crohn's Disease
Location Colon only (continuous from rectum) Mouth to anus (skip lesions)
Rectal involvement Always Often spared
Depth Mucosal Transmural
Complications Toxic megacolon, cancer Fistulas, strictures, abscesses
Smoking Protective Worsens disease
Surgery curative? Yes (colectomy) No (recurrence common)
Crohn's Complications
  • Strictures: Inflammatory (treat medically) vs fibrotic (may need dilation/surgery)
  • Fistulas: Perianal, enterocutaneous, enterovesical, enterovaginal
  • Abscesses: Percutaneous drainage + antibiotics; rule out before starting biologics
  • B12 deficiency: Terminal ileal disease/resection
  • Bile acid diarrhea: Ileal resection >100 cm
  • Oxalate kidney stones: Fat malabsorption → increased oxalate absorption
📘 Treatment Approach
  • Mild: Budesonide (ileal/right colon), mesalamine less effective than in UC
  • Moderate-Severe: Biologics (anti-TNF, vedolizumab, ustekinumab) ± immunomodulators
  • Perianal disease: MRI for fistula mapping, antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole), anti-TNF agents
  • Post-surgical prophylaxis: Anti-TNF or thiopurines to prevent recurrence
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Biologics Safety
  • Before starting anti-TNF: Screen for latent TB (PPD/IGRA), Hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs)
  • Avoid live vaccines on immunosuppression
  • Anti-TNF risks: Infections, reactivation of TB/HBV, demyelination, heart failure exacerbation, drug-induced lupus

Extraintestinal Manifestations

HIGH YIELD
System Manifestation Correlates with IBD Activity?
Musculoskeletal Peripheral arthritis (type 1) Yes
Axial arthropathy/AS No
Skin Erythema nodosum Yes
Pyoderma gangrenosum No
Eye Episcleritis Yes
Uveitis No
Hepatobiliary Primary sclerosing cholangitis No (UC >> Crohn's)
💎 PSC-IBD ASSOCIATION
  • 70-80% of PSC patients have IBD (usually UC)
  • PSC-IBD patients have HIGHER risk of colorectal cancer
  • Annual surveillance colonoscopy recommended from PSC diagnosis
  • Colectomy does NOT prevent PSC progression

🔵 Colon Disorders

💊

Diverticular Disease

HIGH YIELD
Diverticulosis
  • Definition: Outpouchings of colonic mucosa through muscular wall
  • Location: Left-sided (sigmoid) in Western populations; right-sided in Asian populations
  • Risk factors: Age, low fiber diet, obesity, sedentary lifestyle
  • Usually asymptomatic; may cause painless bleeding
Acute Diverticulitis
  • Presentation: LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis
  • Diagnosis: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (preferred)
📘 AGA Diverticulitis Guidelines (2015)
Uncomplicated Diverticulitis:
  • Mild: Outpatient management; antibiotics now selective (not always required)
  • If antibiotics used: Ciprofloxacin + metronidazole OR amoxicillin-clavulanate × 7-10 days
  • Clear liquid diet advancing as tolerated
Complicated Diverticulitis:
  • Abscess <3 cm: IV antibiotics alone
  • Abscess ≥3 cm: Percutaneous drainage + antibiotics
  • Perforation/peritonitis: Surgical intervention
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Colonoscopy After Diverticulitis
  • Perform colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after acute episode to rule out malignancy
  • Especially important if not recently screened
  • Elective sigmoid colectomy: consider after complicated diverticulitis or recurrent episodes in select patients

Microscopic Colitis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Presentation: Chronic watery diarrhea (non-bloody), often nocturnal
  • Demographics: Middle-aged to elderly women
  • Colonoscopy: NORMAL appearing mucosa
  • Diagnosis: Random colonic biopsies (must biopsy even if mucosa looks normal)
Subtypes
  • Collagenous colitis: Thickened subepithelial collagen band (>10 μm)
  • Lymphocytic colitis: Increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (>20/100 epithelial cells)
Treatment
  • First: Stop offending medications (NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs)
  • First-line therapy: Budesonide 9mg daily × 6-8 weeks (highly effective)
  • Maintenance: May need low-dose budesonide for relapsing disease
  • Alternatives: Bismuth, cholestyramine, mesalamine
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: 62-year-old woman with 6 months of watery diarrhea. Colonoscopy shows normal mucosa. Random biopsies show thickened collagen band.

Answer: Collagenous colitis. Treat with budesonide.

Ischemic Colitis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Demographics: Elderly with cardiovascular risk factors
  • Presentation: Sudden onset crampy abdominal pain + bloody diarrhea
  • Location: "Watershed" areas — splenic flexure, rectosigmoid junction
  • Rectum usually spared (dual blood supply)
Diagnosis & Management
  • CT: Colonic wall thickening, thumbprinting
  • Colonoscopy: Segmental erythema, edema, ulcerations; "single stripe sign"
  • Treatment: Supportive (bowel rest, IV fluids, antibiotics if severe)
  • Most cases self-limited; surgery for perforation, gangrene, or stricture
🚨 ACUTE MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
Different from ischemic colitis!
  • Small bowel involvement; pain out of proportion to exam
  • Causes: Arterial embolism (AF), arterial thrombosis, venous thrombosis, NOMI
  • CT angiography for diagnosis
  • Emergency: Surgical exploration, revascularization, resection of necrotic bowel

Irritable Bowel Syndrome

CORE
📘 Rome IV Criteria
Recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week (on average) for past 3 months, associated with ≥2 of:
  • Related to defecation
  • Change in stool frequency
  • Change in stool form/appearance
Subtypes: IBS-D (diarrhea), IBS-C (constipation), IBS-M (mixed)
Treatment by Subtype
  • General: Fiber supplementation, diet modification (low FODMAP)
  • IBS-D: Loperamide, rifaximin, eluxadoline, alosetron (women with severe IBS-D)
  • IBS-C: Linaclotide, lubiprostone, plecanatide, tegaserod (women <65)
  • Pain-predominant: TCAs (amitriptyline), SSRIs, antispasmodics (dicyclomine, hyoscyamine)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: When to Test Further
Alarm features requiring workup:
  • Age >50 without prior colonoscopy
  • Rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss
  • Nocturnal symptoms (waking from sleep)
  • Family history of CRC, IBD, celiac
  • Anemia, elevated inflammatory markers

🔬 Colorectal Cancer Screening

📊

Average Risk Screening

HIGH YIELD
📘 USPSTF / ACS / ACG Guidelines
Start screening at age 45 (updated from 50) for average-risk individuals
Continue until age 75; individualize 76-85; stop after 85
Test Interval Notes
Colonoscopy Every 10 years Gold standard; diagnostic and therapeutic
FIT (fecal immunochemical) Annual Preferred stool test; detects human hemoglobin
FIT-DNA (Cologuard) Every 3 years Higher sensitivity than FIT alone; more false positives
CT colonography Every 5 years Requires bowel prep; colonoscopy if abnormal
Flexible sigmoidoscopy Every 5 years (or 10 with annual FIT) Limited to distal colon
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Positive Stool Test
Any positive stool test (FIT, FIT-DNA, gFOBT) requires colonoscopy for follow-up. Do not repeat the stool test.

Post-Polypectomy Surveillance

HIGH YIELD
📘 USMSTF Post-Polypectomy Guidelines (2020)
Baseline Colonoscopy Findings Surveillance Interval
Normal colonoscopy 10 years
1-2 tubular adenomas <10mm 7-10 years
3-4 tubular adenomas <10mm 3-5 years
5-10 adenomas 3 years
>10 adenomas 1 year (consider genetic testing)
Advanced adenoma (≥10mm, villous, HGD) 3 years
Sessile serrated lesion <10mm, no dysplasia 5-10 years
Sessile serrated lesion ≥10mm OR with dysplasia 3 years
Piecemeal resection of adenoma ≥20mm 6 months (to confirm complete removal)
💎 SERRATED POLYP PATHWAY
  • Alternative pathway to CRC (15-30% of sporadic CRCs)
  • BRAF mutation, MLH1 methylation, MSI-high
  • Sessile serrated lesions often in right colon, flat, mucus-covered
  • Risk of interval cancers due to rapid progression and difficult detection

Hereditary CRC Syndromes

HIGH YIELD
Lynch Syndrome (HNPCC)
  • Genetics: Autosomal dominant; mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2)
  • Cancer risk: CRC (up to 80%), endometrial, ovarian, gastric, urinary tract, others
  • Features: Right-sided CRC, early onset, multiple primary cancers, MSI-high tumors
  • Screening: Colonoscopy every 1-2 years starting age 20-25 (or 2-5 years before youngest case in family)
📝 AMSTERDAM II CRITERIA (Lynch)
"3-2-1 Rule": ≥3 relatives with Lynch-associated cancer, across ≥2 generations, ≥1 diagnosed <50 years
Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP)
  • Genetics: Autosomal dominant; APC gene mutation
  • Features: >100 adenomatous polyps; 100% CRC risk by age 40 if untreated
  • Extracolonic: Duodenal adenomas (leading cause of death after colectomy), desmoid tumors, osteomas, CHRPE
  • Management: Prophylactic colectomy (typically late teens/early 20s); upper GI surveillance
Attenuated FAP & MUTYH-Associated Polyposis
  • Attenuated FAP: Fewer polyps (10-100), later onset, still high CRC risk
  • MAP: Autosomal RECESSIVE; resembles attenuated FAP; screen siblings
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Universal Tumor Testing
All newly diagnosed CRCs should be tested for Lynch syndrome via:
  • Immunohistochemistry (IHC) for MMR proteins, AND/OR
  • MSI testing
If abnormal → germline genetic testing

🟤 Liver Disease

💊

Approach to Abnormal Liver Tests

HIGH YIELD
Hepatocellular vs Cholestatic Pattern
  • Hepatocellular: ALT/AST predominant elevation (ratio of ALT:ALP >5)
  • Cholestatic: ALP/bilirubin predominant (ratio of ALT:ALP <2)
  • Mixed: Ratio 2-5
⚡ BOARD PEARL: AST:ALT Ratio
  • AST:ALT >2:1: Think alcoholic liver disease (AST rarely >500)
  • ALT > AST: Most other causes (NAFLD, viral hepatitis)
  • Very high transaminases (>1000): Acute viral hepatitis, ischemic hepatitis, drug/toxin (acetaminophen), autoimmune flare
Isolated Elevated ALP
  • First: Check GGT or 5'-nucleotidase to confirm hepatic source (vs bone)
  • If hepatic: RUQ ultrasound (biliary dilation?)
  • If no dilation: Consider PBC, PSC, infiltrative disease, medications

NAFLD / MASLD

HIGH YIELD
Terminology Update
  • MASLD: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (new term)
  • MASH: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (formerly NASH)
Diagnosis
  • Imaging: Hepatic steatosis on ultrasound, CT, or MRI
  • Exclusion: Rule out other causes (alcohol, viral, autoimmune, drugs)
  • Fibrosis staging: FIB-4 score, NAFLD fibrosis score, transient elastography (FibroScan), MR elastography
  • Liver biopsy: Gold standard to distinguish simple steatosis from NASH and stage fibrosis
📘 AASLD NAFLD Guidelines
Management:
  • Lifestyle: Weight loss (7-10% body weight improves NASH); exercise
  • Resmetirom: First FDA-approved drug for NASH with fibrosis (thyroid hormone receptor agonist)
  • Vitamin E: Consider in non-diabetic NASH (800 IU daily)
  • Pioglitazone: May use in NASH (with or without diabetes)
  • GLP-1 agonists: Benefit for NAFLD with diabetes/obesity (semaglutide promising)
  • Bariatric surgery: Can improve/resolve NASH in obesity
📊 FIB-4 SCORE
  • Formula: (Age × AST) / (Platelet × √ALT)
  • <1.3: Low risk for advanced fibrosis
  • >2.67: High risk for advanced fibrosis
  • 1.3-2.67: Indeterminate; further testing (elastography, biopsy)

Autoimmune Hepatitis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Demographics: Women > men; bimodal age (10-30 and 40-60)
  • Presentation: Variable — asymptomatic, chronic hepatitis, acute severe/fulminant
  • Associations: Other autoimmune diseases (thyroiditis, RA, UC, Sjögren's)
Diagnosis
  • Labs: Elevated transaminases, hypergammaglobulinemia (IgG)
  • Autoantibodies:
    • Type 1: ANA, anti-smooth muscle (ASMA) — most common
    • Type 2: Anti-LKM1, anti-LC1 — more common in children
  • Liver biopsy: Interface hepatitis, plasma cell infiltration
  • Exclude: Viral hepatitis, Wilson's, drug-induced
Treatment
  • Induction: Prednisone (± azathioprine) — most respond well
  • Maintenance: Azathioprine monotherapy (often can wean steroids)
  • Treatment duration: Usually lifelong; relapse common if stopped
  • Check TPMT before azathioprine (toxicity risk if deficient)

PBC & PSC

HIGH YIELD
Feature Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC) Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
Demographics Middle-aged women (90%) Young-middle aged men
Ducts affected Small intrahepatic bile ducts Medium/large intra- and extrahepatic ducts
Association Other autoimmune diseases, Sjögren's IBD (especially UC) in 70-80%
Antibody Anti-mitochondrial (AMA) — 95% p-ANCA (non-specific)
Imaging Normal ducts on MRCP MRCP: "beading" (strictures and dilations)
Treatment Ursodiol (UDCA) — improves survival No proven medical therapy; treat complications
Cancer risk HCC (if cirrhosis) Cholangiocarcinoma (10-15% lifetime), CRC
⚡ BOARD PEARL: PBC Treatment
  • Ursodiol (UDCA) 13-15 mg/kg/day: First-line for all PBC patients
  • Inadequate response: Add obeticholic acid (FXR agonist)
  • Pruritus: Cholestyramine, rifampin, naltrexone, sertraline

Hereditary Hemochromatosis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Classic triad: Cirrhosis, diabetes, bronze skin ("bronze diabetes")
  • Other manifestations: Cardiomyopathy, arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCP), hypogonadism
  • Typically presents middle age (women later due to menstrual iron loss)
Diagnosis
  • Screen: Transferrin saturation (>45%) and ferritin
  • Confirm: HFE gene testing (C282Y homozygosity most common)
  • Liver biopsy: For staging if suspected cirrhosis or ferritin >1000
  • MRI: Can quantify hepatic iron
Treatment
  • Phlebotomy: Weekly until ferritin <50-100, then maintenance (every 2-4 months)
  • Screen first-degree relatives
  • Avoid: Vitamin C supplements (increases iron absorption), alcohol
  • HCC surveillance if cirrhosis

Wilson Disease

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Genetics: Autosomal recessive; ATP7B gene mutation
  • Age of onset: Usually 5-35 years
  • Liver: Hepatitis, cirrhosis, acute liver failure
  • Neuropsychiatric: Tremor, dysarthria, dystonia, parkinsonism, psychiatric symptoms
  • Eyes: Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper in Descemet's membrane) — not always present
Diagnosis
  • Low serum ceruloplasmin (<20 mg/dL)
  • Elevated 24-hour urine copper (>100 μg)
  • Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp exam
  • Liver biopsy: Elevated hepatic copper content
  • Genetic testing if uncertain
🚨 WILSON'S WITH ACUTE LIVER FAILURE
Look for: Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, low ALP, ALP:bilirubin ratio <4, AST:ALT >2.2
High mortality without transplant
Treatment
  • Chelation: D-penicillamine or trientine (removes copper)
  • Zinc: Blocks intestinal copper absorption (maintenance or mild disease)
  • Lifelong treatment required
  • Screen first-degree relatives

🟤 Cirrhosis & Complications

🚨

Cirrhosis Diagnosis & Staging

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • Clinical + imaging: Often sufficient (nodular liver, splenomegaly, varices)
  • Labs: Low platelets, albumin; elevated INR, bilirubin
  • Elastography: FibroScan or MRE — non-invasive fibrosis assessment
  • Liver biopsy: Gold standard but not always needed
📊 CHILD-PUGH SCORE

Assess: Ascites, Encephalopathy, Bilirubin, Albumin, INR (each 1-3 points)

  • Class A (5-6): Compensated, good prognosis
  • Class B (7-9): Significant functional compromise
  • Class C (10-15): Decompensated, poor prognosis
📊 MELD SCORE

Uses: Bilirubin, INR, Creatinine, Sodium (MELD-Na)

  • Predicts 90-day mortality
  • Used for liver transplant allocation
  • MELD ≥15: Consider transplant referral

Ascites

HIGH YIELD
Diagnostic Paracentesis
  • Perform on ALL new-onset ascites and hospital admissions with ascites
  • SAAG (Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient):
    • ≥1.1 g/dL: Portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure)
    • <1.1 g/dL: Non-portal hypertension (malignancy, TB, nephrotic)
  • Total protein: Low (<2.5) = cirrhosis; High = cardiac ascites, malignancy
📘 AASLD Ascites Guidelines
Treatment:
  • Sodium restriction: 2g/day (88 mEq)
  • Diuretics: Spironolactone 100mg + furosemide 40mg (100:40 ratio); titrate to max 400:160
  • Goal: Weight loss 0.5 kg/day (without edema) or 1 kg/day (with edema)
  • Large volume paracentesis (>5L): Give albumin 6-8 g/L removed
🚨 REFRACTORY ASCITES
  • Diuretic-resistant or diuretic-intractable
  • Options: Serial large-volume paracentesis, TIPS, liver transplant
  • Avoid NSAIDs (cause sodium retention and AKI)

Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis
  • Ascitic fluid PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ — diagnostic threshold
  • Culture often negative (~40% positive)
  • Symptoms may be subtle: Fever, abdominal pain, encephalopathy, renal dysfunction
  • Low threshold to tap any cirrhotic patient with clinical deterioration
📘 SBP Treatment
  • Empiric antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (or cefotaxime) × 5 days
  • Alternative: Fluoroquinolone if no prior FQ prophylaxis
  • IV Albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1 + 1 g/kg on day 3 (reduces mortality and HRS)
  • Repeat paracentesis at 48h if not improving clinically
⚡ BOARD PEARL: SBP Prophylaxis
Primary prophylaxis:
  • GI bleed: Ceftriaxone 1g × 7 days (then norfloxacin/cipro)
  • Low ascitic fluid protein (<1.5) with liver failure or renal dysfunction
Secondary prophylaxis: Lifelong after SBP episode (norfloxacin 400mg daily or TMP-SMX DS daily)

Hepatic Encephalopathy

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Spectrum: Minimal (subtle cognitive deficits) → Overt (confusion, asterixis, coma)
  • Precipitants: Infection, GI bleed, constipation, dehydration, sedatives, hypokalemia, high protein intake, TIPS
  • Asterixis: "Flapping tremor" — not specific for HE
  • Ammonia level: Correlates poorly with severity; don't follow serially
📘 Treatment
  • Identify and treat precipitants (most important!)
  • Lactulose: Titrate to 2-3 soft bowel movements/day (trap ammonia as NH4+)
  • Rifaximin: Add for recurrent HE (reduces recurrence by 50%)
  • Protein restriction: NOT recommended (leads to malnutrition)
📝 CLASSIC BOARD VIGNETTE

Scenario: Cirrhotic patient presents confused with asterixis after large-volume paracentesis without albumin replacement.

Answer: Hepatic encephalopathy likely precipitated by paracentesis-induced circulatory dysfunction. Treat with lactulose, ensure albumin given for future LVPs.

Hepatorenal Syndrome

HIGH YIELD
🚨 HEPATORENAL SYNDROME
Functional renal failure in advanced cirrhosis due to splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction
Diagnostic Criteria
  • Cirrhosis with ascites
  • AKI per KDIGO criteria (Cr increase ≥0.3 in 48h or ≥50% in 7 days)
  • No improvement after 2 days of diuretic withdrawal + albumin challenge (1 g/kg/day)
  • Absence of shock, nephrotoxic drugs, structural kidney disease
Treatment
  • Albumin + vasoconstrictor:
    • Terlipressin (if available) — preferred
    • Norepinephrine (requires ICU)
    • Midodrine + octreotide (less effective)
  • Definitive treatment: Liver transplantation
  • TIPS: May be considered in select patients

Hepatocellular Carcinoma Surveillance

HIGH YIELD
📘 AASLD HCC Surveillance
Who to screen:
  • All patients with cirrhosis (any etiology)
  • Chronic HBV carriers (certain populations regardless of cirrhosis)
How: Ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months
If abnormal: Diagnostic CT or MRI with contrast
Diagnostic Imaging
  • LI-RADS criteria: Standardized reporting for CT/MRI
  • Hallmark features: Arterial phase hyperenhancement + washout + capsule
  • LI-RADS 5: Definite HCC (biopsy often not needed)

🦠 Viral Hepatitis

💊

Hepatitis B

HIGH YIELD
Serology Interpretation
HBsAg Anti-HBs Anti-HBc Interpretation
+ - IgM+ Acute infection
+ - IgG+ Chronic infection
- + + Recovered (immune)
- + - Vaccinated (immune)
- - + Isolated anti-HBc (resolved, window, occult, false+)
⚡ BOARD PEARL: HBeAg Status
  • HBeAg-positive: High replication, high infectivity
  • HBeAg-negative chronic HBV: Can still have active disease (precore/core promoter mutants)
  • Check HBV DNA to assess viral replication
📘 AASLD HBV Treatment Guidelines
Indications for treatment:
  • Immune-active phase (elevated ALT + elevated HBV DNA)
  • Cirrhosis with detectable HBV DNA
  • Family history of HCC
  • Immunosuppression (prophylaxis/treatment)
First-line agents: Entecavir or tenofovir (TAF or TDF) — high barrier to resistance
🚨 HBV REACTIVATION
  • Risk: Immunosuppressive therapy (anti-CD20, chemotherapy, anti-TNF, high-dose steroids)
  • Screen ALL patients before immunosuppression (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs)
  • Prophylaxis: Entecavir or tenofovir in HBsAg+ patients
  • Monitor: Even isolated anti-HBc+ patients at risk with potent immunosuppression

Hepatitis C

HIGH YIELD
Screening & Diagnosis
  • USPSTF recommends: One-time screening for all adults 18-79
  • Screening test: Anti-HCV antibody
  • If positive: Confirm with HCV RNA (viral load)
  • Anti-HCV+ but RNA negative: Cleared infection (spontaneous or treated)
📘 AASLD/IDSA HCV Guidelines
Treatment — Direct-Acting Antivirals (DAAs):
  • ALL patients with chronic HCV should be treated (regardless of fibrosis stage)
  • Pangenotypic regimens: Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir
  • Duration: Usually 8-12 weeks
  • Cure rates (SVR12): >95%
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Post-SVR Management
  • SVR = Cure: Undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks after treatment completion
  • If NO cirrhosis: No further HCV-related follow-up needed
  • If cirrhosis: Continue HCC surveillance indefinitely (risk persists)
  • Reinfection possible: Counsel on risk behaviors

Hepatitis A, D, E

CORE
Virus Transmission Chronicity Key Points
Hepatitis A Fecal-oral No Vaccine-preventable; supportive care; vaccinate HBV/HCV patients
Hepatitis D Blood/body fluids Yes (with HBV) Requires HBV co-infection; increases severity/progression
Hepatitis E Fecal-oral (water) Rare (immunocompromised) High mortality in pregnancy; endemic in developing countries
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Hepatitis D
  • Defective RNA virus — needs HBsAg to infect
  • Test all HBsAg+ patients for anti-HDV
  • Co-infection: Simultaneous HBV + HDV → usually clears
  • Superinfection: HDV in chronic HBV → usually becomes chronic, accelerates disease

💚 Biliary Disease

💊

Cholelithiasis & Cholecystitis

HIGH YIELD
Biliary Colic
  • Presentation: Episodic RUQ/epigastric pain, often postprandial, lasting 30 min to several hours
  • Diagnosis: RUQ ultrasound (gallstones + GB wall normal)
  • Treatment: Elective cholecystectomy if symptomatic
Acute Cholecystitis
  • Presentation: Persistent RUQ pain (>6 hours), fever, Murphy's sign
  • Labs: Leukocytosis; may have mild transaminase/bilirubin elevation
  • Imaging: US (stones, GB wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign)
  • HIDA scan: If US inconclusive; non-filling of GB = cystic duct obstruction
📘 Treatment
  • NPO, IV fluids, pain control, antibiotics
  • Cholecystectomy: Preferably within 72 hours (early surgery preferred)
  • Poor surgical candidate: Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube
🚨 COMPLICATIONS OF CHOLECYSTITIS
  • Gangrenous cholecystitis: Necrosis of GB wall; may be painless in elderly/diabetics
  • Emphysematous cholecystitis: Gas-forming organisms; diabetics; emergent surgery
  • Perforation: Localized or free perforation

Choledocholithiasis & Cholangitis

HIGH YIELD
Choledocholithiasis (CBD Stones)
  • Presentation: RUQ pain, jaundice, elevated LFTs (cholestatic pattern)
  • Diagnosis: MRCP or EUS (non-invasive); ERCP if high probability or therapeutic intent
Acute Cholangitis
  • Charcot's triad: Fever, RUQ pain, jaundice (50-70% of cases)
  • Reynolds' pentad: + Hypotension + Altered mental status (severe/suppurative)
  • Pathogens: E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides
📘 Cholangitis Management
  • Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (pip-tazo, ampicillin-sulbactam, or FQ + metronidazole)
  • Biliary drainage: ERCP within 24-48 hours (emergent if severe/septic)
  • Alternatives: Percutaneous transhepatic drainage or surgical drainage if ERCP fails
📝 CHARCOT'S TRIAD
Fever + RUQ Pain + Jaundice = Cholangitis

Gallstone Pancreatitis

HIGH YIELD
Key Points
  • Most common cause of acute pancreatitis
  • Suspect if: ALT >3× ULN, dilated CBD, visible CBD stone
  • ERCP: Indicated if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction
  • Cholecystectomy: During same admission for mild gallstone pancreatitis (reduces recurrence)

🟡 Pancreatic Disorders

💊

Acute Pancreatitis

HIGH YIELD
Diagnosis (2 of 3 criteria)
  • Characteristic abdominal pain (epigastric, radiating to back)
  • Lipase (or amylase) ≥3× upper limit of normal
  • Imaging findings of pancreatitis
📝 CAUSES OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS ("I GET SMASHED")
Idiopathic, Gallstones, EtOH, Trauma, Steroids, Mumps, Autoimmune, Scorpion stings, Hyperlipidemia/Hypercalcemia, ERCP, Drugs
📘 ACG Acute Pancreatitis Guidelines
Management:
  • Aggressive IV fluids: Lactated Ringer's preferred; goal-directed (watch for fluid overload)
  • Pain control
  • Nutrition: Early oral feeding when tolerated (within 24-48h if mild); enteral preferred over TPN
  • Antibiotics: NOT indicated prophylactically (even in severe/necrotic pancreatitis)
📊 SEVERITY ASSESSMENT
  • BISAP score: BUN >25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age >60, Pleural effusion
  • ≥3 points: Higher mortality risk
  • Ranson's criteria: At admission and 48h (≥3 = severe)
  • Atlanta classification: Mild (no organ failure), Moderate (transient OF), Severe (persistent OF)
Complications
  • Acute peripancreatic fluid collection: <4 weeks, no wall
  • Pseudocyst: >4 weeks, encapsulated, no necrosis
  • Acute necrotic collection: <4 weeks with necrosis
  • Walled-off necrosis (WON): >4 weeks, encapsulated necrosis
  • Infected necrosis: Gas on CT, clinical deterioration; may need drainage/necrosectomy

Chronic Pancreatitis

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Etiology: Alcohol (most common in US), smoking, genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR), autoimmune
  • Symptoms: Recurrent/chronic abdominal pain, steatorrhea, weight loss, diabetes
  • Exocrine insufficiency: Usually after >90% gland destruction
Diagnosis
  • CT/MRCP: Calcifications, ductal dilation, atrophy
  • EUS: Most sensitive for early changes
  • Fecal elastase: <200 μg/g indicates exocrine insufficiency
Management
  • Pain: Abstinence from alcohol/tobacco, analgesics, PERT, celiac plexus block, surgery
  • Exocrine insufficiency: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with meals
  • Endocrine insufficiency: Manage diabetes (brittle, prone to hypoglycemia)
  • Nutrition: Small frequent meals, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

Pancreatic Cystic Lesions

HIGH YIELD
Type Features Malignant Potential
Serous cystadenoma Microcystic, honeycomb appearance, central scar Very low (benign)
Mucinous cystic neoplasm (MCN) Women, body/tail, no communication with duct, ovarian-type stroma Moderate; surgical resection recommended
IPMN - Main duct Main pancreatic duct dilation, mucin at ampulla High (50-70%); surgery usually indicated
IPMN - Branch duct Side branch dilation, communicates with main duct Lower; surveillance vs surgery based on features
Pseudocyst History of pancreatitis, no solid component, low CEA None
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Cyst Fluid Analysis
  • Mucinous (MCN, IPMN): High CEA (>192), viscous, mucin+
  • Serous: Low CEA, low amylase
  • Pseudocyst: High amylase, low CEA

⚡ GI Motility Disorders

💊

Gastroparesis

HIGH YIELD
Etiology
  • Diabetes: Most common identifiable cause
  • Idiopathic: Often post-viral
  • Post-surgical: Vagal nerve injury
  • Medications: Opioids, anticholinergics, GLP-1 agonists
Diagnosis
  • EGD: Rule out mechanical obstruction (required before diagnosis)
  • Gastric emptying scintigraphy (GES): Gold standard; >10% retention at 4 hours
  • Hold medications that affect motility before testing
📘 Treatment
  • Dietary: Small, frequent, low-fat, low-fiber meals
  • Glycemic control (hyperglycemia worsens emptying)
  • Prokinetics: Metoclopramide (FDA black box for tardive dyskinesia — limit to 12 weeks), domperidone (not FDA approved), erythromycin
  • Anti-emetics: Ondansetron, prochlorperazine
  • Refractory: Gastric electrical stimulation, pyloromyotomy, jejunostomy tube

Chronic Constipation

CORE
Evaluation
  • Rome IV criteria for functional constipation
  • Alarm features: Hematochezia, weight loss, family history CRC, age >50 without screening
  • Refractory cases: Anorectal manometry, balloon expulsion test, colonic transit study, defecography
Treatment Ladder
  1. Fiber + fluids
  2. Osmotic laxatives: PEG (MiraLAX), lactulose
  3. Stimulant laxatives: Bisacodyl, senna (not for daily long-term use)
  4. Secretagogues: Linaclotide, lubiprostone, plecanatide (prescription)
  5. Prokinetics: Prucalopride (5-HT4 agonist)
  6. Dyssynergic defecation: Biofeedback therapy (first-line)

🍎 GI Nutrition

💊

Enteral vs Parenteral Nutrition

HIGH YIELD
📘 ASPEN Guidelines
"If the gut works, use it"
  • Enteral nutrition preferred — maintains gut integrity, reduces infections, cheaper
  • TPN indications: Non-functioning GI tract, severe malabsorption, GI obstruction, high-output fistula, short bowel syndrome (initially)
Enteral Access
  • NG tube: Short-term (<4 weeks), intact mental status/gag
  • PEG (gastrostomy): Long-term, functional stomach, aspiration risk acceptable
  • PEJ (jejunostomy): High aspiration risk, gastroparesis, gastric outlet obstruction
🚨 REFEEDING SYNDROME
  • Risk: Severely malnourished patients started on nutrition
  • Mechanism: Insulin surge → intracellular shift of phosphorus, potassium, magnesium
  • Complications: Hypophosphatemia (most important), arrhythmias, heart failure, respiratory failure
  • Prevention: Start low, advance slowly; replete electrolytes; give thiamine before feeding

Short Bowel Syndrome

HIGH YIELD
Clinical Features
  • Definition: Malabsorption due to loss of small bowel (usually <200 cm remaining)
  • Causes: Surgical resection (Crohn's, mesenteric ischemia, trauma), radiation enteritis
  • Symptoms: Diarrhea, steatorrhea, dehydration, malnutrition, micronutrient deficiencies
Management
  • Initial: TPN for fluid/nutrition while gut adapts
  • Oral/enteral: Transition as tolerated; frequent small meals, complex carbs, moderate fat
  • Anti-diarrheal: Loperamide, codeine
  • Acid suppression: PPI (reduces gastric hypersecretion)
  • Teduglutide: GLP-2 analog — promotes intestinal adaptation, reduces TPN dependence
  • Intestinal transplant: Refractory cases with TPN complications
⚡ BOARD PEARL: Ileal Resection Consequences
  • <100 cm resected: Bile acid diarrhea (watery) — treat with cholestyramine
  • >100 cm resected: Fat malabsorption/steatorrhea (bile acid pool depleted) — reduce fat, PERT
  • Also: B12 deficiency, gallstones (cholesterol), kidney stones (oxalate)

🫁 ABIM Gastroenterology Study Guide

Evidence-based content from ACG/AGA/AASLD Guidelines

For educational purposes only. Verify all clinical decisions with current guidelines.

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide 🦠 ID Study Guide ❤️ Cardio Study Guide 💊 Drug Guide
↑ Back to top

🔬 Endocrinology, Diabetes & Metabolism

ABIM Board Review — ADA/Endocrine Society/AACE Guidelines

🎯 Core Endocrine Principles

🩸Two abnormal tests required to diagnose diabetes (asymptomatic)
💊Metformin first-line in T2DM; add SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for cardiorenal disease
🦋TSH is the best screening test for thyroid dysfunction
