🎯Individualize HbA1c targets — tighter control in young, looser in frail elderly
📊Adrenal incidentaloma — rule out pheochromocytoma before any procedure
🧪Calcium disorders: always check PTH simultaneously
🩸 Diabetes 🦋 Thyroid ⚡ Adrenal 🧠 Pituitary 🧪 Calcium 📊 Lipids ⚖️ Obesity 🔬 Reproductive 🧬 MEN Syndromes 🚨 Emergencies

1. Diabetes Mellitus — Overview & Diagnosis Very Common

Diagnostic Criteria (ADA 2024)

TestDiabetesPrediabetes
Fasting glucose≥126 mg/dL100-125 mg/dL (IFG)
2-hour OGTT≥200 mg/dL140-199 mg/dL (IGT)
HbA1c≥6.5%5.7-6.4%
Random glucose≥200 mg/dL + symptoms—
Two abnormal tests required for diagnosis in asymptomatic patients. A single random glucose ≥200 with classic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) is diagnostic.

Type 1 vs Type 2 Diabetes

FeatureType 1Type 2
OnsetUsually <30 yearsUsually >40 years
Body habitusOften leanOften obese (80%)
Ketosis proneYesUsually no
C-peptideLow/absentNormal/high
AutoantibodiesGAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAANegative
PathophysiologyAutoimmune β-cell destructionInsulin resistance + β-cell dysfunction
Initial treatmentInsulin requiredLifestyle + metformin

Other Diabetes Types

  • LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults): T1D presenting in adults (>30 years), slower progression, GAD65 positive
  • MODY (Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young): Autosomal dominant, young onset, no autoantibodies
    • MODY 2 (GCK): Mild fasting hyperglycemia, rarely needs treatment
    • MODY 3 (HNF1A): Progressive, responds to sulfonylureas
  • Gestational diabetes: Glucose intolerance first recognized in pregnancy
  • Secondary diabetes: Pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, Cushing's, acromegaly, drugs (steroids, antipsychotics)
LADA is often misdiagnosed as T2D. Suspect in lean adults with poor response to oral agents. Check GAD65 antibodies.

2. Type 2 Diabetes Management High-Yield

Glycemic Targets (ADA 2024)

ParameterGeneral TargetNotes
HbA1c<7%Individualize: <6.5% if low hypoglycemia risk; <8% if limited life expectancy
Fasting glucose80-130 mg/dLPreprandial
Postprandial glucose<180 mg/dL1-2 hours after meal start
Time in range (CGM)>70%70-180 mg/dL

First-Line Therapy

Metformin remains first-line for most patients with T2D, combined with lifestyle modification. However, ADA 2024 emphasizes organ protection — consider SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA as first-line if ASCVD, HF, or CKD regardless of A1c.

Drug Selection Based on Comorbidities

ComorbidityPreferred AgentKey Benefit
ASCVDGLP-1 RA (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide)Reduces MACE
Heart failure (HFrEF or HFpEF)SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin)Reduces HF hospitalization
CKD (eGFR 20-60)SGLT2iSlows CKD progression
ObesityGLP-1 RA (especially semaglutide, tirzepatide)Significant weight loss
Hypoglycemia concernGLP-1 RA, SGLT2i, DPP-4iLow hypoglycemia risk
Cost concernMetformin, sulfonylureasGeneric availability
SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 RAs have cardiovascular and renal benefits independent of glucose lowering. Use them even if A1c is at goal in patients with ASCVD, HF, or CKD.

Oral Agents Summary

ClassMechanismA1c ReductionKey Concerns
Metformin↓ Hepatic glucose production1-1.5%GI side effects, B12 deficiency, lactic acidosis (rare)
SGLT2i↓ Renal glucose reabsorption0.5-1%UTI, genital mycotic infections, euglycemic DKA, Fournier's
GLP-1 RA↑ Insulin, ↓ glucagon, ↓ appetite1-1.5%Nausea, pancreatitis (rare), thyroid C-cell tumors (rodents)
DPP-4i↑ Endogenous incretins0.5-0.8%Weight neutral, possible HF risk (saxagliptin)
Sulfonylureas↑ Insulin secretion1-1.5%Hypoglycemia, weight gain
Thiazolidinediones↑ Insulin sensitivity (PPARγ)1-1.5%Edema, HF, fractures, weight gain
Hold metformin before iodinated contrast if eGFR <30. Recheck creatinine 48h after procedure before resuming. Hold SGLT2i 3-4 days before major surgery to prevent euglycemic DKA.

3. Insulin Therapy Common

Insulin Types

TypeExamplesOnsetPeakDuration
Rapid-actingLispro, aspart, glulisine5-15 min1-2 hr3-5 hr
Short-actingRegular30-60 min2-4 hr5-8 hr
IntermediateNPH1-2 hr4-12 hr12-18 hr
Long-actingGlargine (U100, U300), detemir1-2 hrNone20-24 hr
Ultra-longDegludec1-2 hrNone42+ hr

Initiating Basal Insulin in T2D

  • Starting dose: 10 units or 0.1-0.2 units/kg/day
  • Titration: Increase by 2-4 units every 3-7 days until fasting glucose at target
  • Target fasting glucose: 80-130 mg/dL

Basal-Bolus Regimen (Type 1 or Advanced T2D)

  • Total daily dose (TDD): 0.4-0.5 units/kg/day (T1D), higher in T2D
  • Basal: 40-50% of TDD
  • Bolus: Divide remaining 50-60% among meals
  • Correction factor: 1800 ÷ TDD = mg/dL drop per unit (rapid insulin)
  • Carb ratio: 500 ÷ TDD = grams of carbs covered per unit
Example: TDD = 50 units
Correction factor = 1800 ÷ 50 = 36 (1 unit lowers BG by 36 mg/dL)
Carb ratio = 500 ÷ 50 = 10 (1 unit covers 10g carbs)
NPH has an unpredictable peak — higher hypoglycemia risk. Long-acting analogs (glargine, degludec) are preferred for basal coverage.

4. Inpatient Diabetes Management Boards Favorite

Glycemic Targets (ADA/Endocrine Society)

SettingTarget GlucoseNotes
General ward140-180 mg/dLAvoid <100 mg/dL
ICU140-180 mg/dL140-180 preferred over tighter control (NICE-SUGAR)
Perioperative<180 mg/dLHold oral agents, use insulin
NICE-SUGAR Trial: Intensive glucose control (81-108) increased mortality vs conventional (144-180) in ICU patients. Target 140-180 mg/dL.

Inpatient Insulin Regimen (Basal-Bolus)

  • Calculate TDD: 0.3-0.5 units/kg/day (reduce if renal failure, elderly, malnourished)
  • Basal: 50% of TDD as glargine or NPH
  • Nutritional (bolus): 50% divided among meals (if eating)
  • Correction: Sliding scale added to scheduled doses
Never use sliding scale insulin alone! Reactive rather than proactive — leads to glycemic variability. Always include scheduled basal ± nutritional insulin.

Special Situations

SituationManagement
NPOContinue basal insulin (may reduce by 20-25%), hold bolus
Tube feeds (continuous)Basal insulin + correction Q6h OR NPH Q8-12h
TPNAdd regular insulin to TPN bag (start 0.1 units/g dextrose)
SteroidsIncrease insulin 20-40%, mainly nutritional/afternoon dosing
Renal failure (eGFR <30)Reduce TDD by 25-50%
Steroids cause primarily postprandial and afternoon/evening hyperglycemia. NPH given with morning steroids can help cover this pattern.

Transition to Outpatient

  • Give first dose of long-acting insulin 2-4 hours before stopping IV insulin
  • Continue home regimen if A1c was at goal on admission
  • If hyperglycemic on admission, discharge on ~80% of inpatient TDD

5. Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) Emergency

Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterMildModerateSevere
Glucose>250 mg/dL (can be lower in euglycemic DKA)
Arterial pH7.25-7.307.00-7.24<7.00
Bicarbonate15-18 mEq/L10-14.9 mEq/L<10 mEq/L
Anion gap>12 mEq/L
KetonesPositive (serum β-hydroxybutyrate >3 mmol/L)
Mental statusAlertAlert/drowsyStupor/coma

Precipitants (The 6 I's)

Infection (most common), Insulin (missed/insufficient), Infarction (MI), Intoxication, Infant (pregnancy), Iatrogenic (steroids, SGLT2i)

Treatment Protocol

1. Fluids (First Priority)

  • Initial: 1-1.5 L NS in first hour
  • Then: 250-500 mL/hr NS (or 0.45% if corrected Na normal/high)
  • When glucose <200: Switch to D5 0.45% NS

2. Insulin

  • Regular insulin: 0.1 units/kg bolus, then 0.1 units/kg/hr infusion
  • OR: 0.14 units/kg/hr infusion (no bolus)
  • Target: Glucose drop 50-70 mg/dL/hr
  • When glucose <200: Reduce infusion to 0.02-0.05 units/kg/hr, keep glucose 150-200 until anion gap closes

3. Potassium

Serum K+Action
<3.3 mEq/LHold insulin, give 20-30 mEq/hr K+ until >3.3
3.3-5.3 mEq/LAdd 20-30 mEq K+ to each liter of fluids
>5.3 mEq/LNo K+ replacement, check Q2h
Initial serum K+ is often normal or high despite total body K+ depletion. Insulin drives K+ into cells — can precipitate fatal hypokalemia. Check K+ before starting insulin.

4. Bicarbonate

  • Only if pH <6.9: Give 100 mEq NaHCO3 in 400 mL water with 20 mEq KCl over 2 hours
  • Repeat until pH >7.0

Resolution Criteria

  • Glucose <200 mg/dL
  • Anion gap ≤12 mEq/L
  • Bicarbonate ≥15 mEq/L
  • pH >7.3
Transition to subcutaneous insulin when DKA resolved AND patient can eat. Give long-acting insulin 2 hours before stopping IV insulin to prevent rebound hyperglycemia/ketosis.

Euglycemic DKA

  • Definition: DKA with glucose <250 mg/dL
  • Causes: SGLT2 inhibitors (most common), pregnancy, fasting, alcohol
  • Management: Same as DKA but start dextrose-containing fluids earlier

6. Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) Emergency

Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterHHSDKA (for comparison)
Glucose>600 mg/dL>250 mg/dL
Serum osmolality>320 mOsm/kgVariable
pH>7.30<7.30
Bicarbonate>18 mEq/L<18 mEq/L
KetonesNegative/tracePositive
Mental statusOften alteredVariable
Effective Serum Osmolality:
2 × Na + (Glucose ÷ 18) = mOsm/kg
(BUN not included — freely crosses membranes)

Key Differences from DKA

  • More common in T2D (vs T1D in DKA)
  • Typically older patients with limited access to water
  • Profound dehydration (may have 8-10 L fluid deficit)
  • Higher mortality than DKA (10-20% vs 1-5%)
  • Enough insulin to prevent ketosis but not enough to prevent hyperglycemia

Treatment

  • Fluids (priority): Aggressive NS (may need 6-10 L total)
    • 1-1.5 L in first hour
    • Switch to 0.45% NS once hemodynamically stable and Na normal/high
  • Insulin: Lower doses than DKA (0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr) — fluids alone lower glucose significantly
  • Potassium: Same as DKA protocol
  • Thromboprophylaxis: High VTE risk — anticoagulate
Avoid rapid correction of osmolality — can cause cerebral edema. Target glucose reduction 50-70 mg/dL/hr and sodium correction ≤10-12 mEq/L/day.

7. Hypoglycemia High-Yield

Definition

  • Level 1: Glucose <70 mg/dL (alert value)
  • Level 2: Glucose <54 mg/dL (clinically significant)
  • Level 3: Severe — altered mental status, needs assistance

Whipple's Triad

  1. Symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia
  2. Low plasma glucose at time of symptoms
  3. Resolution of symptoms with glucose correction

Causes of Hypoglycemia in Non-Diabetics

CategoryCausesClues
Insulinomaβ-cell tumorHigh insulin, high C-peptide, low β-OHB, negative sulfonylurea screen
SulfonylureaAccidental or intentionalHigh insulin, high C-peptide, positive drug screen
Exogenous insulinFactitious or overdoseHigh insulin, LOW C-peptide, negative drug screen
Adrenal insufficiencyCortisol deficiencyCheck cortisol, ACTH
Hepatic failure↓ GluconeogenesisLiver dysfunction obvious
SepsisMultifactorialClinical context
Non-islet cell tumorIGF-II producing tumorsLow insulin, low C-peptide, elevated IGF-II
Post-bariatricDumping syndrome, nesidioblastosisPost-gastric bypass, postprandial
Insulinoma workup: 72-hour fast with Q6h glucose, insulin, C-peptide, β-hydroxybutyrate. End fast when glucose <55 with symptoms OR glucose <45.

Interpretation of Hypoglycemia Labs

DiagnosisInsulinC-peptidePro-insulinSU Screen
Insulinoma↑↑↑Negative
Sulfonylurea↑↑↑Positive
Exogenous insulin↑↓↓Negative
IGF-II tumor↓↓↓Negative
Exogenous insulin suppresses endogenous secretion — C-peptide will be LOW. Insulinoma and sulfonylureas stimulate endogenous insulin — C-peptide will be HIGH.

Treatment

  • Conscious patient: 15-20g fast-acting carbs (glucose tabs, juice), recheck in 15 min
  • Unconscious/unable to swallow: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC or IV dextrose (25g D50)
  • Sulfonylurea overdose: May need octreotide (suppresses insulin) + prolonged dextrose infusion

8. Chronic Diabetic Complications Common

Microvascular Complications

Diabetic Retinopathy

StageFindingsManagement
Mild NPDRMicroaneurysms onlyAnnual exam
Moderate NPDR+ Hemorrhages, hard exudatesQ6-12 month exam
Severe NPDR4-2-1 rule: hemorrhages in 4 quadrants, venous beading in 2, IRMA in 1Q3-6 month exam, consider laser
PDRNeovascularizationPanretinal photocoagulation, anti-VEGF
Screening: T1D — annual exam starting 5 years after diagnosis. T2D — at diagnosis and annually thereafter.

Diabetic Nephropathy

  • Screening: Annual UACR and eGFR (at diagnosis for T2D, after 5 years for T1D)
  • Stages:
    • Moderately increased albuminuria: UACR 30-300 mg/g
    • Severely increased albuminuria: UACR >300 mg/g
  • Treatment:
    • ACEi or ARB (first-line for albuminuria)
    • SGLT2i (add if eGFR ≥20, slows progression — CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)
    • Finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA — FIDELIO, FIGARO)
    • BP goal <130/80 (or <120/80 if tolerated)

Diabetic Neuropathy

TypeFeaturesTreatment
Distal symmetric polyneuropathyStocking-glove, burning, numbnessDuloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin
AutonomicGastroparesis, orthostatic hypotension, resting tachycardia, erectile dysfunctionSymptom-directed
MononeuropathyCN III (pupil-sparing), carpal tunnelUsually self-limited
Diabetic amyotrophyProximal leg weakness, pain, wastingPain control, physical therapy
Diabetic CN III palsy typically spares the pupil (outer parasympathetics preserved). Pupil-involving CN III suggests aneurysm — get imaging!

Macrovascular Complications

  • ASCVD: Leading cause of death in diabetes
    • Statin therapy: moderate-intensity for all 40-75; high-intensity if ASCVD
    • Aspirin: Secondary prevention; primary prevention individualized (50-70 years with CVD risk)
    • BP goal: <130/80 mmHg
  • Peripheral arterial disease: ABI screening if symptoms or >50 years

Diabetic Foot

  • Screen annually: Monofilament (10g), vibration (128 Hz tuning fork), ankle reflexes
  • High-risk: History of ulcer, amputation, Charcot, neuropathy, PAD
  • Management: Proper footwear, daily self-exams, podiatry referral

9. Hypothyroidism Very Common

Causes

TypeCauses
Primary (95%)Hashimoto's thyroiditis (most common in iodine-sufficient areas), post-thyroidectomy, post-RAI, iodine deficiency (worldwide), drugs (amiodarone, lithium, checkpoint inhibitors)
CentralPituitary adenoma, surgery, radiation, Sheehan's syndrome, infiltrative disease

Clinical Features

  • Symptoms: Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, menorrhagia, depression, cognitive slowing
  • Signs: Bradycardia, delayed relaxation of reflexes, periorbital edema, non-pitting edema (myxedema), goiter (Hashimoto's)

Diagnosis

PatternTSHFree T4Interpretation
Primary hypothyroid↑↓Most common
Subclinical hypothyroid↑NormalTreat if TSH >10 or symptoms
Central hypothyroid↓ or Normal↓Check pituitary MRI
In central hypothyroidism, TSH is unreliable (may be low, normal, or mildly elevated). Follow free T4, not TSH, to guide treatment.

Hashimoto's Thyroiditis

  • Most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas
  • Autoimmune: Anti-TPO antibodies (90%+), anti-thyroglobulin
  • Associated with other autoimmune diseases (T1D, celiac, vitiligo)
  • May have initial thyrotoxic phase ("Hashitoxicosis")

Treatment

  • Levothyroxine (T4): First-line
    • Young/healthy: Full replacement dose 1.6 mcg/kg/day
    • Elderly or CAD: Start low (25-50 mcg), titrate slowly
  • Monitoring: TSH in 6-8 weeks, then annually once stable
  • Goal TSH: 0.5-4.0 mU/L (lower half of range for younger patients)
Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, 30-60 min before breakfast or 3+ hours after last meal. Separate from calcium, iron, PPIs by 4 hours.

Subclinical Hypothyroidism

  • Definition: Elevated TSH, normal free T4
  • Treat if:
    • TSH >10 mU/L
    • Symptoms attributable to hypothyroidism
    • Anti-TPO positive (higher progression risk)
    • Pregnancy or planning pregnancy

10. Hyperthyroidism High-Yield

Causes

CauseRAIUKey Features
Graves' disease↑ DiffuseTRAb positive, ophthalmopathy, pretibial myxedema
Toxic multinodular goiter↑ PatchyOlder patients, nodular goiter
Toxic adenoma↑ Focal ("hot" nodule)Single hyperfunctioning nodule
Thyroiditis (subacute, painless)↓Transient, self-limited
Exogenous thyroid hormone↓Low thyroglobulin
Amiodarone-induced (Type I)↑ or ↓Iodine-induced, underlying thyroid disease
Amiodarone-induced (Type II)↓Destructive thyroiditis
RAIU differentiates high uptake (Graves', toxic nodules) from low uptake (thyroiditis, exogenous). Essential for treatment planning.

Clinical Features

  • Symptoms: Palpitations, anxiety, tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss despite increased appetite, diarrhea, menstrual irregularity
  • Signs: Tachycardia, AFib (10-15%), hyperreflexia, warm moist skin, lid lag, goiter

Graves' Disease-Specific

  • Ophthalmopathy: Proptosis, periorbital edema, diplopia (extraocular muscle inflammation)
  • Dermopathy: Pretibial myxedema (orange-peel texture)
  • Thyroid acropachy: Clubbing, periosteal new bone (rare)
  • TRAb (TSI): Pathognomonic, can follow for remission

Treatment

Antithyroid Drugs (ATDs)

DrugMechanismKey Concerns
MethimazoleBlocks TPO, inhibits T4 synthesisFirst-line (except 1st trimester); teratogenic (aplasia cutis); agranulocytosis (0.3%)
PTUBlocks TPO + peripheral T4→T3Used in 1st trimester, thyroid storm; hepatotoxicity
Agranulocytosis: Check WBC if fever, sore throat. If ANC <500, stop ATD immediately and never rechallenge.

Other Treatments

  • Beta-blockers: Symptom control (propranolol preferred — also blocks T4→T3)
  • Radioactive iodine (RAI): Definitive treatment, causes hypothyroidism
  • Surgery: Large goiter, severe ophthalmopathy, pregnancy, concern for malignancy
Treatment Selection:
• Graves' (young, small goiter): ATDs first (30-50% remission after 12-18 months)
• Graves' (relapse, large goiter): RAI or surgery
• Toxic nodule: RAI or surgery (ATDs don't cause remission)
• Pregnancy: PTU 1st trimester, methimazole 2nd/3rd

11. Thyroid Storm Emergency

Clinical Features

  • Fever (often >104°F)
  • Tachycardia out of proportion to fever
  • Altered mental status (agitation, delirium, coma)
  • GI symptoms: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice
  • Cardiovascular: AFib, high-output heart failure

Burch-Wartofsky Score

Score ≥45 = thyroid storm; 25-44 = impending storm

Treatment (Order Matters!)

StepTreatmentRationale
1. Beta-blockerPropranolol 60-80mg Q4-6h (or esmolol drip)Control symptoms, ↓ T4→T3 conversion
2. PTU500-1000mg load, then 250mg Q4hBlock synthesis + T4→T3 conversion
3. IodineSSKI or Lugol's 1 hour AFTER PTUBlocks hormone release (Wolff-Chaikoff)
4. GlucocorticoidsHydrocortisone 100mg Q8h↓ T4→T3, treat relative adrenal insufficiency
5. SupportiveCooling, fluids, treat precipitantAddress underlying cause
Give PTU before iodine! Iodine provides substrate for more hormone synthesis if given first.

12. Thyroiditis High-Yield

Types Comparison

TypeCausePainRAIUCourse
Subacute (de Quervain)Viral (post-URI)Yes (neck tenderness)↓Hyper → hypo → recovery (weeks-months)
Painless/SilentAutoimmuneNo↓Hyper → hypo → recovery or permanent hypo
PostpartumAutoimmuneNo↓2-6 months postpartum; may recur with future pregnancies
Hashimoto'sAutoimmune (chronic)NoVariableProgressive hypothyroidism
Drug-inducedAmiodarone, checkpoint inhibitors, lithiumUsually no↓Variable
Subacute thyroiditis: Elevated ESR, tender thyroid, recent URI. Often misdiagnosed as pharyngitis. Treat with NSAIDs or steroids for pain. Beta-blockers for hyperthyroid symptoms.

Amiodarone-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction

TypeMechanismRAIUTreatment
Type 1 (hyperthyroidism)Iodine-induced (Jod-Basedow)↑ or normalMethimazole ± perchlorate
Type 2 (hyperthyroidism)Destructive thyroiditis↓Glucocorticoids
HypothyroidismWolff-Chaikoff effect—Levothyroxine
Type 1 vs Type 2 often hard to distinguish — may need to treat both simultaneously. Color-flow Doppler: Type 1 = increased vascularity; Type 2 = decreased.

13. Thyroid Nodules & Cancer Boards Favorite

Evaluation

  • TSH first:
    • Low TSH → Thyroid scan to identify "hot" (hyperfunctioning) nodules — low malignancy risk, no FNA needed
    • Normal/high TSH → Proceed to ultrasound
  • Ultrasound features: Risk stratify nodules

ATA Ultrasound Risk Stratification

PatternFeaturesCancer RiskFNA Threshold
High suspicionSolid hypoechoic + microcalcifications, irregular margins, taller than wide, ETE>70-90%≥1 cm
IntermediateSolid hypoechoic without high-risk features10-20%≥1 cm
Low suspicionIsoechoic/hyperechoic, partially cystic5-10%≥1.5 cm
Very lowSpongiform, purely cystic<3%≥2 cm or observe
BenignPurely cystic<1%No FNA

Bethesda System (FNA Cytology)

CategoryMalignancy RiskManagement
I. Non-diagnostic5-10%Repeat FNA
II. Benign<3%Follow clinically
III. AUS/FLUS10-30%Repeat FNA or molecular testing
IV. Follicular neoplasm25-40%Lobectomy or molecular testing
V. Suspicious for malignancy50-75%Lobectomy or total thyroidectomy
VI. Malignant97-99%Total thyroidectomy

Thyroid Cancer Types

Type% of CasesFeaturesPrognosis
Papillary80-85%Psammoma bodies, Orphan Annie nuclei, lymphatic spreadExcellent (>95% 10-year survival)
Follicular10-15%Hematogenous spread (lung, bone), capsular/vascular invasion on histologyGood
Medullary3-5%Parafollicular C-cells, calcitonin, MEN2 association (25%)Moderate
Anaplastic1-2%Elderly, rapidly growing, locally invasiveVery poor (median survival 6 months)
Medullary thyroid cancer: Always check RET proto-oncogene. If positive, screen for MEN2 (pheochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism) before surgery.

14. Cushing's Syndrome Boards Favorite

Etiology

TypeACTHCause% of Endogenous
ACTH-dependent↑Pituitary adenoma (Cushing's disease) — 70%
Ectopic ACTH (small cell lung, carcinoid) — 15%		85%
ACTH-independent↓Adrenal adenoma, carcinoma, bilateral hyperplasia15%
Iatrogenic↓Exogenous glucocorticoids (most common overall)—

Clinical Features

  • Central obesity, supraclavicular fat pads, buffalo hump
  • Moon facies, facial plethora
  • Skin: Thin, easy bruising, purple striae (>1 cm wide), poor wound healing
  • Proximal muscle weakness
  • Metabolic: Hyperglycemia, hypertension, hypokalemia
  • Osteoporosis, vertebral fractures
  • Psychiatric: Depression, psychosis
  • Hirsutism, acne (androgen excess in adrenal tumors)
Cushing's features: "CUSHING" — Central obesity, Unusual infections, Striae (purple), Hyperglycemia/HTN, Immune suppression, Neuro-psych changes, Growth retardation (kids)

Diagnostic Workup

Step 1: Confirm Hypercortisolism (any 2 of 3)

  • 24-hour urine free cortisol (>3× ULN highly specific)
  • Late-night salivary cortisol (×2 elevated)
  • 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol >1.8 μg/dL = abnormal)

Step 2: ACTH Level

  • ACTH suppressed (<5 pg/mL): Adrenal source → CT adrenals
  • ACTH elevated or normal (>20): ACTH-dependent → Next step

Step 3: Localize ACTH Source

  • High-dose dexamethasone suppression: Pituitary usually suppresses; ectopic does not
  • Pituitary MRI: May show adenoma (but 10% of normals have incidentalomas)
  • Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS): Gold standard for distinguishing pituitary vs ectopic
IPSS: Central-to-peripheral ACTH ratio >2 at baseline OR >3 after CRH = pituitary source. Essential when MRI is negative or equivocal.

Treatment

  • Cushing's disease (pituitary): Transsphenoidal surgery
  • Adrenal adenoma: Adrenalectomy
  • Ectopic ACTH: Resect tumor if possible; medical therapy if not
  • Medical therapy: Ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat (steroidogenesis inhibitors); mifepristone (glucocorticoid receptor antagonist); pasireotide (pituitary-directed)

15. Adrenal Insufficiency Emergency

Classification

TypeCauseACTHAldosterone
Primary (Addison's)Autoimmune (most common in developed), TB, adrenal hemorrhage, metastases↑↓
SecondaryPituitary disease, chronic steroid use (most common overall)↓Normal
TertiaryHypothalamic disease, chronic steroid withdrawal↓Normal

Clinical Features

FeaturePrimarySecondary/Tertiary
HyperpigmentationYes (high ACTH)No
HyperkalemiaYes (low aldosterone)No
HyponatremiaYesYes (ADH effect)
HypotensionYesLess severe
  • Common symptoms: Fatigue, weakness, anorexia, weight loss, nausea, abdominal pain
  • Salt craving (primary only — aldosterone deficiency)
  • Hyperpigmentation: Skin creases, buccal mucosa, scars (ACTH cross-reacts with melanocortin receptors)
Hyperpigmentation in adrenal insufficiency = PRIMARY. High ACTH stimulates melanocytes. Absent in secondary/tertiary.

Diagnosis

  • Morning cortisol: <3 μg/dL = adrenal insufficiency; >15-18 = normal (rules out)
  • ACTH stimulation test (Cosyntropin test):
    • Give 250 mcg IV cosyntropin
    • Check cortisol at 30 and 60 minutes
    • Normal: Cortisol ≥18-20 μg/dL
  • ACTH level: Distinguishes primary (high) from secondary (low)

Treatment

  • Glucocorticoid replacement:
    • Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day divided (⅔ AM, ⅓ PM) OR
    • Prednisone 3-5 mg/day
  • Mineralocorticoid (primary only): Fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day
  • Stress dosing: Double or triple dose for illness; IV hydrocortisone 50-100mg Q8h for major stress/surgery

Adrenal Crisis

  • Triggers: Infection, surgery, trauma, stopping steroids
  • Presentation: Hypotension/shock, fever, altered mental status, abdominal pain
  • Treatment:
    • Hydrocortisone 100mg IV bolus, then 50-100mg Q6-8h
    • Aggressive IV fluids (NS)
    • Treat precipitant
In suspected adrenal crisis, give hydrocortisone immediately — do not wait for lab confirmation. Can draw cortisol/ACTH first but don't delay treatment.

16. Pheochromocytoma & Paraganglioma High-Yield

Overview

  • Pheochromocytoma: Catecholamine-secreting tumor from adrenal medulla
  • Paraganglioma: Extra-adrenal location (sympathetic chain)
  • ~40% are hereditary: MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDH mutations

Clinical Features

5 P's: Pressure (HTN), Pain (headache), Perspiration, Palpitations, Pallor
  • Hypertension: Paroxysmal (50%) or sustained (50%)
  • Classic triad: Headache, diaphoresis, palpitations
  • Episodic symptoms triggered by exercise, anesthesia, certain drugs
  • Hyperglycemia (catecholamines oppose insulin)

When to Suspect

  • Resistant hypertension
  • Adrenal incidentaloma
  • Hypertensive crisis with anesthesia/surgery
  • Syndromic features (MEN2, VHL, NF1)
  • Family history of pheochromocytoma

Diagnosis

  • First-line: Plasma free metanephrines OR 24-hour urine metanephrines and catecholamines
  • Plasma metanephrines: Sensitivity >95% — best for screening high-risk patients
  • If positive: CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis
  • If imaging negative: MIBG scan or 68Ga-DOTATATE PET (for metastatic/paraganglioma)
Plasma metanephrines have high sensitivity but lower specificity. False positives with TCAs, decongestants, acute illness. If moderately elevated (2-4× ULN), may need to repeat off interfering meds.

Treatment

  • Alpha-blockade first: Phenoxybenzamine (irreversible) or doxazosin (10-14 days pre-op)
    • Goal: Seated BP <130/80, standing SBP >90
  • Then beta-blockade: Add after adequate alpha-blockade (prevents unopposed alpha stimulation and hypertensive crisis)
  • Volume expansion: High-salt diet, IV fluids (catecholamines cause volume contraction)
  • Surgery: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy (definitive)
Never give beta-blockers before alpha-blockade! Unopposed alpha stimulation → hypertensive crisis.

Genetic Testing

  • Recommend for all patients with pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma
  • Key syndromes:
    • MEN2A/2B: RET mutation (medullary thyroid cancer, hyperparathyroidism)
    • VHL: VHL mutation (renal cell carcinoma, hemangioblastomas)
    • NF1: Café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas
    • SDH mutations: High paraganglioma and malignancy risk

17. Primary Aldosteronism Common

Overview

  • Most common cause of secondary hypertension (5-10% of HTN)
  • Causes:
    • Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (60-70%)
    • Aldosterone-producing adenoma (30-40%)
    • Rare: Familial, carcinoma

When to Screen

  • Resistant hypertension (≥3 drugs including diuretic)
  • Hypertension with hypokalemia (spontaneous or diuretic-induced)
  • Hypertension with adrenal incidentaloma
  • Hypertension with sleep apnea
  • Family history of early-onset HTN or stroke

Diagnosis

Step 1: Screening — Aldosterone/Renin Ratio (ARR)

  • Positive: Aldosterone >10-15 ng/dL AND ARR >30
  • Conditions: Morning, seated, correct hypokalemia first
  • Hold interfering meds if possible: MRAs (6 weeks), diuretics (2-4 weeks), ACEi/ARBs (2 weeks)

Step 2: Confirmatory Testing

  • Oral sodium loading test: High-salt diet × 3 days, then 24h urine aldosterone (>12 μg/day confirms)
  • Saline infusion test: 2L NS over 4h; aldosterone >10 ng/dL confirms

Step 3: Subtype Differentiation

  • CT adrenals: May show adenoma (but 30% are <1 cm and may miss)
  • Adrenal vein sampling (AVS): Gold standard for lateralization if surgical candidate
CT alone is inadequate for subtype differentiation. Even if adenoma visible, AVS confirms lateralization and rules out bilateral disease.

Treatment

SubtypeTreatment
Unilateral adenomaLaparoscopic adrenalectomy (cure rate 30-60%)
Bilateral hyperplasiaMineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone)

18. Adrenal Incidentaloma High-Yield

Definition

Adrenal mass ≥1 cm discovered on imaging performed for unrelated indication. Found in 4-5% of CT scans.

Key Questions

  1. Is it malignant? (Size, imaging characteristics)
  2. Is it functional? (Hormone excess)

Malignancy Risk Assessment

FeatureBenign (Adenoma)Malignant
Size<4 cm>4 cm (especially >6 cm)
HU on unenhanced CT≤10 HU (lipid-rich)>10 HU
Washout>50% absolute washout<50% washout
MarginsSmooth, homogeneousIrregular, heterogeneous
GrowthStableRapid growth
≤10 HU on unenhanced CT = adenoma (highly specific). If indeterminate, can do washout study or MRI with chemical shift.

Functional Workup (All Incidentalomas)

ConditionTest
PheochromocytomaPlasma free metanephrines (all patients)
Cortisol excess1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Primary aldosteronismAldosterone/renin ratio (if hypertension or hypokalemia)
Androgen excessDHEA-S (if virilization or large mass)

Management

  • Surgery if:
    • Size >4 cm (or growing)
    • Imaging features suggestive of malignancy
    • Functional (especially pheochromocytoma)
  • Follow-up if benign-appearing and non-functional:
    • Repeat imaging at 6-12 months, then annually × 1-2 years if stable
    • Repeat hormonal workup annually × 5 years

Autonomous Cortisol Secretion ("Mild" Cushing's)

  • Common finding (5-30% of incidentalomas)
  • Cortisol doesn't suppress on DST but no overt Cushing's features
  • May cause HTN, diabetes, osteoporosis
  • Consider surgery if associated comorbidities

19. Pituitary Adenomas — Overview Common

Classification

TypeSizeHormone Secretion
Microadenoma<10 mmFunctioning or non-functioning
Macroadenoma≥10 mmFunctioning or non-functioning

Functioning Adenomas (by frequency)

  1. Prolactinoma (40-50%)
  2. Non-functioning (30%)
  3. GH-secreting (acromegaly) (15%)
  4. ACTH-secreting (Cushing's disease) (10%)
  5. TSH-secreting (<1%)
  6. Gonadotroph (rare, usually non-functioning)

Mass Effect Symptoms

  • Visual field defects: Bitemporal hemianopia (optic chiasm compression)
  • Headache
  • Hypopituitarism: From compression of normal pituitary
  • Cranial nerve palsies: CN III, IV, VI (cavernous sinus invasion)

Evaluation of Pituitary Mass

  • MRI with gadolinium: Best imaging modality
  • Visual field testing: If mass near/compressing chiasm
  • Hormonal evaluation:
    • Prolactin (always check)
    • IGF-1 (screen for acromegaly)
    • 24h urine cortisol or overnight DST
    • TSH, free T4
    • LH, FSH, testosterone/estradiol
    • 8 AM cortisol (assess for deficiency)
Always check prolactin before surgery! Prolactinomas are treated medically (dopamine agonists), not surgically. Prolactin >200 ng/mL with macroadenoma = prolactinoma until proven otherwise.

"Stalk Effect"

  • Any mass compressing pituitary stalk can cause hyperprolactinemia (usually mild, <100-150 ng/mL)
  • Due to interruption of dopamine inhibition from hypothalamus
  • Distinguish from prolactinoma by degree of PRL elevation and size correlation

20. Prolactinoma High-Yield

Clinical Features

WomenMen
Oligomenorrhea/amenorrheaDecreased libido, erectile dysfunction
GalactorrheaGalactorrhea (less common)
InfertilityInfertility
Usually microadenomas (early detection)Usually macroadenomas (delayed presentation)

Diagnosis

  • Prolactin level correlates with tumor size:
    • Microprolactinoma: PRL 50-200 ng/mL
    • Macroprolactinoma: PRL >200 ng/mL (often >1000)
  • "Hook effect": Very large tumors may have falsely low prolactin due to assay saturation — request serial dilutions if suspected
If large macroadenoma with only mildly elevated prolactin (<100), suspect stalk effect from non-functioning adenoma, NOT prolactinoma.

Causes of Hyperprolactinemia (DDx)

  • Physiologic: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, stress, nipple stimulation
  • Medications: Antipsychotics, metoclopramide, SSRIs, verapamil
  • Hypothyroidism (TRH stimulates prolactin)
  • Renal failure, cirrhosis
  • Stalk effect (any sellar/suprasellar mass)

Treatment

First-Line: Dopamine Agonists

DrugDosingNotes
Cabergoline0.25-1 mg twice weeklyPreferred: more effective, better tolerated
Bromocriptine1.25-2.5 mg daily, titrate to 5-7.5 mgMore nausea; preferred in pregnancy (more safety data)
  • Goals: Normalize prolactin, restore gonadal function, shrink tumor
  • Monitoring: PRL levels q1-2 months initially, MRI at 3-6 months
  • Duration: May attempt withdrawal after 2+ years if normalized PRL and tumor shrinkage

Surgery

  • Transsphenoidal surgery if:
    • Intolerant/resistant to dopamine agonists
    • Cystic tumor (less responsive)
    • Patient preference
    • Apoplexy with visual compromise
Prolactinoma is the ONE pituitary tumor treated medically, not surgically. Even giant prolactinomas respond to dopamine agonists.

21. Acromegaly Boards Favorite

Clinical Features

  • Soft tissue growth: Enlarged hands/feet (ring size increase), coarse facial features, frontal bossing, prognathism
  • Skin: Oily, skin tags (associated with colon polyps)
  • Cardiovascular: Hypertension, cardiomyopathy (concentric LVH), arrhythmias
  • Metabolic: Diabetes/IGT (GH is counter-regulatory)
  • Sleep apnea (very common)
  • Carpal tunnel syndrome
  • Arthropathy
  • Headache, visual field defects (tumor mass effect)
Acromegaly features: "ABCDEF" — Arthralgia, Blood pressure↑, Carpal tunnel, Diabetes, Enlarged organs, Field defects (visual)

Diagnosis

  1. Screen: IGF-1 level (elevated, age/sex adjusted)
  2. Confirm: Oral glucose tolerance test — GH should suppress to <1 ng/mL (or <0.4 with sensitive assay) after 75g glucose. Failure to suppress = acromegaly.
  3. Localize: Pituitary MRI
IGF-1 has a long half-life and reflects integrated GH secretion. Random GH is pulsatile and unreliable for screening.

Treatment

First-Line: Surgery

  • Transsphenoidal adenomectomy
  • Cure rate 80-90% for microadenomas, 40-50% for macroadenomas

Medical Therapy (Adjuvant or If Surgery Fails)

ClassDrugMechanismNotes
Somatostatin analogsOctreotide LAR, lanreotideInhibit GH secretionFirst-line medical; normalize IGF-1 in 50-70%
GH receptor antagonistPegvisomantBlocks GH actionMost effective for IGF-1 normalization; doesn't shrink tumor
Dopamine agonistCabergolineInhibits GH secretionMay work if mild elevation or co-secreting PRL

Radiation

  • Reserved for refractory cases
  • Slow onset of effect (years)
  • Risk of hypopituitarism

Surveillance

  • Colonoscopy: Increased colon cancer risk; screen at diagnosis
  • Echocardiogram: Evaluate for cardiomyopathy
  • Sleep study: High prevalence of sleep apnea

22. Hypopituitarism Common

Causes

CategoryExamples
TumorsPituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma, metastases
InfiltrativeSarcoidosis, hemochromatosis, histiocytosis
InflammatoryLymphocytic hypophysitis, IgG4 disease
VascularSheehan's syndrome (postpartum), pituitary apoplexy
IatrogenicSurgery, radiation
TraumaTBI (often underrecognized)
CongenitalGenetic mutations, empty sella

Order of Hormone Loss

"Go Look For The Adenoma" — GH, LH/FSH, TSH, ACTH (order of sensitivity to compression/damage)

Clinical Features by Deficiency

HormoneDeficiency Symptoms
GH↓ Muscle mass, ↑ fat, fatigue, ↓ QoL
LH/FSHAmenorrhea, ↓ libido, infertility, osteoporosis
TSHFatigue, cold intolerance, constipation
ACTHFatigue, hypotension, hypoglycemia (no hyperpigmentation)
ADHDiabetes insipidus (polyuria, polydipsia)
ProlactinUnable to lactate (rarely clinically significant)

Sheehan's Syndrome

  • Postpartum pituitary necrosis from hemorrhage/hypotension
  • Presentation: Failure to lactate, amenorrhea post-delivery, fatigue
  • May present years later with chronic hypopituitarism

Pituitary Apoplexy

  • Hemorrhage or infarction of pituitary adenoma
  • Presentation: Sudden severe headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, altered consciousness, hypotension
  • Treatment: IV corticosteroids immediately; surgery if visual compromise
Pituitary apoplexy is an emergency. Give hydrocortisone 100mg IV immediately, then surgical evaluation.

Hormone Replacement

  • Cortisol first (before thyroid to avoid adrenal crisis)
  • Then levothyroxine (follow free T4, not TSH)
  • Sex steroids for quality of life, bone health
  • GH if symptomatic and confirmed deficiency
  • Desmopressin for diabetes insipidus

23. Diabetes Insipidus High-Yield

Types

TypeMechanismADH LevelResponse to Desmopressin
Central DI↓ ADH production (pituitary/hypothalamus)LowUrine concentrates
Nephrogenic DIKidney resistance to ADHHighNo response
Primary polydipsiaExcessive water intakeAppropriately lowPartial response
Gestational DIPlacental vasopressinaseLow (degraded)Responds to desmopressin

Causes

Central DI

  • Pituitary surgery (most common)
  • Trauma, tumors, infiltrative disease
  • Idiopathic (30%)

Nephrogenic DI

  • Drugs: Lithium (most common), amphotericin, foscarnet
  • Hypercalcemia, hypokalemia
  • CKD, sickle cell disease
  • Genetic (rare)

Diagnosis

  • Clinical: Polyuria (>3 L/day), polydipsia, nocturia
  • Labs:
    • Serum Na: Normal or elevated
    • Serum osmolality: >295 mOsm/kg
    • Urine osmolality: <300 mOsm/kg (dilute urine despite high serum osm)
  • Water deprivation test: If diagnosis uncertain
    • Withhold fluids until serum osm >295 or 3% weight loss
    • Give desmopressin and remeasure urine osm

Interpretation of Water Deprivation Test

ConditionAfter DehydrationAfter Desmopressin
Central DIUrine stays dilute (<300)Urine concentrates (>600)
Nephrogenic DIUrine stays diluteUrine stays dilute
Primary polydipsiaUrine concentrates (>500)—

Treatment

  • Central DI: Desmopressin (DDAVP) — intranasal, oral, or SQ
  • Nephrogenic DI:
    • Treat underlying cause (stop lithium if possible)
    • Thiazide diuretics (paradoxically reduce urine output)
    • Low-sodium, low-protein diet
    • Amiloride (for lithium-induced)
Monitor sodium closely when treating DI. Overcorrection with desmopressin can cause hyponatremia.

24. SIADH Very Common

Diagnostic Criteria

  • Hyponatremia (serum Na <135 mEq/L)
  • Decreased serum osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
  • Urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated)
  • Urine Na >40 mEq/L (renal salt wasting)
  • Euvolemic
  • Normal thyroid, adrenal, renal function

Causes

"SIADH Causes" — CNS disorders (stroke, trauma, infection), Lung disease (pneumonia, SCLC), Drugs, pain, nausea, surgery
CategoryExamples
CNSStroke, hemorrhage, infection, trauma
PulmonaryPneumonia, TB, SCLC (ectopic ADH)
MalignancySmall cell lung cancer (most common), head/neck
DrugsSSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, MDMA
OtherPain, nausea, post-operative

Treatment

SeverityManagement
Mild/chronic (Na 125-134)Fluid restriction 1-1.5 L/day
ModerateFluid restriction + salt tablets ± loop diuretic
RefractoryUrea (oral osmotic), tolvaptan (vasopressin antagonist)
Severe/symptomatic3% hypertonic saline (see correction rates below)

Hypertonic Saline for Severe Symptomatic Hyponatremia

  • Indications: Seizures, altered mental status, acute severe hyponatremia
  • Goal: Raise Na by 4-6 mEq/L in first 24 hours (1-2 mEq/L/hr for first 2-3 hours if symptomatic)
  • Maximum correction: ≤8-10 mEq/L in first 24 hours, ≤18 mEq/L in 48 hours
Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS): From overly rapid correction. Risk factors: chronic hyponatremia, hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition. Presents days later with dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis.

25. Hypercalcemia High-Yield

Causes

PTH-DependentPTH-Independent
Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common outpatient)Malignancy (most common inpatient): PTHrP, bone mets, myeloma
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH)Granulomatous disease (sarcoid, TB) — 1,25-vit D
LithiumVitamin D intoxication
Tertiary hyperparathyroidismMilk-alkali syndrome
Immobilization, thyrotoxicosis, adrenal insufficiency
First step in hypercalcemia workup: Check PTH.
• PTH elevated/inappropriately normal → Primary HPT (or FHH)
• PTH suppressed → Non-PTH mediated (malignancy, vitamin D, etc.)

Clinical Features

"Stones, Bones, Groans, Moans, and Psychiatric Overtones"
Kidney stones, bone pain/osteoporosis, abdominal pain/constipation, fatigue/weakness, depression/confusion

Treatment of Acute Hypercalcemia

InterventionMechanismNotes
IV NS (first-line)Volume expansion, ↑ Ca excretion2-4 L/day; most patients are volume depleted
Furosemide↑ CalciuresisOnly after volume repletion
BisphosphonatesInhibit osteoclastsZoledronic acid 4mg IV; takes 2-4 days to work
Calcitonin↓ Bone resorption, ↑ renal excretionRapid onset but tachyphylaxis in 48h
DenosumabRANKL inhibitorFor bisphosphonate-refractory
DialysisDirect Ca removalSevere, refractory, or renal failure
Glucocorticoids↓ 1,25-vit DFor granulomatous disease, lymphoma, vit D toxicity

26. Primary Hyperparathyroidism Common

Etiology

  • Single adenoma: 85%
  • Hyperplasia (4-gland): 10-15%
  • Parathyroid carcinoma: <1%

Laboratory Findings

LabFinding
CalciumElevated
PTHElevated or inappropriately normal
PhosphorusLow-normal (PTH causes phosphaturia)
Urine calciumNormal-high
1,25-vitamin DOften elevated

Distinguish from FHH

  • Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH): Benign, no treatment needed
  • Calcium/creatinine clearance ratio:
    • <0.01 = FHH
    • >0.02 = Primary HPT
    • 0.01-0.02 = Indeterminate (consider genetic testing)

Indications for Surgery (Asymptomatic Primary HPT)

2022 International Guidelines — Surgery if ANY:
  • Serum calcium >1 mg/dL above normal
  • Creatinine clearance <60 mL/min
  • T-score ≤-2.5 at any site OR vertebral fracture
  • Age <50
  • Kidney stones or nephrocalcinosis

Preoperative Localization

  • Sestamibi scan: Parathyroid adenoma shows persistent uptake
  • Ultrasound: Operator-dependent but useful
  • 4D CT: High sensitivity for localization
  • Note: Localization studies are for surgical planning, NOT diagnosis

Medical Management (If Surgery Not Pursued)

  • Adequate hydration
  • Avoid thiazides (increase calcium)
  • Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis
  • Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) — lowers calcium but doesn't address bone disease

27. Hypocalcemia Common

Causes

MechanismCauses
↓ PTHPost-surgical (most common), autoimmune, infiltrative, radiation
PTH resistancePseudohypoparathyroidism (Albright hereditary osteodystrophy)
Vitamin D deficiencyNutritional, malabsorption, CKD (↓ 1,25-vit D)
OtherAcute pancreatitis, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, citrated blood transfusion, severe hypomagnesemia
Severe hypomagnesemia causes hypocalcemia by impairing PTH secretion AND causing PTH resistance. Correct magnesium first!

Clinical Features

  • Neuromuscular: Paresthesias (perioral, fingers), muscle cramps, tetany, seizures
  • Chvostek's sign: Facial twitch with tapping facial nerve
  • Trousseau's sign: Carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation (more specific)
  • Cardiac: Prolonged QT, arrhythmias

Treatment

Acute Symptomatic Hypocalcemia

  • IV calcium gluconate: 1-2 g (10-20 mL of 10% solution) over 10-20 min
  • Then continuous infusion: 0.5-1.5 mg/kg/hr elemental calcium
  • Monitor: Calcium Q4-6h, telemetry
  • Correct hypomagnesemia

Chronic Hypocalcemia

  • Calcium supplementation: 1-3 g elemental calcium daily (divided doses)
  • Calcitriol: 0.25-1 mcg daily (especially if hypoparathyroidism)
  • Goal: Low-normal calcium (avoid hypercalciuria → nephrolithiasis)

28. Osteoporosis Very Common

Definitions (WHO)

DiagnosisT-score
Normal≥-1.0
Osteopenia (low bone mass)-1.0 to -2.5
Osteoporosis≤-2.5
Severe osteoporosis≤-2.5 + fragility fracture

Who to Screen

  • All women ≥65 years
  • All men ≥70 years
  • Postmenopausal women <65 and men 50-69 with risk factors
  • Adults with fragility fracture
  • Conditions/medications associated with bone loss

Secondary Causes to Evaluate

  • Vitamin D deficiency (25-OH vitamin D)
  • Calcium intake (dietary assessment)
  • Hyperparathyroidism (calcium, PTH)
  • Hyperthyroidism (TSH)
  • Hypogonadism
  • Multiple myeloma (SPEP, CBC)
  • Celiac disease (if GI symptoms)
  • Cushing's syndrome (if suspected)

FRAX Tool

  • Estimates 10-year probability of hip fracture and major osteoporotic fracture
  • Uses clinical risk factors ± femoral neck BMD
  • Treat if: Hip fracture risk ≥3% OR major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%

Treatment

ClassAgentsNotes
Bisphosphonates (first-line)Alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid, ibandronateAntiresorptive; drug holiday after 5y oral or 3y IV
DenosumabProlia (60mg SQ Q6 months)RANKL inhibitor; rebound bone loss if stopped
Anabolic agentsTeriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumabFor severe osteoporosis; follow with antiresorptive
SERMRaloxifeneReduces vertebral (not hip) fractures; VTE risk
HRTEstrogenConsidered in early menopause; not first-line for osteoporosis
Bisphosphonate adverse effects: Esophagitis (take upright with water), ONJ (dental exam first), atypical femoral fractures (prodromal thigh pain).

Vitamin D and Calcium

  • Vitamin D: Goal 25-OH vitamin D >30 ng/mL; supplement 800-2000 IU daily
  • Calcium: 1000-1200 mg daily (diet + supplements)

29. Lipid Disorders Very Common

ATP Guidelines/ACC-AHA Statin Benefit Groups

  1. Clinical ASCVD (secondary prevention)
  2. LDL ≥190 mg/dL (severe hypercholesterolemia)
  3. Diabetes, age 40-75
  4. Primary prevention with 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%

Statin Intensity

IntensityLDL ReductionExamples
High≥50%Atorvastatin 40-80mg, Rosuvastatin 20-40mg
Moderate30-49%Atorvastatin 10-20mg, Rosuvastatin 5-10mg, Simvastatin 20-40mg
Low<30%Simvastatin 10mg, Pravastatin 10-20mg

LDL Goals

PopulationLDL Goal
Very high-risk ASCVD<70 mg/dL (or 50% reduction)
High-risk ASCVD<70 mg/dL
Diabetes + ASCVD<55 mg/dL (ESC) or <70 (ADA)

Non-Statin Therapies

DrugMechanismLDL ReductionIndication
Ezetimibe↓ Intestinal absorption~18%Add if statin insufficient
PCSK9 inhibitors↓ LDL receptor degradation50-60%ASCVD not at goal, familial hypercholesterolemia
Bempedoic acid↓ Cholesterol synthesis (upstream of statins)~18%Statin intolerant
InclisiransiRNA targeting PCSK9~50%ASCVD, FH (twice yearly injection)

Hypertriglyceridemia

  • Mild-moderate (150-499): Lifestyle, optimize glucose, consider statin
  • Severe (≥500): Risk of pancreatitis — fibrates, omega-3, very low fat diet
  • Very severe (≥1000): Hospitalization if acute pancreatitis risk
Icosapent ethyl (Vascepa): REDUCE-IT trial showed CV benefit in patients with TG 135-499 on statin. Pure EPA, not fish oil.

Familial Hypercholesterolemia

  • Heterozygous FH: LDL 190-400 mg/dL, early ASCVD
  • Homozygous FH: LDL >500 mg/dL, ASCVD in childhood
  • Treatment: High-intensity statin + ezetimibe + PCSK9 inhibitor; may need LDL apheresis

30. Obesity Very Common

Classification

BMI (kg/m²)Classification
18.5-24.9Normal
25-29.9Overweight
30-34.9Class I Obesity
35-39.9Class II Obesity
≥40Class III (Severe) Obesity

Pharmacotherapy Options

DrugMechanismWeight LossKey Points
Semaglutide (Wegovy)GLP-1 RA~15-17%Weekly injection; GI side effects; CI in MTC/MEN2
Tirzepatide (Zepbound)GLP-1/GIP dual agonist~20-22%Weekly injection; most effective to date
Phentermine-topiramateSympathomimetic + anticonvulsant~10%CI in pregnancy, glaucoma, hyperthyroidism, CVD
Naltrexone-bupropionOpioid antagonist + antidepressant~5-6%CI in seizures, opioid use, uncontrolled HTN
OrlistatLipase inhibitor~3%GI side effects; fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption

Bariatric Surgery Indications

Consider bariatric surgery if:
  • BMI ≥40
  • BMI ≥35 with obesity-related comorbidity (T2D, OSA, HTN, etc.)
  • BMI ≥30 with T2D not achieving glycemic goals

Bariatric Surgery Types

ProcedureMechanismWeight Loss
Roux-en-Y gastric bypassRestrictive + malabsorptive~30% total body weight
Sleeve gastrectomyRestrictive (removes 80% stomach)~25-30%
Adjustable gastric bandRestrictive only~15-20% (less common now)

Post-Bariatric Complications

  • Dumping syndrome: Rapid gastric emptying → vasomotor symptoms, diarrhea
  • Nutritional deficiencies: B12, iron, calcium, vitamin D, thiamine
  • Hypoglycemia: Late postprandial (nesidioblastosis)
  • Gallstones: Rapid weight loss
  • Marginal ulcer, stricture, internal hernia

31. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) Common

Rotterdam Criteria (2 of 3)

  1. Oligo/anovulation (irregular cycles)
  2. Clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism
  3. Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (≥12 follicles per ovary or volume >10 mL)

*Must exclude other causes: thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, CAH, Cushing's

Clinical Features

  • Menstrual: Oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea, anovulatory infertility
  • Hyperandrogenism: Hirsutism, acne, androgenic alopecia
  • Metabolic: Insulin resistance, obesity (50-70%), T2D, dyslipidemia
  • Associated: OSA, NAFLD, depression, endometrial hyperplasia/cancer

Laboratory Evaluation

  • Total/free testosterone (mildly elevated)
  • LH:FSH ratio (often >2:1, not diagnostic)
  • Rule out: TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP (if CAH suspected)
  • Metabolic: Fasting glucose, OGTT, lipid panel
PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory infertility. Screen all PCOS patients for glucose intolerance with OGTT.

Treatment

GoalTreatment
Menstrual regulationCombined OCP, cyclic progestin
HirsutismOCP, spironolactone, electrolysis/laser
Ovulation inductionLetrozole (first-line), clomiphene, gonadotropins
MetabolicWeight loss, metformin (especially if IGT)
Endometrial protectionProgestin withdrawal Q1-3 months if not on OCP
Letrozole is now first-line for ovulation induction (better live birth rates than clomiphene per NEJM trial).

32. Menopause & Hormone Therapy

Definitions

  • Menopause: 12 consecutive months without menses (average age 51)
  • Perimenopause: Transition period with irregular cycles
  • Premature ovarian insufficiency: Menopause before age 40

Symptoms

  • Vasomotor: Hot flashes, night sweats
  • Genitourinary: Vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urgency
  • Other: Sleep disturbance, mood changes

Hormone Therapy Indications

  • Bothersome vasomotor symptoms
  • Prevention of bone loss (when other options unsuitable)
  • Premature menopause (until average age of menopause)

Contraindications to Systemic HRT

  • Breast cancer (current or history)
  • Endometrial cancer
  • VTE or thrombophilia
  • Active liver disease
  • CAD, stroke
  • Unexplained vaginal bleeding
Key Points:
• Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration
• Initiate within 10 years of menopause or age <60 (lower CVD risk)
• With intact uterus: estrogen + progestogen (prevents endometrial hyperplasia)
• After hysterectomy: estrogen alone

Non-Hormonal Options for Vasomotor Symptoms

  • Paroxetine (Brisdelle) — FDA approved for hot flashes
  • Venlafaxine, SSRIs
  • Gabapentin
  • Fezolinetant (NK3 receptor antagonist — 2023 FDA approved)

33. Male Hypogonadism High-Yield

Classification

TypeLH/FSHTestosteroneCauses
Primary (testicular)↑↓Klinefelter (47,XXY), trauma, infection, radiation, chemotherapy
Secondary (central)↓ or Normal↓Pituitary adenoma, hyperprolactinemia, Kallmann syndrome, obesity, opioids

Clinical Features

  • Sexual: Decreased libido, erectile dysfunction
  • Physical: Decreased muscle mass, increased body fat, gynecomastia
  • Other: Fatigue, depression, decreased bone density, anemia

Diagnosis

  • Morning total testosterone (8-10 AM) on 2 separate occasions
  • Low testosterone: <300 ng/dL (confirm with repeat + free testosterone)
  • Then check: LH, FSH (distinguish primary vs secondary)
  • If secondary: Prolactin, MRI pituitary
Many "low T" symptoms are nonspecific. Confirm with AM testosterone on 2 occasions before initiating therapy.

Treatment

  • Testosterone replacement: Gels, injections, patches, pellets
  • Monitoring: Testosterone levels, hematocrit (polycythemia risk), PSA, lipids
  • Contraindications: Prostate or breast cancer, severe sleep apnea, hematocrit >50%, desire for fertility
Testosterone suppresses spermatogenesis. Do NOT give to men desiring fertility. Use clomiphene or gonadotropins instead.

Klinefelter Syndrome (47,XXY)

  • Most common sex chromosome aneuploidy in males (1:500-1000)
  • Features: Tall stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, infertility, learning disabilities
  • Labs: ↓ Testosterone, ↑ LH/FSH, ↑ estradiol
  • Treatment: Testosterone replacement starting at puberty

34. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 Boards Favorite

MEN1 — "3 P's"

Parathyroid (95%) + Pituitary (40%) + Pancreatic islet tumors (40-70%)
TumorFrequencyFeatures
Primary hyperparathyroidism95%Usually multigland hyperplasia; first manifestation
Pituitary adenoma40%Prolactinoma most common, also GH, ACTH
Enteropancreatic NETs40-70%Gastrinoma (ZES), insulinoma, non-functioning
Adrenal tumors20-40%Usually non-functioning adenomas

Genetics

  • MEN1 gene (menin) — tumor suppressor, chromosome 11q13
  • Autosomal dominant
  • Screen first-degree relatives with genetic testing

Screening Protocol for MEN1 Carriers

  • Annual: Calcium, PTH, prolactin, IGF-1, fasting glucose, chromogranin A
  • Periodic imaging: MRI pituitary (Q3-5 years), CT/MRI pancreas (Q1-3 years)

Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome

  • Gastrinoma: Most common functioning NET in MEN1
  • Features: Severe peptic ulcers, diarrhea, GERD
  • Diagnosis: Fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL, gastric pH <2, secretin stimulation test
  • Treatment: High-dose PPI; surgery if localized and non-MEN1

35. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 2 High-Yield

MEN2 Subtypes

FeatureMEN2AMEN2B
Medullary thyroid cancer95%100%
Pheochromocytoma50%50%
Primary hyperparathyroidism20-30%Rare
Mucosal neuromasNoYes
Marfanoid habitusNoYes
PrognosisBetterMore aggressive MTC

Genetics

  • RET proto-oncogene — chromosome 10
  • Autosomal dominant, high penetrance
  • Specific mutations correlate with aggressiveness and timing of prophylactic thyroidectomy

Medullary Thyroid Cancer

  • Arises from parafollicular C-cells
  • Tumor marker: Calcitonin (also CEA)
  • Treatment: Total thyroidectomy with lymph node dissection
Before thyroidectomy in MEN2, always rule out pheochromocytoma — undiagnosed pheo can cause perioperative crisis.

Prophylactic Thyroidectomy Timing

  • Highest risk mutations (MEN2B): First year of life
  • High risk: Before age 5
  • Moderate risk: Based on calcitonin levels or by age 5-10

36. Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia High-Yield

21-Hydroxylase Deficiency (95% of CAH)

TypeFeaturesLabs
Classic salt-wastingNeonatal adrenal crisis, ambiguous genitalia (females)↓↓ Cortisol, ↓↓ aldosterone, ↑↑ 17-OHP
Classic simple virilizingVirilization, early puberty↓ Cortisol, normal aldosterone, ↑↑ 17-OHP
Non-classic (late-onset)Hirsutism, acne, oligomenorrhea (like PCOS)Normal cortisol, mild ↑ 17-OHP
Non-classic CAH mimics PCOS. Check morning 17-OHP — if >200 ng/dL, perform ACTH stimulation test to confirm.

Diagnosis

  • 17-hydroxyprogesterone: Elevated (morning sample, follicular phase in women)
  • ACTH stimulation test: 17-OHP >1000 ng/dL post-stimulation diagnostic
  • Newborn screening: 17-OHP on heel stick (may miss non-classic)

Treatment

  • Classic CAH: Glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement
  • Non-classic CAH: Glucocorticoid if symptomatic or fertility desired; OCPs for hirsutism
  • Monitoring: 17-OHP, androgens (goal: suppress but avoid over-treatment)

37. Carcinoid Tumors & Syndrome Boards Favorite

Overview

  • Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors
  • Common sites: GI tract (small bowel, appendix, rectum), lungs
  • Carcinoid syndrome: Occurs with hepatic metastases (or primary bronchial/ovarian)

Carcinoid Syndrome Features

"F-D-B-W": Flushing, Diarrhea, Bronchoconstriction, Wheezing (right-sided heart disease)
  • Flushing: Episodic, triggered by alcohol, stress, catecholamines
  • Diarrhea: Secretory (from serotonin)
  • Carcinoid heart disease: Right-sided valve fibrosis (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonic stenosis)
  • Bronchoconstriction

Diagnosis

  • 24-hour urine 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite) — elevated
  • Chromogranin A — elevated (nonspecific)
  • Imaging: CT/MRI, somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Octreoscan), 68Ga-DOTATATE PET

Treatment

  • Surgery: Curative if localized
  • Somatostatin analogs: Octreotide LAR, lanreotide — control symptoms, slow tumor growth
  • PRRT: Lutetium-177 DOTATATE for progressive disease
  • Carcinoid crisis prevention: Octreotide before surgery/procedures
Carcinoid crisis: Life-threatening hypotension, bronchospasm during surgery. Give octreotide 250-500 mcg IV before and during procedure.

38. Secondary Endocrine Hypertension

When to Suspect Secondary HTN

  • Onset before age 30 or after age 55
  • Resistant hypertension (≥3 drugs including diuretic)
  • Sudden worsening of previously controlled BP
  • Hypokalemia (unprovoked or diuretic-induced)
  • Paroxysmal symptoms (pheo)
  • Clinical features of Cushing's, acromegaly

Endocrine Causes of HTN

CauseCluesScreening Test
Primary aldosteronismHypokalemia, resistant HTNAldosterone/renin ratio
PheochromocytomaParoxysmal symptoms, headache, diaphoresisPlasma free metanephrines
Cushing's syndromeCentral obesity, striae, easy bruising1mg DST, 24h urine cortisol
HyperthyroidismTachycardia, weight loss, tremorTSH
HypothyroidismFatigue, weight gain, diastolic HTNTSH
HyperparathyroidismHypercalcemia, kidney stonesCalcium, PTH
AcromegalyCoarse features, enlarged handsIGF-1

39. Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy High-Yield

Normal Physiologic Changes

  • ↑ TBG (estrogen effect) → ↑ Total T4/T3
  • hCG has TSH-like activity → mild ↓ TSH in 1st trimester
  • Use trimester-specific reference ranges

Hypothyroidism in Pregnancy

  • Risks: Miscarriage, preterm delivery, impaired fetal neurodevelopment
  • Treatment: Levothyroxine — increase dose ~25-30% as soon as pregnancy confirmed
  • Goal TSH: <2.5 mU/L (especially 1st trimester)
  • Monitoring: TSH every 4 weeks in 1st half, then once per trimester

Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy

ConditionFeaturesManagement
Gestational thyrotoxicosis1st trimester, hCG-mediated, often with hyperemesisSelf-limited; supportive care
Graves' diseaseTRAb positive, goiter, ophthalmopathyAntithyroid drugs
PTU in 1st trimester (methimazole causes aplasia cutis, choanal atresia)
Methimazole in 2nd/3rd trimester (PTU hepatotoxicity risk)

Postpartum Thyroiditis

  • Occurs 2-6 months postpartum
  • Phase 1: Thyrotoxicosis (destructive) — beta-blocker for symptoms
  • Phase 2: Hypothyroidism — levothyroxine if symptomatic
  • Phase 3: Recovery (80%) or permanent hypothyroidism (20%)

40. Diabetes in Pregnancy Common

Types

  • Pregestational: T1D or T2D before pregnancy
  • Gestational (GDM): Glucose intolerance first diagnosed in pregnancy

GDM Screening

  • When: 24-28 weeks (earlier if high risk)
  • One-step (75g OGTT): Any 1 value abnormal = GDM
    • Fasting ≥92 mg/dL
    • 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL
    • 2-hour ≥153 mg/dL
  • Two-step: 50g GCT → if ≥140, then 100g OGTT

Glycemic Targets in Pregnancy

TimeTarget
Fasting<95 mg/dL
1-hour postprandial<140 mg/dL
2-hour postprandial<120 mg/dL
A1c<6% (without hypoglycemia)

Treatment

  • First-line: Medical nutrition therapy, exercise
  • Medications: Insulin preferred; metformin and glyburide sometimes used
  • Avoid: All other oral agents

Maternal/Fetal Risks

  • Maternal: Preeclampsia, C-section, progression to T2D
  • Fetal: Macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycemia, congenital anomalies (pregestational), respiratory distress
Postpartum: Screen for persistent diabetes at 4-12 weeks (75g OGTT). Then screen every 1-3 years (high T2D risk).

41. Endocrine Emergencies — Summary Emergency

EmergencyKey FeaturesTreatment
DKAHigh glucose, AG metabolic acidosis, ketonesFluids, insulin, K+ replacement
HHSVery high glucose (>600), hyperosmolar, minimal ketonesAggressive fluids, lower-dose insulin
Thyroid stormFever, tachycardia, AMS, hyperthyroidPTU → iodine → steroids → beta-blocker
Myxedema comaHypothermia, AMS, hyponatremia, severe hypothyroidIV levothyroxine + hydrocortisone, supportive
Adrenal crisisHypotension, shock, hypoglycemia, hyponatremiaIV hydrocortisone 100mg + fluids
Hypercalcemic crisisCalcium >14, AMS, dehydrationIV fluids, bisphosphonate, calcitonin
Pituitary apoplexySudden headache, visual loss, hypopituitarismIV hydrocortisone, surgery if visual loss
Pheo crisisSevere HTN, arrhythmiasPhentolamine, nitroprusside (NOT beta-blocker first)

42. Myxedema Coma Emergency

Clinical Features

  • Hypothermia (may be profound)
  • Altered mental status (confusion to coma)
  • Hyponatremia
  • Hypoglycemia, hypotension, bradycardia
  • Hypoventilation (CO2 retention)

Precipitants

  • Infection, cold exposure, medications (sedatives, narcotics)
  • Noncompliance with levothyroxine
  • Surgery, trauma, MI

Treatment

  • IV levothyroxine: Loading dose 200-400 mcg, then 50-100 mcg/day
  • IV hydrocortisone: 50-100mg Q8h (until adrenal insufficiency ruled out)
  • Supportive: Passive rewarming, ventilatory support, treat precipitant
Always give hydrocortisone with thyroid hormone — treating hypothyroidism can precipitate adrenal crisis if underlying adrenal insufficiency exists.

43. GI Neuroendocrine Tumors

TumorHormoneFeaturesDiagnosis
InsulinomaInsulinHypoglycemia (Whipple's triad)72h fast: low glucose, high insulin, high C-peptide
GastrinomaGastrinSevere PUD, diarrhea (ZES)Fasting gastrin >1000, secretin stimulation
VIPomaVIPWatery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria (WDHA)Elevated VIP level
GlucagonomaGlucagonNecrolytic migratory erythema, diabetes, weight lossElevated glucagon >500 pg/mL
SomatostatinomaSomatostatinDiabetes, gallstones, steatorrheaElevated somatostatin

44. Drug-Induced Endocrine Disorders

DrugEndocrine Effect
AmiodaroneHypo- or hyperthyroidism (Type I or II)
LithiumHypothyroidism, nephrogenic DI, hyperparathyroidism
Checkpoint inhibitorsHypophysitis, thyroiditis, adrenalitis, T1D
GlucocorticoidsAdrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis
OpioidsHypogonadism (via hypothalamic suppression)
KetoconazoleAdrenal insufficiency (inhibits steroidogenesis)
SSRIsSIADH, hyperprolactinemia
AntipsychoticsHyperprolactinemia, metabolic syndrome

45. Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndromes

TypeFeaturesNotes
APS-1 (APECED)Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, adrenal insufficiencyAIRE gene mutation; childhood onset
APS-2 (Schmidt)Adrenal insufficiency + autoimmune thyroid disease ± T1DMost common APS; adult onset
If one autoimmune endocrine disorder is diagnosed, screen for others (thyroid, adrenal, T1D, celiac, pernicious anemia).

46. Bone Turnover Markers

MarkerTypeUse
P1NPFormationMonitor anabolic therapy response
Bone-specific ALPFormationPaget's disease monitoring
CTXResorptionMonitor antiresorptive therapy
NTX (urine)ResorptionMonitor treatment response

47. Vitamin D Physiology & Disorders

Vitamin D Metabolism

  • Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol): Skin synthesis (UV) or dietary
  • 25-OH vitamin D: Liver hydroxylation — storage form, measured clinically
  • 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D (calcitriol): Kidney hydroxylation — active form

Interpretation

25-OH Vitamin DStatus
<12 ng/mLDeficient
12-20 ng/mLInsufficient
20-50 ng/mLSufficient
>50 ng/mLPotentially harmful
Check 25-OH vitamin D for deficiency. Check 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D only if granulomatous disease, CKD, or hypercalcemia workup.

48. Euthyroid Sick Syndrome (Non-Thyroidal Illness)

Pattern

  • Low T3 (decreased peripheral conversion)
  • Normal or low T4
  • Normal, low, or mildly elevated TSH
  • Elevated reverse T3

Key Points

  • Occurs in severe illness (sepsis, MI, surgery, starvation)
  • Adaptive response — do NOT treat with thyroid hormone
  • May look like central hypothyroidism
  • Recheck thyroid function after illness recovery

49. Endocrine Screening Guidelines Summary

ConditionWho to ScreenTest
DiabetesAll adults ≥35 (or any age if overweight + risk factors)Fasting glucose, A1c, or OGTT
ThyroidNo universal screening; consider in pregnancy, elderlyTSH
OsteoporosisWomen ≥65, men ≥70, or younger with risk factorsDXA
Primary aldosteronismResistant HTN, hypokalemiaAldosterone/renin ratio
PheochromocytomaParoxysmal HTN, adrenal incidentaloma, hereditary syndromesPlasma free metanephrines
Cushing'sClinical features, adrenal incidentaloma, uncontrolled diabetes1mg DST, 24h urine cortisol

50. High-Yield Board Pearls

Diabetes

  • 💎 SGLT2i and GLP-1 RA have cardiorenal benefits independent of glucose control
  • 💎 Never use sliding scale alone — always add basal insulin
  • 💎 Check K+ before starting insulin in DKA
  • 💎 Low C-peptide with hypoglycemia = exogenous insulin

Thyroid

  • 💎 Low RAIU = thyroiditis or exogenous; high RAIU = Graves' or toxic nodule
  • 💎 Always check prolactin before pituitary surgery (prolactinomas are treated medically)
  • 💎 PTU before iodine in thyroid storm
  • 💎 PTU in 1st trimester, methimazole in 2nd/3rd

Adrenal

  • 💎 IPSS = gold standard for distinguishing pituitary vs ectopic Cushing's
  • 💎 Alpha-blocker before beta-blocker in pheochromocytoma
  • 💎 Give stress-dose steroids in adrenal crisis — don't wait for labs
  • 💎 Hyperpigmentation = primary adrenal insufficiency (high ACTH)

Pituitary

  • 💎 Prolactinoma is the ONLY pituitary tumor treated primarily with medication
  • 💎 Large mass + mildly elevated prolactin = stalk effect, not prolactinoma
  • 💎 Replace cortisol BEFORE thyroid to avoid adrenal crisis

Calcium/Bone

  • 💎 First step in hypercalcemia workup: check PTH
  • 💎 Correct magnesium before treating hypocalcemia
  • 💎 Bisphosphonate drug holiday after 5 years oral or 3 years IV

MEN Syndromes

  • 💎 MEN1: "3 P's" — Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreatic
  • 💎 MEN2: Always rule out pheo before thyroidectomy
  • 💎 All medullary thyroid cancer patients need RET genetic testing

🔬 ABIM Endocrinology Study Guide

ADA • Endocrine Society • AACE Guidelines

BUILD #1 • 50 SECTIONS • December 2025

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide 🦠 ID Guide ❤️ Cardio Guide 🫁 GI Guide 🦴 Rheum Guide

For educational purposes only. Always use clinical judgment.

↑ Back to top

💧 Nephrology

ABIM Board Review — KDIGO/ASN Guidelines • 51 Topics

🎯 Core Nephrology Principles

🔍Classify AKI as pre-renal, intrinsic, or post-renal before workup
💊ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i are cornerstones of CKD progression slowing
🧪Spot urine protein/Cr ratio replaces 24-hour urine in most cases
⚡Treat the underlying cause first — dialysis is supportive
📊Acid-base: always calculate anion gap and compensatory response
🚫Avoid NSAIDs, contrast, and nephrotoxins in CKD stage 3+
⚠️ AKI 🔴 ATN 🟡 AIN 🔵 Glomerular Disease 💧 Nephrotic 🔥 Nephritic/RPGN 🦋 Lupus Nephritis 🧬 ANCA Vasculitis 📉 CKD 🧂 Hyponatremia ⚡ Hyperkalemia 🪨 Calcium 🧪 Acid-Base 📊 Hypertension 🪨 Nephrolithiasis 🫧 PKD 🔄 Dialysis 🏥 Transplant 🔬 Urinalysis 📐 Formulas

1. AKI Overview & Classification Very Common

KDIGO Definition

  • ↑ SCr ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h, OR
  • ↑ SCr ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, OR
  • UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6h

Staging

StageCreatinineUrine Output
11.5-1.9× OR ≥0.3 increase<0.5 mL/kg/h × 6-12h
22.0-2.9× baseline<0.5 mL/kg/h × ≥12h
3≥3× OR ≥4.0 OR RRT<0.3 mL/kg/h × ≥24h OR anuria

Prerenal vs Intrinsic vs Postrenal

TypeCausesFENaBUN:CrSediment
PrerenalHypovolemia, HF, cirrhosis, NSAIDs/ACEi<1%>20:1Bland, hyaline casts
IntrinsicATN, AIN, GN>2%10-15:1Casts (muddy brown, RBC, WBC)
PostrenalBPH, stones, tumorVariableVariableBland
FENa = (UNa × PCr) / (PNa × UCr) × 100
FEUrea = (UUrea × PCr) / (PUrea × UCr) × 100 → Use if on diuretics (<35% = prerenal)
FENa <1% also seen in: contrast nephropathy, rhabdomyolysis, early obstruction, acute GN

2. Acute Tubular Necrosis Common

Causes

TypeEtiologies
IschemicProlonged prerenal, sepsis, surgery, hemorrhage
NephrotoxicAminoglycosides, amphotericin, cisplatin, contrast
PigmentRhabdomyolysis (myoglobin), hemolysis

Phases

  1. Initiation: Injury occurs
  2. Maintenance: Oliguria, Cr peaks (1-2 wks)
  3. Recovery: Polyuria, Cr falls

UA Findings

  • Muddy brown granular casts — pathognomonic
  • Renal tubular epithelial cells
  • FENa >2%

Rhabdomyolysis

  • Labs: CK >5× ULN, ↑K, ↓Ca, ↑PO4, ↑uric acid
  • UA: + blood on dipstick, NO RBCs on micro
  • Tx: Aggressive NS (goal UOP 200-300 mL/h)
Dipstick + blood but no RBCs = myoglobinuria or hemoglobinuria

3. Acute Interstitial Nephritis Boards Favorite

Causes

CategoryExamples
Drugs (>70%)NSAIDs, PPIs, β-lactams, fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, rifampin, allopurinol
InfectionPyelonephritis, Legionella, CMV
AutoimmuneSjögren's, SLE, sarcoid, IgG4-RD, TINU
Classic triad (only ~10%): Fever + Rash + Eosinophilia

Diagnosis

  • UA: WBC casts, sterile pyuria, mild proteinuria (<1 g)
  • Urine eosinophils: Poor sensitivity (~30%) — don't rely on it!
  • Biopsy: Gold standard — interstitial inflammation

Treatment

  • Stop offending agent — most important step
  • Steroids: If no improvement after 5-7 days

4. Contrast-Induced Nephropathy

Risk Factors

  • CKD (most important), diabetes + CKD
  • Volume depletion, high contrast volume
  • Concurrent nephrotoxins, heart failure

Prevention

InterventionEvidence
IV isotonic saline✓ Proven benefit
Low-osmolar contrast✓ Preferred
Minimize volume (<100 mL)✓ Helpful
N-acetylcysteine✗ No benefit (ACT trial)
Sodium bicarbonate✗ No benefit (PRESERVE)
PRESERVE Trial: Neither NAC nor bicarb superior to saline alone

5. Hepatorenal Syndrome High-Yield

Pathophysiology

Splanchnic vasodilation → ↓ effective arterial volume → renal vasoconstriction

Types

TypeFeaturesSurvival
HRS-AKIRapid, often after SBP or GI bleed~2 weeks
HRS-CKDGradual, refractory ascites~6 months

Diagnosis (Exclusion)

  • Cirrhosis + ascites + AKI
  • No improvement with albumin 1 g/kg × 2 days
  • No shock, nephrotoxins, obstruction
  • Bland sediment, FENa <1%

Treatment

  • Albumin + vasoconstrictors (midodrine/octreotide OR norepinephrine)
  • Definitive: Liver transplant (only cure)

6. Glomerulonephritis Overview

Nephrotic vs Nephritic

FeatureNephroticNephritic
Proteinuria>3.5 g/day<3.5 g/day
HematuriaMinimalProminent (RBC casts)
EdemaSevereMild
AlbuminLowNormal/mildly low
LipidsElevatedNormal
BPVariableElevated

Complement Levels

LOW ComplementNORMAL Complement
Post-infectious GNIgA nephropathy
Lupus nephritisANCA vasculitis
MPGNAnti-GBM
CryoglobulinemiaFSGS, MCD, Membranous
Endocarditis-associated
Low C3 + Low C4 = Classic pathway (lupus, cryo)
Low C3 + Normal C4 = Alternative pathway (post-infectious, MPGN)

7. Nephrotic Syndrome Common

Definition

  • Proteinuria >3.5 g/24h
  • Hypoalbuminemia (<3 g/dL)
  • Edema
  • Hyperlipidemia

Causes by Age

PopulationMost Common
ChildrenMinimal Change Disease
Adults (Black)FSGS
Adults (White)Membranous
DiabeticsDiabetic nephropathy
ElderlyMembranous, Amyloidosis

Complications

  • Thromboembolism: Loss of antithrombin III → DVT, PE, renal vein thrombosis
  • Infection: Loss of immunoglobulins
  • Hyperlipidemia
Renal vein thrombosis: Most common with membranous nephropathy. Sudden flank pain, hematuria, ↑ proteinuria

8. Nephritic Syndrome & RPGN

Features

  • Hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts)
  • Hypertension
  • Oliguria, azotemia
  • Mild proteinuria

RPGN Classification

TypeIF PatternDiseases
Type ILinear IgGAnti-GBM (Goodpasture)
Type IIGranularLupus, post-infectious, IgA, MPGN
Type IIIPauci-immuneANCA (GPA, MPA, EGPA)

Post-Infectious GN

  • Cause: Group A strep
  • Timing: 1-3 wks post-pharyngitis, 3-6 wks post-skin
  • Labs: ↓C3, ↑ASO
  • Biopsy: Subepithelial humps ("starry sky")
RBC casts = GN. Pathognomonic for glomerular bleeding.

9. IgA Nephropathy Most Common GN

Key Features

  • Most common primary GN worldwide
  • Young males
  • Synpharyngitic: Gross hematuria during or within days of URI
  • Normal complement
IgA vs Post-strep:
IgA = hematuria DURING URI, normal C3
Post-strep = hematuria 1-3 wks AFTER, low C3

Prognosis (Poor Factors)

  • Proteinuria >1 g/day
  • Hypertension
  • ↑ Cr at diagnosis
  • Crescents on biopsy

Treatment

  • ACEi/ARB — first-line
  • SGLT2i — renoprotective
  • Steroids if proteinuria >1g despite supportive care

10. Membranous Nephropathy Boards Favorite

Etiology

Primary (75%)Secondary (25%)
Anti-PLA2R Ab (70-80%)Malignancy (lung, colon, prostate)
Anti-THSD7A AbHepatitis B, SLE, drugs (NSAIDs, gold)
Screen for malignancy in all patients >50 with membranous nephropathy!

Key Points

  • Highest VTE risk of all nephrotic causes
  • Rule of thirds: 1/3 remit, 1/3 stable, 1/3 progress
  • Biopsy: "Spike and dome" on silver stain

Treatment

  • Supportive: ACEi/ARB, consider anticoagulation if albumin <2.5
  • Immunosuppression: Rituximab (first-line), cyclophosphamide

11. FSGS

Causes

PrimarySecondary
IdiopathicHIV (collapsing variant)
Obesity, sickle cell, heroin
APOL1 variants (African Americans)
  • Most common nephrotic syndrome in African Americans
  • Heavy proteinuria, HTN, progressive CKD
  • Collapsing FSGS: HIV-associated, worst prognosis

Treatment

  • Primary: Steroids (4-6 months), CNI if steroid-resistant
  • Secondary: Treat underlying cause

12. Minimal Change Disease

  • Most common nephrotic syndrome in children
  • Associations: NSAIDs, Hodgkin lymphoma
  • Sudden onset nephrotic syndrome
  • Normal GFR, normal complement

Biopsy

  • LM: Normal
  • IF: Negative
  • EM: Podocyte foot process effacement only

Treatment

Highly steroid-responsive — 90% children, 80% adults respond

If no steroid response, reconsider diagnosis — may be FSGS (sampling error)

13. Lupus Nephritis High-Yield

ISN/RPS Classification

ClassDescriptionTreatment
IMinimal mesangialSupportive
IIMesangial proliferativeSupportive
IIIFocal proliferative (<50%)Immunosuppression
IVDiffuse proliferative (≥50%)Immunosuppression (worst)
VMembranousIf nephrotic → immunosuppression
VISclerotic (>90%)Supportive (irreversible)

Labs

  • ↓C3, ↓C4 (classic pathway)
  • Anti-dsDNA correlates with activity
"Full house" on IF: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q all positive = pathognomonic for lupus

Treatment (Class III/IV)

  • Induction: MMF or cyclophosphamide + steroids
  • Maintenance: MMF or azathioprine
  • All: Hydroxychloroquine

14. ANCA-Associated GN Emergency

Diseases

DiseaseANCAFeatures
GPA (Wegener's)PR3 (c-ANCA)Upper airway, lungs, kidneys
MPAMPO (p-ANCA)Kidneys, lungs (no upper airway)
EGPA (Churg-Strauss)MPO (60%)Asthma, eosinophilia

Renal

  • RPGN with crescents
  • Pauci-immune (little IF staining)
  • RBC casts

Treatment

  • Induction: Pulse steroids + rituximab (preferred) or cyclophosphamide
  • Maintenance: Rituximab or azathioprine
  • PLEX: For DAH or severe renal disease
RAVE Trial: Rituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide, superior for relapse

15. Anti-GBM Disease Emergency

  • Antibodies to type IV collagen
  • Goodpasture: Anti-GBM + pulmonary hemorrhage
  • Risk: smoking, hydrocarbons

Diagnosis

  • Anti-GBM antibody positive
  • Linear IgG on IF — pathognomonic
  • 30% have concurrent ANCA
Treat immediately! Delay = irreversible damage
• Plasmapheresis (daily until Ab negative)
• Cyclophosphamide + steroids
IF patterns: Linear = Anti-GBM | Granular = immune complex | Pauci-immune = ANCA

16. Diabetic Nephropathy Most Common ESRD

Natural History

  1. Hyperfiltration
  2. Microalbuminuria (30-300 mg/g)
  3. Macroalbuminuria (>300 mg/g)
  4. Declining GFR
  5. ESRD

Pathology

  • Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules — pathognomonic
  • GBM thickening, mesangial expansion

Suspect Non-DM Cause If:

  • No retinopathy (usually present with nephropathy)
  • Active sediment (RBC casts)
  • Rapid GFR decline
  • DM duration <5 years (T1DM)

Treatment

AgentBenefit
ACEi/ARBFirst-line, ↓ proteinuria
SGLT2iRenoprotective (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)
Finerenone↓ progression (FIDELIO-DKD)
BP <130/80Slows progression

17. Renal Tubular Acidosis Boards Favorite

Comparison

FeatureType 1 (Distal)Type 2 (Proximal)Type 4
DefectCan't secrete H+Can't reabsorb HCO3↓ Aldosterone
Urine pH>5.5<5.5<5.5
Serum K+LOWLOWHIGH
StonesYes (CaPO4)RareNo
AssociationsSjögren's, ampho BFanconi, MMDM, ACEi, NSAIDs
Type 4 = HyperKalemia (the only RTA with HIGH K)

Diagnosis

  • NAGMA + positive urine anion gap = RTA
  • Urine AG = UNa + UK - UCl
  • Positive UAG = impaired NH4+ excretion

18. Thrombotic Microangiopathy Emergency

Features (All TMA)

  • MAHA: schistocytes, ↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, ↑indirect bili
  • Thrombocytopenia
  • Negative Coombs, normal PT/PTT

Types

TypeMechanismFeaturesTreatment
TTPADAMTS13 <10%Neuro sx prominentPLEX, caplacizumab
HUSShiga toxinBloody diarrhea → AKISupportive (NO abx)
aHUSComplement dysregNo diarrhea, recurrentEculizumab
TTP = Emergency. Start PLEX immediately — don't wait for ADAMTS13. Mortality >90% without treatment.

19. Renal Artery Stenosis

Types

TypeDemographicsLocation
Atherosclerotic (90%)Older, male, CVD risk factorsProximal 1/3
FMD (10%)Young womenDistal 2/3, "string of beads"

Clinical Clues

  • Resistant HTN
  • Cr rise >30% after ACEi/ARB
  • Flash pulmonary edema
  • Asymmetric kidney sizes
  • Abdominal bruit

Treatment

  • Atherosclerotic: Medical therapy (CORAL trial — no stent benefit)
  • FMD: Angioplasty (high cure rate)

20. Atheroembolic Disease Boards Favorite

Triggers

  • Vascular procedure (cath, surgery)
  • Anticoagulation (destabilizes plaque)

Classic Triad

  • Livedo reticularis, blue toe syndrome
  • AKI (subacute, stepwise)
  • Eosinophilia

Labs

  • ↑ Eosinophils (80%)
  • ↓ Complement (C3, C4)
  • ↑ ESR
Classic boards scenario: Elderly + AKI + livedo + eosinophilia after cardiac cath

Diagnosis

  • Clinical + cholesterol clefts on biopsy

21. CKD Staging & Management Very Common

KDIGO Staging

StageGFRDescription
G1≥90Normal (with kidney damage)
G260-89Mild ↓
G3a45-59Mild-moderate ↓
G3b30-44Moderate-severe ↓
G415-29Severe ↓
G5<15Kidney failure

Albuminuria

CategoryUACR (mg/g)
A1<30
A230-300
A3>300

Renoprotective Therapy

AgentIndication
ACEi/ARBFirst-line if proteinuria
SGLT2ieGFR ≥20, with or without DM
FinerenoneDiabetic nephropathy

22. CKD-Mineral Bone Disease High-Yield

Pathophysiology

  1. ↓ GFR → ↓ PO4 excretion → ↑ PO4
  2. ↓ 1,25-vit D production → ↓ Ca absorption
  3. ↑ PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
  4. ↑ FGF-23

Lab Pattern

LabEarly CKDLate CKD
PhosphorusNormal↑
CalciumNormal↓
PTH↑↑↑
1,25-vit D↓↓↓

Treatment

  • Phosphate binders: Sevelamer, lanthanum, calcium-based
  • Vitamin D: Calcitriol (active)
  • Calcimimetic: Cinacalcet
Calciphylaxis: Painful necrotic skin lesions from vascular calcification. Treat with sodium thiosulfate.

23. Anemia of CKD

Pathophysiology

  • Primary: ↓ EPO production
  • Contributing: Iron deficiency, ↑ hepcidin, uremia

Iron Targets

ParameterTarget
Ferritin>200 (HD), >100 (non-HD)
TSAT>20%

Treatment

  1. Iron first (IV preferred in HD)
  2. ESA (epoetin, darbepoetin) — target Hgb 10-11
TREAT/CHOIR: Hgb >13 with ESAs → ↑ stroke, no CV benefit. Target 10-11.

24. Hyponatremia Very Common High-Yield

Approach

Step 1: Serum Osmolality

OsmCategoryCauses
<280Hypotonic (true)See below
280-295Isotonic (pseudo)Hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia
>295HypertonicHyperglycemia, mannitol

Step 2: Volume Status (Hypotonic)

VolumeUNaCauses
Hypovolemic<20GI losses, burns
>20Diuretics, salt-wasting
Euvolemic>20SIADH, hypothyroid, adrenal insufficiency
Hypervolemic<20CHF, cirrhosis
>20CKD

SIADH Criteria

  • Serum osm <275, Urine osm >100
  • UNa >30, euvolemic
  • Normal thyroid/adrenal
