Correction rate: ≤8-10 mEq/L per 24h (risk of osmotic demyelination if too fast)

Treatment

SettingTreatment
Acute symptomatic3% saline (↑ 4-6 mEq in first hours)
HypovolemicNS
SIADHFluid restriction, salt tabs, vaptans
HypervolemicFluid/Na restriction, diuretics

25. Hypernatremia

Definition

Na >145 = Free water deficit

Causes

  • Water loss: Insensible, GI, osmotic diuresis
  • Inadequate intake: Altered mental status, intubated
  • Diabetes insipidus: Central or nephrogenic

DI Types

TypeCauseTreatment
Central↓ ADH (pituitary)DDAVP
NephrogenicADH resistance (lithium, hypercalcemia)Thiazides, amiloride
Free water deficit = TBW × [(Na/140) - 1]
TBW = 0.6 × wt (men), 0.5 × wt (women)

Correction: ≤10-12 mEq/24h (risk cerebral edema if too fast)

26. Hypokalemia

Causes

MechanismExamples
ShiftInsulin, β-agonists, alkalosis
GI lossVomiting, diarrhea
Renal lossDiuretics, hyperaldosteronism, RTA 1/2

ECG Changes

  • Flattened T waves
  • U waves
  • ST depression
Always check Mg! Hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia.

27. Hyperkalemia Emergency

Causes

  • Shift: Acidosis, insulin deficiency, β-blockers, cell lysis
  • ↓ Excretion: AKI, CKD, hypoaldo, K-sparing diuretics
  • Drugs: ACEi, ARB, spironolactone, TMP-SMX

ECG Changes (Progressive)

  1. Peaked T waves
  2. PR prolongation, flat P
  3. QRS widening
  4. Sine wave → VF/asystole

Treatment

AgentMechanismOnset
Calcium gluconateStabilize membraneMinutes
Insulin + glucoseShift K into cells15-30 min
AlbuterolShift30 min
BicarbShift (if acidotic)30-60 min
Loop diureticsExcretionHours
Patiromer/SZCGI bindingHours
DialysisRemovalImmediate

28. Calcium Disorders

Hypercalcemia

Most common: Hyperparathyroidism (outpatient), Malignancy (inpatient)

Symptoms

"Stones, bones, groans, moans, psychiatric overtones"

Workup

  • PTH high: Primary hyperparathyroidism
  • PTH low: Malignancy, granulomatous, vitamin D toxicity

Treatment

  1. IV NS (volume first)
  2. Loop diuretics (after euvolemic)
  3. Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid)
  4. Calcitonin (rapid but tachyphylaxis)
  5. Dialysis (severe)

Hypocalcemia

  • Causes: Hypoparathyroid (post-surgical), vitamin D deficiency, CKD, hypomagnesemia
  • Signs: Chvostek (facial twitch), Trousseau (carpal spasm)
  • Treatment: IV calcium gluconate if symptomatic

29. Phosphorus & Magnesium Disorders

Hyperphosphatemia

  • Causes: CKD, tumor lysis, rhabdo, hypoparathyroid
  • Treatment: Dietary restriction, phosphate binders, dialysis

Hypophosphatemia

  • Causes: Refeeding syndrome, insulin, respiratory alkalosis, alcoholism
  • Symptoms: Weakness, rhabdo, respiratory failure, hemolysis
Refeeding syndrome: Severe ↓PO4, ↓K, ↓Mg when malnourished patients fed. Can be fatal.

Hypomagnesemia

  • Causes: Diuretics, PPIs, alcoholism, aminoglycosides
  • Effect: Causes refractory hypokalemia and hypocalcemia
  • Treatment: IV Mg sulfate if severe

30. Acid-Base Approach Must-Know

5-Step Approach

  1. pH: Acidemia (<7.35) or alkalemia (>7.45)?
  2. Primary disorder (pCO2 vs HCO3)
  3. Calculate expected compensation
  4. Anion gap (if metabolic acidosis)
  5. Delta-delta (if AGMA)
Anion Gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) | Normal: 8-12
Corrected AG: Add 2.5 for each 1 g/dL ↓ albumin

Compensation Formulas

DisorderExpected Compensation
Metabolic acidosispCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2
Metabolic alkalosispCO2 = 0.7(HCO3) + 21 ± 2
Acute resp acidosisHCO3 ↑ 1 per 10 ↑pCO2
Chronic resp acidosisHCO3 ↑ 3.5 per 10 ↑pCO2
Acute resp alkalosisHCO3 ↓ 2 per 10 ↓pCO2
Chronic resp alkalosisHCO3 ↓ 5 per 10 ↓pCO2
Delta-Delta = (AG - 12) / (24 - HCO3)
<1: concurrent NAGMA | 1-2: pure AGMA | >2: concurrent met alk

31. Metabolic Acidosis Common

Anion Gap Acidosis

GOLDMARK: Glycols, Oxoproline, L-lactate, D-lactate, Methanol, Aspirin, Renal failure, Ketoacidosis

Toxic Alcohols

ToxinMetaboliteClue
MethanolFormic acidVisual symptoms
Ethylene glycolOxalic acidCa oxalate crystals, AKI

Treatment: Fomepizole + hemodialysis

Non-Anion Gap Acidosis

HARDASS: Hyperalimentation, Acetazolamide, RTA, Diarrhea, Addison's, Spironolactone, Saline

Urine Anion Gap

UAG = UNa + UK - UCl

  • Negative: GI loss (appropriate NH4+ excretion)
  • Positive: RTA (impaired NH4+)

32. Metabolic Alkalosis

Classification by Urine Cl

UClCategoryCausesTreatment
<20Saline-responsiveVomiting, NG suction, diuretics (past)IV NS
>20Saline-resistantHyperaldosteronism, Cushing's, Bartter/GitelmanTreat cause

Bartter vs Gitelman

FeatureBartterGitelman
MimicsLoop diureticThiazide
Urine Ca↑↓
MgNormal↓

33. Essential Hypertension Very Common

Classification (ACC/AHA 2017)

CategorySBPDBP
Normal<120<80
Elevated120-129<80
Stage 1130-13980-89
Stage 2≥140≥90

First-Line Agents

ClassCompelling Indication
ACEi/ARBDM, CKD with proteinuria, HFrEF
ThiazideOsteoporosis, Black patients
CCB (DHP)Elderly, Black patients
SPRINT: SBP <120 reduced CV events/mortality in high-risk non-diabetics

34. Secondary Hypertension High-Yield

When to Suspect

  • Onset <30 or >55
  • Resistant HTN (≥3 drugs)
  • Sudden worsening
  • Hypokalemia

Causes

CauseCluesTest
Primary aldosteronism↓K, resistant HTNAldo:renin >30
RASBruit, Cr rise with ACEi, flash edemaDuplex/CTA
PheochromocytomaEpisodic HTN, sweating, palpitationsPlasma metanephrines
Cushing'sCentral obesity, striae24h urine cortisol
OSASnoring, obesitySleep study

35. Hypertensive Emergency Emergency

Definition

HTN emergency: Severe BP + end-organ damage (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, AKI, dissection)

Treatment Goals

  • General: ↓ MAP 20-25% in first hour
  • Aortic dissection: SBP <120, HR <60 in 20 min
  • Ischemic stroke: Usually don't treat unless >220/120

IV Agents

DrugBest For
LabetalolMost emergencies, pregnancy
NicardipineMost emergencies, stroke
NitroprussideRapid titration (avoid in renal failure)
NitroglycerinACS, pulmonary edema
EsmololAortic dissection
Aortic dissection: β-blocker FIRST (esmolol), THEN vasodilator

36. Nephrolithiasis Common

Stone Types

Type%X-rayRisk Factors
Calcium oxalate80%OpaqueHypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria
Uric acid10%LucentGout, low pH, tumor lysis
Struvite5%OpaqueUTI (Proteus), staghorn
Cystine1%FaintCystinuria, hexagonal crystals

Diagnosis

Non-contrast CT = Gold standard

Management

  • Analgesia (NSAIDs, opioids)
  • α-blockers (tamsulosin) for stones <10mm
  • Intervention if: infection, obstruction, >10mm
Infected obstructed kidney = emergency! Urgent decompression + antibiotics

Prevention

StonePrevention
CalciumFluids, thiazides, citrate
Uric acidAlkalinize urine, allopurinol
CystineAlkalinize, D-penicillamine

37. Polycystic Kidney Disease

ADPKD

Gene%ESRD Age
PKD1 (chr 16)85%~55 yrs
PKD2 (chr 4)15%~70 yrs

Extrarenal Manifestations

  • Liver cysts (most common)
  • Intracranial aneurysms (10-15%)
  • MVP, colonic diverticula

Management

  • BP control (ACEi/ARB)
  • Tolvaptan: Slows progression (TEMPO 3:4)
  • Screen for aneurysms if family Hx of rupture

38. Dialysis Indications & Modalities Must-Know

Urgent Indications

AEIOU:
Acidosis (severe, refractory)
Electrolytes (refractory hyperK)
Ingestions (toxic alcohols, lithium, aspirin)
Overload (volume, refractory)
Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)

Modalities

TypeAccessKey Points
HemodialysisAV fistula (best), graft, catheter3×/week, 4h sessions
PeritonealTenckhoff catheterDaily, at home, preserves residual function

Access Planning

  • Refer when GFR <30
  • AV fistula: create 6 months early (maturation time)

39. Kidney Transplantation

Benefits

  • Superior survival vs dialysis
  • Better quality of life
  • Living donor > deceased donor outcomes

Immunosuppression

PhaseAgents
InductionBasiliximab, thymoglobulin
MaintenanceTacrolimus + MMF + prednisone

Complications

  • Rejection: Cellular (T-cell) or antibody-mediated
  • Infections: CMV, BK virus (nephropathy)
  • Malignancy: Skin cancer, PTLD
  • Drug toxicity: CNI nephrotoxicity

40. Urinalysis Interpretation

Urine Sediment

FindingSuggests
Muddy brown castsATN
RBC castsGlomerulonephritis
WBC castsPyelonephritis, AIN
Hyaline castsPrerenal (concentrated urine)
Fatty casts, oval fat bodiesNephrotic syndrome
Dysmorphic RBCsGlomerular bleeding

Proteinuria Quantification

MethodNotes
DipstickDetects albumin only; misses light chains
UPCR (spot)Correlates with 24h protein
UACRPreferred for DM/HTN screening
24h collectionGold standard but cumbersome

41. Key Nephrology Formulas

CKD-EPI GFR: Standard for GFR estimation

FENa = (UNa × PCr) / (PNa × UCr) × 100
<1% = prerenal | >2% = intrinsic

FEUrea = (UUrea × PCr) / (PUrea × UCr) × 100
<35% = prerenal (use if on diuretics)

Anion Gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
Normal 8-12; correct for albumin

Corrected Na = Measured Na + 1.6 × [(glucose-100)/100]

Free Water Deficit = TBW × [(Na/140) - 1]

Osmolar Gap = Measured Osm - Calculated Osm
Calculated = 2(Na) + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8
Gap >10 = toxic alcohol

42. Drug Dosing in CKD

Drugs Requiring Adjustment

DrugConcern
MetforminAvoid if GFR <30; hold before contrast
GabapentinReduce dose, ↑ interval
EnoxaparinReduce dose if GFR <30
DOACsVaries by agent; check GFR cutoffs
AllopurinolStart low, titrate slowly

Drugs to Avoid

DrugReason
NSAIDs↓ GFR, hyperK, fluid retention
Gadolinium (GFR <30)NSF risk
NitrofurantoinIneffective, neurotoxicity
MeperidineNeurotoxic metabolite

43. Landmark Nephrology Trials

TrialFinding
SPRINTSBP <120 ↓ CV events in high-risk non-DM
CREDENCECanagliflozin ↓ renal outcomes in DKD
DAPA-CKDDapagliflozin benefits CKD ± diabetes
EMPA-KIDNEYEmpagliflozin ↓ progression in CKD
FIDELIO-DKDFinerenone ↓ CKD progression in DKD
RAVERituximab = cyclophosphamide for ANCA
PRESERVENAC/bicarb no better than saline for CIN
CORALNo stent benefit over medical Rx for atherosclerotic RAS
TREAT/CHOIRHigh Hgb targets with ESA → ↑ stroke
PATHWAY-2Spironolactone best add-on for resistant HTN

44. Board-Style Practice Questions

Q1. AKI Classification

65M with CHF presents with Cr 2.4 (baseline 1.0). BUN 56. UNa 8, FENa 0.4%. Bland sediment. Most likely?

Answer: Prerenal AKI (low FENa, high BUN:Cr, bland sediment)

Q2. AIN

45F started on omeprazole 3 weeks ago. Now Cr 2.8, fever, rash, eosinophilia. UA shows WBC casts. Next step?

Answer: Stop PPI. If no improvement in 5-7 days, consider steroids.

Q3. Glomerulonephritis

22M with gross hematuria during URI. Normal C3. UA shows RBC casts. Most likely diagnosis?

Answer: IgA nephropathy (synpharyngitic hematuria, normal complement)

Q4. Nephrotic Syndrome

60M with new nephrotic syndrome, anti-PLA2R positive. What screening is essential?

Answer: Age-appropriate cancer screening (membranous + age >50 = rule out malignancy)

Q5. RTA

55F with diabetes on lisinopril. K 6.2, HCO3 18, pH 7.30. Urine pH 5.2. Which RTA?

Answer: Type 4 RTA (hyperkalemia, acidosis, low urine pH, on ACEi)

Q6. Hyponatremia

68F with SCLC. Na 118, Sosm 260, Uosm 450, UNa 45. Euvolemic. Treatment?

Answer: SIADH from SCLC. Fluid restriction first-line. Correct ≤8-10 mEq/24h.

Q7. Hyperkalemia

K 7.2 with peaked T waves on ECG. First treatment?

Answer: IV calcium gluconate (stabilizes myocardium)

Q8. TMA

35F with MAHA, plt 25K, Cr 3.2, confusion. ADAMTS13 pending. Management?

Answer: Start plasma exchange immediately for presumed TTP. Don't wait for ADAMTS13.

Q9. Secondary HTN

45M with resistant HTN, K 2.9, HCO3 32. Most likely cause?

Answer: Primary aldosteronism (resistant HTN + hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis)

Q10. Dialysis

Patient with ESRD, pericardial friction rub, BUN 180. Indication for dialysis?

Answer: Uremic pericarditis — emergent dialysis indication

Q11. Atheroembolic

70M with AKI, livedo reticularis, eosinophilia 2 days after cardiac cath. Diagnosis?

Answer: Atheroembolic disease (cholesterol emboli)

Q12. Anti-GBM

28M smoker with hemoptysis and RPGN. IF shows linear IgG. Treatment?

Answer: Plasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + steroids for anti-GBM disease

Q13. SGLT2i

Patient with T2DM and CKD (eGFR 35, UACR 450) on max ACEi. What to add for renoprotection?

Answer: SGLT2 inhibitor (proven benefit in CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)

Q14. Acid-Base

pH 7.28, pCO2 24, HCO3 11, AG 22. What's the primary disorder and is compensation appropriate?

Answer: AGMA. Expected pCO2 = 1.5(11)+8 = 24.5. Compensation appropriate.

Q15. Contrast

Patient with CKD (GFR 40) needs CT with contrast. Prevention strategy?

Answer: IV isotonic saline before and after. NAC and bicarb have no proven benefit (PRESERVE).

45. Rhabdomyolysis Emergency High-Yield

Definition

Skeletal muscle breakdown → release of intracellular contents (myoglobin, CK, K, PO4, uric acid)

Causes

RHABDO:
Rest (prolonged immobilization, crush injury)
Hyperthermia, hypothermia, hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia
Alcohol, amphetamines
Bacterial/viral infections (influenza, legionella)
Drugs (statins, colchicine, antipsychotics, cocaine)
Overexertion (marathon, seizures, status epilepticus)

Clinical Features

  • Muscle pain, weakness, swelling
  • Dark "tea-colored" urine
  • May be asymptomatic initially

Laboratory Findings

FindingExplanation
CK >5× ULNDiagnostic (often >10,000); peaks 24-72h
↑ PotassiumReleased from cells — can be severe
↑ PhosphorusReleased from cells
↓ CalciumCa deposits in damaged muscle
↑ Uric acidFrom purine breakdown
↑ LDH, ASTReleased from muscle (ALT less affected)
Anion gap acidosisLactic acid, organic acids

Urinalysis

Classic finding: Dipstick positive for blood, but NO RBCs on microscopy
→ Dipstick detects heme (myoglobin cross-reacts with hemoglobin)

Acute Kidney Injury Mechanism

  • Myoglobin casts cause tubular obstruction
  • Direct tubular toxicity (free iron from myoglobin)
  • Renal vasoconstriction
  • Worse in acidic urine (myoglobin precipitates at pH <5.6)

Treatment

InterventionDetails
Aggressive IV fluidsNS at 200-300 mL/h initially; goal UOP 200-300 mL/h
Monitor K+Treat hyperkalemia aggressively
Avoid calciumUnless symptomatic hypocalcemia or hyperK with ECG changes
DialysisIf severe AKI, refractory hyperK, volume overload
Bicarbonate controversy: Previously used to alkalinize urine; no clear benefit in trials. May worsen hypocalcemia.

Compartment Syndrome

  • Complicates crush injuries
  • Pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch
  • Requires emergent fasciotomy

46. Myeloma Kidney High-Yield

Overview

Renal involvement occurs in ~50% of multiple myeloma patients; leading cause of morbidity

Types of Renal Involvement

TypeMechanismFeatures
Cast nephropathyLight chains + Tamm-Horsfall protein → tubular obstructionMost common; AKI, bland sediment
Light chain depositionLight chain deposition in glomeruli/tubulesNephrotic syndrome, CKD
AL AmyloidosisLight chain fibrils deposit as amyloidNephrotic syndrome, enlarged kidneys
HypercalcemiaVolume depletion, vasoconstrictionAKI, polyuria
HyperviscosityImpaired renal blood flowRare, more common in Waldenström

Cast Nephropathy (Myeloma Cast Nephropathy)

  • AKI with bland sediment
  • Dipstick negative for protein (detects albumin, not light chains)
  • SPEP/UPEP/free light chains — diagnostic
  • Biopsy: large, fractured casts with giant cell reaction
Key point: Dipstick-negative proteinuria with high protein on 24h collection = think light chains (myeloma, AL amyloid)

Precipitating Factors for AKI

  • Volume depletion
  • Hypercalcemia
  • IV contrast — historically avoided but newer evidence suggests iso-osmolar contrast may be safer
  • NSAIDs, loop diuretics (increase cast formation)
  • Infections

Treatment

  • Treat underlying myeloma — chemotherapy, stem cell transplant
  • IV fluids — maintain high urine flow
  • Avoid nephrotoxins
  • Treat hypercalcemia
  • Plasmapheresis — may help in severe cast nephropathy (controversial)
  • Dialysis if needed

AL Amyloidosis

  • Nephrotic-range proteinuria (albumin, not just light chains)
  • Large or normal-sized kidneys
  • Congo red stain → apple-green birefringence
  • Poor prognosis; treat underlying plasma cell dyscrasia

47. Membranoproliferative GN (MPGN) Boards Favorite

Overview

Pattern of glomerular injury with mesangial proliferation and capillary wall thickening ("tram-tracking")

Modern Classification

TypeMechanismCauses
Immune complex-mediatedIg + C3 depositsHep C (most common), Hep B, autoimmune (lupus), infections
Complement-mediated (C3 glomerulopathy)C3 only (no Ig)Alternative pathway dysregulation; C3 nephritic factor

Clinical Features

  • Can present as nephrotic OR nephritic syndrome
  • Low C3 (and low C4 if immune complex type)
  • Progressive CKD common

Hepatitis C-Associated MPGN

  • Most common cause of MPGN
  • Associated with cryoglobulinemia (mixed type II)
  • Features: palpable purpura, arthralgias, neuropathy
  • Treatment: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) for HCV
Low C3 + Low C4 = Classic pathway (immune complex: lupus, Hep C/cryo)
Low C3 + Normal C4 = Alternative pathway (C3 glomerulopathy, post-infectious)

Light Microscopy

  • Mesangial proliferation
  • Capillary wall thickening
  • "Tram-tracking" — double contour of GBM

Treatment

TypeTreatment
Hep C-relatedDAAs (curative in many cases)
Lupus-relatedImmunosuppression per lupus nephritis protocol
C3 glomerulopathySupportive; eculizumab in some cases
AllACEi/ARB for proteinuria

48. Post-Infectious Glomerulonephritis Common

Overview

Immune complex-mediated GN occurring after infection (classically Group A Streptococcus)

Epidemiology

  • Most common in children (2-14 years)
  • Can occur in adults (especially diabetics, alcoholics, immunocompromised)
  • Declining incidence in developed countries

Timing ("Latent Period")

Infection SiteLatent Period
Pharyngitis1-3 weeks
Skin (impetigo)3-6 weeks
Key distinction: Post-infectious GN has latent period vs IgA nephropathy which is synpharyngitic (hematuria during or within days of URI)

Clinical Features

  • Nephritic syndrome: hematuria (gross or micro), hypertension, edema
  • RBC casts on UA
  • Oliguria, mild proteinuria

Laboratory Findings

TestFinding
C3Low (normalizes in 6-8 weeks)
C4Normal (alternative pathway)
ASO titerElevated (pharyngitis)
Anti-DNase BElevated (skin infection)

Biopsy Findings

  • LM: Diffuse proliferative GN
  • IF: Granular "starry sky" IgG + C3
  • EM: Subepithelial "humps" (immune deposits)

Prognosis & Treatment

  • Children: Excellent prognosis; >95% recover completely
  • Adults: Higher risk of CKD, hypertension
  • Treatment: Supportive (BP control, diuretics, salt restriction)
  • Antibiotics for active infection (do not prevent GN)
When to biopsy: Atypical features, C3 not normalizing by 8 weeks, progressive renal dysfunction, nephrotic-range proteinuria

49. Respiratory Acidosis & Alkalosis High-Yield

Respiratory Acidosis (↑ pCO2)

Definition: pCO2 >45 mmHg with acidemia

Causes

CategoryExamples
CNS depressionOpioids, sedatives, stroke, encephalopathy
NeuromuscularGuillain-Barré, myasthenia, ALS, muscular dystrophy
Chest wallKyphoscoliosis, flail chest, obesity hypoventilation
Airway obstructionCOPD, severe asthma, OSA
Lung parenchymaSevere pneumonia, ARDS, pulmonary edema

Compensation

AcuteChronic
HCO3 ↑ 1 per 10 ↑pCO2HCO3 ↑ 3.5 per 10 ↑pCO2

Treatment

  • Treat underlying cause
  • Improve ventilation (BiPAP, intubation if severe)
  • Avoid O2-induced hypercapnia in COPD retainers

Respiratory Alkalosis (↓ pCO2)

Definition: pCO2 <35 mmHg with alkalemia

Causes

CategoryExamples
Hypoxia-drivenHigh altitude, pneumonia, PE, pulmonary edema
CNS stimulationAnxiety, pain, fever, meningitis, stroke, salicylate toxicity
HormonalPregnancy, hyperthyroidism
MechanicalOverventilation on ventilator
Liver failureHepatic encephalopathy (progesterone-like effect)

Compensation

AcuteChronic
HCO3 ↓ 2 per 10 ↓pCO2HCO3 ↓ 5 per 10 ↓pCO2
Salicylate toxicity: Primary respiratory alkalosis + primary metabolic acidosis (AGMA). Classic mixed disorder.

50. Mixed Acid-Base Disorders Must-Know

When to Suspect Mixed Disorder

  • Compensation is more or less than expected
  • pH is normal with abnormal pCO2 and HCO3
  • Delta-delta ratio is abnormal

Delta-Delta Ratio

Delta-Delta = ΔAG / ΔHCO3 = (AG - 12) / (24 - HCO3)

<1: Combined AGMA + NAGMA (or pre-existing metabolic alkalosis corrected)
1-2: Pure AGMA
>2: AGMA + metabolic alkalosis

Common Mixed Disorders

DisorderClinical ScenarioABG Pattern
AGMA + Resp alkalosisSalicylate toxicity, sepsis↓pH, ↓pCO2 more than expected, ↓HCO3
AGMA + Met alkalosisDKA + vomitingpH variable, ↓HCO3, delta-delta >2
AGMA + NAGMADKA with NS resuscitationDelta-delta <1
Met alk + Resp alkalosisCOPD + diuretics, liver diseaseMarkedly ↑pH, ↓pCO2, ↑HCO3
Met alk + Resp acidosisCOPD + vomitingpH near normal, ↑pCO2, ↑HCO3
Triple disorderAlcoholic + vomitingAGMA + met alk + resp alk

Approach to ABG Interpretation

  1. Identify primary disorder (pH direction + pCO2 or HCO3)
  2. Calculate expected compensation
  3. If met acidosis: calculate anion gap
  4. If AGMA: calculate delta-delta
  5. Look for additional disorders if compensation is off
Salicylate toxicity board pearl: Early: resp alk (direct CNS stimulation). Late: AGMA. Classic mixed disorder with both.

Practice Example

pH 7.40, pCO2 25, HCO3 15, AG 20

  • Normal pH suggests mixed disorder
  • Low HCO3 = metabolic acidosis; Low pCO2 = respiratory alkalosis
  • AG elevated (20) = AGMA present
  • This is AGMA + respiratory alkalosis (think salicylate, sepsis)

51. Hematuria Workup Common

Definition

  • Microscopic: ≥3 RBCs/HPF on 2-3 properly collected specimens
  • Gross: Visible blood in urine

Glomerular vs Non-Glomerular

FeatureGlomerularNon-Glomerular
RBC morphologyDysmorphic RBCsNormal (isomorphic) RBCs
RBC castsPresentAbsent
ProteinuriaOften presentUsually absent/minimal
ColorBrown/"cola-colored"Pink/red, may have clots
ClotsAbsentMay be present
Key point: RBC casts and dysmorphic RBCs = GLOMERULAR origin. Clots = NON-glomerular.

Glomerular Causes

  • IgA nephropathy (most common cause of glomerular hematuria)
  • Thin basement membrane disease (benign familial hematuria)
  • Alport syndrome
  • Post-infectious GN
  • Lupus nephritis, ANCA vasculitis

Non-Glomerular Causes

LocationCauses
KidneyStones, RCC, PKD, pyelonephritis, papillary necrosis
UreterStones, urothelial cancer
BladderCancer, cystitis, radiation, stones
ProstateBPH, prostatitis, prostate cancer
UrethraTrauma, urethritis, stricture

Workup Algorithm

Step 1: Confirm hematuria

  • Rule out false positives: menstruation, myoglobin, beets, rifampin
  • Repeat UA if initial positive

Step 2: Determine origin

  • UA with microscopy — look for casts, dysmorphic RBCs
  • If glomerular: nephrology workup
  • If non-glomerular: urology workup

Step 3: Urology workup (non-glomerular)

Age & RiskWorkup
Low risk (<40, no risk factors)UA, cystoscopy optional
Intermediate/High riskCT urogram + cystoscopy

Risk factors for malignancy

  • Age >50
  • Smoking history
  • Occupational exposure (dyes, chemicals)
  • Gross hematuria
  • Prior pelvic radiation
  • Cyclophosphamide exposure
Red flags requiring urgent workup: Gross hematuria in adults, especially age >50 or with risk factors — must rule out malignancy

Nephrology Study Guide for ABIM Board Review

v1.1 BUILD #9 | January 2026 | 51 Topics

↑ Back to top

🫁 Pulmonology

ABIM Board Review — ATS/GOLD/CHEST Guidelines

🎯 Core Pulmonology Principles

📊PFTs first — classify obstruction vs restriction before imaging
🚭Smoking cessation is the most impactful intervention in COPD
💨BiPAP reduces intubation and mortality in AECOPD with acidosis
🩸Use PERC/Wells before ordering CTPA — avoid low-yield imaging
😴AHI ≥15 or ≥5 with symptoms defines OSA requiring treatment
🔬ILD diagnosis requires HRCT ± bronchoscopy ± surgical biopsy
💨 Obstructive 📉 Restrictive 🔬 ILD 🦠 Infections 🩸 VTE 🫧 Pleural 🎗️ Lung Cancer 😴 Sleep 🏥 Critical Care 📊 PFTs

1. Asthma Very Common

Definition & Pathophysiology

  • Chronic airway inflammation with reversible airflow obstruction
  • Bronchial hyperresponsiveness
  • Key cells: Eosinophils, mast cells, Th2 lymphocytes

Diagnosis

  • Spirometry: FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70 with ≥12% AND ≥200 mL improvement post-bronchodilator
  • Methacholine challenge: PC20 <4 mg/mL (positive if FEV1 drops ≥20%)
  • Peak flow variability: >20% diurnal variation
  • FeNO: >50 ppb suggests eosinophilic inflammation

GINA Classification (2023)

SeverityDaytime SymptomsNight SymptomsFEV1
Intermittent≤2 days/week≤2×/month>80%
Mild Persistent>2 days/week3-4×/month≥80%
Moderate PersistentDaily>1×/week60-80%
Severe PersistentThroughout dayOften nightly<60%

Treatment Stepwise Approach

StepControllerReliever
1PRN low-dose ICS-formoterolPRN ICS-formoterol
2Daily low-dose ICS OR PRN ICS-formoterolPRN SABA or ICS-formoterol
3Low-dose ICS-LABAPRN ICS-formoterol preferred
4Medium-dose ICS-LABAPRN ICS-formoterol
5High-dose ICS-LABA + add-on (LAMA, biologic)PRN ICS-formoterol
GINA 2023: PRN ICS-formoterol (SMART therapy) now preferred over SABA-only as reliever at ALL steps. Reduces exacerbations.

Biologics for Severe Asthma

DrugTargetIndication
OmalizumabIgEAllergic asthma, elevated IgE
Mepolizumab, BenralizumabIL-5/IL-5REosinophilic asthma (≥150-300)
DupilumabIL-4/IL-13Eosinophilic or FeNO ≥25
TezepelumabTSLPSevere asthma (any phenotype)

2. Acute Asthma Exacerbation Emergency

Severity Assessment

FeatureMild-ModerateSevereLife-Threatening
Talks inSentencesWordsCannot speak
RRIncreased>30Exhaustion
HR100-120>120Bradycardia
SpO290-95%<90%<90%
PEF>50%≤50%<25%
PaCO2<42Normal (concerning)Elevated
Normal or rising PaCO2 in acute asthma = impending respiratory failure. Patient is tiring and cannot maintain hyperventilation.

Treatment

  • Oxygen: Target SpO2 93-95%
  • SABA: Albuterol nebulizer Q20min × 3, then Q1-4h
  • Ipratropium: Add in severe exacerbations
  • Systemic corticosteroids: Prednisone 40-60mg daily × 5-7 days (or IV methylprednisolone)
  • Magnesium sulfate: 2g IV over 20 min (severe, not responding)
  • Consider: BiPAP, heliox, intubation if deteriorating
Discharge criteria: PEF >70% predicted, sustained on Q4h bronchodilators, SpO2 >94% on room air.

3. COPD Very Common

Definition

  • Chronic airflow limitation that is NOT fully reversible
  • FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator
  • Emphysema + chronic bronchitis phenotypes

GOLD Spirometric Classification

StageFEV1 (% predicted)
GOLD 1 (Mild)≥80%
GOLD 2 (Moderate)50-79%
GOLD 3 (Severe)30-49%
GOLD 4 (Very Severe)<30%

GOLD 2024 ABE Assessment

GroupExacerbationsmMRC/CATInitial Treatment
A0-1 (no hospitalization)mMRC 0-1 or CAT <10Bronchodilator
B0-1 (no hospitalization)mMRC ≥2 or CAT ≥10LABA + LAMA
E≥2 moderate OR ≥1 hospitalizedAnyLABA + LAMA (+ ICS if eos ≥300)
GOLD 2024: ICS added to LABA/LAMA if blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL and continued exacerbations. Avoid ICS if eosinophils <100.

Interventions That Improve Survival

"STOP" dying from COPD:
Smoking cessation
Treatment with oxygen (if hypoxemic)
Operation (lung volume reduction in select patients)
Pulmonic rehab (reduces mortality in some studies)

Oxygen Therapy

  • Indications: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg OR SpO2 ≤88% at rest
  • PaO2 56-59 with cor pulmonale, polycythemia, or pulmonary HTN
  • Use ≥15 hours/day for mortality benefit

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency

  • Test ALL symptomatic patients with COPD, especially if <45 years, basilar emphysema, or family history
  • PiZZ genotype: Severe deficiency (<50 mg/dL)
  • Treatment: IV augmentation therapy if FEV1 35-65%

4. COPD Exacerbation Emergency

Definition

Acute worsening of dyspnea, cough, and/or sputum beyond normal day-to-day variation

Triggers

  • Infectious (70%): Viral (rhinovirus, influenza), bacterial (H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis, S. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa in severe COPD)
  • Non-infectious: Pollution, weather, PE, CHF, non-adherence

Treatment

InterventionDetails
BronchodilatorsSABA ± ipratropium
Systemic steroidsPrednisone 40mg × 5 days
AntibioticsIf increased sputum purulence + dyspnea or volume; or if requiring mechanical ventilation
OxygenTarget SpO2 88-92% (avoid suppressing hypoxic drive)
NIV (BiPAP)pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45; reduces intubation and mortality
BiPAP in AECOPD: Reduces mortality, intubation, and hospital stay. Use early when pH <7.35 with hypercapnia.

Antibiotic Selection

SeverityFirst-LineAlternative
Mild-ModerateAmoxicillin-clavulanate, doxycycline, azithromycinRespiratory fluoroquinolone
Severe/Pseudomonas riskRespiratory FQ or anti-pseudomonal β-lactam—

5. Bronchiectasis High-Yield

Definition

Permanent airway dilation from chronic inflammation/infection

Etiologies

CategoryCauses
Post-infectiousTB, NTM, severe pneumonia (most common worldwide)
GeneticCystic fibrosis, primary ciliary dyskinesia, alpha-1 AT
ImmuneCVID, HIV, hypogammaglobulinemia
AspirationRecurrent aspiration
AutoimmuneRA, Sjögren's, IBD
ABPACentral bronchiectasis, Aspergillus sensitization

Diagnosis

  • CT chest: Bronchial dilation (internal diameter > adjacent vessel = "signet ring sign")
  • Lack of normal bronchial tapering
  • Bronchi visible within 1 cm of pleura

Workup for Etiology

  • Immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM)
  • Aspergillus IgE, total IgE (ABPA screen)
  • CF sweat chloride or genetics if <40 years
  • Sputum culture (including AFB, fungal)
  • Consider: Ciliary biopsy, alpha-1 AT, autoimmune serologies

Management

  • Airway clearance: Chest physiotherapy, oscillating devices, hypertonic saline nebulizer
  • Chronic macrolide: Azithromycin 3×/week if ≥3 exacerbations/year (reduces exacerbations)
  • Inhaled antibiotics: For chronic Pseudomonas
  • Treat underlying cause

6. Cystic Fibrosis High-Yield

Genetics & Pathophysiology

  • Autosomal recessive — CFTR gene mutation
  • F508del most common mutation (70%)
  • Defective chloride channel → thick secretions

Clinical Manifestations

SystemFeatures
PulmonaryChronic productive cough, bronchiectasis, recurrent infections (Pseudomonas, S. aureus)
GIPancreatic insufficiency (85%), meconium ileus, DIOS, rectal prolapse
HepaticBiliary cirrhosis
EndocrineCF-related diabetes (CFRD)
ReproductiveMale infertility (CBAVD), reduced female fertility
OtherSinusitis, nasal polyps, osteoporosis

Diagnosis

  • Sweat chloride test: ≥60 mEq/L diagnostic
  • CFTR genetic testing: 2 disease-causing mutations
  • Newborn screening (immunoreactive trypsinogen)

CFTR Modulators (Game-Changers)

DrugMechanismIndication
Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta)Corrector + potentiator≥1 F508del mutation (90% of patients)
Ivacaftor (Kalydeco)PotentiatorGating mutations
Trikafta has transformed CF care — dramatically improves FEV1, reduces exacerbations, and improves quality of life. Eligible for ~90% of CF patients.

7. Interstitial Lung Disease — Overview Boards Favorite

Classification

CategoryExamples
Known causeDrug-induced, CTD-ILD, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, pneumoconioses
Idiopathic interstitial pneumoniasIPF, NSIP, COP, AIP, DIP, RB-ILD, LIP
GranulomatousSarcoidosis, HP
OtherLAM, pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, eosinophilic pneumonia

Clinical Features

  • Progressive dyspnea on exertion
  • Dry cough
  • Exam: "Velcro" crackles (bibasilar), clubbing (IPF)

PFT Pattern

  • Restrictive: ↓ TLC, ↓ FVC, FEV1/FVC normal or ↑
  • ↓ DLCO: Often reduced out of proportion

HRCT Patterns

PatternFeaturesAssociations
UIPBasal, peripheral reticular + honeycombing + traction bronchiectasisIPF, CTD-ILD, drug, asbestosis
NSIPBasal ground-glass + reticular, subpleural sparingCTD-ILD, drug, idiopathic
OPPatchy consolidation, peribronchovascular, migratoryCOP, infection, drug
HPUpper/mid zone ground-glass, centrilobular nodules, mosaicBird, mold exposure

8. Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis High-Yield

Definition

  • Chronic, progressive fibrosing ILD of unknown cause
  • UIP pattern on HRCT or biopsy
  • Exclusion of known causes

Risk Factors

  • Age >60 (rare <50)
  • Male sex
  • Smoking history
  • GERD

Diagnosis

  • HRCT UIP pattern: Diagnostic if definite UIP + no identifiable cause
  • UIP features: Basal/peripheral predominance, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticular abnormality, absence of inconsistent features
  • Surgical lung biopsy: If HRCT indeterminate and would change management
Definite UIP on HRCT in appropriate clinical context = IPF diagnosis without biopsy. "Probable UIP" may also suffice with multidisciplinary discussion.

Treatment

DrugMechanismBenefit
PirfenidoneAntifibrotic (TGF-β inhibition)Slows FVC decline
NintedanibTyrosine kinase inhibitorSlows FVC decline
  • Lung transplantation: Only curative option
  • Supplemental O2: For hypoxemia
  • Pulmonary rehabilitation
DO NOT use steroids or immunosuppressants in IPF — PANTHER trial showed harm (prednisone + azathioprine + NAC increased mortality).

Prognosis

  • Median survival 3-5 years from diagnosis
  • Acute exacerbations carry high mortality (>50%)

9. Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis Boards Favorite

Definition

Immune-mediated ILD from inhalation of organic antigens

Common Causes

DiseaseAntigenExposure
Bird fancier's lungAvian proteinsPigeons, parakeets, chickens
Farmer's lungThermophilic actinomycetesMoldy hay
Hot tub lungM. avium complexHot tubs, showers
Humidifier lungVariousContaminated HVAC

Classification

TypeTimingFeatures
Acute4-8h post-exposureFlu-like symptoms, dyspnea, fever, cough; resolves with avoidance
Chronic (fibrotic)InsidiousProgressive dyspnea, fibrosis on CT, may be irreversible

Diagnosis

  • History: Exposure identification is key!
  • HRCT: Upper/mid zone ground-glass, centrilobular nodules, mosaic attenuation, air trapping
  • BAL: Lymphocytosis (>30%), low CD4:CD8 ratio
  • Serum precipitins: Supportive but not diagnostic

Treatment

  • Antigen avoidance: Most important!
  • Corticosteroids: For acute/subacute symptomatic disease
  • Antifibrotics: Consider for progressive fibrotic HP
Chronic fibrotic HP can look like IPF on CT (honeycombing, fibrosis). Key difference: HP has upper/mid distribution, air trapping, and often identifiable exposure.

10. Sarcoidosis Common

Overview

  • Multisystem granulomatous disease of unknown cause
  • Non-caseating granulomas
  • Peak: 25-35 years; higher incidence in Black and Scandinavian populations

Organ Involvement

OrganManifestations
Lung (>90%)Bilateral hilar adenopathy, parenchymal infiltrates, fibrosis
SkinErythema nodosum (good prognosis), lupus pernio (bad prognosis)
EyeUveitis (anterior > posterior)
HeartAV block, arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy
NeuroCN VII palsy (most common), meningitis, mass lesions
OtherHypercalcemia, hepatosplenomegaly, arthritis

Chest Radiograph Staging

StageFindingsSpontaneous Remission
0Normal—
IBilateral hilar adenopathy55-90%
IIAdenopathy + parenchymal infiltrates40-70%
IIIParenchymal infiltrates only10-20%
IVFibrosis0%

Diagnosis

  • Clinical + radiographic findings
  • Tissue biopsy: Non-caseating granulomas (exclude infection, malignancy)
  • ACE level: Elevated in 60% but neither sensitive nor specific

Treatment

  • Observation: Many patients remit spontaneously (Stage I)
  • Corticosteroids: First-line when treatment needed
  • Steroid-sparing: Methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate
  • Anti-TNF: Infliximab for refractory disease
Löfgren syndrome: Bilateral hilar adenopathy + erythema nodosum + arthralgias ± fever. Good prognosis, often self-limited.

11. Pneumoconioses Boards Favorite

DiseaseExposureImagingAssociations
AsbestosisShipyard, insulation, constructionLower lobe fibrosis, pleural plaquesMesothelioma, lung cancer (synergistic with smoking)
SilicosisMining, sandblasting, quarryUpper lobe nodules, eggshell calcification of hilar nodesTB risk increased
Coal worker'sCoal miningUpper lobe nodules, PMFCaplan syndrome (RA + nodules)
BerylliosisAerospace, electronicsLooks like sarcoidosisBeLPT for diagnosis
Pleural plaques = asbestos exposure marker. Eggshell calcification of hilar nodes = silicosis. Upper lobe = silicosis/coal; lower lobe = asbestosis.

12. Pulmonary Embolism Emergency

Clinical Presentation

  • Dyspnea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, cough, hemoptysis
  • Tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxemia
  • Signs of DVT (unilateral leg swelling)

Pretest Probability — Wells Score for PE

CriteriaPoints
Clinical signs of DVT3
PE most likely diagnosis3
Heart rate >1001.5
Immobilization or surgery in past 4 weeks1.5
Previous DVT/PE1.5
Hemoptysis1
Malignancy1

Low: <2; Moderate: 2-6; High: >6

Diagnostic Approach

  • Low pretest: D-dimer → if negative, PE excluded
  • High pretest or positive D-dimer: CT pulmonary angiography
  • If CTPA contraindicated: V/Q scan, lower extremity ultrasound

Risk Stratification

CategoryFeaturesMortality
Massive (High-risk)Hemodynamic instability (SBP <90, shock)25-50%
Submassive (Intermediate)RV dysfunction (echo/CT) or elevated troponin/BNP3-15%
Low-riskHemodynamically stable, no RV dysfunction<1%

Treatment

SeverityTreatment
Low-riskAnticoagulation (DOAC preferred), consider outpatient
SubmassiveAnticoagulation, close monitoring, consider escalation if deteriorating
MassiveSystemic thrombolysis (tPA) or catheter-directed therapy + anticoagulation
Anticoagulation duration:
• Provoked (surgery, immobilization): 3 months
• Unprovoked: ≥3 months, then reassess for indefinite
• Cancer-associated: DOAC or LMWH, continue while cancer active
Thrombolysis contraindications: Recent surgery/trauma, active bleeding, hemorrhagic stroke, ischemic stroke <3 months, intracranial malignancy.

13. Pulmonary Hypertension High-Yield

Definition (2022 ESC/ERS)

mPAP >20 mmHg at rest by right heart catheterization

WHO Classification

GroupCategoryExamples
1Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)Idiopathic, heritable, CTD, drugs, HIV, porto-pulmonary
2Left heart diseaseHFrEF, HFpEF, valvular disease (MOST COMMON)
3Lung disease/hypoxiaCOPD, ILD, OSA, altitude
4Chronic thromboembolic (CTEPH)Unresolved PE
5MultifactorialSarcoidosis, sickle cell, metabolic

Hemodynamic Definitions

TypemPAPPAWPPVR
Pre-capillary (Groups 1,3,4,5)>20≤15≥3 WU
Post-capillary (Group 2)>20>15<3 WU
Combined>20>15≥3 WU

Workup

  • Echo (screen), BNP
  • PFTs, HRCT (Group 3)
  • V/Q scan (rule out CTEPH — Group 4)
  • CTD serologies, HIV, LFTs
  • Right heart catheterization: Required for diagnosis and treatment decisions

Treatment of PAH (Group 1)

  • General: Diuretics, oxygen, anticoagulation (selected patients)
  • Vasodilator testing: If positive → CCB (rare)
  • Combination therapy: ERA + PDE5i or ERA + prostacyclin analog
ClassExamples
Endothelin receptor antagonists (ERA)Ambrisentan, bosentan, macitentan
PDE-5 inhibitorsSildenafil, tadalafil
sGC stimulatorRiociguat
Prostacyclin pathwayEpoprostenol (IV), treprostinil, selexipag
CTEPH (Group 4) is potentially curable with pulmonary thromboendarterectomy (PTE). Always screen with V/Q scan — CTPA may miss it.

14. Pleural Effusion Common

Light's Criteria (Exudate if ANY)


1. Pleural protein / Serum protein > 0.5
2. Pleural LDH / Serum LDH > 0.6
3. Pleural LDH > 2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH

Transudates

  • Heart failure (most common)
  • Cirrhosis (hepatic hydrothorax)
  • Nephrotic syndrome
  • Hypoalbuminemia

Exudates

CauseClues
Parapneumonic/empyemapH <7.2, glucose <60, positive culture/Gram stain
MalignancyBloody, low glucose, positive cytology
TBLymphocytic, low glucose, elevated ADA (>40)
RheumatoidVery low glucose (<30), low pH, high LDH
LupusANA positive in fluid
PancreatitisElevated amylase (left-sided)
Esophageal ruptureVery high amylase (salivary), low pH
ChylothoraxMilky, triglycerides >110 mg/dL

When to Drain (Complicated Parapneumonic/Empyema)

  • Pus or positive Gram stain/culture
  • pH <7.2
  • Glucose <60 mg/dL
  • Loculated effusion
If Light's criteria suggest exudate but clinical picture is transudate (e.g., diuresed CHF), check serum-pleural albumin gradient (>1.2 g/dL = transudate).

15. Pneumothorax Emergency

Types

TypeCause
Primary spontaneousRupture of apical blebs; tall, thin young males, smokers
Secondary spontaneousUnderlying lung disease (COPD, CF, Pneumocystis, LAM)
TraumaticBlunt/penetrating trauma, iatrogenic (central line, thoracentesis)
TensionOne-way valve mechanism → hemodynamic collapse

Tension Pneumothorax

  • Clinical diagnosis: Hypotension, JVD, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds
  • Treatment: Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS, MCL) → chest tube
Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis. Do NOT wait for imaging. Needle decompression, then chest tube.

Management

ScenarioTreatment
Small primary, stableObservation, supplemental O2 (accelerates reabsorption)
Large primary or symptomaticAspiration or small-bore chest tube
Secondary pneumothoraxChest tube (higher complication risk)
TensionNeedle decompression → chest tube
RecurrentPleurodesis or VATS

16. ARDS Emergency

Berlin Definition (2012)

CriteriaRequirement
TimingWithin 1 week of insult or new/worsening symptoms
ImagingBilateral opacities not fully explained by effusion, collapse, nodules
OriginNot fully explained by cardiac failure/fluid overload
OxygenationPaO2/FiO2 ≤300 with PEEP ≥5 cm H2O

Severity

SeverityP/F RatioMortality
Mild200-30027%
Moderate100-20032%
Severe<10045%

Evidence-Based Management

InterventionEvidence
Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW)ARDSNet — reduces mortality
Plateau pressure <30 cm H2OLung-protective ventilation
Conservative fluid managementFACTT — improves oxygenation, ventilator-free days
Prone positioning (if P/F <150)PROSEVA — reduces mortality in severe ARDS
Neuromuscular blockade (early severe)ACURASYS — may reduce mortality
ECMO (refractory)EOLIA — consider in severe refractory ARDS
Lung-protective ventilation: Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, plateau <30, permissive hypercapnia acceptable. Proved mortality benefit.

17. Mechanical Ventilation Basics High-Yield

Modes

ModeDescriptionUse
Volume Control (AC/VC)Set Vt, rate; variable pressureMost common initial mode
Pressure Control (PC)Set pressure, rate; variable VtHigh airway pressures, comfort
Pressure Support (PS)Patient-triggered, set pressureWeaning, spontaneous trials
SIMVGuaranteed rate + spontaneous breathsWeaning (less used now)

Initial Settings

  • Tidal volume: 6-8 mL/kg IBW (6 in ARDS)
  • Rate: 12-16 (adjust for pH/PaCO2)
  • FiO2: Start 100%, wean to SpO2 92-96%
  • PEEP: 5-8 cm H2O (higher in ARDS)

Troubleshooting High Airway Pressures

FindingCauseExamples
↑ Peak AND ↑ PlateauLow complianceARDS, pulmonary edema, atelectasis, pneumothorax
↑ Peak, normal PlateauHigh resistanceBronchospasm, mucus plug, kinked tube

Liberation from Ventilation

  • Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT): PS 5-8/PEEP 5 or T-piece × 30-120 min
  • RSBI: RR/Vt (L) — <105 predicts success
  • Passed SBT: Proceed to extubation if cough, mental status, secretions adequate

18. Obstructive Sleep Apnea Very Common

Definition

  • AHI ≥5 with symptoms OR AHI ≥15 regardless of symptoms
  • AHI = apneas + hypopneas per hour of sleep

Severity

SeverityAHI
Mild5-14
Moderate15-29
Severe≥30

Clinical Features

  • Snoring, witnessed apneas
  • Excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale)
  • Morning headaches, nocturia
  • Associated: HTN (especially resistant), A-fib, stroke, metabolic syndrome

Diagnosis

  • Polysomnography (PSG): Gold standard
  • Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT): For high pretest probability, no significant comorbidities

Treatment

  • CPAP: First-line for moderate-severe OSA
  • Weight loss: Significant improvement
  • Oral appliance: Mild-moderate OSA or CPAP intolerant
  • Surgery: Selected cases (UPPP, maxillomandibular advancement)
  • Hypoglossal nerve stimulation: CPAP-intolerant with moderate-severe OSA
All patients with resistant hypertension should be screened for OSA. Treatment of OSA modestly reduces BP.

19. Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome

Definition

  • BMI ≥30 kg/m²
  • Awake daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 >45 mmHg)
  • Exclusion of other causes of hypoventilation
  • 90% have concurrent OSA

Treatment

  • CPAP: If concurrent OSA without severe hypercapnia
  • BiPAP: If significant hypercapnia or CPAP failure
  • Weight loss: Most effective long-term

20. Lung Cancer Common

Types

Type%LocationFeatures
Adenocarcinoma40%PeripheralMost common; non-smokers; EGFR, ALK, ROS1 mutations
Squamous cell25%CentralCavitation; hypercalcemia (PTHrP)
Small cell (SCLC)15%CentralAggressive; paraneoplastic syndromes; SIADH, Cushing's, Lambert-Eaton
Large cell10%PeripheralPoorly differentiated

Screening (USPSTF 2021)

  • Age 50-80 years
  • ≥20 pack-year smoking history
  • Current smoker or quit within past 15 years
  • Annual low-dose CT

Staging (NSCLC)

  • Stage I-II: Surgical resection (curative intent)
  • Stage III: Chemoradiation ± surgery
  • Stage IV: Systemic therapy (targeted therapy if driver mutation, immunotherapy, chemo)

SCLC

  • Limited stage: Confined to one hemithorax/radiation field → chemoradiation, prophylactic cranial irradiation
  • Extensive stage: Chemo + immunotherapy (atezolizumab)

Paraneoplastic Syndromes

SyndromeTumor TypeMechanism
HypercalcemiaSquamous cellPTHrP
SIADHSCLCADH secretion
Cushing'sSCLCEctopic ACTH
Lambert-EatonSCLCAnti-VGCC antibodies
Hypertrophic osteoarthropathyNSCLCClubbing, periostitis

21. Solitary Pulmonary Nodule High-Yield

Definition

Round lesion ≤3 cm, surrounded by lung parenchyma, no adenopathy/atelectasis

Benign vs Malignant Features

BenignMalignant
Stable ≥2 yearsNew or growing
<6 mm, smooth borders>8 mm, spiculated
Central/popcorn calcificationEccentric calcification
Fat density (hamartoma)Part-solid (ground-glass component)

Management (Fleischner Society)

  • <6 mm solid: No routine follow-up (low risk); optional 12-month CT (high risk)
  • 6-8 mm solid: CT at 6-12 months; consider 18-24 month follow-up
  • >8 mm solid: CT at 3 months, PET, and/or biopsy based on probability
  • Ground-glass (pure): CT at 6-12 months; if persistent, annual follow-up × 5 years
  • Part-solid: Higher malignancy risk — CT at 3-6 months, then annual × 5 years
Ground-glass nodules (especially part-solid) have higher malignancy risk than solid nodules of the same size. Follow longer.

22. Pulmonary Function Tests High-Yield

Spirometry Patterns

PatternFEV1FVCFEV1/FVCExamples
Obstructive↓↓↓ or N<0.70Asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis
Restrictive↓↓↓N or ↑ILD, chest wall disease, neuromuscular
Mixed↓↓↓↓<0.70Combined disease

Lung Volumes

FindingObstructiveRestrictive
TLC↑ (air trapping)↓
RV↑↑↓
RV/TLC↑N or ↓

DLCO Interpretation

DLCOCauses
DecreasedILD, emphysema, pulmonary vascular disease (PE, PAH), anemia
IncreasedPolycythemia, pulmonary hemorrhage, asthma, left-to-right shunt, obesity
Low DLCO: "PEACE" — Pulmonary vascular, Emphysema, Anemia, Collagen vascular/ILD, Emboli

Bronchodilator Response

  • Positive: ≥12% AND ≥200 mL increase in FEV1 or FVC
  • Suggests reversible airflow obstruction (asthma)

23. ABG Interpretation High-Yield

Systematic Approach

  1. Acidemia or alkalemia? pH <7.35 = acidemia; pH >7.45 = alkalemia
  2. Primary disorder? Check PaCO2 (respiratory) and HCO3 (metabolic)
  3. Compensation appropriate? Calculate expected compensation
  4. If metabolic acidosis: Calculate anion gap
  5. If elevated AG: Calculate delta-delta ratio

Compensation Formulas


Metabolic acidosis: Expected PaCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 (±2) [Winter's]
Metabolic alkalosis: Expected PaCO2 = 0.7 × HCO3 + 21 (±2)
Respiratory acidosis (acute): HCO3 ↑ 1 per 10 mmHg ↑ PaCO2
Respiratory acidosis (chronic): HCO3 ↑ 3.5 per 10 mmHg ↑ PaCO2
Respiratory alkalosis (acute): HCO3 ↓ 2 per 10 mmHg ↓ PaCO2
Respiratory alkalosis (chronic): HCO3 ↓ 5 per 10 mmHg ↓ PaCO2

Anion Gap


AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
Normal = 8-12 mEq/L

Delta-Delta Ratio


ΔAG / ΔHCO3 = (AG - 12) / (24 - HCO3)
<1 = concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis
1-2 = pure AG metabolic acidosis
>2 = concurrent metabolic alkalosis

24. Community-Acquired Pneumonia Very Common

Common Pathogens

  • Typical: S. pneumoniae (most common), H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis
  • Atypical: Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella
  • Viral: Influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2

Severity Assessment — CURB-65

CriteriaPoints
Confusion1
Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >20)1
Respiratory rate ≥301
Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)1
Age ≥651

0-1: Outpatient; 2: Consider admission; ≥3: ICU consideration

Treatment (ATS/IDSA 2019)

SettingRegimen
Outpatient, healthyAmoxicillin OR doxycycline OR macrolide (if local resistance <25%)
Outpatient, comorbiditiesAmoxicillin-clavulanate + macrolide OR respiratory FQ
Inpatient, non-severeβ-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory FQ
Inpatient, severe/ICUβ-lactam + macrolide (or FQ); add vanc/linezolid if MRSA risk

25. Tuberculosis High-Yield

Latent vs Active TB

FeatureLatent TB (LTBI)Active TB
SymptomsNoneCough, fever, night sweats, weight loss
CXRNormalAbnormal (cavities, infiltrates)
InfectiousNoYes
TST/IGRAPositiveUsually positive

Diagnosis of Active TB

  • Sputum AFB smear × 3
  • Sputum culture (gold standard)
  • Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT)
  • CXR: Upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, miliary pattern

Treatment of Active TB

"RIPE": Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
2 months intensive (RIPE) → 4 months continuation (RI)

LTBI Treatment

  • 3HP: INH + rifapentine weekly × 12 weeks (preferred)
  • 4R: Rifampin daily × 4 months
  • 9H: INH daily × 9 months

Drug Toxicities

DrugToxicity
IsoniazidHepatitis, peripheral neuropathy (give B6), drug-induced lupus
RifampinHepatitis, orange secretions, drug interactions (CYP450 inducer)
PyrazinamideHepatitis, hyperuricemia
EthambutolOptic neuritis (check visual acuity)

45. High-Yield Board Pearls

Obstructive Disease

  • 💎 FEV1/FVC <0.70 = obstruction; REVERSIBILITY (≥12% + 200mL) suggests asthma
  • 💎 Normal or rising PaCO2 in acute asthma = impending respiratory failure
  • 💎 GOLD 2024: ICS in COPD if eosinophils ≥300; avoid if <100
  • 💎 Only smoking cessation and O2 (if hypoxemic) proven to improve COPD survival

Interstitial Lung Disease

  • 💎 UIP pattern = honeycombing, basal/peripheral, traction bronchiectasis
  • 💎 NO steroids in IPF — PANTHER trial showed harm
  • 💎 Chronic HP can mimic IPF; look for upper lobe involvement, exposure history
  • 💎 Pleural plaques = asbestos; eggshell nodes = silicosis

Pulmonary Vascular

  • 💎 V/Q scan is screening test for CTEPH (CTPA may miss it)
  • 💎 Right heart cath required for PH diagnosis and treatment decisions
  • 💎 Massive PE = hemodynamic instability → thrombolysis

Critical Care

  • 💎 ARDS: Low Vt (6 mL/kg IBW), plateau <30, prone if P/F <150
  • 💎 BiPAP in AECOPD reduces intubation and mortality
  • 💎 Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD (avoid suppressing hypoxic drive)

Pleural Disease

  • 💎 Light's criteria: any 1 of 3 = exudate
  • 💎 pH <7.2 in parapneumonic effusion = drain
  • 💎 Tension pneumothorax = clinical diagnosis, immediate decompression

Lung Cancer

  • 💎 Screening: Age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current or quit <15 years → annual LDCT
  • 💎 SCLC: Central, aggressive, paraneoplastic (SIADH, Cushing's, Lambert-Eaton)
  • 💎 Squamous: Central, cavitates, hypercalcemia (PTHrP)

🫁 ABIM Pulmonology Study Guide

ATS • GOLD • CHEST Guidelines

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide 🦠 ID Guide ❤️ Cardio 🔬 Endo
↑ Back to top

🩸 Hematology/Oncology

ABIM Board Review — ASH/ASCO/NCCN Guidelines

🎯 Core Heme/Onc Principles

🔬Start anemia workup with MCV — morphology directs next steps
💉Transfuse at Hgb <7 in most; <8 for ACS or symptomatic anemia
🩸DOACs preferred over warfarin for VTE (except mechanical valve, APS)
👀Watch and wait for asymptomatic low-grade lymphoma/CLL
🧬Hypercoagulability workup not indicated for provoked VTE
🚨Oncologic emergencies: cord compression, TLS, febrile neutropenia — act immediately
🔴 Anemia 🩹 Bleeding 🩸 Thrombosis 🔬 WBC Disorders 🟣 Lymphoma 🧬 Leukemia 🦴 Myeloma 📈 MPN 💉 Transfusion 🚨 Onc Emergencies

1. Iron Deficiency Anemia Very Common

Etiology

  • Blood loss: GI (most common in men/postmenopausal women), menstrual (premenopausal women)
  • Decreased absorption: Celiac, gastric bypass, H. pylori, PPI use
  • Increased demand: Pregnancy, growth
  • Dietary: Rare in developed countries

Laboratory Findings

TestFinding
MCV↓ (microcytic)
Ferritin↓ (most specific)
Serum iron↓
TIBC↑
Transferrin saturation↓ (<20%)
RDW↑
Peripheral smearMicrocytic, hypochromic, target cells
Ferritin is an acute phase reactant. May be falsely normal/elevated in inflammation. If ferritin 30-100 ng/mL with inflammation, still consider iron deficiency.

Workup

  • Men and postmenopausal women: GI evaluation mandatory (upper and lower endoscopy)
  • Premenopausal women: GI workup if heavy menses don't explain, or if >50 or GI symptoms
  • Celiac serology if malabsorption suspected

Treatment

  • Oral iron: Ferrous sulfate 325mg (65mg elemental) daily-TID; take on empty stomach with vitamin C
  • IV iron: If intolerant, malabsorption, ongoing blood loss, or need rapid repletion
  • Reticulocyte response in 1 week; Hgb increase in 2-4 weeks
  • Continue 3-6 months after Hgb normalizes to replete stores

2. Anemia of Chronic Disease Common

Pathophysiology

  • Inflammatory cytokines → increased hepcidin
  • Hepcidin blocks iron release from macrophages and absorption
  • Functional iron deficiency despite adequate stores

Laboratory Findings

TestACDIron DeficiencyACD + Iron Def
Ferritin↑ or normal↓Low-normal
Serum iron↓↓↓
TIBC↓ or normal↑↓ or normal
Transferrin sat↓↓↓

Treatment

  • Treat underlying condition
  • ESAs (erythropoietin) in CKD, cancer-related anemia
  • IV iron if concurrent iron deficiency
  • Transfusion if symptomatic

3. Macrocytic Anemia High-Yield

Causes

MegaloblasticNon-Megaloblastic
B12 deficiencyAlcoholism
Folate deficiencyLiver disease
Drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea)Hypothyroidism
MDS
Reticulocytosis

B12 Deficiency

Causes

  • Pernicious anemia: Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (most specific), anti-parietal cell Ab
  • Gastric surgery, atrophic gastritis
  • Ileal resection or disease (Crohn's)
  • Metformin (long-term use)
  • Strict veganism

Clinical Features

  • Hematologic: Megaloblastic anemia, pancytopenia, hypersegmented neutrophils
  • Neurologic: Subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts), peripheral neuropathy, cognitive changes
  • Glossitis
Neurologic symptoms can occur without anemia. Low-normal B12 (200-400 pg/mL) with symptoms → check methylmalonic acid (elevated in B12 deficiency, normal in folate deficiency).

Folate Deficiency

  • Causes: Dietary (alcoholism), malabsorption, increased demand (pregnancy, hemolysis), drugs (phenytoin, TMP-SMX)
  • No neurologic symptoms (unlike B12)
  • Elevated homocysteine; normal MMA
Never give folate alone if B12 status unknown! Folate can correct anemia but worsen neurologic damage in B12 deficiency.

4. Hemolytic Anemia — Overview High-Yield

Laboratory Findings

TestFinding
Reticulocyte count↑ (appropriately responding marrow)
LDH↑
Indirect bilirubin↑
Haptoglobin↓ (binds free Hgb)

Classification

Intrinsic (RBC defect)Extrinsic (external cause)
Membrane: Hereditary spherocytosis, elliptocytosisImmune: Warm/cold AIHA
Enzyme: G6PD, pyruvate kinaseMicroangiopathic: TTP, HUS, DIC
Hemoglobin: Sickle cell, thalassemiaMechanical: Prosthetic valve
PNHInfections: Malaria, Babesia, Clostridium

Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT/Coombs)

  • Positive: Immune-mediated hemolysis (AIHA, drug-induced, transfusion reaction)
  • Negative: Non-immune (G6PD, TTP, PNH, hereditary spherocytosis)

5. Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia Boards Favorite

Warm AIHA

  • Antibody: IgG (binds at 37°C)
  • Site: Extravascular (spleen)
  • Smear: Spherocytes
  • Causes: Idiopathic, CLL, SLE, drugs (methyldopa, penicillin, cephalosporins)
  • Treatment: Corticosteroids (first-line), rituximab, splenectomy

Cold Agglutinin Disease

  • Antibody: IgM (binds at <37°C)
  • Site: Complement-mediated, intravascular
  • Causes: Mycoplasma, EBV, lymphoproliferative disorders
  • Treatment: Avoid cold, rituximab (steroids less effective)
Drug-induced AIHA: Methyldopa causes warm AIHA via drug-induced autoantibodies. Penicillin causes hapten-mediated hemolysis (drug binds to RBC).

6. G6PD Deficiency Boards Favorite

Overview

  • X-linked recessive
  • Most common enzyme deficiency worldwide
  • Episodic hemolysis triggered by oxidative stress

Triggers

"Fava beans and drugs that cause havoc":
Fava beans, Sulfonamides, Dapsone, Primaquine, Nitrofurantoin, Rasburicase, Infections

Clinical Features

  • Acute hemolytic crisis 1-3 days after exposure
  • Jaundice, dark urine, back pain
  • Smear: Bite cells, Heinz bodies (require special stain)

Diagnosis

  • G6PD enzyme assay: Check when NOT in acute hemolysis (young RBCs have higher G6PD levels)

Treatment

  • Remove/avoid trigger
  • Supportive care, transfusion if severe
  • Self-limited once trigger removed

7. Sickle Cell Disease High-Yield

Pathophysiology

  • HbS (βS mutation) → polymerization under hypoxia/stress → sickling
  • Hemolysis + vaso-occlusion

Acute Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Vaso-occlusive crisisPain in bones, chest, abdomenIV fluids, opioids, incentive spirometry
Acute chest syndromeFever + new infiltrate + respiratory symptomsAntibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin), exchange transfusion if severe
Stroke10% of children; ischemic > hemorrhagicExchange transfusion, chronic transfusion program
Aplastic crisisParvovirus B19 → sudden ↓ Hgb, ↓ reticTransfusion, usually self-limited
Splenic sequestrationRapid splenic enlargement, ↓ Hgb (children)Transfusion, splenectomy if recurrent
PriapismProlonged painful erectionHydration, analgesia, aspiration, exchange transfusion
Acute chest syndrome is the leading cause of death in sickle cell. Consider if fever, hypoxia, new infiltrate. Triggers: infection, fat embolism, hypoventilation.

Chronic Complications

  • Functional asplenia → infection risk (encapsulated organisms)
  • Pulmonary hypertension
  • Chronic kidney disease
  • Avascular necrosis (femoral head)
  • Retinopathy
  • Cholelithiasis (pigmented stones)

Disease-Modifying Therapies

DrugMechanismIndication
Hydroxyurea↑ HbF productionAll patients with recurrent crises
Voxelotor↑ Hgb-O2 affinity, prevents sicklingHgb-SS
CrizanlizumabAnti-P-selectin, prevents adhesionReduce VOC frequency
L-glutamineReduces oxidative stressReduce VOC

Curative

  • Allogeneic stem cell transplant: Curative in select patients
  • Gene therapy: Emerging (exagamglogene autotemcel approved 2023)

8. Thalassemia Boards Favorite

Overview

  • Decreased production of α or β globin chains
  • Microcytic anemia with normal/elevated iron stores

β-Thalassemia

TypeGenotypeFeatures
Trait (minor)β/β⁺ or β/β⁰Mild microcytic anemia, target cells, ↑ HbA2 (>3.5%)
Intermediaβ⁺/β⁺ or β⁺/β⁰Moderate anemia, splenomegaly, occasional transfusion
Major (Cooley's)β⁰/β⁰Severe anemia, transfusion-dependent, iron overload

α-Thalassemia

DeletionsSyndromeFeatures
1 gene (-α/αα)Silent carrierNormal
2 genes (--/αα or -α/-α)TraitMild microcytic anemia
3 genes (--/-α)HbH diseaseModerate hemolytic anemia, HbH inclusions
4 genes (--/--)Hb Bart'sHydrops fetalis, incompatible with life
Mentzer index: MCV/RBC count. <13 = thalassemia trait; >13 = iron deficiency. Helps differentiate microcytic anemias.

Diagnosis

  • β-thal trait: Hgb electrophoresis shows ↑ HbA2, ↑ HbF
  • α-thal: Normal electrophoresis (need genetic testing or Hb Bart's in newborns)

Treatment

  • Trait: No treatment needed; genetic counseling
  • Major: Chronic transfusions + iron chelation (deferoxamine, deferasirox)
  • Curative: Allogeneic HSCT, gene therapy (betibeglogene autotemcel)

9. Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura Emergency

Pathophysiology

  • ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10% activity)
  • Acquired (autoantibody) or hereditary
  • Uncleaved ultra-large vWF multimers → platelet aggregation → microthrombi

Classic Pentad (rarely complete)

FAT RN: Fever, microangiopathic hemolytic Anemia, Thrombocytopenia, Renal dysfunction, Neurologic changes

Diagnosis

  • Thrombocytopenia + MAHA (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin)
  • ADAMTS13 activity <10% (send before plasma exchange)
  • PLASMIC score: Predicts ADAMTS13 deficiency
Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 results to treat! Untreated TTP has >90% mortality. Start plasma exchange empirically.

Treatment

  • Plasma exchange (PEX): First-line, removes antibody and replenishes ADAMTS13
  • Corticosteroids: Prednisone 1 mg/kg
  • Caplacizumab: Anti-vWF nanobody; accelerates platelet recovery
  • Rituximab: For refractory/relapsing cases
TTP vs HUS: TTP = neuro predominant, ADAMTS13 <10%. HUS = renal predominant, often Shiga toxin (STEC-HUS).

10. Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia Emergency

Types

TypeFeatures
Type 1Non-immune, mild drop in platelets, benign
Type 2 (HIT)Immune-mediated, prothrombotic, clinically significant

HIT (Type 2)

  • Antibodies to PF4-heparin complexes
  • Platelet activation → thrombosis (50% if untreated)
  • Onset: 5-10 days after heparin start (or <24h if prior exposure)
  • Platelet drop ≥50% from baseline

4T Score

Category2 Points1 Point0 Points
Thrombocytopenia>50% drop, nadir ≥2030-50% drop, nadir 10-19<30% drop, nadir <10
TimingDay 5-10 or <1d if prior>10 days or unclear<4 days without prior
ThrombosisNew thrombosisProgressive/recurrentNone
Other causeNo other causePossible other causeDefinite other cause

0-3: Low probability; 4-5: Intermediate; 6-8: High

Diagnosis

  • PF4-heparin ELISA: High sensitivity, lower specificity
  • Serotonin release assay (SRA): Gold standard, high specificity

Management

  • Stop all heparin immediately (including flushes)
  • Start non-heparin anticoagulant: Argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux
  • Do NOT give warfarin until platelets >150 (causes skin necrosis)
  • Transition to DOAC or warfarin when stable
HIT is a prothrombotic state despite low platelets. Thrombosis risk 50% if untreated. Always anticoagulate, never transfuse platelets.

11. Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura Common

Overview

  • Autoantibodies against platelet glycoproteins (GPIIb/IIIa)
  • Isolated thrombocytopenia; normal WBC and Hgb
  • Diagnosis of exclusion

Clinical Features

  • Mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, gingival)
  • No splenomegaly (vs hypersplenism)

Workup

  • CBC, peripheral smear (isolated thrombocytopenia, large platelets)
  • HIV, HCV testing (can cause ITP)
  • H. pylori (treat if positive)
  • Bone marrow biopsy: Only if atypical features or >60 years

Treatment

IndicationTreatment
Plt >30, asymptomaticObservation
Plt <30 or bleedingCorticosteroids (prednisone or dexamethasone)
Severe bleedingIVIG + steroids ± platelet transfusion
RefractoryRituximab, TPO-RA (romiplostim, eltrombopag), splenectomy

12. Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation Emergency

Pathophysiology

  • Systemic activation of coagulation → widespread microvascular thrombosis
  • Consumption of platelets and clotting factors → bleeding

Causes

  • Sepsis (most common)
  • Malignancy (especially APL, adenocarcinoma)
  • Trauma, burns
  • Obstetric complications (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism)
  • Transfusion reactions

Laboratory Findings

TestFinding
Platelets↓
PT/PTT↑
Fibrinogen↓
D-dimer↑↑
SmearSchistocytes

Treatment

  • Treat underlying cause (most important)
  • If bleeding: Platelets (if <50), FFP (if elevated INR), cryoprecipitate (if fibrinogen <100)
  • Anticoagulation: Consider if thrombosis predominant (rare)

13. Hemophilia A & B High-Yield

Overview

TypeDeficiencyInheritance
Hemophilia AFactor VIIIX-linked recessive
Hemophilia B (Christmas)Factor IXX-linked recessive

Clinical Features

  • Deep tissue bleeding: hemarthrosis (hallmark), muscle hematomas, retroperitoneal
  • Delayed bleeding after trauma
  • No petechiae (platelet-type bleeding)

Laboratory Findings

  • PTT: Prolonged
  • PT: Normal
  • Mixing study: Corrects (factor deficiency, not inhibitor)
  • Confirm with factor VIII/IX activity levels

Severity

SeverityFactor LevelBleeding
Mild5-40%After major trauma/surgery
Moderate1-5%After minor trauma
Severe<1%Spontaneous

Treatment

  • Factor replacement: Recombinant factor VIII or IX
  • Desmopressin (DDAVP): For mild hemophilia A (releases vWF and factor VIII)
  • Emicizumab: Bispecific antibody mimicking factor VIII (for hemophilia A prophylaxis)
  • Avoid NSAIDs and IM injections
Up to 30% of severe hemophilia A patients develop factor VIII inhibitors (antibodies). Treat with bypassing agents (factor VIIa, FEIBA).

14. von Willebrand Disease Common

Overview

  • Most common inherited bleeding disorder
  • vWF: Mediates platelet adhesion, carries factor VIII

Types

TypeDefectLabs
Type 1 (75%)Partial quantitative deficiency↓ vWF:Ag, ↓ vWF:RCo
Type 2Qualitative defectVariable; 2A, 2B, 2M, 2N subtypes
Type 3Complete deficiency (severe)Absent vWF, very low factor VIII

Clinical Features

  • Mucocutaneous bleeding (menorrhagia, epistaxis, easy bruising)
  • Bleeding after dental procedures, surgery

Laboratory Findings

  • PTT: May be prolonged (if factor VIII low)
  • Bleeding time: Prolonged (if performed)
  • vWF:Ag (antigen) and vWF:RCo (activity) decreased

Treatment

  • DDAVP: First-line for Type 1 (releases vWF from endothelium)
  • vWF-containing factor concentrates: For Type 2, Type 3, or major bleeding
  • Antifibrinolytics: Tranexamic acid for mucosal bleeding
DDAVP is contraindicated in Type 2B vWD — can worsen thrombocytopenia (mutant vWF binds platelets excessively).

15. Hypercoagulable States High-Yield

Inherited Thrombophilias

ConditionMechanismRisk
Factor V LeidenResistance to activated protein CMost common; 5-7× VTE risk (heterozygous)
Prothrombin G20210AIncreased prothrombin levels3× VTE risk
Protein C/S deficiency↓ Anticoagulant proteinsWarfarin-induced skin necrosis
Antithrombin deficiency↓ Heparin cofactorHeparin resistance

Acquired Thrombophilias

ConditionFeatures
Antiphospholipid syndromeArterial + venous thrombosis, pregnancy loss, lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2GP1
MalignancyUnprovoked VTE; consider cancer screening
PNHVenous thrombosis (unusual sites), hemolysis, cytopenias
Myeloproliferative neoplasmsSplanchnic vein thrombosis, erythromelalgia

When to Test

  • VTE at young age (<45)
  • Recurrent VTE
  • VTE at unusual site (mesenteric, cerebral)
  • Strong family history
  • Warfarin-induced skin necrosis (protein C/S)
Testing during acute VTE or on anticoagulation gives unreliable results. Wait 2-4 weeks after completing anticoagulation.

16. Acute Myeloid Leukemia High-Yield

Overview

  • Clonal expansion of myeloid blasts (≥20% in marrow)
  • Most common acute leukemia in adults

Clinical Features

  • Bone marrow failure: Anemia (fatigue), thrombocytopenia (bleeding), neutropenia (infections)
  • Leukostasis: WBC >100K → pulmonary/CNS symptoms (emergency)
  • DIC: Especially APL
  • Gingival hyperplasia: Monocytic subtypes (M4, M5)
  • Auer rods: Pathognomonic for AML

Key Subtypes

SubtypeGeneticsFeatures
APL (M3)t(15;17) PML-RARADIC, treat with ATRA + arsenic, good prognosis
Core binding factort(8;21), inv(16)Favorable prognosis
FLT3-mutatedFLT3-ITDPoor prognosis, midostaurin
NPM1-mutatedNPM1Favorable (if no FLT3)
APL emergency: t(15;17). High DIC and bleeding risk. Start ATRA immediately on suspicion — don't wait for cytogenetics.

Treatment

  • Induction: "7+3" (cytarabine 7 days + anthracycline 3 days)
  • Consolidation: High-dose cytarabine or allogeneic HSCT
  • Targeted therapies: FLT3 inhibitors (midostaurin), IDH inhibitors, venetoclax

17. Chronic Myeloid Leukemia Boards Favorite

Pathophysiology

  • t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome → BCR-ABL1 fusion
  • Constitutively active tyrosine kinase

Clinical Features

  • Often asymptomatic (incidental leukocytosis)
  • Fatigue, weight loss, night sweats
  • Massive splenomegaly
  • WBC very high (often >100K) with left shift, basophilia

Phases

PhaseFeatures
Chronic<10% blasts, most patients at diagnosis
Accelerated10-19% blasts, basophils >20%, other criteria
Blast crisis≥20% blasts (myeloid or lymphoid)

Diagnosis

  • CBC with differential showing leukocytosis, left shift, basophilia
  • Low LAP score (vs leukemoid reaction)
  • Confirm: BCR-ABL1 by FISH or PCR, cytogenetics for Ph chromosome

Treatment

  • Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs): Imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib
  • Monitor BCR-ABL1 transcripts by PCR
  • Deep molecular response → possible TKI discontinuation
  • Allogeneic HSCT for TKI-resistant or blast crisis
TKIs have transformed CML into a chronic manageable disease. Most patients achieve near-normal life expectancy with treatment.

18. Hodgkin Lymphoma High-Yield

Pathology

  • Reed-Sternberg cells: "Owl-eye" appearance, CD15+/CD30+
  • Bimodal age distribution (20s and >55)

Clinical Features

  • Painless lymphadenopathy (cervical most common)
  • B symptoms: Fever >38°C, night sweats, >10% weight loss in 6 months
  • Alcohol-induced lymph node pain (rare but classic)
  • Contiguous spread (vs NHL)

Subtypes

SubtypeFeatures
Nodular sclerosisMost common (70%), young adults, mediastinal
Mixed cellularityOlder adults, EBV associated
Lymphocyte-richBest prognosis
Lymphocyte-depletedWorst prognosis, HIV-associated

Staging (Ann Arbor)

StageDefinition
ISingle lymph node region
II≥2 regions, same side of diaphragm
IIIBoth sides of diaphragm
IVExtranodal involvement (marrow, liver)

A = no B symptoms; B = B symptoms present

Treatment

  • Limited stage (I-II): ABVD × 2-4 cycles ± radiation
  • Advanced (III-IV): ABVD × 6 cycles or escalated BEACOPP
  • Relapsed: Salvage chemo → autologous HSCT
  • Brentuximab vedotin: Anti-CD30 ADC for relapsed
  • Checkpoint inhibitors: PD-1 inhibitors for refractory
Hodgkin lymphoma is highly curable (>80% overall). Long-term survivors need monitoring for secondary malignancies (breast, lung) and cardiac disease from treatment.

19. Multiple Myeloma High-Yield

Overview

  • Clonal plasma cell neoplasm producing monoclonal protein
  • Median age 70 years; more common in Black patients

Clinical Features — CRAB

Calcium elevation (>11)
Renal insufficiency (Cr >2 or CrCl <40)
Anemia (Hgb <10 or 2g below normal)
Bone lesions (lytic, osteoporosis, pathologic fractures)

Other Features

  • Infections: Impaired humoral immunity
  • Hyperviscosity: Visual changes, bleeding, neurologic symptoms
  • Amyloidosis: AL type (light chain)
  • Neuropathy

Diagnosis

  • SPEP/UPEP: M-spike (monoclonal protein)
  • Serum free light chains: Kappa/lambda ratio
  • Bone marrow biopsy: ≥10% clonal plasma cells
  • Skeletal survey or PET/CT: Lytic lesions

Diagnostic Criteria (≥1 CRAB or biomarker)

  • Clonal plasma cells ≥10% OR biopsy-proven plasmacytoma
  • PLUS ≥1 myeloma-defining event: CRAB or biomarker (≥60% plasma cells, FLC ratio ≥100, >1 MRI lesion)

Treatment

  • Transplant-eligible: Induction (VRd: bortezomib, lenalidomide, dexamethasone) → autologous HSCT → maintenance
  • Transplant-ineligible: VRd or Dara-VRd, continue until progression
  • Supportive: Bisphosphonates, pain control, infection prophylaxis
Avoid NSAIDs and nephrotoxins. Light chain cast nephropathy is a medical emergency — treat myeloma and consider plasmapheresis.

50. High-Yield Board Pearls

Anemia

  • 💎 Low ferritin = iron deficiency. Normal ferritin with inflammation doesn't rule it out.
  • 💎 B12 deficiency can cause neuro symptoms WITHOUT anemia. Check MMA if borderline.
  • 💎 Spherocytes + positive DAT = warm AIHA. Spherocytes + negative DAT = hereditary spherocytosis.
  • 💎 G6PD assay is falsely normal during acute hemolysis — retest later.

Bleeding Disorders

  • 💎 Prolonged PTT + normal PT + mixing corrects = factor deficiency (VIII, IX, XI, XII).
  • 💎 Prolonged PTT + doesn't correct = inhibitor (lupus anticoagulant or factor inhibitor).
  • 💎 vWD: Most common inherited bleeding disorder. DDAVP for Type 1.
  • 💎 DIC: Treat the underlying cause. Replace products only if bleeding.

Thrombosis

  • 💎 HIT = prothrombotic! Stop heparin AND start non-heparin anticoagulant.
  • 💎 TTP: Don't wait for ADAMTS13 — start plasma exchange immediately.
  • 💎 Warfarin skin necrosis = protein C/S deficiency. Bridge with heparin.

Leukemia

  • 💎 APL [t(15;17)] = DIC. Start ATRA immediately on suspicion.
  • 💎 CML = Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22), BCR-ABL1. TKIs are curative.
  • 💎 CLL = CD5+/CD23+ B-cells. Watch and wait if asymptomatic.
  • 💎 Auer rods = AML (not ALL).

Lymphoma

  • 💎 Reed-Sternberg cells = Hodgkin. CD15+/CD30+.
  • 💎 "Starry sky" pattern = Burkitt lymphoma. t(8;14) c-MYC.
  • 💎 Hodgkin spreads contiguously; NHL spreads hematogenously.

Myeloma

  • 💎 CRAB criteria: Calcium, Renal, Anemia, Bone lesions.
  • 💎 Rouleaux formation on smear = myeloma or Waldenström's.
  • 💎 Lytic lesions on skeletal survey (not bone scan — no blastic activity).

Transfusion

  • 💎 TRALI = noncardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6h of transfusion.
  • 💎 TACO = volume overload. Diuretics help (vs TRALI where they don't).
  • 💎 Febrile non-hemolytic = most common reaction. Cytokines in product.

🩸 ABIM Hematology/Oncology Study Guide

ASH • ASCO • NCCN Guidelines

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide 🫁 Pulm 🔬 Endo
↑ Back to top

🦴 Rheumatology

ABIM Board Review — ACR Guidelines • Autoimmune & Inflammatory Conditions

🎯 Core Rheumatology Principles

🔬ANA is positive in 15–20% of healthy people — only test with clinical CTD features
💊Methotrexate is anchor DMARD in RA — use before biologics
⚡Gout: urate-lowering therapy target is serum urate <6 mg/dL
🧪Synovial fluid analysis is essential — always tap an acutely inflamed joint
🦋SLE: rule out infection before attributing flare to disease activity
📊Osteoporosis screening starts at 65 in women (or earlier with risk factors)
🦴 Rheumatoid Arthritis 🔗 Spondyloarthropathies 💎 Gout & Pseudogout 🦠 Septic Arthritis 🦵 Osteoarthritis 🦋 SLE 🖐️ Scleroderma 💪 Myositis 💧 Sjögren's 🩸 APS 🔥 PMR & GCA 🧬 Vasculitis 🔵 Sarcoidosis 🦷 Osteoporosis 🌀 Fibromyalgia 🧪 Serologies 💊 DMARDs & Biologics 🚨 Emergencies

1. Rheumatoid Arthritis Common

Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

  • Prevalence: 0.5-1% of population, F:M = 3:1
  • Peak onset: 30-50 years (but can occur at any age)
  • Pathophysiology: Autoimmune synovitis → pannus formation → cartilage/bone destruction
  • Genetic association: HLA-DR4 (shared epitope), PTPN22
  • Environmental triggers: Smoking (strongest modifiable risk factor), periodontal disease, silica exposure

Clinical Presentation

MCP/PIP/Wrist — RA loves the small joints of hands (spares DIPs!)

Joint Involvement

  • Classic pattern: Symmetric polyarthritis of small joints
  • Hands: MCP, PIP (spares DIP)
  • Wrists: Very common, early involvement
  • Feet: MTP joints
  • Other: Knees, ankles, elbows, shoulders
  • Cervical spine: C1-C2 subluxation (check before intubation!)
  • Morning stiffness: >1 hour (inflammatory pattern)

Extra-Articular Manifestations

SystemManifestationNotes
SkinRheumatoid nodulesExtensor surfaces, 20-30% of seropositive RA
PulmonaryILD, pleural effusions, nodulesILD is leading cause of RA mortality; UIP or NSIP pattern
CardiacPericarditis, accelerated atherosclerosisCV disease is #1 cause of death in RA
OcularScleritis, episcleritis, keratoconjunctivitis siccaScleritis can be vision-threatening
HematologicAnemia of chronic disease, Felty syndromeFelty = RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia
NeurologicCarpal tunnel, cervical myelopathyC1-C2 instability can cause cord compression
VascularRheumatoid vasculitisRare but serious; digital infarcts, mononeuritis multiplex
Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia) increases infection risk. These patients need aggressive RA treatment.

Diagnostic Workup

Serologies

TestSensitivitySpecificityClinical Utility
RF (Rheumatoid Factor)70-80%80%Can be positive in other conditions (HCV, Sjögren's, age)
Anti-CCP (ACPA)60-70%95%More specific; predicts erosive disease
ESR/CRPVariableLowDisease activity monitoring
Anti-CCP can be positive years before clinical RA develops. Double-positive (RF+ and anti-CCP+) predicts more aggressive, erosive disease.

Imaging

  • X-ray (early): Soft tissue swelling, periarticular osteopenia, joint space narrowing
  • X-ray (late): Erosions (marginal), subluxations, deformities
  • Ultrasound: Synovitis, erosions (more sensitive than X-ray early)
  • MRI: Bone marrow edema, early erosions, synovitis

2010 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria for RA

Score ≥6/10 = definite RA (requires at least 1 swollen joint)

CategoryPoints
Joint Involvement
1 large joint0
2-10 large joints1
1-3 small joints2
4-10 small joints3
>10 joints (at least 1 small)5
Serology
Negative RF and negative ACPA0
Low-positive RF or low-positive ACPA2
High-positive RF or high-positive ACPA3
Acute Phase Reactants
Normal CRP and normal ESR0
Abnormal CRP or abnormal ESR1
Duration of Symptoms
<6 weeks0
≥6 weeks1

Treatment

Treatment Goals

  • Treat-to-target: Aim for remission or low disease activity
  • Window of opportunity: Early aggressive treatment prevents joint damage
  • Monitoring: DAS28, CDAI, or SDAI scores every 3-6 months

First-Line: csDMARDs

Methotrexate (MTX) — Anchor drug for RA
  • Dose: Start 7.5-15mg weekly, titrate to 20-25mg weekly
  • Folate: Folic acid 1mg daily (reduces side effects)
  • Monitoring: CBC, CMP, LFTs at baseline, then every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 8-12 weeks
  • Contraindications: Pregnancy (teratogenic), liver disease, heavy alcohol use, severe renal impairment
  • Side effects: GI upset, stomatitis, hepatotoxicity, cytopenias, pneumonitis
DrugMechanismKey MonitoringNotable Side Effects
MethotrexateFolate antagonistCBC, LFTs, creatinineHepatotoxicity, pneumonitis
LeflunomidePyrimidine synthesis inhibitorCBC, LFTsHepatotoxicity, teratogenic
SulfasalazineAnti-inflammatoryCBCGI upset, rash, cytopenias
HydroxychloroquineMultipleAnnual eye exam (after 5 yrs)Retinal toxicity (rare)

Second-Line: Biologics & Targeted Synthetic DMARDs

ClassDrugsKey Considerations
TNF inhibitorsAdalimumab, Etanercept, Infliximab, Certolizumab, GolimumabScreen for TB, Hep B; avoid in CHF, demyelinating disease
IL-6 inhibitorsTocilizumab, SarilumabCan mask infection (suppresses CRP); monitor lipids
T-cell costimulationAbataceptAvoid live vaccines
B-cell depletionRituximabScreen for Hep B; infusion reactions
JAK inhibitorsTofacitinib, Baricitinib, UpadacitinibVTE risk, herpes zoster risk; avoid in age >65 with CV risk
Before starting biologics: Screen for latent TB (PPD or IGRA), Hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs), and Hepatitis C. Update vaccinations (no live vaccines on biologics).

Prognosis & Monitoring

  • Poor prognostic factors: High-titer RF, anti-CCP positive, early erosions, extra-articular disease, high disease activity
  • CV risk: RA increases CV mortality 1.5-2x; treat traditional risk factors aggressively
  • Remission rates: 20-40% achieve sustained remission with modern therapy
↑ Back to Table of Contents

2. Spondyloarthropathies Boards Favorite

Overview

Group of inflammatory arthritides sharing clinical features:

  • Axial involvement: Sacroiliitis, spondylitis
  • Peripheral arthritis: Asymmetric, oligoarticular
  • Enthesitis: Inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions
  • Dactylitis: "Sausage digits"
  • Extra-articular: Uveitis, psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease
  • RF negative (seronegative arthropathies)
  • HLA-B27 association

Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS)

Clinical Features

  • Demographics: Young men (20-40), M:F = 3:1
  • Cardinal symptom: Inflammatory back pain
    • Onset <40 years
    • Insidious onset
    • Morning stiffness >30 minutes
    • Improves with exercise, worsens with rest
    • Nocturnal pain (wakes from sleep)
  • Sacroiliitis: Alternating buttock pain
  • Decreased spinal mobility: Positive Schober test (normal ≥5 cm)
  • Chest expansion: Reduced (<5 cm at nipple level)

Extra-Articular Manifestations

SystemManifestationFrequency
EyeAcute anterior uveitis (AAU)25-40%
CardiacAortic regurgitation, conduction defects2-10%
PulmonaryApical fibrosis (late)Rare
GIIBD (microscopic or overt)5-10%
RenalIgA nephropathy, AA amyloidosisRare
