Acute anterior uveitis (AAU) is the most common extra-articular manifestation of AS. Presents with acute, unilateral red eye with pain and photophobia. HLA-B27 positive in >50% of AAU.

Imaging

  • X-ray sacroiliac joints: Erosions → sclerosis → fusion (bilateral symmetric)
  • X-ray spine: Squaring of vertebrae, syndesmophytes, "bamboo spine" (late)
  • MRI: Bone marrow edema at sacroiliac joints (early disease before X-ray changes)

Treatment

  • Exercise: Essential — maintain spinal mobility, swimming excellent
  • NSAIDs: First-line, continuous use may slow radiographic progression
  • TNF inhibitors: Highly effective for refractory disease
  • IL-17 inhibitors: Secukinumab, ixekizumab
  • JAK inhibitors: Tofacitinib, upadacitinib (newer option)
  • csDMARDs (MTX, SSZ): NOT effective for axial disease; may help peripheral arthritis

Psoriatic Arthritis (PsA)

Clinical Patterns

  1. Asymmetric oligoarthritis (most common, 30-50%)
  2. Symmetric polyarthritis (RA-like, 15-25%)
  3. DIP arthritis (5-10%) — associated with nail changes
  4. Spondylitis/sacroiliitis (5-20%)
  5. Arthritis mutilans (<5%) — destructive, "pencil-in-cup"

Key Features

  • Dactylitis: "Sausage digit" — entire finger/toe swollen
  • Enthesitis: Achilles tendinitis, plantar fasciitis
  • Nail changes: Pitting, onycholysis, oil spots — correlate with DIP involvement
  • Skin: Psoriasis (skin changes may precede, follow, or be absent)
15-20% of PsA patients have no visible psoriasis. Check scalp, umbilicus, gluteal cleft, and nails carefully!

Treatment

DomainTreatment Options
Peripheral arthritisMTX, leflunomide, SSZ, apremilast, TNFi, IL-17i, IL-23i, JAKi
Axial diseaseNSAIDs, TNFi, IL-17i, JAKi (NOT MTX)
Enthesitis/dactylitisNSAIDs, TNFi, IL-17i, IL-23i
SkinIL-17i, IL-23i often preferred

Reactive Arthritis (Formerly Reiter's Syndrome)

  • Trigger: Post-infectious (GI: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia; GU: Chlamydia)
  • Timing: 1-4 weeks after infection
  • Classic triad: Arthritis + urethritis + conjunctivitis ("can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree")
  • Other: Keratoderma blennorrhagicum, circinate balanitis, oral ulcers
  • Treatment: NSAIDs, treat underlying infection if persistent; DMARDs for chronic

Enteropathic Arthritis

  • Associated with: Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis
  • Types:
    • Peripheral (parallels bowel activity) — treat the gut
    • Axial (independent of bowel activity) — treat like AS
  • Caution: NSAIDs may flare IBD; TNFi (infliximab, adalimumab) preferred
↑ Back to Table of Contents

3. Gout & Pseudogout Common

Gout (Monosodium Urate Crystal Disease)

Risk Factors

  • Hyperuricemia causes: Underexcretion (90%) vs overproduction (10%)
  • Diet: Red meat, organ meats, shellfish, alcohol (especially beer), fructose
  • Medications: Thiazides, loop diuretics, low-dose aspirin, cyclosporine
  • Comorbidities: CKD, metabolic syndrome, obesity, HTN

Clinical Stages

  1. Asymptomatic hyperuricemia: No treatment indicated (most never develop gout)
  2. Acute gouty arthritis: Sudden, severe monoarticular inflammation
  3. Intercritical gout: Asymptomatic periods between attacks
  4. Chronic tophaceous gout: Tophi, chronic arthritis, joint destruction

Acute Gout Presentation

  • Classic: Podagra (1st MTP) — 50% of first attacks
  • Character: Rapid onset (peaks 12-24h), exquisitely tender, erythema, warmth
  • Other sites: Ankle, knee, wrist, elbow
  • Triggers: Dietary indiscretion, alcohol, dehydration, surgery, illness, medication changes
Serum uric acid may be NORMAL during acute attack (consumed into crystals). Don't use acute level to rule out gout!

Diagnosis

Gold Standard: Arthrocentesis showing negatively birefringent, needle-shaped MSU crystals under polarized light microscopy
FindingGout (MSU)Pseudogout (CPPD)
Crystal shapeNeedle-shapedRhomboid/rectangular
BirefringenceNegative (yellow parallel)Positive (blue parallel)
ImagingErosions with overhanging edges ("rat bite")Chondrocalcinosis
Joint distribution1st MTP, ankle, kneeKnee, wrist, shoulder

Treatment: Acute Gout

AgentDosingConsiderations
NSAIDsIndomethacin 50mg TID or Naproxen 500mg BID × 5-7 daysAvoid if CKD, GI bleeding, CVD
Colchicine1.2mg, then 0.6mg 1h later; then 0.6mg daily/BIDBest within 36h of onset; reduce dose in CKD
CorticosteroidsPrednisone 30-40mg × 5 days OR intra-articularGood for CKD, multiple comorbidities
IL-1 inhibitorsAnakinra 100mg daily × 3 daysRefractory cases
Do NOT start or stop urate-lowering therapy (ULT) during acute attack — can prolong/worsen flare. Treat the acute attack first.

Treatment: Chronic Gout / Urate-Lowering Therapy

Indications for ULT:

  • ≥2 attacks per year
  • Tophi
  • Chronic gouty arthritis
  • Urate nephropathy or nephrolithiasis
  • CKD stage ≥3
DrugMechanismDosingNotes
AllopurinolXanthine oxidase inhibitorStart 100mg daily, titrate to goalDose reduce in CKD; HLA-B*5801 screening in high-risk populations
FebuxostatXanthine oxidase inhibitor40-80mg dailyAlternative if allopurinol intolerant; avoid in CVD (CARES trial)
ProbenecidUricosuric500mg BIDRequires good renal function; contraindicated in nephrolithiasis
PegloticaseUricase (converts urate to allantoin)IV q2 weeksRefractory tophaceous gout; immunogenic

Goal: Serum urate <6 mg/dL (or <5 if tophi)

Allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome (AHS): Severe DRESS-like reaction. Risk factors: CKD, HLA-B*5801 (common in Han Chinese, Korean, Thai). Start low (100mg) and go slow.

Pseudogout (CPPD Crystal Arthritis)

  • Crystals: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate
  • Classic joints: Knee (most common), wrist, shoulder
  • Imaging: Chondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification) on X-ray
  • Associations: Hemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism, hypothyroidism, hypomagnesemia

Treatment: Same as gout (NSAIDs, colchicine, steroids). No urate-lowering equivalent exists for CPPD.

↑ Back to Table of Contents

4. Septic Arthritis Emergency

Overview

  • Definition: Bacterial infection of joint space — orthopedic emergency
  • Risk factors: RA, prosthetic joint, diabetes, immunosuppression, IVDU, skin infection

Microbiology

PopulationCommon Organisms
Adults (non-gonococcal)S. aureus (#1), Streptococcus
Sexually active young adultsN. gonorrhoeae
IVDUS. aureus, Pseudomonas
Prosthetic jointCoNS, S. aureus

Clinical Presentation

  • Classic: Acute monoarthritis — hot, swollen, extremely painful
  • Location: Knee (most common), hip, ankle, wrist
  • Fever: Present in ~60%

Disseminated Gonococcal Infection

  • Demographics: Young, sexually active
  • Bacteremic phase: Migratory polyarthralgias, tenosynovitis, pustular skin lesions
  • Septic phase: Monoarthritis

Diagnosis

Joint aspiration is MANDATORY before antibiotics. Delay = irreversible joint destruction.
ParameterSeptic Arthritis
WBC>50,000/μL (often >100,000)
PMN%>90%
Gram stainPositive 50-75%
CulturePositive 70-90%
WBC >50,000 suggests septic but isn't definitive — gout can too. Send Gram stain, culture, AND crystals on every tap!

Treatment

ScenarioEmpiric Antibiotics
StandardVancomycin + Ceftriaxone
GonococcalCeftriaxone 1g IV + Azithromycin 1g
  • Duration: 2-4 weeks depending on organism
  • Drainage: Essential — serial aspiration, arthroscopy, or open
↑ Back to Table of Contents

5. Osteoarthritis Very Common

Overview

  • Definition: Degenerative joint disease — cartilage loss, subchondral bone changes, osteophyte formation
  • Epidemiology: Most common arthritis; affects >30 million Americans; increases with age
  • Risk factors: Age, obesity, joint injury, repetitive stress, genetics, female sex

Clinical Features

  • Pain: Worse with activity, better with rest (opposite of inflammatory)
  • Morning stiffness: <30 minutes (vs >1 hour in RA)
  • Gelling: Stiffness after prolonged sitting
  • No systemic symptoms

Joint Distribution

Commonly AffectedTypically Spared
DIP joints (Heberden's nodes)MCP joints
PIP joints (Bouchard's nodes)Wrists
First CMC (base of thumb)Elbows
Hips, KneesShoulders, Ankles
Cervical/lumbar spine
OA affects DIPs; RA spares DIPs. If you see DIP involvement, think OA or PsA, NOT RA.

X-ray Findings

JOSS: Joint space narrowing, Osteophytes, Subchondral sclerosis, Subchondral cysts

Treatment

ModalityDetails
Non-pharmacologicWeight loss (best for knee), exercise, PT, assistive devices
TopicalNSAIDs (diclofenac gel), capsaicin
OralAcetaminophen (limited efficacy), NSAIDs (short courses)
Intra-articularCorticosteroids (short-term), hyaluronic acid (controversial)
SurgeryJoint replacement for refractory cases

OA vs RA Comparison

FeatureOARA
Morning stiffness<30 min>1 hour
Pain patternWorse with useWorse at rest/morning
DIP involvementYesNo
MCP/wristRareClassic
ESR/CRPNormalElevated
X-rayOsteophytes, sclerosisErosions, osteopenia
↑ Back to Table of Contents

6. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Common Boards Favorite

Epidemiology

  • Prevalence: 20-150 per 100,000
  • F:M ratio: 9:1 (during childbearing years)
  • Peak onset: 15-44 years
  • Higher prevalence/severity: African American, Hispanic, Asian populations

Clinical Manifestations

SOAP BRAIN MD — SLE criteria
  • Serositis
  • Oral ulcers
  • Arthritis
  • Photosensitivity
  • Blood disorders
  • Renal disease
  • ANA
  • Immunologic (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, antiphospholipid)
  • Neurologic
  • Malar rash
  • Discoid rash
SystemManifestationsKey Points
SkinMalar rash, discoid rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopeciaMalar rash spares nasolabial folds; discoid causes scarring
JointsNon-erosive arthritis, Jaccoud's arthropathyUnlike RA: no erosions, reducible deformities
RenalLupus nephritis (Class I-VI)Most important prognostic factor; proteinuria, active sediment
HematologicCytopenias, hemolytic anemia, lymphopenia, thrombocytopeniaLymphopenia (<1000) correlates with disease activity
SerositisPleuritis, pericarditisMay present as chest pain
NeurologicSeizures, psychosis, cognitive dysfunction, stroke19 different neuropsychiatric manifestations
CardiacPericarditis, Libman-Sacks endocarditis, accelerated atherosclerosisLeading cause of late death = CV disease
PulmonaryPleuritis, ILD, shrinking lung syndrome, pulmonary HTNShrinking lung = diaphragmatic weakness

Lupus Nephritis — Critical Topic

ClassDescriptionTreatment
IMinimal mesangialNone specific
IIMesangial proliferativeConservative/low-dose immunosuppression
IIIFocal proliferative (<50% glomeruli)Induction: MMF or Cyclophosphamide + steroids
IVDiffuse proliferative (≥50% glomeruli)Most aggressive treatment; induction + maintenance
VMembranousIf nephrotic: immunosuppression
VIAdvanced sclerosisSupportive; prepare for RRT
Class IV lupus nephritis is the most common and severe form. Treatment: Induction with MMF or IV cyclophosphamide + steroids, followed by maintenance with MMF or azathioprine.

Autoantibodies in SLE

AntibodySensitivitySpecificityClinical Association
ANA95%+LowScreening test; if negative, SLE very unlikely
Anti-dsDNA60-70%95%Correlates with disease activity, lupus nephritis
Anti-Smith (Sm)25-30%99%Most specific for SLE
Anti-Ro (SSA)30%LowNeonatal lupus, congenital heart block, photosensitivity
Anti-La (SSB)15%ModerateUsually with anti-Ro; less nephritis
Anti-RNP30%LowMCTD overlap
Antiphospholipid30-40%ModerateThrombosis, pregnancy loss
Anti-Ro positive pregnancy: Monitor fetal heart (weekly from 16-26 weeks) for congenital heart block. IVIG and dexamethasone may be used if block detected.

Disease Activity Monitoring

  • C3/C4: Low in active disease (consumption)
  • Anti-dsDNA: Rising titers often precede flare
  • ESR: Elevated in active disease
  • CRP: Usually normal in lupus flare (elevated suggests infection)
  • Urinalysis: Active sediment (RBC casts, proteinuria) = nephritis
CRP is usually NOT elevated in SLE flares (unlike most inflammatory conditions). If CRP is high in a lupus patient, think infection first!

Treatment

All Patients

  • Hydroxychloroquine: EVERYONE with SLE (reduces flares, mortality, thrombosis, damage accrual)
  • Sun protection: Sunscreen, protective clothing
  • Cardiovascular risk reduction: Aggressive management
  • Vaccinations: Annual flu, pneumococcal (no live vaccines if immunosuppressed)

By Severity

ManifestationTreatment
Skin, joints (mild)HCQ ± NSAIDs ± low-dose prednisone
Moderate diseaseAdd azathioprine or methotrexate
Nephritis (Class III/IV/V)Induction: MMF or CYC + steroids; Maintenance: MMF or AZA
Severe/refractoryRituximab, belimumab, voclosporin (nephritis)
NeuropsychiatricHigh-dose steroids ± cyclophosphamide
Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) — Foundation of SLE treatment
  • Dose: 200-400mg daily (≤5 mg/kg/day to reduce retinal toxicity)
  • Benefits: Reduces flares by 50%, reduces damage accrual, improves survival, reduces thrombosis
  • Monitoring: Baseline eye exam, then annual after 5 years (or earlier if risk factors)
  • Safe in pregnancy
↑ Back to Table of Contents

7. Drug-Induced Lupus Boards Favorite

High-Risk Drugs

Classic drugs — "HIP": Hydralazine, Isoniazid, Procainamide
Risk LevelDrugs
High (1-20%)Procainamide, Hydralazine
ModerateIsoniazid, Minocycline, Anti-TNF agents
LowerSulfasalazine, Methyldopa, Phenytoin, PPIs

Clinical Features

  • Common: Arthralgias, myalgias, serositis, fever, rash
  • Uncommon: Renal disease, CNS involvement (unlike SLE)
Drug-induced lupus typically spares kidneys and CNS. If nephritis or CNS disease present, think idiopathic SLE.

Serologies

AntibodyDrug-InducedIdiopathic SLE
ANAPositive (>95%)Positive
Anti-histonePositive (>95%)Positive (50-70%)
Anti-dsDNAUsually negativeOften positive
ComplementNormalOften low

Treatment

  • Stop the offending drug — usually sufficient
  • NSAIDs/short-course steroids for symptoms
  • Resolution: 1-2 weeks for symptoms; months for serologies
↑ Back to Table of Contents

8. Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) Boards Favorite

Classification

FeatureLimited Cutaneous (lcSSc)Diffuse Cutaneous (dcSSc)
Skin involvementDistal to elbows/knees, faceProximal + trunk
AntibodyAnti-centromere (ACA)Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I)
CourseSlowly progressiveRapidly progressive early
Internal organsLate PAH, less ILDEarly ILD, renal crisis
PrognosisBetterWorse
CREST syndrome (limited cutaneous SSc):
Calcinosis, Raynaud's, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasias

Clinical Manifestations

Skin

  • Raynaud's phenomenon: Almost universal (>95%), often first symptom
  • Skin thickening: Starts in fingers, may progress proximally
  • Digital ulcers: Painful, can become infected
  • Sclerodactyly: Tight, shiny skin on fingers
  • Calcinosis: Subcutaneous calcium deposits
  • Telangiectasias: Face, hands

GI (>90% of patients)

  • Esophageal dysmotility: GERD, dysphagia, Barrett's
  • Small bowel: Malabsorption, bacterial overgrowth, pseudo-obstruction
  • Colon: Constipation, wide-mouth diverticula

Pulmonary (leading cause of death)

  • ILD: More common in dcSSc, anti-Scl-70 positive
  • PAH: More common in lcSSc, anti-centromere positive
  • Screening: Annual PFTs, echocardiogram

Renal — Scleroderma Renal Crisis (SRC)

Scleroderma renal crisis: Acute hypertensive emergency with AKI. Occurs in 10-15% of dcSSc, often within first 4 years. Risk factors: diffuse skin disease, anti-RNA polymerase III, corticosteroid use (>15mg/day).

Treatment: ACE inhibitors (captopril) — even if creatinine rises initially. Continue even if dialysis needed. Do NOT use calcium channel blockers initially.

Autoantibodies

AntibodySubsetClinical Association
Anti-centromere (ACA)lcSScPAH, less ILD, better prognosis
Anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase I)dcSScILD, worse prognosis
Anti-RNA polymerase IIIdcSScScleroderma renal crisis, cancer association
Anti-U3 RNP (fibrillarin)dcSScPAH, skeletal muscle involvement
Anti-PM-SclOverlapMyositis overlap

Treatment

ManifestationTreatment
Raynaud'sAvoid cold, CCB (nifedipine, amlodipine), PDE5 inhibitors
Digital ulcersCCB, PDE5 inhibitors, prostacyclin analogs (iloprost), bosentan for prevention
GERDPPI (high dose), lifestyle modifications
ILDMMF, nintedanib, tocilizumab; rituximab for refractory
PAHERA (bosentan, ambrisentan), PDE5i (sildenafil), prostacyclins
Renal crisisACE inhibitor — lifesaving!
SkinMTX may help early; no great options
Avoid corticosteroids >15mg/day in diffuse SSc — increases risk of scleroderma renal crisis!
↑ Back to Table of Contents

9. Inflammatory Myopathies

Classification

  • Dermatomyositis (DM)
  • Polymyositis (PM)
  • Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM)
  • Inclusion body myositis (IBM)
  • Antisynthetase syndrome

Dermatomyositis

Skin Findings

  • Heliotrope rash: Violaceous discoloration of eyelids
  • Gottron's papules: Erythematous papules over MCP/PIP/DIP (pathognomonic)
  • Gottron's sign: Same distribution but macular
  • Shawl sign: V-neck and upper back erythema
  • Mechanic's hands: Cracked, rough skin on fingers (also antisynthetase)
  • Calcinosis: Especially in juvenile DM

Muscle Involvement

  • Proximal weakness: Difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs, lifting arms
  • Symmetric
  • CK: Elevated (often 10-50× normal)
  • Aldolase: Also elevated
Malignancy association: DM has 3-8× increased cancer risk. Screen with age-appropriate cancer screening + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Highest risk in first 1-3 years after diagnosis.

Polymyositis

  • Similar muscle involvement as DM
  • NO skin manifestations
  • Rare diagnosis — many former PM cases now reclassified as IMNM or antisynthetase

Inclusion Body Myositis

  • Age: >50 years, M > F
  • Distribution: Asymmetric, involves finger flexors and quadriceps (falls, difficulty gripping)
  • CK: Normal to mildly elevated
  • Treatment: Does NOT respond to immunosuppression
  • Biopsy: Rimmed vacuoles, inclusion bodies
IBM is the most common myopathy in patients >Key clue: weakness of finger flexors (difficulty gripping) + quadriceps weakness + poor response to steroids.

Antisynthetase Syndrome

  • Antibody: Anti-Jo-1 (most common), anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, others
  • Classic features: Myositis + ILD + arthritis + mechanic's hands + Raynaud's + fever
  • ILD: Can be severe and progressive

Myositis-Specific Antibodies

AntibodyAssociation
Anti-Jo-1Antisynthetase syndrome, ILD
Anti-Mi-2Classic DM, good prognosis
Anti-MDA5Amyopathic DM, rapidly progressive ILD (high mortality)
Anti-TIF1-γDM with high cancer risk
Anti-NXP2DM with calcinosis, cancer risk
Anti-SRPIMNM, severe myopathy, cardiac involvement
Anti-HMGCRIMNM, statin-associated (can occur without statin)

Treatment

  • First-line: High-dose prednisone (1mg/kg/day)
  • Steroid-sparing: Methotrexate, azathioprine
  • Refractory: IVIG, rituximab, mycophenolate
  • ILD: MMF, azathioprine, rituximab, nintedanib
  • Physical therapy: Essential for maintaining function
↑ Back to Table of Contents

10. Sjögren's Syndrome

Overview

  • Definition: Chronic autoimmune disease targeting exocrine glands (salivary, lacrimal)
  • Demographics: F:M = 9:1, peak 40-60 years
  • Types: Primary (alone) vs Secondary (with RA, SLE, scleroderma)

Clinical Features

Sicca Symptoms (Hallmark)

  • Keratoconjunctivitis sicca: Dry eyes, gritty sensation, photosensitivity
  • Xerostomia: Dry mouth, difficulty swallowing dry foods, dental caries
  • Parotid enlargement: Bilateral, episodic

Extraglandular Manifestations

SystemManifestation
JointsArthralgias, non-erosive arthritis
SkinPurpura, xerosis, annular erythema
PulmonaryILD, bronchiectasis, lymphocytic interstitial pneumonia
RenalInterstitial nephritis, RTA type 1 (distal)
NeurologicPeripheral neuropathy, CNS involvement (rare)
HematologicLeukopenia, hypergammaglobulinemia, cryoglobulinemia
Lymphoma risk: 5-10% lifetime risk of B-cell lymphoma (especially MALT). Monitor for persistent parotid swelling, lymphadenopathy, cryoglobulinemia.

Diagnosis

Serologies

  • Anti-Ro (SSA): 60-70% sensitive
  • Anti-La (SSB): 30-40% sensitive, usually with anti-Ro
  • RF: Positive in 50-70%
  • ANA: Positive in 80%

Objective Tests

  • Schirmer test: Filter paper in eye, <5mm wetting in 5 min = abnormal
  • Ocular staining: Rose bengal, lissamine green
  • Salivary flow: Unstimulated flow <0.1mL/min abnormal
  • Minor salivary gland biopsy: Focal lymphocytic sialadenitis (focus score ≥1)

Treatment

  • Dry eyes: Artificial tears, cyclosporine drops (Restasis), punctal plugs
  • Dry mouth: Frequent sips of water, sugar-free gum, pilocarpine, cevimeline
  • Dental care: Fluoride, regular dental visits
  • Systemic disease: HCQ for arthralgias, fatigue; immunosuppressants for severe organ involvement
↑ Back to Table of Contents

11. Mixed Connective Tissue Disease

Overview

  • Definition: Overlap syndrome with features of SLE, scleroderma, and polymyositis
  • Hallmark: High-titer anti-U1 RNP antibodies
  • Demographics: F:M = 9:1; peak 15-35 years

Clinical Features

  • Raynaud's: Nearly universal (>90%)
  • Swollen "sausage" fingers
  • Arthritis
  • Myositis: Proximal weakness, elevated CK
  • Sclerodactyly
  • Esophageal dysmotility
  • Pulmonary: ILD, PAH (leading cause of death)
MCTD has less severe renal and CNS disease than SLE. If nephritis/CNS prominent, reconsider diagnosis.

Diagnosis

  • Anti-U1 RNP: High titer (required)
  • ANA: Positive, speckled pattern
  • Anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm: Usually negative

Treatment

Treat based on predominant manifestation (Raynaud's → CCB; arthritis → HCQ/MTX; myositis → steroids)

↑ Back to Table of Contents

12. Antiphospholipid Syndrome Boards Favorite

Definition

Autoimmune thrombophilia characterized by arterial/venous thrombosis and/or pregnancy morbidity with persistent antiphospholipid antibodies.

Clinical Manifestations

Thrombosis

  • Venous (most common): DVT, PE, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
  • Arterial: Stroke, TIA, MI (often in young patients)
  • Unusual sites: Hepatic veins (Budd-Chiari), renal, adrenal

Pregnancy Complications

  • Recurrent early miscarriages (<10 weeks)
  • Late fetal loss (>10 weeks)
  • Preterm delivery due to preeclampsia/placental insufficiency

Other Manifestations

  • Livedo reticularis
  • Thrombocytopenia (moderate, usually >50k)
  • Cardiac valve disease (Libman-Sacks endocarditis)
  • Nephropathy (thrombotic microangiopathy)

Antiphospholipid Antibodies

TestNotes
Lupus anticoagulant (LA)Prolongs PTT but causes thrombosis; strongest predictor
Anticardiolipin (aCL)IgG and IgM; medium-high titer more significant
Anti-β2-glycoprotein IIgG and IgM; most specific

Revised Sapporo Criteria for APS

≥1 Clinical + ≥1 Laboratory criterion

Clinical:

  • Vascular thrombosis (arterial, venous, or small vessel)
  • Pregnancy morbidity (≥3 early losses, ≥1 late loss, or preterm for preeclampsia)

Laboratory (must be positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart):

  • Lupus anticoagulant
  • Anticardiolipin IgG or IgM (medium-high titer)
  • Anti-β2GP1 IgG or IgM
"Triple positive" APS (all 3 antibodies positive) has highest thrombosis and pregnancy complication risk. These patients need more aggressive management.

Treatment

ScenarioTreatment
First venous thrombosisWarfarin INR 2-3 (indefinite)
Arterial thrombosisWarfarin INR 2-3 + aspirin OR higher INR 3-4
Recurrent thrombosis on warfarinHigher INR target, add aspirin, or LMWH
Pregnancy (without prior thrombosis)Low-dose aspirin ± prophylactic LMWH
Pregnancy (with prior thrombosis)Therapeutic LMWH + low-dose aspirin
Do NOT use DOACs in APS — TRAPS trial showed increased thrombosis with rivaroxaban vs warfarin in triple-positive APS. Warfarin remains standard.

Catastrophic APS (CAPS)

  • Definition: ≥3 organs involved, develops over <1 week, small vessel thrombosis on histology
  • Mortality: 30-50% even with treatment
  • Treatment: Anticoagulation + high-dose steroids + plasma exchange + IVIG ± rituximab
↑ Back to Table of Contents

13. Polymyalgia Rheumatica & Giant Cell Arteritis Time-Sensitive

Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR)

Key Features

  • Age: Almost always >50 years (peak 70-80)
  • Symptoms: Bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain/stiffness
  • Morning stiffness: >45 minutes
  • Onset: Often sudden (patients can identify exact day)
  • Constitutional: Fatigue, malaise, low-grade fever, weight loss
  • NO true weakness: Pain limits function, but strength is normal
PMR causes pain, not weakness. If true proximal muscle weakness exists, think inflammatory myopathy and check CK.

Diagnosis

  • ESR: Usually >40 mm/h (often >100)
  • CRP: Elevated
  • CK: Normal (distinguishes from myositis)
  • RF, Anti-CCP: Negative
  • Ultrasound: Bilateral subacromial/subdeltoid bursitis, biceps tenosynovitis, hip synovitis
Dramatic steroid response: Improvement within 24-72 hours of starting prednisone is virtually diagnostic. If no response, reconsider diagnosis.

Treatment

  • Prednisone: 12.5-25 mg daily (NOT high-dose like GCA)
  • Taper: Slowly over 1-2 years
    • Reduce by 2.5mg every 2-4 weeks until 10mg
    • Below 10mg: reduce by 1mg every 4-8 weeks
  • Relapse: Common (30-50%), especially below 10mg
  • Steroid-sparing: Methotrexate, tocilizumab if relapsing

Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis)

GCA is an OPHTHALMOLOGIC EMERGENCY. Untreated, can cause permanent blindness within hours to days. Start steroids IMMEDIATELY if suspected — do not wait for biopsy.

Clinical Features

  • Age: >50 years (same as PMR)
  • Headache: New-onset, often temporal
  • Scalp tenderness: Pain when combing hair
  • Jaw claudication: Pain with chewing (highly specific)
  • Visual symptoms: Amaurosis fugax, diplopia, vision loss
  • Temporal artery: Tender, thickened, nodular, decreased pulse
  • PMR symptoms: Present in 40-60% of GCA patients
GCA Red Flags — JAW claudication, Amaurosis/visual changes, Headache (new)

Diagnosis

  • ESR: Usually very high (>50, often >100 mm/h)
  • CRP: Elevated
  • Temporal artery biopsy: Gold standard — granulomatous inflammation with giant cells. Get 2-3cm segment; consider bilateral. Remains positive for 2-6 weeks after starting steroids
  • Temporal artery ultrasound: "Halo sign" (hypoechoic ring) is suggestive

Treatment

ScenarioTreatment
GCA without visual symptomsPrednisone 40-60 mg daily (1 mg/kg)
GCA with visual symptomsIV methylprednisolone 1g daily × 3 days, then high-dose oral
MaintenanceSlow taper over 1-2 years; add aspirin 81mg daily
Relapsing/steroid-sparingTocilizumab (GiACTA trial), methotrexate
10-30% of PMR patients have GCA. Always ask about headache, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms at every PMR visit!

Large Vessel GCA

  • Can involve aorta and major branches
  • Presents with arm claudication, subclavian/axillary bruits, BP discrepancy between arms
  • Risk of aortic aneurysm/dissection — screen with CTA or PET-CT
  • May present without cranial symptoms (biopsy-negative)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

14. Vasculitis Must-Know

Classification by Vessel Size

Vessel SizeDiseasesKey Features
LargeGCA, TakayasuAorta and major branches
MediumPAN, KawasakiVisceral arteries, aneurysms
Small (immune complex)IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemicSkin, kidneys, GI
Small (ANCA-associated)GPA, MPA, EGPALungs, kidneys, sinuses

ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (AAV)

ANCA Patterns

ANCATargetPrimary Association
c-ANCA (cytoplasmic)PR3 (proteinase 3)GPA (>80%)
p-ANCA (perinuclear)MPO (myeloperoxidase)MPA, EGPA

Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener's)

  • Classic triad: Upper airway + Lower airway + Kidney
  • Upper airway: Chronic sinusitis, saddle nose deformity, subglottic stenosis
  • Lungs: Nodules (can cavitate), hemorrhage, infiltrates
  • Kidney: Pauci-immune crescentic GN (RPGN)
  • Other: Scleritis, mononeuritis multiplex
  • ANCA: PR3/c-ANCA (80-90%)

Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA)

  • Similar to GPA but: No granulomas, no upper airway disease
  • Pulmonary-renal syndrome: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage + RPGN
  • ANCA: MPO/p-ANCA (60-70%)

Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss)

  • Key feature: Asthma + eosinophilia + vasculitis
  • Phases: Allergic (asthma, rhinitis) → Eosinophilic (infiltrates) → Vasculitic
  • Cardiac: Eosinophilic myocarditis (leading cause of death)
  • ANCA: MPO/p-ANCA (40-60%)

AAV Treatment

PhaseRegimen
Induction (severe)Rituximab OR Cyclophosphamide + High-dose steroids
Induction (non-severe)Rituximab + reduced steroids (PEXIVAS, LoVAS)
MaintenanceRituximab OR Azathioprine (2+ years minimum)
RefractoryPlasma exchange (consider in DAH, severe renal)
RAVE trial established rituximab as equivalent (or superior for relapsing disease) to cyclophosphamide for AAV induction. Rituximab is now preferred for many patients.

Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)

  • Medium vessel: Aneurysms at branch points, NO glomerulonephritis (spares small vessels)
  • Features: Livedo reticularis, skin nodules, mononeuritis multiplex, testicular pain, mesenteric ischemia
  • Association: Hepatitis B (treat the HBV!)
  • ANCA: Negative
  • Imaging: Mesenteric angiography shows microaneurysms

IgA Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)

  • Classic: Children after URI
  • Tetrad: Palpable purpura (buttocks, legs) + arthritis + abdominal pain + nephritis
  • Usually self-limited: Supportive care, steroids for severe GI/renal
↑ Back to Table of Contents

15. Behçet's Disease Boards Favorite

Overview

  • "Silk Road disease": Highest in Turkey, Middle East, East Asia
  • Demographics: Peak 20-40 years
  • Genetics: HLA-B51 association

Clinical Features

Classic Triad: Oral ulcers + Genital ulcers + Uveitis
SystemFeatures
MucocutaneousOral ulcers (required), genital ulcers, erythema nodosum, pathergy
OcularAnterior uveitis with hypopyon, posterior uveitis (can cause blindness)
VascularDVT, arterial aneurysms, Budd-Chiari
NeurologicMeningoencephalitis, dural sinus thrombosis
GIUlcers mimicking Crohn's

Pathergy Test

  • Prick skin with sterile needle → pustule at 24-48h = positive

Diagnosis (ISG Criteria)

Required: Recurrent oral ulcers (≥3 times/12 months)

Plus 2 of: Genital ulcers, eye lesions, skin lesions, positive pathergy

Behçet's thrombosis is inflammatory — treat with immunosuppression, NOT primarily anticoagulation.
↑ Back to Table of Contents

16. Adult-Onset Still's Disease Boards Favorite

Clinical Features

  • Quotidian fever: Daily spikes to ≥39°C, typically evening, returns to normal
  • Evanescent salmon-colored rash: Appears with fever
  • Arthritis: Can become destructive
  • Pharyngitis: Severe, non-exudative
  • Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly

Laboratory Findings

TestFinding
FerritinMarkedly elevated (often >10,000)
Glycosylated ferritin<20% (normally 50-80%)
WBCLeukocytosis with neutrophilia
ANA/RFNegative
Ferritin >10,000 + glycosylated ferritin <20% = Still's disease (or MAS). Boards favorite!

Complications

Macrophage Activation Syndrome (MAS): Pancytopenia, very high ferritin, high TG, low fibrinogen. Life-threatening!

Treatment

  • Mild: NSAIDs, low-dose steroids
  • Moderate-severe: High-dose corticosteroids
  • Refractory: Anakinra (IL-1), tocilizumab (IL-6)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

17. Sarcoidosis High-Yield

Overview

  • Definition: Multisystem granulomatous disease
  • Epidemiology: Higher in African Americans; peak 25-35 years
  • Pathology: Non-caseating granulomas

Clinical Manifestations

Pulmonary (>90%)

StageCXR FindingsRemission Rate
IBilateral hilar LAD alone55-90%
IIBHL + pulmonary infiltrates40-70%
IIIInfiltrates without BHL10-20%
IVPulmonary fibrosis0%

Extrapulmonary

SystemFeatures
SkinErythema nodosum, lupus pernio
EyesUveitis
CardiacAV block, cardiomyopathy, sudden death
NeuroCN VII palsy, meningitis
MetabolicHypercalcemia (granuloma 1,25-vitamin D)

Löfgren's Syndrome

Acute triad: BHL + Erythema nodosum + Migratory polyarthritis (ankles)
Excellent prognosis — usually self-limited

Treatment

  • Observation: Many remit (especially Stage I)
  • Indications: Progressive pulmonary, hypercalcemia, cardiac, neuro, ocular
  • First-line: Corticosteroids
  • Steroid-sparing: MTX, AZA; TNFi for refractory
↑ Back to Table of Contents

18. IgG4-Related Disease

Overview

  • Definition: Fibro-inflammatory condition with IgG4+ plasma cell infiltration
  • Demographics: Middle-aged to elderly men
  • Pattern: Mass-forming lesions in multiple organs

Organ Involvement

OrganManifestation
PancreasAutoimmune pancreatitis (type 1)
BiliarySclerosing cholangitis
Salivary/LacrimalSialadenitis, dacryoadenitis
RetroperitoneumRetroperitoneal fibrosis
KidneyTubulointerstitial nephritis
AortaAortitis/periaortitis
ThyroidRiedel's thyroiditis

Diagnosis

  • Serum IgG4: Elevated (but normal in 30-50%)
  • Histology: IgG4+ plasma cells, storiform fibrosis, obliterative phlebitis

Treatment

  • First-line: Corticosteroids (dramatic response)
  • Steroid-sparing: Rituximab (very effective)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

19. Amyloidosis Boards Favorite

Types

TypePrecursorAssociation
AL (Primary)Light chainsMyeloma, MGUS
AA (Secondary)Serum amyloid AChronic inflammation (RA, IBD, FMF)
ATTR (hereditary)Mutant TTRFamilial polyneuropathy
ATTR (wild-type)Normal TTRSenile cardiac (elderly)

AL Amyloidosis Features

  • Heart: Restrictive cardiomyopathy, low voltage ECG
  • Kidney: Nephrotic syndrome
  • Nerve: Peripheral neuropathy, carpal tunnel
  • GI: Macroglossia (pathognomonic), hepatomegaly
  • Skin: Periorbital purpura ("raccoon eyes")
Macroglossia + periorbital purpura = think AL amyloidosis/plasma cell dyscrasia

Diagnosis

  • Tissue biopsy: Congo red → apple-green birefringence
  • Sites: Abdominal fat pad (80% sensitive), rectal, bone marrow

Treatment

TypeTreatment
ALTreat plasma cell dyscrasia (bortezomib, SCT)
AATreat underlying inflammation
ATTRTafamidis (FDA approved)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

20. Osteoporosis Very Common High-Yield

Definitions

CategoryT-score
Normal≥ -1.0
Osteopenia-1.0 to -2.5
Osteoporosis≤ -2.5
Severe osteoporosis≤ -2.5 + fragility fracture

Screening (USPSTF)

  • All women ≥65 years
  • Postmenopausal women <65 with risk factors
  • Modality: DXA of hip and lumbar spine

FRAX Score

  • Estimates 10-year fracture probability
  • Treat if: ≥3% hip fracture OR ≥20% major osteoporotic fracture risk

Treatment Options

ClassDrugsKey Points
BisphosphonatesAlendronate, Zoledronic acidFirst-line; avoid CrCl <30-35
RANK-L inhibitorDenosumabRebound bone loss if stopped
AnabolicTeriparatide, RomosozumabSevere/refractory; 2-year limit
SERMsRaloxifeneVertebral only; VTE risk
Bisphosphonate Holiday: After 5 years oral or 3 years IV, consider drug holiday in low-risk patients.

Adverse Effects

  • Bisphosphonates: ONJ, atypical femoral fractures
  • Denosumab: Hypocalcemia, rebound when stopped
  • Teriparatide: Osteosarcoma risk (avoid if Paget's)
  • Romosozumab: CV risk

GIOP Prevention

  • Risk: ≥2.5mg prednisone daily × ≥3 months
  • Prophylaxis: Calcium + Vitamin D + bisphosphonate
↑ Back to Table of Contents

21. Paget's Disease of Bone Boards Favorite

Overview

  • Definition: Focal disorder of accelerated bone remodeling
  • Sites: Pelvis, spine, skull, femur, tibia

Clinical Features

  • Asymptomatic: Most patients (incidental)
  • Bone pain, deformities (bowing, skull enlargement)
  • Hearing loss: CN VIII compression
  • Complications: High-output HF (rare), osteosarcoma (<1%)

Laboratory

Classic: Markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase with NORMAL calcium, phosphorus, PTH

Treatment

  • Indications: Pain, hypercalcemia, neurologic, pre-surgery, high-risk sites
  • First-line: Zoledronic acid 5mg IV × 1 (often only dose needed)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

22. Osteonecrosis (Avascular Necrosis)

Risk Factors

"ASEPTIC": Alcohol, Steroids, Emboli (sickle cell), Pancreatitis, Trauma, Idiopathic, Caisson disease
  • Glucocorticoids: Most common non-traumatic cause
  • Alcohol, SLE, HIV, Radiation

Clinical Presentation

  • Pain: Hip/groin, worse with weight bearing
  • Most common site: Femoral head
  • May be bilateral

Diagnosis

  • X-ray: May be normal early; later crescent sign, collapse
  • MRI: Gold standard — "double line sign"

Treatment

  • Early: Core decompression
  • Late (collapse): Total hip arthroplasty
↑ Back to Table of Contents

23. Fibromyalgia Common Boards Favorite

Overview

  • Definition: Chronic widespread pain with central sensitization
  • Epidemiology: 2-4% population; F:M = 7:1

Clinical Features

  • Chronic widespread pain: >3 months, multiple regions
  • Fatigue: Unrefreshing sleep
  • "Fibro fog": Cognitive difficulties
  • Associated: IBS, headaches, depression
Fibromyalgia is a diagnosis of EXCLUSION. All labs should be normal. If inflammatory markers elevated, look elsewhere.

Workup (to Exclude Other Conditions)

  • CBC, ESR/CRP, TSH, Vitamin D, CK — all should be normal
  • Avoid shotgun ANA testing!

Treatment

Non-Pharmacologic (First-Line)

  • Aerobic exercise: Most effective intervention
  • CBT, sleep hygiene, stress reduction

Pharmacologic

ClassDrugs
SNRIs (FDA approved)Duloxetine, Milnacipran
AnticonvulsantsPregabalin (FDA), Gabapentin
TCAsAmitriptyline (low dose at bedtime)
Avoid: Opioids (not effective, dependence risk), high-dose NSAIDs (not effective), corticosteroids (not inflammatory)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

24. Bursitis, Tendinopathy & Carpal Tunnel

Bursitis

TypeLocationKey Features
TrochantericLateral hipPain lying on side
OlecranonElbowConsider septic if red/hot
PrepatellarAnterior knee"Housemaid's knee"
Pes anserineMedial kneeCommon in OA, obesity
SubacromialShoulderPainful arc 60-120°

Tendinopathies

  • Lateral epicondylitis: "Tennis elbow" — pain with wrist extension
  • Medial epicondylitis: "Golfer's elbow" — pain with wrist flexion
  • De Quervain's: Thumb/radial wrist; positive Finkelstein
  • Achilles: Posterior heel; fluoroquinolone risk

Carpal Tunnel Syndrome

  • Symptoms: Numbness in median distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial ring)
  • Worse at night
  • Tests: Phalen, Tinel
  • Advanced: Thenar atrophy
  • Associations: Pregnancy, hypothyroidism, DM, RA, amyloid
  • Treatment: Splinting, steroids, surgery
↑ Back to Table of Contents

25. Back Pain: Inflammatory vs Mechanical & Red Flags

Inflammatory vs Mechanical

FeatureInflammatoryMechanical
Age of onset<40 yearsAny age
OnsetInsidiousOften acute
Morning stiffness>30-60 min<30 min
Effect of exerciseImprovesWorsens
Effect of restWorsensImproves
Night painYes (2nd half)Uncommon

Red Flags

Urgent evaluation needed:
  • Cauda equina: Urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, bilateral weakness
  • Cancer: History of cancer, weight loss, age >50, rest/night pain
  • Infection: Fever, IVDU, immunosuppression
  • Fracture: Trauma, osteoporosis, steroids
↑ Back to Table of Contents

26. Raynaud's Phenomenon

Definition

Episodic vasospasm causing digital color changes with cold/stress

White → Blue → Red (pallor → cyanosis → rubor)

Primary vs Secondary

FeaturePrimarySecondary
Age15-30>30
Tissue damageNoneUlcers, gangrene
ANANegativeOften positive
Nailfold capillariesNormalAbnormal

Secondary Causes

  • CTD: Scleroderma (>95%), SLE, MCTD, Sjögren's
  • Drugs: Beta-blockers, ergotamines
  • Occupational: Vibration injury
Raynaud's is often the first symptom of scleroderma, appearing years before other features. Always examine nailfold capillaries!

Treatment

  • Lifestyle: Avoid cold, keep whole body warm, stop smoking
  • First-line: CCB (nifedipine, amlodipine)
  • Second-line: PDE5 inhibitors, topical nitrates
  • Severe: IV prostacyclins, bosentan
↑ Back to Table of Contents

27. Autoantibodies & Serologies

Quick Reference Table

AntibodyPrimary AssociationClinical Significance
ANASLE (95%+), many othersScreening; if negative, SLE unlikely
Anti-dsDNASLESpecific; correlates with nephritis and activity
Anti-SmithSLEMost specific for SLE
Anti-Ro (SSA)Sjögren's, SLENeonatal lupus, photosensitivity
Anti-La (SSB)Sjögren'sUsually with anti-Ro
Anti-RNPMCTD, SLERequired for MCTD diagnosis
RFRA (70-80%)Also positive in Sjögren's, HCV, age
Anti-CCPRAMost specific; predicts erosive disease
c-ANCA/PR3GPAActive disease marker
p-ANCA/MPOMPA, EGPAAlso drug-induced vasculitis
Anti-centromereLimited SScPAH risk
Anti-Scl-70Diffuse SScILD risk
Anti-Jo-1Antisynthetase syndromeILD, myositis
Anti-Mi-2DMClassic DM, good prognosis
Anti-MDA5DMRapidly progressive ILD
Anti-GBMGoodpasture'sPulmonary-renal syndrome

Complement Levels

PatternDisease
Low C3, Low C4SLE (active), cryoglobulinemia
Low C3, Normal C4Post-strep GN, MPGN, C3 glomerulopathy
Normal C3, Low C4Hereditary angioedema, cryoglobulinemia
Normal C3, Normal C4Most other autoimmune diseases
↑ Back to Table of Contents

28. Rheumatologic Imaging

X-ray Findings by Disease

DiseaseClassic Findings
RAPeriarticular osteopenia, symmetric joint space narrowing, marginal erosions, subluxations
OAJoint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, subchondral cysts
GoutErosions with overhanging edges ("rat bite"), soft tissue tophi
PseudogoutChondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification)
PsAPencil-in-cup deformity, DIP involvement, asymmetric
ASSacroiliitis (bilateral symmetric), bamboo spine, syndesmophytes

Ultrasound in Rheumatology

  • Synovitis: Effusion, synovial hypertrophy, increased Doppler signal
  • Erosions: Cortical breaks (more sensitive than X-ray early)
  • Crystal disease: "Double contour sign" (gout), hyperechoic deposits
  • Enthesitis: Thickening, hypoechogenicity, Doppler signal
  • GCA: "Halo sign" around temporal artery

MRI Applications

  • Sacroiliitis: Bone marrow edema before X-ray changes (early SpA)
  • RA: Synovitis, bone marrow edema, early erosions
  • Myositis: Muscle edema (STIR sequence), fatty replacement
  • Avascular necrosis: Early detection before X-ray changes
↑ Back to Table of Contents

29. Arthrocentesis & Synovial Fluid Analysis

Synovial Fluid Classification

TypeWBC (cells/μL)AppearanceDifferential
Normal<200Clear, viscous—
Non-inflammatory200-2,000Clear, yellowOA, trauma, AVN
Inflammatory2,000-50,000Cloudy, yellowRA, gout, PsA, reactive
Septic>50,000PurulentBacterial infection
WBC >50,000 is concerning for septic arthritis but not definitive. Gout can also cause very high WBC counts. Always send for Gram stain and culture!

Crystal Analysis

CrystalShapeBirefringenceColor When Parallel to Polarizer
MSU (gout)NeedleNegativeYellow
CPPD (pseudogout)RhomboidPositiveBlue
Gout = Yellow when Parallel — "Yellow taxi PARALLEL parked" (negative birefringence)

What to Order

  • Routine: Cell count with differential, Gram stain, culture
  • Crystal analysis: Polarized microscopy
  • If TB suspected: AFB smear/culture
  • If gonococcal: NAAT (more sensitive than culture)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

30. NSAIDs

Mechanism

  • Inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) → ↓ prostaglandin synthesis
  • COX-1: Constitutive — GI protection, platelet function, renal blood flow
  • COX-2: Inducible — inflammation, pain, fever

Drug Classes

TypeExamplesNotes
Non-selectiveIbuprofen, Naproxen, IndomethacinHigher GI risk
COX-2 selectiveCelecoxibLower GI risk, same CV risk

Adverse Effects

SystemEffectNotes
GIPUD, bleedingUse PPI if high risk
CV↑ MI, strokeAll NSAIDs (except naproxen may be neutral)
RenalAKI, hyperkalemia, Na retentionAvoid if CKD, HF
HematologicPlatelet dysfunctionHold before surgery

Contraindications

  • Active PUD or GI bleeding
  • CKD (especially GFR <30)
  • CHF (fluid retention)
  • Third trimester pregnancy (premature ductus closure)
  • Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease
↑ Back to Table of Contents

31. Glucocorticoid Management

Equivalent Doses

DrugEquivalent DoseDurationMineralocorticoid Effect
Hydrocortisone20 mgShort++
Prednisone5 mgIntermediate+
Methylprednisolone4 mgIntermediate+
Dexamethasone0.75 mgLong0

Side Effects by Duration

Short-term (days-weeks)

  • Hyperglycemia
  • Insomnia, mood changes
  • Fluid retention
  • Increased appetite

Long-term (months-years)

  • Metabolic: Cushingoid features, weight gain, diabetes
  • Bone: Osteoporosis, avascular necrosis
  • Infection: Increased risk of opportunistic infections
  • Cardiovascular: HTN, dyslipidemia
  • Ocular: Cataracts, glaucoma
  • GI: PUD (especially with NSAIDs)
  • Skin: Thin skin, easy bruising
  • Adrenal: HPA axis suppression

Prophylaxis for Chronic Steroids

IndicationIntervention
Bone protection (if prednisone ≥2.5mg × ≥3 months)Calcium 1000-1200mg + Vitamin D 800-1000 IU daily; DEXA; consider bisphosphonate
GI protectionPPI if on NSAIDs or high-risk
PJP prophylaxisTMP-SMX if high-dose/prolonged (>20mg × >1 month, especially with other immunosuppression)
VaccinationsPneumococcal, influenza annually

Tapering Principles

  • Risk of HPA suppression: >20mg/day for >3 weeks
  • Slow taper: Reduce by 10-20% every 1-2 weeks
  • Monitor for flare: Symptoms and inflammatory markers
  • Stress dosing: May need increased dose during surgery/illness if HPA suppressed
↑ Back to Table of Contents

32. DMARDs & Biologics

Conventional Synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs)

DrugMechanismMonitoringKey Toxicities
MethotrexateFolate antagonistCBC, LFTs, Cr q8-12wkHepatotoxicity, pneumonitis, cytopenias
LeflunomidePyrimidine synthesis inhibitorCBC, LFTsHepatotoxicity, teratogenic
SulfasalazineAnti-inflammatoryCBCGI upset, rash, cytopenias
HydroxychloroquineMultipleEye exam annually (after 5yr)Retinal toxicity
AzathioprinePurine antagonistCBC, LFTs; TPMT levelCytopenias, hepatotoxicity
MycophenolatePurine synthesis inhibitorCBCGI upset, cytopenias, teratogenic

Biologic DMARDs

ClassDrugsUsesKey Considerations
TNF inhibitorsAdalimumab, Etanercept, Infliximab, Certolizumab, GolimumabRA, SpA, PsA, IBDTB screening, avoid in CHF, demyelinating disease
IL-6 inhibitorsTocilizumab, SarilumabRA, GCAMasks infection (suppresses CRP), monitor lipids
IL-17 inhibitorsSecukinumab, IxekizumabPsA, AS, psoriasisMay worsen IBD
IL-23 inhibitorsUstekinumab, Guselkumab, RisankizumabPsA, psoriasis, IBDGenerally well-tolerated
B-cell depletionRituximabRA, AAV, SLEHep B screening, infusion reactions
T-cell costimulationAbataceptRAAvoid live vaccines
IL-1 inhibitorsAnakinra, CanakinumabGout, autoinflammatoryInjection site reactions
BLyS inhibitorBelimumabSLEApproved for lupus nephritis

Targeted Synthetic DMARDs (JAK Inhibitors)

DrugJAK SelectivityBlack Box Warnings
TofacitinibJAK1, JAK3VTE, serious infections, malignancy, CV events
BaricitinibJAK1, JAK2Same as above
UpadacitinibJAK1 selectiveSame as above
Pre-biologic Checklist:
  • TB screening (IGRA or PPD)
  • Hepatitis B serology (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs)
  • Hepatitis C antibody
  • Update vaccinations (pneumococcal, influenza, Shingrix)
  • No live vaccines while on biologics
↑ Back to Table of Contents

33. Vaccination in Immunosuppression High-Yield

General Principles

  • Update vaccines BEFORE starting immunosuppression when possible
  • Live vaccines contraindicated on most immunosuppressive therapy
  • Inactivated vaccines are safe but may be less effective

Live Vaccines to AVOID

  • MMR
  • Varicella (chickenpox)
  • Zoster (live — Zostavax)
  • Yellow fever
  • Oral typhoid
  • Intranasal influenza
  • BCG

Recommended Vaccines

VaccineRecommendation
Influenza (injectable)Annual
Pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23)All immunosuppressed
Hepatitis BIf seronegative
Shingrix (recombinant zoster)Age ≥50; SAFE on immunosuppression
COVID-19Per current guidelines

Timing with Rituximab

  • Vaccinate ≥4 weeks BEFORE rituximab if possible
  • Vaccinate ≥6 months AFTER rituximab for optimal response
  • B-cell recovery needed for vaccine response
Shingrix (recombinant) is SAFE and RECOMMENDED for immunosuppressed patients age ≥50. Zostavax (live) is contraindicated.
↑ Back to Table of Contents

34. Pregnancy & Rheumatic Disease High-Yield

Medication Safety

SafeContraindicated
HydroxychloroquineMethotrexate
Sulfasalazine (+ folate)Leflunomide
AzathioprineMycophenolate
Low-dose prednisoneCyclophosphamide
CertolizumabWarfarin (1st trimester)
LMWH/HeparinNSAIDs (3rd trimester)

Disease-Specific Considerations

SLE

  • Plan pregnancy during remission (≥6 months)
  • Continue HCQ: Reduces flares, safe
  • Flare risk: Increased during pregnancy/postpartum

Anti-Ro (SSA) Positive

Neonatal lupus / CHB: 2% risk of complete heart block. Monitor fetal HR weekly 16-26 weeks.

Antiphospholipid Syndrome

  • Without prior thrombosis: Low-dose aspirin ± prophylactic LMWH
  • With prior thrombosis: Therapeutic LMWH + aspirin

RA

  • Improvement: 50-75% improve during pregnancy
  • Postpartum flare: Common
↑ Back to Table of Contents

35. Rheumatologic Emergencies Critical

Must-Know Emergencies

Giant Cell Arteritis with Visual Symptoms
• Risk: Permanent blindness within hours
• Action: IV methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days IMMEDIATELY
• Don't wait: Start steroids before biopsy
Scleroderma Renal Crisis
• Presentation: Acute HTN + AKI in diffuse SSc
• Treatment: ACE inhibitor (captopril) — even if creatinine rises
• Avoid: High-dose steroids precipitate SRC
Pulmonary-Renal Syndrome (AAV)
• Presentation: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage + RPGN
• Causes: GPA, MPA, anti-GBM disease
• Treatment: High-dose steroids + rituximab/cyclophosphamide + consider plasma exchange
Catastrophic Antiphospholipid Syndrome
• Definition: ≥3 organs, <1 week, microangiopathy
• Mortality: 30-50%
• Treatment: Anticoagulation + steroids + PLEX + IVIG
Septic Arthritis
• Action: Joint aspiration BEFORE antibiotics (unless septic shock)
• Treatment: IV antibiotics + surgical washout/drainage
• Remember: RA patients on immunosuppression at high risk
Cervical Spine Instability (RA)
• Risk: C1-C2 subluxation → cord compression
• Presentation: Neck pain, myelopathy, sudden death
• Action: Cervical spine imaging before intubation in severe RA
Anti-MDA5 Dermatomyositis with ILD
• Course: Rapidly progressive ILD, often amyopathic
• Mortality: Very high if untreated
• Treatment: Aggressive triple therapy (steroids + calcineurin inhibitor + cyclophosphamide)

Drug Toxicities

DrugToxicityManagement
MTXPneumonitisStop MTX, steroids if severe
MTXPancytopeniaStop MTX, leucovorin rescue
BiologicsSerious infection/sepsisHold biologic, broad-spectrum antibiotics
CyclophosphamideHemorrhagic cystitisMesna prophylaxis, hydration
AllopurinolDRESS/AHSStop immediately, supportive care, steroids
HCQRetinal toxicityStop HCQ, ophthalmology follow-up
↑ Back to Table of Contents

36. Landmark Trials

Rheumatoid Arthritis

TrialFinding
TICORATreat-to-target improves outcomes in RA
RA-BEAMBaricitinib superior to adalimumab in MTX-IR patients
ORAL SurveillanceTofacitinib CV and cancer risks vs TNFi (led to black box)

Lupus

TrialFinding
ALMSMMF non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for lupus nephritis induction
BLISS-LNBelimumab + standard of care improves lupus nephritis outcomes
AURORAVoclosporin added to MMF improves lupus nephritis response

Vasculitis

TrialFinding
RAVERituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for AAV; superior for relapsing disease
PEXIVASReduced-dose steroids non-inferior in AAV; plasma exchange no benefit for most
GiACTATocilizumab effective for GCA, allows lower steroid doses

Gout

TrialFinding
CARESFebuxostat associated with higher CV mortality than allopurinol
FASTFebuxostat non-inferior to allopurinol for CV outcomes (conflicting with CARES)

Antiphospholipid Syndrome

TrialFinding
TRAPSRivaroxaban inferior to warfarin in triple-positive APS — stopped early
↑ Back to Table of Contents

37. Classification Criteria Quick Reference

Key Points About Classification vs Diagnosis

  • Classification criteria are designed for research, not diagnosis
  • Clinicians can diagnose disease even if criteria not fully met
  • But criteria are useful frameworks for clinical reasoning

Summary of Major Criteria

DiseaseKey Criteria Elements
RA (2010 ACR/EULAR)≥6/10 points: joints involved, serology, APR, duration
SLE (2019 ACR/EULAR)ANA ≥1:80 as entry + ≥10 points from clinical/immunologic domains
SSc (2013 ACR/EULAR)≥9 points: skin thickening, fingertip lesions, telangiectasia, Raynaud's, antibodies, PAH/ILD
PMR (2012 ACR/EULAR)Age ≥50 + bilateral shoulder pain + elevated ESR/CRP + scoring system
Gout (2015 ACR/EULAR)≥8 points: clinical, lab, imaging findings; or proven crystals
Axial SpA (ASAS)Sacroiliitis on imaging + ≥1 SpA feature OR HLA-B27 + ≥2 SpA features
GPA/EGPA/MPA2022 ACR/EULAR criteria (score-based with clinical and lab findings)
↑ Back to Table of Contents

38. High-Yield Clinical Pearls

Diagnostic Pearls

  • 💎 Negative ANA essentially rules out SLE — sensitivity >95%
  • 💎 CRP usually normal in SLE flares — if elevated, think infection
  • 💎 Uric acid can be normal during gout attack — don't use to rule out
  • 💎 DIP involvement: OA or PsA, NOT RA
  • 💎 Morning stiffness >1 hour: inflammatory arthritis; <30 min: mechanical
  • 💎 Normal CK: PMR, NOT myositis
  • 💎 Jaw claudication: highly specific for GCA

Treatment Pearls

  • 💎 Start steroids immediately for suspected GCA — don't wait for biopsy
  • 💎 ACE inhibitors for scleroderma renal crisis — even if Cr rises
  • 💎 Don't start/stop ULT during acute gout attack
  • 💎 HCQ for everyone with SLE — reduces flares and mortality
  • 💎 Warfarin (not DOACs) for APS — TRAPS trial
  • 💎 MTX doesn't work for axial SpA — use biologics
  • 💎 Rituximab = preferred for relapsing AAV — RAVE trial

Safety Pearls

  • 💎 Screen for TB before starting biologics
  • 💎 HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol in high-risk populations
  • 💎 Eye exam for HCQ — baseline, then annually after 5 years
  • 💎 Check TPMT before azathioprine
  • 💎 Avoid steroids >15mg in diffuse SSc — precipitates renal crisis
  • 💎 C-spine imaging before intubating severe RA patients
↑ Back to Table of Contents

39. Board-Style Practice Questions

Question 1

A 55-year-old woman presents with 3 months of bilateral shoulder and hip pain with morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. ESR is 85 mm/h. She reports new headaches for 1 week. What is the next best step?

Answer: Start high-dose prednisone (40-60mg) immediately and arrange temporal artery biopsy. New headache in PMR suggests GCA — a medical emergency.

Question 2

A 35-year-old man with inflammatory back pain presents with acute, painful red eye. What is the diagnosis?

Answer: Acute anterior uveitis in ankylosing spondylitis. HLA-B27 positive in >90% of AS.

Question 3

A 28-year-old woman with SLE and positive antiphospholipid antibodies has her first DVT. What anticoagulation?

Answer: Warfarin INR 2-3, indefinitely. DOACs contraindicated in APS (TRAPS trial).

Question 4

A patient with diffuse systemic sclerosis develops acute HTN (210/120) and AKI. Diagnosis and treatment?

Answer: Scleroderma renal crisis. ACE inhibitor (captopril), even if creatinine rises.

Question 5

A 65-year-old man has acute monoarthritis of the first MTP. Uric acid is 6.2 mg/dL. Does this rule out gout?

Answer: No. Uric acid can be normal during acute attacks. Gold standard: arthrocentesis.

Question 6

A patient on hydralazine for 2 years develops arthralgias, fever, pleuritis. ANA+, anti-histone+, anti-dsDNA−, normal complement. Diagnosis?

Answer: Drug-induced lupus. Stop hydralazine.

Question 7

A 30-year-old Turkish man has recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and painful red eye. What additional finding supports the diagnosis?

Answer: Positive pathergy test. This is Behçet's disease.

Question 8

A 25-year-old woman has daily fevers to 39.5°C, salmon-colored rash, arthritis, ferritin 15,000. ANA/RF negative. Diagnosis?

Answer: Adult-onset Still's disease.

Question 9

A 45-year-old woman has bilateral hilar LAD, erythema nodosum, and ankle arthritis. Prognosis?

Answer: Excellent. Löfgren's syndrome usually self-resolves.

Question 10

A 70-year-old has elevated alk phos, normal Ca/PO4/PTH, thickened skull on X-ray. Diagnosis?

Answer: Paget's disease of bone.

Question 11

A patient with widespread pain, fatigue, poor sleep has normal labs. Most effective treatment?

Answer: Aerobic exercise. For fibromyalgia, exercise is most effective.

Question 12

A 60-year-old on prednisone 20mg × 6 months has T-score -1.8 at hip. Management?

Answer: Start bisphosphonate. GIOP has lower treatment threshold.

Question 13

A patient starting adalimumab — which vaccines before therapy?

Answer: Shingrix, pneumococcal, influenza, hepatitis B. Screen for latent TB.

Question 14

A pregnant woman with SLE is anti-Ro positive. Required monitoring?

Answer: Weekly fetal HR monitoring 16-26 weeks for CHB (2% risk).

Question 15

Nephrotic syndrome, restrictive CMP, macroglossia, periorbital purpura. Diagnosis and next step?

Answer: AL amyloidosis. Fat pad biopsy and serum free light chains.

↑ Back to Table of Contents

Rheumatology Study Guide

Internal Medicine Residency Preparation

Version 2.0 | December 2025 | 39 Topics

For educational purposes only. Always use clinical judgment and consult current guidelines.

↑ Back to top

🧠 Neurology

ABIM Board Review — AAN/AHA Guidelines

🎯 Core Neurology Principles

⏱️Stroke: time is brain — tPA within 4.5h, thrombectomy within 24h
🚫No imaging for stable primary headache without red flags
🎯HINTS exam is more sensitive than early MRI for posterior stroke
💊Generic ASMs are effective — reserve brand-name for treatment failures
🔄Epley maneuver for BPPV — costs nothing and is highly effective
🧪LP is required for suspected SAH even if CT is negative
🧠 Stroke ⚡ Seizures 🤕 Headache 🔵 MS 🔄 Movement 🦶 Neuropathy 💪 Myopathy 🧩 Dementia 🦠 CNS Infections 🔍 Neuro Exam

1. Ischemic Stroke Emergency

Definition

Acute neurologic deficit due to cerebral ischemia with infarction on imaging

Etiologies (TOAST Classification)

CategoryExamples%
Large artery atherosclerosisCarotid stenosis, intracranial stenosis20%
CardioembolismA-fib, LV thrombus, endocarditis, PFO25%
Small vessel (lacunar)Lipohyalinosis, HTN-related25%
Other determinedDissection, vasculitis, hypercoagulable5%
CryptogenicUnknown after full workup25%

Clinical Syndromes by Vascular Territory

ArteryClinical Features
MCAContralateral face/arm > leg weakness, hemisensory loss, aphasia (dominant) or neglect (non-dominant), gaze preference toward lesion
ACAContralateral leg > arm weakness, abulia, urinary incontinence
PCAContralateral homonymous hemianopia, visual agnosia, memory deficits
BasilarComa, quadriplegia, locked-in syndrome, cranial nerve deficits
PICA (lateral medulla)Wallenberg syndrome: vertigo, dysphagia, Horner's, crossed sensory loss

Lacunar Syndromes

SyndromeLocationFeatures
Pure motorPosterior limb internal capsule, ponsFace, arm, leg weakness (no sensory/cortical signs)
Pure sensoryThalamusFace, arm, leg numbness
Ataxic hemiparesisPons, internal capsuleWeakness + ipsilateral ataxia
Dysarthria-clumsy handPons, internal capsuleDysarthria + hand clumsiness

Acute Management

IV Alteplase (tPA)

  • Window: Within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
  • Dose: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg); 10% bolus, 90% over 60 min
  • BP goal: <185/110 before, <180/105 after
tPA Contraindications: Recent surgery/trauma (14 days), prior ICH, BP >185/110 uncontrolled, platelets <100K, INR >1.7, glucose <50 or >400, large infarct (>1/3 MCA territory)

Mechanical Thrombectomy

  • Window: Up to 24 hours with favorable imaging (DAWN/DEFUSE-3)
  • Indication: Large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, basilar)
  • Requirements: NIHSS ≥6, mismatch on perfusion imaging
Thrombectomy window extended to 24 hours if CT perfusion shows salvageable tissue (core-penumbra mismatch). Don't exclude based on time alone!

Secondary Prevention

EtiologyPrevention
AtheroscleroticHigh-intensity statin, antiplatelet, BP control, lifestyle
Cardioembolic (A-fib)Anticoagulation (DOAC preferred)
LacunarAntiplatelet, BP control
Carotid stenosis ≥70%CEA or CAS
Dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days after minor stroke/TIA reduces recurrence (CHANCE, POINT trials). Then single antiplatelet.

2. Intracerebral Hemorrhage Emergency

Etiologies

CauseLocationNotes
HypertensionBasal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellumMost common
Cerebral amyloid angiopathyLobar (cortical)Elderly, recurrent, associated with dementia
AnticoagulationAnyExpanding hematoma
Vascular malformationAnyAVM, cavernoma
TumorAnyMetastatic melanoma, RCC, choriocarcinoma
Hemorrhagic conversionIschemic territoryAfter large stroke or thrombolysis

Management

  • BP control: Target SBP <140 mmHg (if presenting SBP 150-220)
  • Reversal of anticoagulation:
    • Warfarin: Vitamin K + 4-factor PCC (or FFP)
    • DOACs: Idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet alfa (Xa inhibitors), or 4F-PCC
  • ICP management: HOB 30°, osmotic therapy if needed
  • Surgery: Consider for cerebellar hemorrhage >3cm with deterioration
Cerebellar hemorrhage: Can rapidly cause brainstem compression. Neurosurgical emergency — evacuate if >3cm or neurologic deterioration.

3. Subarachnoid Hemorrhage Emergency

Etiology

  • Aneurysmal (85%): Berry aneurysm rupture; locations: ACA/AComm (30%), ICA/PComm (25%), MCA (20%)
  • Non-aneurysmal: Perimesencephalic, trauma, AVM

Clinical Features

  • "Thunderclap headache": Worst headache of life, maximal at onset
  • Nausea, vomiting, photophobia
  • Meningismus
  • Altered consciousness, focal deficits (depending on location)
  • "Sentinel headache": Warning leak days before major rupture

Diagnosis

  • Non-contrast CT: >95% sensitive within 6 hours (decreases over time)
  • Lumbar puncture: If CT negative and high suspicion
    • Xanthochromia (yellow CSF from bilirubin)
    • Elevated RBCs that don't clear
  • CTA or DSA: To identify aneurysm

Complications

ComplicationTimingPrevention/Treatment
RebleedingFirst 24 hoursEarly aneurysm repair (clip or coil)
VasospasmDays 3-14 (peak day 7)Nimodipine, "Triple-H" therapy, angioplasty
HydrocephalusAcute or delayedEVD, VP shunt
HyponatremiaDays 2-10SIADH or cerebral salt wasting
SeizuresAny timeProphylaxis controversial (short-term)
Nimodipine 60mg Q4h × 21 days: Improves outcomes after SAH (reduces vasospasm-related infarcts). Give to ALL aneurysmal SAH patients.

4. Transient Ischemic Attack High-Yield

Definition

Transient neurologic dysfunction due to focal ischemia WITHOUT infarction on imaging

ABCD² Score (7-Day Stroke Risk)

FactorPoints
Age ≥601
BP ≥140/90 at presentation1
Clinical: Unilateral weakness2
Clinical: Speech impairment without weakness1
Duration ≥60 min2
Duration 10-59 min1
Diabetes1

Score ≥4: Higher risk, consider admission

Workup

  • Brain MRI with DWI (detect silent infarct)
  • Vessel imaging: CTA/MRA head and neck
  • Cardiac: ECG, telemetry, echo (TTE ± TEE)
  • Labs: CBC, BMP, lipids, glucose, A1c
  • Consider prolonged cardiac monitoring for cryptogenic (loop recorder)

Management

  • Antiplatelet: Aspirin immediately; dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) × 21 days for high-risk
  • Statin: High-intensity
  • BP control: Target <130/80 after acute phase
  • Carotid revascularization: If ipsilateral stenosis ≥50-70%

5. Seizures & Epilepsy Common

Classification

TypeFeatures
Focal (partial)Begins in one hemisphere; aware or impaired awareness
GeneralizedInvolves both hemispheres from onset; tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic, atonic
Focal to bilateral tonic-clonicStarts focal, then generalizes

Epilepsy Definition

  • ≥2 unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart, OR
  • 1 seizure + high recurrence risk (>60%), OR
  • Diagnosis of epilepsy syndrome

Provoked Seizure Causes

VITAMINS: Vascular (stroke), Infection, Trauma, Autoimmune, Metabolic (Na, glucose, uremia), Idiopathic/Ingestion (drugs, alcohol withdrawal), Neoplasm, Sleep deprivation

Workup

  • Labs: BMP (Na, glucose, Ca), tox screen
  • EEG: Interictal abnormalities, classify seizure type
  • MRI brain: Structural lesions
  • LP: If infection suspected

Antiseizure Medications

DrugUseKey Toxicities
LevetiracetamBroad spectrum, first-lineMood changes, irritability
LamotrigineFocal, generalized, pregnancy-safeSJS/TEN (slow titration)
ValproateGeneralized (esp. JME)Teratogenic, hepatotoxicity, weight gain, tremor
CarbamazepineFocal seizuresHyponatremia, aplastic anemia, SJS (HLA-B*1502)
PhenytoinFocal, status epilepticusGingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, osteoporosis, zero-order kinetics
OxcarbazepineFocal seizuresHyponatremia (more than CBZ)
Valproate is teratogenic (neural tube defects). Avoid in women of childbearing potential. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are safer in pregnancy.

When NOT to Treat After First Seizure

  • Provoked seizure with reversible cause
  • Normal EEG and MRI
  • Low recurrence risk

6. Status Epilepticus Emergency

Definition

  • Seizure lasting >5 minutes, OR
  • ≥2 seizures without return to baseline

Treatment Protocol

TimeTreatment
0-5 minABCs, O2, IV access, glucose, thiamine if indicated
5-20 min (emergent)Benzodiazepine: Lorazepam 4mg IV (repeat × 1) OR Midazolam 10mg IM
20-40 min (urgent)2nd line AED: Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg, Valproate 40 mg/kg, OR Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg
>40 min (refractory)Anesthetic: Propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital drip + intubation + continuous EEG
Lorazepam IV is first-line for status epilepticus. If no IV access, give midazolam IM (RAMPART trial showed non-inferiority).

7. Primary Headache Disorders Very Common

Migraine

Diagnostic Criteria

  • ≥5 attacks lasting 4-72 hours
  • At least 2 of: Unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, aggravated by activity
  • At least 1 of: Nausea/vomiting, photophobia + phonophobia
  • Aura: Visual (scintillating scotoma), sensory, or speech; develops over 5-60 min

Treatment

AcutePreventive (if ≥4 headache days/month)
NSAIDs, acetaminophenPropranolol, topiramate, valproate
Triptans (5-HT1B/1D agonists)Amitriptyline
Gepants (CGRP antagonists)CGRP mAbs (erenumab, fremanezumab)
Antiemetics (metoclopramide)Botox (chronic migraine)
Triptans contraindicated in: CAD, uncontrolled HTN, prior stroke, hemiplegic migraine, basilar migraine. Avoid within 24h of ergots.

Tension-Type Headache

  • Bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate
  • No nausea, no more than one of photo/phonophobia
  • Treatment: NSAIDs, acetaminophen; preventive with amitriptyline

Cluster Headache

  • Unilateral severe orbital/temporal pain, 15-180 minutes
  • Autonomic features: Lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis, eyelid edema
  • Occurs in "clusters" (weeks-months) with remission periods
  • Acute treatment: High-flow O2 (100%), sumatriptan SC
  • Preventive: Verapamil, lithium, prednisone bridge
Cluster headache: Male predominance, circadian pattern (often same time nightly), patient paces/agitates (vs migraine patients prefer stillness).

8. Secondary Headache Red Flags High-Yield

SNOOP4 Red Flags

Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) or secondary risk factors (HIV, cancer)
Neurologic symptoms or signs
Onset sudden (thunderclap)
Older age (new onset >50)
Pattern change or progressive
Positional
Precipitated by Valsalva
Papilledema

Secondary Causes

CauseFeatures
SAHThunderclap, "worst headache of life"
MeningitisFever, neck stiffness, photophobia
TumorProgressive, worse in morning, with focal signs
Temporal arteritisAge >50, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, vision changes
Idiopathic intracranial HTNObese young woman, papilledema, pulsatile tinnitus
Intracranial hypotensionPositional (better lying down), post-LP
Cerebral venous thrombosisProgressive, seizures, focal signs, hypercoagulable

9. Multiple Sclerosis Boards Favorite

Epidemiology

  • Young adults (20-40 years), female predominance (3:1)
  • Higher prevalence in northern latitudes

Clinical Presentations

SyndromeFeatures
Optic neuritisUnilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement, APD
Transverse myelitisWeakness, sensory level, bowel/bladder dysfunction
Internuclear ophthalmoplegiaImpaired adduction, nystagmus in abducting eye (MLF lesion)
Lhermitte's signElectric shock down spine with neck flexion
Uhthoff's phenomenonWorsening symptoms with heat/exercise

Disease Types

TypeFeatures%
Relapsing-remitting (RRMS)Discrete attacks with full or partial recovery85%
Secondary progressive (SPMS)Gradual worsening after RRMS phase—
Primary progressive (PPMS)Gradual worsening from onset, no relapses10-15%

Diagnosis (McDonald Criteria 2017)

  • Dissemination in space: ≥2 CNS lesions in different locations (periventricular, cortical, infratentorial, spinal cord)
  • Dissemination in time: ≥2 attacks OR new lesion on follow-up MRI OR simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions
  • CSF: Oligoclonal bands (present in >90%)

Treatment

Acute Relapse

  • IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3-5 days
  • Plasma exchange for steroid-refractory

Disease-Modifying Therapies

TierExamplesNotes
Platform (lower efficacy)Interferons, glatiramerInjectable, well-tolerated
ModerateDimethyl fumarate, teriflunomideOral
High efficacyNatalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumabMonitor for PML (natalizumab)
InductionAlemtuzumab, HSCTAggressive disease
Natalizumab and PML risk: Risk increases with JC virus antibody positivity, prior immunosuppression, and duration >2 years. Check JCV antibody.

10. Parkinson's Disease Common

Cardinal Features

TRAP: Tremor (rest), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability

Clinical Features

  • Tremor: "Pill-rolling," 4-6 Hz, worse at rest, improves with action
  • Rigidity: Cogwheel (ratchety), lead-pipe
  • Bradykinesia: Slow movements, hypomimia, micrographia, shuffling gait
  • Postural instability: Late finding, falls
  • Non-motor: REM sleep behavior disorder, anosmia, constipation, depression

Pathophysiology

  • Loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta
  • Lewy bodies (α-synuclein aggregates)

Treatment

Drug ClassExamplesNotes
Levodopa/carbidopaSinemetMost effective; motor fluctuations with long-term use
Dopamine agonistsPramipexole, ropiniroleYounger patients; impulse control disorders
MAO-B inhibitorsSelegiline, rasagilineMild effect; early disease or adjunct
COMT inhibitorsEntacaponeExtend levodopa effect
AnticholinergicsTrihexyphenidylTremor; avoid in elderly (confusion)
Amantadine—Dyskinesias

Motor Complications

  • Wearing off: End-of-dose deterioration
  • On-off fluctuations: Unpredictable motor state changes
  • Dyskinesias: Involuntary movements at peak dose
REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams) often precedes motor symptoms by years. Consider early PD/synucleinopathy.

11. Atypical Parkinsonism High-Yield

ConditionKey FeaturesResponse to Levodopa
Parkinson's diseaseAsymmetric tremor, slow progressionGood
MSAAutonomic failure (orthostasis, urinary), cerebellar signsPoor
PSPVertical gaze palsy, early falls, axial rigidityPoor
CBDAsymmetric apraxia, alien limb, cortical sensory lossPoor
DLBVisual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, RBDModerate (but sensitive to antipsychotics)
Drug-inducedSymmetric, no tremor, antipsychotic/antiemetic useResolves with drug cessation
Vascular parkinsonismLower body predominant, "lower half parkinsonism"Poor
Red flags for atypical parkinsonism: Early falls, poor levodopa response, symmetric onset, vertical gaze palsy, prominent autonomic failure, hallucinations at presentation.

12. Essential Tremor

Features

  • Bilateral action/postural tremor (4-12 Hz)
  • Hands most common; also head, voice
  • Improves with alcohol
  • Family history common (autosomal dominant)
  • No other neurologic signs

vs Parkinson's Tremor

FeatureEssential TremorParkinson's
TypeAction/posturalRest
FrequencyHigher (8-12 Hz)Lower (4-6 Hz)
SymmetryBilateralAsymmetric
Head tremorCommon ("no-no")Rare
Alcohol effectImprovesNo effect

Treatment

  • First-line: Propranolol, primidone
  • Second-line: Topiramate, gabapentin
  • Refractory: Deep brain stimulation, focused ultrasound

13. Peripheral Neuropathy Common

Pattern Recognition

PatternCauses
Length-dependent (stocking-glove)Diabetes (most common), B12, alcohol, uremia, drugs
Mononeuritis multiplexVasculitis, diabetes, sarcoid, leprosy
PolyradiculopathyGBS, CIDP, Lyme, CMV (HIV)
AutonomicDiabetes, amyloid, autoimmune

Workup

  • Glucose/A1c, B12, TSH
  • SPEP/UPEP (paraprotein)
  • If indicated: HIV, Lyme, ANA, ANCA, paraneoplastic panel
  • EMG/NCS: Axonal vs demyelinating

Axonal vs Demyelinating

FeatureAxonalDemyelinating
NCS amplitudeReducedMay be preserved
Conduction velocityNear normalSlowed
ExamplesDiabetes, toxic, nutritionalGBS, CIDP, Charcot-Marie-Tooth

14. Guillain-Barré Syndrome Emergency

Clinical Features

  • Acute ascending symmetric weakness
  • Areflexia or hyporeflexia
  • Often preceded by infection (Campylobacter, CMV, EBV, influenza, COVID)
  • Nadir by 4 weeks (usually 2-3 weeks)
  • Respiratory failure: Monitor FVC (intubate if <20 mL/kg or declining)
  • Autonomic: BP fluctuations, arrhythmias

Variants

VariantFeatures
AIDPClassic demyelinating form (most common)
AMANAxonal, motor, associated with Campylobacter
Miller FisherAtaxia, ophthalmoplegia, areflexia; anti-GQ1b antibodies

Diagnosis

  • CSF: Albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal WBC)
  • NCS: Demyelinating features (prolonged distal latency, conduction block)

Treatment

  • IVIG OR Plasmapheresis (equally effective)
  • Corticosteroids NOT effective in GBS
  • ICU for respiratory and autonomic monitoring
  • VTE prophylaxis, PT/OT
Monitor FVC every 4-6 hours. Intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF <-30 cmH2O, or >30% decline. Don't wait for hypoxia.

15. Myasthenia Gravis High-Yield

Pathophysiology

  • Autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR) at neuromuscular junction
  • Associated with thymic abnormalities (thymoma 10-15%, hyperplasia 65%)

Clinical Features

  • Fatigable weakness: Worsens with use, improves with rest
  • Ocular: Ptosis, diplopia (most common initial symptoms)
  • Bulbar: Dysarthria, dysphagia, facial weakness
  • Limb: Proximal > distal
  • Diurnal variation: Worse later in day

Diagnosis

  • AChR antibodies: Positive in 85% generalized, 50% ocular
  • Anti-MuSK antibodies: In seronegative patients (more bulbar/respiratory)
  • Ice pack test: Improves ptosis (cooling improves NMJ function)
  • Repetitive nerve stimulation: Decremental response >10%
  • Single-fiber EMG: Most sensitive
  • CT chest: Evaluate for thymoma

Treatment

CategoryTreatment
SymptomaticPyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor)
ImmunotherapyPrednisone, azathioprine, mycophenolate
ThymectomyThymoma or AChR-positive generalized MG
Rapid actionIVIG, plasmapheresis
NewerEculizumab, efgartigimod (FcRn inhibitor)

Myasthenic Crisis

  • Respiratory failure from weakness
  • Triggers: Infection, surgery, medication changes, drugs
  • Treatment: ICU, intubation if needed, IVIG or PLEX
Drugs that worsen MG: Aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, magnesium, neuromuscular blockers, penicillamine.

16. Dementia Syndromes Common

Overview

TypeKey FeaturesPathology
Alzheimer'sInsidious memory loss, language, visuospatialAmyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles
VascularStepwise decline, focal deficits, CVD risk factorsMulti-infarct, white matter disease
Lewy bodyVisual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, parkinsonismα-synuclein Lewy bodies
FrontotemporalPersonality/behavior change (bvFTD) OR language (PPA)Tau or TDP-43
Normal pressure hydrocephalusTriad: Wet, wobbly, wacky (incontinence, gait, dementia)Enlarged ventricles, normal pressure LP

Reversible Causes (Must Exclude)

DEMENTIA: Drugs, Emotional (depression), Metabolic (B12, thyroid), Eyes/ears, Normal pressure hydrocephalus, Tumor, Infection, Alcohol

Workup

  • Cognitive testing (MMSE, MoCA)
  • Labs: B12, TSH, RPR, HIV, metabolic panel
  • Brain MRI
  • Consider: LP (AD biomarkers), PET, genetic testing

Alzheimer's Treatment

  • Cholinesterase inhibitors: Donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine (mild-moderate)
  • NMDA antagonist: Memantine (moderate-severe)
  • Anti-amyloid: Lecanemab, donanemab (early AD with confirmed amyloid)

17. Bacterial Meningitis Emergency

Clinical Features

  • Fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, altered mental status
  • Kernig's sign: Pain with knee extension while hip flexed
  • Brudzinski's sign: Hip flexion with passive neck flexion
  • Petechial rash (Neisseria meningitidis)

CSF Findings

ParameterBacterialViralTB/Fungal
WBC>1000 (PMN)<500 (lymph)<500 (lymph)
Protein↑↑Normal/↑↑↑
Glucose↓Normal↓
Opening pressure↑Normal↑

Empiric Treatment (Don't Delay!)

Age GroupEmpiric Therapy
<50 yearsCeftriaxone + vancomycin
≥50 years or immunocompromisedCeftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (Listeria coverage)
  • Dexamethasone: Give 15-20 min before or with first antibiotic dose (reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis)
Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP. If LP delayed, give empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone immediately. Blood cultures can help identify organism.

18. Viral Encephalitis Emergency

HSV Encephalitis

  • Most common sporadic viral encephalitis
  • Features: Fever, altered mental status, seizures, focal deficits
  • Temporal lobe predilection: Personality changes, aphasia, olfactory hallucinations
  • MRI: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in temporal lobes (often hemorrhagic)
  • CSF: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, RBCs, HSV PCR positive

Treatment

  • Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h × 14-21 days
  • Start empirically if HSV encephalitis suspected — don't wait for PCR
HSV encephalitis is fatal without treatment. Start acyclovir empirically in any patient with encephalitis and temporal lobe findings.

45. High-Yield Board Pearls

Stroke

  • 💎 tPA window 4.5 hours; thrombectomy up to 24h with favorable imaging
  • 💎 Dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) × 21 days after minor stroke/TIA
  • 💎 Normal or rising PaCO2 in acute asthma = impending respiratory failure
  • 💎 Nimodipine for ALL SAH patients × 21 days (vasospasm prevention)

Seizures

  • 💎 Lorazepam IV first-line for status epilepticus
  • 💎 Valproate teratogenic — avoid in pregnancy
  • 💎 Carbamazepine: Check HLA-B*1502 (SJS risk in Asians)

Headache

  • 💎 Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise
  • 💎 Cluster headache: High-flow O2 + sumatriptan SC; verapamil for prevention
  • 💎 New headache >50 years = consider temporal arteritis

Multiple Sclerosis

  • 💎 INO (impaired adduction with nystagmus in abducting eye) = MLF lesion, classic for MS
  • 💎 Oligoclonal bands in CSF in >90% of MS
  • 💎 Natalizumab: Check JCV antibody (PML risk)

Movement Disorders

  • 💎 PD tremor: Rest, 4-6 Hz, asymmetric, pill-rolling
  • 💎 Essential tremor: Action, higher frequency, bilateral, improves with alcohol
  • 💎 Early falls + vertical gaze palsy = PSP

Neuromuscular

  • 💎 GBS: Ascending weakness + areflexia + albuminocytologic dissociation; steroids DON'T work
  • 💎 Myasthenia: Fatigable weakness, AChR antibodies, thymoma association
  • 💎 Aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones worsen MG

Dementia

  • 💎 NPH triad: Wet, wobbly, wacky (incontinence, gait, dementia) — treatable!
  • 💎 DLB: Visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations; sensitive to antipsychotics

CNS Infections

  • 💎 Don't delay antibiotics for LP in suspected meningitis
  • 💎 Dexamethasone before/with antibiotics in bacterial meningitis
  • 💎 HSV encephalitis: Temporal lobe, start acyclovir empirically

🧠 ABIM Neurology Study Guide

AAN • AHA/ASA Guidelines

📚 Clinical Suite 🩺 IM Guide ❤️ Cardio 🩸 Heme/Onc
↑ Back to top

🧠 Psychiatry

Depression • Anxiety • Substance Use • Somatoform • Capacity Evaluation

v1.0 BUILD #8

Depression Anxiety Bipolar Psychosis Substance Use Somatic Board Pearls

1. Major Depressive Disorder — Overview

▼

DSM-5 Criteria

📋 Diagnosis: ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks (must include #1 or #2)

SIG E CAPS mnemonic:

  1. Sleep disturbance (insomnia or hypersomnia)
  2. Interest loss (anhedonia)
  3. Guilt or worthlessness
  4. Energy loss / fatigue
  5. Concentration problems
  6. Appetite changes (increased or decreased)
  7. Psychomotor agitation or retardation
  8. Suicidal ideation

Required: Must include depressed mood OR anhedonia

Severity

  • Mild: 5-6 symptoms, minor impairment
  • Moderate: Between mild and severe
  • Severe: Most symptoms, marked impairment, may have psychotic features

Screening

ToolDescription
PHQ-2 2 questions: depressed mood + anhedonia. Score ≥3 = positive screen → do PHQ-9
