PHQ-9 9 questions = 9 DSM criteria. Score 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 mod-severe, ≥20 severe

Differential Diagnosis

  • Medical causes: Hypothyroidism, anemia, B12/folate deficiency, Cushing's, Parkinson's, stroke, malignancy
  • Medications: Beta-blockers, corticosteroids, interferon, isotretinoin, opioids
  • Substance-induced: Alcohol, cannabis, sedatives
  • Bipolar disorder: Screen for prior manic/hypomanic episodes before starting antidepressant

2. Depression — Treatment

▼

Treatment Approach by Severity

SeverityFirst-line Treatment
Mild Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) OR medication; patient preference
Moderate Medication AND/OR psychotherapy
Severe Medication + psychotherapy; consider ECT if refractory/psychotic

Antidepressant Classes

ClassExamplesKey Features
SSRIs Sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, citalopram First-line; generally well tolerated
SNRIs Venlafaxine, duloxetine, desvenlafaxine Also for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia
Bupropion — No sexual side effects; smoking cessation; lower seizure threshold
Mirtazapine — Sedating, weight gain; good for insomnia, poor appetite
TCAs Amitriptyline, nortriptyline Anticholinergic; cardiac toxicity in overdose; neuropathic pain
MAOIs Phenelzine, tranylcypromine Last resort; dietary restrictions; hypertensive crisis with tyramine
✓ First-Line Choices
  • Sertraline — Good safety profile, fewer drug interactions
  • Escitalopram — Well tolerated, once daily
  • Fluoxetine — Long half-life, good for non-adherence; can be activating

Key Side Effects

Side EffectAgentsManagement
Sexual dysfunction SSRIs, SNRIs Switch to bupropion or mirtazapine; add bupropion
Weight gain Mirtazapine, paroxetine, TCAs Switch to bupropion, sertraline
QTc prolongation Citalopram (dose-dependent) Max 40mg (20mg if >60 or hepatic impairment)
Hypertension Venlafaxine, duloxetine Monitor BP; dose-dependent
Seizures Bupropion Contraindicated in seizure disorder, eating disorders

Treatment Timeline

  • Response onset: 2-4 weeks for initial improvement
  • Full response: 6-8 weeks at therapeutic dose
  • If no response at 4-6 weeks: Optimize dose → switch → augment
  • Duration: Continue at least 6-9 months after remission; lifelong if recurrent
🚫 Black Box Warning

Antidepressants may increase suicidal thinking in patients <25 years, especially in first weeks. Monitor closely. Risk decreases with age; benefit exceeds risk in adults.

3. Suicide Risk Assessment

▼

Risk Factors

🚨 SAD PERSONS Mnemonic
  • Sex (male)
  • Age (elderly, young adults)
  • Depression
  • Previous attempt (strongest predictor)
  • Ethanol/substance abuse
  • Rational thinking loss (psychosis)
  • Social support lacking
  • Organized plan
  • No spouse/partner
  • Sickness (chronic illness)

Assessment Components

  • Suicidal ideation: Passive vs active; frequency, intensity
  • Plan: Specific method, access to means
  • Intent: Does patient intend to act?
  • Prior attempts: Number, lethality, what prevented completion
  • Protective factors: Social support, reasons for living, religious beliefs

Management

Risk LevelAction
Low (passive ideation, no plan, protective factors) Outpatient management with close follow-up
Moderate (ideation with plan, no intent) Safety planning, remove access to means, frequent contact
High (plan + intent, prior attempts, impaired judgment) Psychiatric hospitalization (voluntary or involuntary)

Safety Planning

  1. Recognize warning signs
  2. Internal coping strategies
  3. Social contacts for distraction
  4. Family/friends who can help
  5. Professional resources (crisis line, ED)
  6. Reduce access to lethal means

4. Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)

▼

DSM-5 Criteria

  • Excessive anxiety/worry about multiple events occurring more days than not for ≥6 months
  • Difficulty controlling worry
  • ≥3 of: Restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance
  • Causes significant distress or impairment
  • Not due to substance or medical condition

Treatment

✓ First-Line Treatment
  • SSRIs: Escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine
  • SNRIs: Venlafaxine XR, duloxetine
  • CBT: Equally effective as medication; combination often best

Other Options

  • Buspirone: Non-sedating, no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks; good adjunct
  • Benzodiazepines: Rapid relief but avoid long-term; risk of dependence
  • Pregabalin: Alternative for some patients
  • Hydroxyzine: PRN for acute anxiety
⚠️ Avoid
  • Long-term benzodiazepines (dependence, falls in elderly, cognitive impairment)
  • Beta-blockers (not effective for GAD; useful for performance anxiety only)

5. Panic Disorder

▼

DSM-5 Criteria

  • Recurrent unexpected panic attacks
  • ≥1 attack followed by ≥1 month of:
    • Persistent concern about additional attacks, OR
    • Maladaptive behavior change (avoidance)

Panic Attack Symptoms (≥4)

📋 Panic Attack = Abrupt surge of intense fear with ≥4 of:
  • Palpitations / racing heart
  • Sweating
  • Trembling / shaking
  • Shortness of breath
  • Choking sensation
  • Chest pain
  • Nausea / abdominal distress
  • Dizziness / lightheadedness
  • Chills or hot flashes
  • Paresthesias
  • Derealization / depersonalization
  • Fear of losing control / "going crazy"
  • Fear of dying

Peak within minutes; typically resolves in 20-30 minutes

Treatment

  • First-line: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine) or SNRIs
  • CBT: Highly effective; includes exposure therapy
  • Acute attacks: Benzodiazepine PRN (lorazepam, alprazolam) — limit use
  • Avoid caffeine, stimulants

Differential

  • Hyperthyroidism
  • Pheochromocytoma
  • Cardiac arrhythmia
  • Pulmonary embolism
  • Stimulant/caffeine use
  • Medication side effects

6. Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)

▼

DSM-5 Criteria

Exposure to traumatic event (experienced, witnessed, learned about, or repeated exposure to details) PLUS symptoms from each category lasting >1 month:

CategorySymptoms
Intrusion Flashbacks, nightmares, distressing memories, psychological/physiological reactivity to cues
Avoidance Avoiding memories, thoughts, feelings; avoiding external reminders
Negative cognition/mood Amnesia, negative beliefs, distorted blame, negative emotional state, detachment, anhedonia
Arousal/reactivity Irritability, reckless behavior, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration problems, sleep disturbance

Related Diagnoses

  • Acute Stress Disorder: Same symptoms, 3 days to 1 month post-trauma
  • Adjustment Disorder: Symptoms within 3 months of stressor, don't meet PTSD criteria

Treatment

✓ Evidence-Based Treatments
  • Trauma-focused psychotherapy: Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), EMDR
  • SSRIs: Sertraline, paroxetine (FDA-approved for PTSD)
  • SNRIs: Venlafaxine
  • Prazosin: For PTSD-related nightmares (alpha-1 blocker)
🚫 NOT Recommended for PTSD
  • Benzodiazepines (may worsen outcomes)
  • Antipsychotics as monotherapy
  • Debriefing immediately after trauma

7. Bipolar Disorder

▼

Types

TypeDefinition
Bipolar I ≥1 manic episode (may have depressive episodes)
Bipolar II ≥1 hypomanic episode + ≥1 major depressive episode (no full mania)
Cyclothymia ≥2 years of hypomanic + depressive symptoms not meeting full criteria

Manic Episode Criteria

🔥 DIG FAST Mnemonic (≥3 symptoms, ≥1 week)
  • Distractibility
  • Indiscretion / impulsivity (risky behavior)
  • Grandiosity
  • Flight of ideas / racing thoughts
  • Activity/Agitation increased
  • Sleep decreased (feels rested with little sleep)
  • Talkativeness (pressured speech)

Duration: ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalized)

Severity: Marked impairment or psychotic features

Hypomania vs Mania

  • Hypomania: ≥4 days, change from baseline, observable by others, no marked impairment, no psychosis, no hospitalization
  • Mania: ≥7 days, marked impairment, may have psychosis, often hospitalized

Treatment — Acute Mania

  • Mood stabilizers: Lithium, valproate
  • Atypical antipsychotics: Quetiapine, aripiprazole, olanzapine, risperidone
  • Benzodiazepines: Adjunct for agitation/insomnia

Treatment — Bipolar Depression

✓ First-Line Options
  • Quetiapine (monotherapy)
  • Lurasidone (monotherapy or with lithium/valproate)
  • Olanzapine-fluoxetine combination (OFC)
  • Lamotrigine (good for maintenance, depression prevention)
🚫 Avoid in Bipolar

Antidepressant monotherapy — can trigger mania/rapid cycling. If antidepressant needed, use with mood stabilizer.

Lithium Monitoring

  • Therapeutic range: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L (higher end for acute mania)
  • Baseline: TSH, creatinine, calcium, ECG, pregnancy test
  • Monitoring: Lithium level, TSH, creatinine every 6 months
  • Toxicity signs: Tremor → ataxia → confusion → seizures
⚠️ Lithium Toxicity Risk
  • Dehydration, diarrhea, vomiting
  • NSAIDs (increase lithium levels)
  • ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazide diuretics
  • Renal impairment

8. Schizophrenia & Psychotic Disorders

▼

DSM-5 Criteria for Schizophrenia

  • ≥2 of the following for significant portion of 1 month (≥1 must be #1, 2, or 3):
    1. Delusions
    2. Hallucinations
    3. Disorganized speech
    4. Disorganized or catatonic behavior
    5. Negative symptoms (flat affect, avolition, alogia)
  • Continuous signs for ≥6 months
  • Functional decline
  • Rule out schizoaffective, mood disorders, substances, medical conditions

Positive vs Negative Symptoms

Positive (Excess)Negative (Deficit)
Hallucinations (auditory most common)Flat/blunted affect
Delusions (paranoid, grandiose, referential)Alogia (poverty of speech)
Disorganized speech (tangential, loose associations)Avolition (lack of motivation)
Disorganized behaviorAnhedonia
Social withdrawal

Treatment

✓ Antipsychotics
  • Second-generation (atypical): Risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, lurasidone, paliperidone
  • First-generation (typical): Haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine
  • Clozapine: Reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failed ≥2 adequate trials)

Antipsychotic Side Effects

Side EffectCommon Agents
Metabolic syndrome (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia)Clozapine, olanzapine > quetiapine > risperidone
QTc prolongationZiprasidone, haloperidol, thioridazine
Extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS)First-generation > risperidone > other SGAs
Prolactin elevationRisperidone, paliperidone, haloperidol
AgranulocytosisClozapine (requires REMS monitoring)
SeizuresClozapine

Clozapine Monitoring (REMS)

  • Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) weekly × 6 months → every 2 weeks × 6 months → monthly
  • Hold if ANC <1500; discontinue if <1000
  • Also monitor: metabolic panel, lipids, ECG

Extrapyramidal Symptoms

TypeTimingTreatment
Acute dystonia Hours to days Benztropine or diphenhydramine IM/IV
Akathisia Days to weeks Reduce dose, switch agent; propranolol, benzodiazepine
Parkinsonism Weeks to months Reduce dose, benztropine, amantadine
Tardive dyskinesia Months to years Prevention is key; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine)

9. Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome

▼
🚨 NMS — Life-Threatening Emergency

Cause: Dopamine receptor blockade (antipsychotics, antiemetics, withdrawal of dopamine agonists)

Clinical Features ("FEVER")

  • Fever (hyperthermia, often >40°C)
  • Encephalopathy (altered mental status, confusion)
  • Vitals unstable (autonomic instability — tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis)
  • Elevated CK (often >1000 U/L)
  • Rigidity ("lead-pipe")

Labs

  • Elevated CK (may be >10,000)
  • Leukocytosis
  • Metabolic acidosis
  • Myoglobinuria (risk of AKI)

Treatment

  1. Stop offending agent immediately
  2. Supportive care: IV fluids, cooling, ICU monitoring
  3. Dantrolene (muscle relaxant)
  4. Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist)
  5. Amantadine (alternative)
  6. Benzodiazepines for agitation
  7. Monitor for rhabdomyolysis → AKI

Differential: Serotonin Syndrome

FeatureNMSSerotonin Syndrome
CauseDopamine blockadeSerotonin excess
OnsetDays to weeksHours (rapid)
MuscleRigidity ("lead-pipe")Hyperreflexia, clonus, myoclonus
TreatmentDantrolene, bromocriptineCyproheptadine, benzodiazepines

10. Substance Use Disorders — Overview

▼

DSM-5 Criteria (≥2 in 12 months)

📋 11 Criteria (any substance)
  1. Using more than intended
  2. Unsuccessful efforts to cut down
  3. Significant time spent obtaining/using/recovering
  4. Craving
  5. Failure to fulfill major obligations
  6. Continued use despite social/interpersonal problems
  7. Giving up important activities
  8. Use in hazardous situations
  9. Continued use despite physical/psychological problems
  10. Tolerance
  11. Withdrawal

Severity: 2-3 = mild; 4-5 = moderate; ≥6 = severe

Screening Tools

ToolUse
CAGEAlcohol (Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener) — ≥2 = positive
AUDIT-CAlcohol — 3 questions; ≥4 men, ≥3 women = positive
Single-item screening"How many times in the past year have you used [substance]?"
DASTDrug Abuse Screening Test

11. Alcohol Use Disorder

▼

Alcohol Withdrawal

Time After Last DrinkSymptoms
6-24 hoursTremor, anxiety, diaphoresis, nausea, insomnia
12-48 hoursWithdrawal seizures (generalized tonic-clonic)
12-48 hoursAlcoholic hallucinosis (visual, auditory — with clear sensorium)
48-96 hoursDelirium tremens (DTs): Confusion, agitation, hallucinations, autonomic instability, fever
🚨 Delirium Tremens Risk Factors
  • Prior DTs or withdrawal seizures
  • Heavy, prolonged drinking
  • Concurrent illness
  • Older age

Mortality: Up to 5% if untreated; requires ICU

Treatment of Withdrawal

  • Benzodiazepines: First-line (chlordiazepoxide, lorazepam, diazepam)
  • Use symptom-triggered dosing (CIWA-Ar protocol)
  • Thiamine: Before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
  • Electrolyte repletion (magnesium, potassium)
  • IV fluids

Medications for Alcohol Use Disorder

MedicationMechanismNotes
Naltrexone Opioid antagonist Reduces craving/heavy drinking; oral or monthly injection; contraindicated if on opioids
Acamprosate NMDA/GABA modulation Maintains abstinence; renally cleared; safe in liver disease
Disulfiram Aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor Causes unpleasant reaction with alcohol; requires motivation/adherence
⚠️ Wernicke Encephalopathy (Medical Emergency)

Triad: Confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

Treatment: High-dose IV thiamine (500mg TID × 3 days) BEFORE glucose

If untreated → Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible memory impairment, confabulation)

12. Opioid Use Disorder

▼

Opioid Intoxication

📋 Classic Triad
  • Miosis (pinpoint pupils)
  • Respiratory depression
  • Decreased consciousness

Treatment: Naloxone (Narcan) — 0.4-2mg IV/IM/SC; may need repeated doses

Opioid Withdrawal

NOT life-threatening (unlike alcohol, benzodiazepine withdrawal)

  • Symptoms: Lacrimation, rhinorrhea, yawning, piloerection ("goose bumps"), diarrhea, muscle aches, dilated pupils, anxiety, insomnia
  • Onset: 8-24 hours after last use (short-acting); 36-72 hours (methadone)

Medications for Opioid Use Disorder (MOUD)

MedicationMechanismNotes
Buprenorphine (+ naloxone) Partial μ-agonist X-waiver no longer required; can prescribe in office; ceiling effect (safer)
Methadone Full μ-agonist Must dispense from licensed program (OTP); QTc prolongation
Naltrexone Opioid antagonist Monthly injection (Vivitrol); patient must be opioid-free 7-10 days
✓ Key Points
  • MOUD significantly reduces mortality and overdose
  • Any physician can prescribe buprenorphine (DEA X-waiver eliminated 2023)
  • Long-term/indefinite treatment is appropriate — not "just substitution"

Symptomatic Treatment of Withdrawal

  • Clonidine: Autonomic symptoms (monitor for hypotension)
  • Loperamide: Diarrhea
  • NSAIDs: Muscle aches
  • Ondansetron: Nausea/vomiting
  • Trazodone: Insomnia

13. Other Substance Use

▼

Benzodiazepine Withdrawal

🚨 Potentially Life-Threatening
  • Similar to alcohol withdrawal: anxiety, tremor, seizures
  • Can be delayed (days to weeks) with long-acting agents
  • Treatment: Gradual taper; may substitute long-acting for short-acting

Stimulants (Cocaine, Amphetamines)

  • Intoxication: Euphoria, tachycardia, HTN, hyperthermia, dilated pupils, paranoia, psychosis
  • Complications: MI (even in young), stroke, arrhythmia
  • Treatment: Supportive; benzodiazepines for agitation; avoid beta-blockers alone (unopposed alpha)
  • Withdrawal: "Crash" — fatigue, depression, hypersomnia, hyperphagia (not dangerous)

Cannabis

  • Intoxication: Euphoria, impaired coordination, red eyes, increased appetite, tachycardia
  • Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome: Cyclic vomiting with hot showers for relief; treat with capsaicin cream, haloperidol
  • Withdrawal: Irritability, insomnia, decreased appetite (mild, not dangerous)

Tobacco Cessation

TreatmentDetails
VareniclineMost effective single agent; partial nicotine agonist
Bupropion SRStart 1-2 weeks before quit date; seizure risk
NRTPatch, gum, lozenge, inhaler, spray; can combine
CombinationNRT + varenicline or bupropion may be most effective

14. Somatic Symptom & Related Disorders

▼

Overview

DisorderKey Features
Somatic Symptom Disorder ≥1 distressing somatic symptom + excessive thoughts/feelings/behaviors related to symptoms; ≥6 months
Illness Anxiety Disorder Preoccupation with having/acquiring serious illness; minimal or no somatic symptoms; high health anxiety
Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological) Neurological symptoms (weakness, movement, sensory) incompatible with recognized conditions; not feigned
Factitious Disorder Intentional production/feigning of symptoms for sick role (no external incentive)
Malingering Intentional production/feigning of symptoms for external gain (not a disorder)

Somatic Symptom Disorder

  • Focus is on excessive response to symptoms, not whether symptoms are medically explained
  • Patients are not intentionally producing symptoms
  • Treatment: Scheduled visits, validation, CBT, treat comorbid depression/anxiety, avoid unnecessary tests/procedures

Conversion Disorder

📋 Clinical Clues
  • Symptoms inconsistent with known neurological conditions
  • Positive findings on exam:
    • Hoover sign (hip extension weakness reverses with contralateral hip flexion)
    • Give-way weakness
    • Psychogenic tremor (entrainment with contralateral tapping)
    • Tubular/tunnel vision
  • Often follows stressor

Factitious Disorder vs Malingering

FeatureFactitious DisorderMalingering
Intentional symptomsYesYes
MotivationAssume sick role (internal)External gain (money, avoid work)
Conscious awarenessMay be awareFully aware
ClassificationMental disorderNot a disorder

15. Eating Disorders

▼

Anorexia Nervosa

📋 DSM-5 Criteria
  1. Restriction of energy intake → significantly low body weight
  2. Intense fear of gaining weight despite being underweight
  3. Disturbance in body image perception

Subtypes: Restricting type vs Binge-eating/purging type

Medical Complications

  • Cardiac: Bradycardia, hypotension, arrhythmias, QTc prolongation
  • Electrolytes: Hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, hypomagnesemia
  • Endocrine: Amenorrhea, hypothyroidism, low estrogen/testosterone
  • GI: Constipation, delayed gastric emptying
  • Bone: Osteoporosis
  • Hematologic: Anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia
🚨 Refeeding Syndrome
  • Occurs with nutritional rehabilitation in severely malnourished patients
  • Hypophosphatemia is the hallmark (also low K, Mg)
  • Can cause cardiac failure, arrhythmias, seizures, death
  • Prevention: Start feeding slowly ("start low, go slow"), monitor electrolytes, supplement phosphate

Bulimia Nervosa

  • Recurrent binge eating with compensatory behaviors (purging, laxatives, exercise, fasting)
  • At least weekly for 3 months
  • Self-evaluation unduly influenced by weight/shape
  • Complications: Dental erosion, parotid enlargement, Russell's sign (knuckle calluses), hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, esophageal tears

Binge Eating Disorder

  • Recurrent binge eating WITHOUT compensatory behaviors
  • Often associated with obesity
  • Treatment: CBT, lisdexamfetamine (only FDA-approved medication)

16. Personality Disorders

▼

Clusters

ClusterDescriptionTypes
A ("Odd/Eccentric") Odd, eccentric behavior Paranoid, Schizoid, Schizotypal
B ("Dramatic") Dramatic, emotional, erratic Antisocial, Borderline, Histrionic, Narcissistic
C ("Anxious") Anxious, fearful Avoidant, Dependent, Obsessive-Compulsive

High-Yield Disorders

Borderline Personality Disorder

  • Unstable relationships, self-image, affect
  • Impulsivity, recurrent self-harm/suicidal behavior
  • Fear of abandonment
  • "Splitting" (idealizing → devaluing)
  • Treatment: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT)

Antisocial Personality Disorder

  • Disregard for and violation of rights of others
  • Deceitfulness, impulsivity, aggression, lack of remorse
  • Must be ≥18 with history of conduct disorder before 15

Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder (OCPD)

  • Preoccupation with orderliness, perfectionism, control
  • Ego-syntonic (patient sees as normal)
  • Different from OCD (which is ego-dystonic with intrusive thoughts)

17. Sleep Disorders

▼

Insomnia

  • Difficulty initiating, maintaining, or early morning awakening
  • First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I)
  • Pharmacotherapy (short-term): Low-dose trazodone, melatonin, Z-drugs (zolpidem, eszopiclone)
  • Avoid in elderly: Benzodiazepines, antihistamines, anticholinergics

OSA

  • Diagnosis: Polysomnography (AHI ≥5 with symptoms or AHI ≥15)
  • Treatment: CPAP is first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; oral appliances; surgery for anatomical issues

Restless Legs Syndrome

  • Urge to move legs, worse at rest, worse at night, relieved by movement
  • Check: Iron studies (treat if ferritin <75)
  • Treatment: Gabapentin/pregabalin preferred; dopamine agonists (risk of augmentation)

18. High-Yield Board Pearls

▼

Depression Pearls

  • 💎 PHQ-9 ≥10 = screen positive, needs further evaluation
  • 💎 Screen for bipolar before starting antidepressant (risk of triggering mania)
  • 💎 Bupropion: No sexual side effects; contraindicated in seizures, eating disorders
  • 💎 Antidepressants take 2-4 weeks for initial effect; 6-8 weeks for full response

Anxiety Pearls

  • 💎 SSRIs/SNRIs are first-line for GAD, panic, PTSD
  • 💎 Avoid long-term benzodiazepines — use for acute/short-term only
  • 💎 Prazosin for PTSD nightmares
  • 💎 Beta-blockers only for performance anxiety — not effective for generalized anxiety

Bipolar Pearls

  • 💎 Antidepressant monotherapy is contraindicated in bipolar
  • 💎 Lithium: Narrow therapeutic window; interacts with NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides
  • 💎 Valproate: Teratogenic (neural tube defects) — avoid in pregnancy
  • 💎 Quetiapine is effective for both acute mania and bipolar depression

Substance Use Pearls

  • 💎 Alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures, DTs)
  • 💎 Opioid withdrawal is NOT life-threatening — treat symptoms, start MOUD
  • 💎 Give thiamine BEFORE glucose in suspected Wernicke encephalopathy
  • 💎 Naltrexone: Good for alcohol AND opioid use disorder; contraindicated if on opioids
  • 💎 Any physician can prescribe buprenorphine (X-waiver eliminated)

Psychosis/NMS Pearls

  • 💎 Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; requires ANC monitoring
  • 💎 NMS: Fever + rigidity + altered mental status + elevated CK; treat with dantrolene, bromocriptine
  • 💎 Serotonin syndrome: Rapid onset, hyperreflexia, clonus; treat with cyproheptadine
  • 💎 Tardive dyskinesia: Late-onset, treat with VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine)

Somatic/Capacity Pearls

  • 💎 Conversion disorder: Neurologic symptoms incompatible with anatomy; positive signs on exam
  • 💎 Factitious disorder: Intentional symptoms for sick role (internal motivation)
  • 💎 Malingering: Intentional symptoms for external gain (not a disorder)
  • 💎 Capacity: Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, Choice — decision-specific

🧠 Psychiatry Study Guide

APA • ASAM Guidelines

📚 All Study Guides 💎 Board Pearls 💰 High Value Care
↑ Back to top

🏥 General Internal Medicine

Preventive Care • Perioperative Medicine • Ethics • Quality & Safety

v1.0 BUILD #8

Preventive Care Perioperative Ethics Patient Safety End of Life Board Pearls

1. Preventive Care Overview

▼

Levels of Prevention

LevelDefinitionExamples
PrimaryPrevent disease before it occursVaccinations, smoking cessation, exercise
SecondaryEarly detection of diseaseCancer screening, HTN screening, diabetes screening
TertiaryReduce complications of existing diseaseCardiac rehab, diabetes education, stroke prevention
QuaternaryPrevent overdiagnosis/overtreatmentAvoiding unnecessary tests, deprescribing

USPSTF Grading System

📊 Recommendation Grades
  • Grade A: High certainty of substantial benefit → RECOMMEND
  • Grade B: High certainty of moderate benefit OR moderate certainty of substantial benefit → RECOMMEND
  • Grade C: Moderate certainty of small benefit → OFFER SELECTIVELY based on patient circumstances
  • Grade D: Evidence shows no benefit or harms outweigh benefits → DO NOT OFFER
  • Grade I: Insufficient evidence → Cannot make recommendation
⚠️ Board Pearl

Know the difference between Grade A/B (recommend) vs Grade C (shared decision-making) vs Grade D (do not recommend). ABIM loves testing Grade D recommendations!

2. Cancer Screening Recommendations

▼

USPSTF Grade A/B Cancer Screening

CancerPopulationMethodInterval
Colorectal Age 45-75 Colonoscopy, FIT, FIT-DNA, CT colonography, sigmoidoscopy Varies by method
Breast Women 40-74 Mammography Every 2 years
Cervical Women 21-65 Pap ± HPV cotesting Every 3-5 years
Lung Age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current/quit <15y Low-dose CT Annual

Colorectal Cancer Screening Details

✓ Screening Options (All Acceptable)
  • Colonoscopy: Every 10 years
  • FIT (fecal immunochemical test): Annual
  • FIT-DNA (Cologuard): Every 1-3 years
  • Flexible sigmoidoscopy: Every 5 years
  • CT colonography: Every 5 years

When to Stop Screening

  • Age 75-85: Individualize based on health, prior screening, preferences
  • Age >85: Do not screen
  • Life expectancy <10 years: Generally stop screening

Breast Cancer Screening

  • Age 40-49: Decision to start should be individual (Grade B)
  • Age 50-74: Biennial mammography (Grade B)
  • Age ≥75: Insufficient evidence (Grade I)
  • BRCA carriers: Start screening at age 25-30 with MRI

Cervical Cancer Screening

📋 Current Recommendations
  • Age 21-29: Pap smear every 3 years (no HPV testing)
  • Age 30-65: Pap + HPV every 5 years (preferred) OR Pap every 3 years OR HPV alone every 5 years
  • Age >65: Stop if adequate prior screening (3 consecutive negative Paps or 2 negative cotests in 10 years, most recent within 5 years)
  • Post-hysterectomy: No screening if cervix removed for benign disease

Lung Cancer Screening

LDCT Criteria:

  • Age 50-80 years
  • ≥20 pack-year smoking history
  • Current smoker OR quit within past 15 years
  • No symptoms of lung cancer
🚫 Do NOT Screen (Grade D)
  • Ovarian cancer (CA-125, ultrasound)
  • Prostate cancer (PSA) — routine screening; shared decision for ages 55-69
  • Pancreatic cancer
  • Thyroid cancer (neck palpation, ultrasound)
  • Testicular cancer
  • Bladder cancer

3. Cardiovascular Screening & Prevention

▼

Hypertension Screening

  • Adults ≥18: Screen annually (Grade A)
  • Confirm elevated readings with out-of-office measurements (ABPM or home BP)
  • Diagnosis: ≥130/80 on repeated measurements

Lipid Screening

  • Age 40-75: Screen for dyslipidemia (Grade B)
  • Age 20-39: Screen if other risk factors present
  • Non-fasting lipid panel acceptable for initial screening

Statin Therapy for Primary Prevention

✓ Initiate Statin (USPSTF Grade B)
  • Age 40-75 with ≥1 CVD risk factor (dyslipidemia, diabetes, HTN, smoking)
  • AND 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%

10-Year ASCVD Risk Calculator Inputs

  • Age, sex, race
  • Total cholesterol, HDL, LDL
  • Systolic BP, treatment status
  • Diabetes status
  • Smoking status

Aspirin for Primary Prevention

🚫 Updated 2022 Recommendations
  • Age 40-59, ≥10% ASCVD risk: Small benefit; shared decision-making (Grade C)
  • Age ≥60: Do NOT initiate aspirin for primary prevention (Grade D)

Key trials: ASPREE, ARRIVE, ASCEND showed bleeding risk often exceeds benefit

Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Screening

  • Men 65-75 who have ever smoked: One-time ultrasound (Grade B)
  • Men 65-75 never smoked: Selective screening (Grade C)
  • Women who never smoked: Do not screen (Grade D)

4. Infectious Disease Prevention

▼

Adult Immunization Schedule Highlights

VaccineRecommendationNotes
Influenza Annual, all adults High-dose or adjuvanted for age ≥65
COVID-19 Updated vaccine annually Additional doses for immunocompromised
Tdap/Td Tdap once, then Td every 10 years Tdap each pregnancy (27-36 weeks)
Pneumococcal PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23 All adults ≥65; younger if risk factors
Shingles (RZV) 2 doses, age ≥50 Preferred over live ZVL; OK if immunocompromised
Hepatitis B All adults 19-59; ≥60 with risk factors 3-dose or 2-dose (Heplisav-B) series
HPV Through age 26; shared decision 27-45 2 doses if started <15; otherwise 3 doses
RSV Single dose, age ≥60 Shared decision-making; pregnancy dose available

Pneumococcal Vaccination

📋 2024 Recommendations
  • PCV20 (Prevnar 20): Single dose — simplest approach
  • OR PCV15 + PPSV23: PCV15 first, then PPSV23 ≥1 year later
  • Indications for age <65: Immunocompromising conditions, CSF leaks, cochlear implants, chronic heart/lung/liver disease, diabetes, smoking, alcoholism

Hepatitis Screening

  • Hepatitis B: Screen all adults at least once (Grade B)
  • Hepatitis C: Screen all adults 18-79 at least once (Grade B)

HIV Screening

  • Screen all adults 15-65 at least once (Grade A)
  • Screen younger/older if at increased risk
  • All pregnant women (Grade A)

STI Screening

InfectionWho to Screen
Chlamydia/GonorrheaSexually active women <25; older if risk factors
SyphilisAll pregnant women; MSM; persons at increased risk
HIVAll adults 15-65; all pregnant women

5. Other Important Screenings

▼

Diabetes Screening

  • Adults 35-70 who are overweight/obese: Screen (Grade B)
  • Methods: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, or 2-hour OGTT
  • Screen earlier if additional risk factors (family history, GDM, PCOS, high-risk ethnicity)

Osteoporosis Screening

  • Women ≥65: DEXA scan (Grade B)
  • Postmenopausal women <65: Screen if 10-year fracture risk ≥ that of 65-year-old white woman (use FRAX)
  • Men: No routine screening recommendation

Depression Screening

  • Screen all adults (Grade B)
  • Use validated tool: PHQ-2, PHQ-9, or equivalent
  • Must have systems in place for diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up

Unhealthy Alcohol Use

  • Screen all adults ≥18 (Grade B)
  • Use validated screening tool: AUDIT-C, single question
  • Provide brief behavioral counseling for those with risky drinking

Tobacco Use

  • Ask all adults about tobacco use (Grade A)
  • Provide cessation interventions for those who use tobacco
  • Interventions: behavioral counseling + pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT, bupropion)

Obesity

  • Screen all adults for obesity (Grade B)
  • Offer intensive behavioral interventions for BMI ≥30
⚠️ Common Board Question

What screenings are NOT recommended (Grade D)?

  • Thyroid cancer screening
  • Ovarian cancer screening
  • Routine PSA screening
  • Routine ECG in asymptomatic adults
  • Carotid artery stenosis screening in asymptomatic adults
  • Aspirin for primary prevention in age ≥60

6. Perioperative Medicine Overview

▼

Surgical Risk Categories

RiskCardiac RiskExamples
Low (<1%) <1% MACE Cataract, endoscopy, superficial procedures, breast biopsy
Intermediate (1-5%) 1-5% MACE Orthopedic, urologic, thoracic, abdominal, head/neck
High (>5%) >5% MACE Aortic/major vascular, peripheral vascular

Functional Capacity (METs)

📊 Metabolic Equivalents
  • 1 MET: Eating, dressing, walking around house
  • 4 METs: Climbing a flight of stairs, walking up a hill, heavy housework
  • ≥10 METs: Strenuous sports (swimming, tennis, skiing)

Key threshold: ≥4 METs = adequate functional capacity = low risk regardless of risk factors

Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI)

Risk FactorPoints
High-risk surgery (vascular, thoracic, abdominal)1
History of ischemic heart disease1
History of heart failure1
History of cerebrovascular disease1
Preoperative insulin therapy1
Preoperative creatinine >2 mg/dL1
✓ RCRI Interpretation
  • 0 points: 0.4% risk of major cardiac event
  • 1 point: 0.9% risk
  • 2 points: 6.6% risk
  • ≥3 points: 11% risk

7. Preoperative Cardiac Risk Assessment

▼

ACC/AHA Stepwise Approach

  1. Emergency surgery? → Proceed with risk stratification and modification perioperatively
  2. Active cardiac condition? (unstable angina, decompensated HF, significant arrhythmia, severe valvular disease) → Evaluate and treat before elective surgery
  3. Low-risk surgery? → Proceed without cardiac testing
  4. Functional capacity ≥4 METs? → Proceed without further testing
  5. <4 METs or unknown + elevated risk? → Consider stress testing if it will change management
🚫 When NOT to Order Preoperative Stress Testing
  • Low-risk surgery
  • Patient has good functional capacity (≥4 METs)
  • Result will not change management
  • Revascularization not indicated regardless of result

Preoperative ECG

  • Not routine — obtain only if clinically indicated
  • Consider if: known CVD, arrhythmia, structural heart disease, or high-risk surgery in patients with risk factors
  • NOT recommended for low-risk surgery in asymptomatic patients

Preoperative Echocardiography

  • NOT routine
  • Indicated if: suspected severe valvular disease, new dyspnea of unknown cause, known HF with worsening symptoms
  • Recent echo (<1 year) with no clinical change = no repeat needed

8. Perioperative Medication Management

▼

Beta-Blockers

✓ Continue

Patients already taking beta-blockers should continue perioperatively

🚫 Do NOT Start De Novo

Routine initiation of beta-blockers before surgery is NOT recommended (POISE trial showed increased stroke and death)

Statins

  • Continue in patients already taking
  • Reasonable to start preoperatively in vascular surgery patients

ACE Inhibitors / ARBs

  • May hold on morning of surgery — risk of intraoperative hypotension
  • Resume when hemodynamically stable postoperatively
  • If held, ensure restart before discharge

Antiplatelet Agents

ScenarioManagement
Primary prevention aspirin Hold 7-10 days before surgery
Secondary prevention aspirin Continue perioperatively (except high-bleed-risk surgery)
Recent BMS (<30 days) Delay elective surgery; DAPT required
Recent DES (<6 months) Delay elective surgery if possible
DES >6 months, stable May hold P2Y12 inhibitor 5-7 days; continue aspirin

Anticoagulants

DrugHold Before SurgeryWhen to Resume
Warfarin5 days12-24 hours post-op (low bleed risk)
Dabigatran2-4 days (based on CrCl)24-48 hours post-op
Rivaroxaban/Apixaban2-3 days24-48 hours post-op

Bridging Anticoagulation

⚠️ When to Bridge (BRIDGE Trial)
  • High-risk for thrombosis: Mechanical mitral valve, recent VTE (<3 months), high-risk thrombophilia → Bridge with LMWH
  • Moderate/Low risk Afib: Do NOT bridge — similar thrombotic outcomes, more bleeding

Diabetes Medications

  • Metformin: Hold on day of surgery (lactic acidosis risk with contrast/hypoperfusion)
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Hold 3-4 days before surgery (euglycemic DKA risk)
  • Sulfonylureas: Hold on day of surgery
  • Insulin: Reduce long-acting by 25-50%; hold short-acting

9. Perioperative Pulmonary Evaluation

▼

Risk Factors for Pulmonary Complications

Patient FactorsSurgical Factors
Age >60Thoracic surgery
COPDUpper abdominal surgery
Current smokingSurgery >3 hours
CHFGeneral anesthesia
Functional dependenceEmergency surgery
OSANeurosurgery (head-up position)
Low albumin (<3.5)

Preoperative Pulmonary Function Tests

🚫 PFTs NOT Routinely Recommended
  • PFTs do not predict postoperative pulmonary complications better than clinical assessment
  • Indications: Lung resection surgery (to assess resectability), unexplained dyspnea
  • Patients with known COPD/asthma should be optimized clinically (not with PFTs)

Preoperative Chest X-ray

  • NOT routine
  • Obtain if: acute cardiopulmonary symptoms or signs, new/unexplained findings on exam

Risk Reduction Strategies

  • Smoking cessation: Ideally ≥8 weeks before surgery; any duration helps
  • Optimize COPD/asthma: Ensure on appropriate maintenance therapy
  • Incentive spirometry: Pre- and postoperatively
  • Lung expansion maneuvers
  • Early mobilization
  • Avoid long-acting neuromuscular blockers

10. Perioperative VTE Prophylaxis

▼

VTE Risk Stratification

Risk LevelExamplesProphylaxis
Very Low Minor surgery, mobile patient Early ambulation only
Low Minor surgery, moderate risk factors Mechanical (SCDs) OR pharmacologic
Moderate Major surgery, medical illness LMWH or UFH or fondaparinux
High Hip/knee replacement, hip fracture, major trauma, cancer surgery Pharmacologic + mechanical; extended prophylaxis (4 weeks)

Pharmacologic Options

  • LMWH: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily or 30mg BID
  • UFH: 5000 units SC q8-12h
  • Fondaparinux: 2.5mg SC daily

High-Bleed-Risk Patients

  • Use mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) alone
  • Add pharmacologic when bleeding risk decreases

Extended VTE Prophylaxis

✓ Extended Prophylaxis (4-5 weeks) Recommended
  • Total hip arthroplasty
  • Total knee arthroplasty
  • Hip fracture surgery
  • Major abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery

11. Medical Ethics Principles

▼

Four Core Principles (Beauchamp & Childress)

PrincipleDefinitionExample
Autonomy Respect patient's right to make decisions Informed consent, respecting refusal
Beneficence Act in patient's best interest Recommending appropriate treatment
Non-maleficence Do no harm Avoiding unnecessary procedures
Justice Fair distribution of resources Triage, allocation decisions

Informed Consent

📋 Elements of Valid Informed Consent
  1. Disclosure: Nature of procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives
  2. Understanding: Patient comprehends information
  3. Voluntariness: Decision made without coercion
  4. Competence: Patient has capacity to decide
  5. Consent: Patient agrees to proceed

Exceptions to Informed Consent

  • Emergency: Immediate threat to life, patient unable to consent, no surrogate available
  • Waiver: Patient voluntarily waives right to information
  • Therapeutic privilege: Disclosure would harm patient (rarely justified)
  • Incapacity: Surrogate decision-maker provides consent

12. Decision-Making Capacity

▼

Capacity vs. Competence

  • Capacity: Clinical determination by physician; decision-specific
  • Competence: Legal determination by court; global

Four Elements of Capacity

🧠 Assess Capacity
  1. Understanding: Can patient state the medical problem and proposed treatment?
  2. Appreciation: Does patient acknowledge how this applies to their situation?
  3. Reasoning: Can patient weigh risks/benefits and consider alternatives?
  4. Choice: Can patient clearly communicate a decision?

Key Principles

  • Capacity is decision-specific — patient may have capacity for some decisions but not others
  • Capacity is time-specific — may fluctuate (delirium, medications)
  • Disagreement with physician does NOT equal lack of capacity
  • Capacity may need reassessment if circumstances change
⚠️ Board Pearl

A patient who refuses recommended treatment is NOT automatically lacking capacity. You must assess the four elements. A patient with capacity has the right to refuse any treatment, even life-saving therapy.

When Patient Lacks Capacity

  1. Identify surrogate decision-maker (healthcare proxy > spouse > adult children > parents > other family)
  2. Surrogate should use substituted judgment (what patient would want)
  3. If patient's wishes unknown, use best interest standard

13. Advance Directives

▼

Types of Advance Directives

TypeDescription
Living Will Written document specifying treatment preferences in specific situations (e.g., terminal illness, PVS)
Healthcare Proxy/Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare Designates a person to make healthcare decisions if patient loses capacity
POLST/MOLST Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment — actionable medical orders signed by physician

Key Points

  • Advance directives only take effect when patient lacks capacity
  • Patient can revoke or change at any time while having capacity
  • Oral statements to family/physicians are valid (though written is preferred)
  • Healthcare proxy can override written directive if in patient's best interest

DNR/DNI Orders

  • DNR: No CPR (chest compressions, defibrillation)
  • DNI: No endotracheal intubation
  • DNR/DNI does NOT mean "do not treat" — other interventions can continue
  • DNR must be addressed preoperatively — may be suspended during surgery with patient consent

14. Confidentiality & Privacy

▼

HIPAA Basics

  • Protected Health Information (PHI) cannot be disclosed without patient authorization
  • Applies to all healthcare providers and their business associates
  • Patients have right to access and amend their records

Exceptions to Confidentiality

⚠️ When Disclosure is Permitted/Required
  • Mandatory reporting: Suspected abuse (child, elder, domestic), gunshot wounds, certain infectious diseases
  • Imminent harm to identifiable third party: Duty to warn/protect (Tarasoff)
  • Public health: Reportable diseases to health departments
  • Court order/subpoena
  • Workers' compensation claims
  • When patient consents

Adolescent Confidentiality

  • Minors may consent to care for: STIs, substance abuse, contraception, mental health (varies by state)
  • Emancipated minors can consent to all care
  • Mature minor doctrine may apply

15. End-of-Life Care

▼

Withholding vs. Withdrawing Treatment

📋 Key Concept

Ethically and legally, withholding and withdrawing treatment are equivalent. There is no ethical obligation to continue a treatment that is no longer beneficial or desired.

Palliative Care vs. Hospice

FeaturePalliative CareHospice
PrognosisAny serious illness<6 months life expectancy
Curative treatmentMay continueForegone (comfort focus)
SettingAny (hospital, clinic, home)Usually home or facility
InsuranceStandard billingMedicare Hospice Benefit

Medical Aid in Dying (MAID)

  • Legal in some US states (Oregon, Washington, California, etc.)
  • Requirements: terminal illness (<6 months), decision-making capacity, multiple requests, waiting periods
  • Physician prescribes lethal medication; patient self-administers
  • Distinguished from euthanasia (physician administers — illegal in US)

Palliative Sedation

  • Use of sedatives to reduce consciousness in dying patients with refractory symptoms
  • Ethically acceptable when: symptoms refractory, death imminent, patient/surrogate consent
  • Intent is comfort, not hastening death (principle of double effect)

16. Patient Safety

▼

Types of Medical Errors

TypeDefinitionExample
Adverse Event Harm from medical care (not disease) Drug reaction, surgical complication
Preventable Adverse Event Adverse event due to error Wrong-site surgery, medication error
Near Miss Error caught before reaching patient Wrong medication caught at pharmacy
Never Event Serious, preventable event that should never occur Wrong-site surgery, retained foreign body

Swiss Cheese Model

Errors occur when multiple system failures align (like holes in swiss cheese slices lining up). Improving safety requires multiple layers of defense.

Error Disclosure

✓ When to Disclose
  • All harmful errors should be disclosed to patient/family
  • Disclosure should be: prompt, honest, empathetic, include plan to prevent recurrence
  • Near misses: disclose to patient if appropriate; report internally

Malpractice

Four elements required ("Four D's"):

  1. Duty: Physician-patient relationship exists
  2. Dereliction: Breach of standard of care
  3. Direct causation: Breach directly caused harm
  4. Damages: Patient suffered harm

17. Quality Improvement

▼

Quality Measures

TypeDefinitionExample
Structure Resources and setting ICU nurse-to-patient ratio
Process What is done to patient % patients with MI receiving aspirin
Outcome Result of care Mortality rate, readmission rate

PDSA Cycle

📊 Plan-Do-Study-Act
  1. Plan: Identify problem, develop hypothesis, plan intervention
  2. Do: Implement intervention on small scale
  3. Study: Analyze results, compare to predictions
  4. Act: If successful, implement broadly; if not, revise and repeat

Root Cause Analysis

  • Systematic analysis of adverse event to identify contributing factors
  • Focus on system failures, not individual blame
  • Uses tools like: fishbone diagram, 5 Whys, failure mode analysis

High-Reliability Organizations

  • Preoccupation with failure
  • Reluctance to simplify
  • Sensitivity to operations
  • Commitment to resilience
  • Deference to expertise

18. Interpreting Diagnostic Tests

▼

Key Definitions

TermDefinitionMnemonic
Sensitivity TP / (TP + FN) — ability to detect disease SnNOut: Sensitive test, Negative result rules OUT
Specificity TN / (TN + FP) — ability to confirm absence SpPIn: Specific test, Positive result rules IN
PPV TP / (TP + FP) — probability disease present if positive Affected by prevalence
NPV TN / (TN + FN) — probability no disease if negative Affected by prevalence

Likelihood Ratios

  • LR+ = Sensitivity / (1 - Specificity) — how much more likely positive result in diseased
  • LR- = (1 - Sensitivity) / Specificity — how much more likely negative result in diseased
  • LR+ >10 or LR- <0.1 are most useful

Effect of Prevalence

  • Sensitivity and specificity are constant regardless of prevalence
  • PPV increases with higher prevalence
  • NPV decreases with higher prevalence
⚠️ Board Pearl

For a screening test in low-prevalence population, even with high sensitivity/specificity, PPV will be low (many false positives). This is why screening is targeted to higher-risk populations.

19. Evidence-Based Medicine

▼

Hierarchy of Evidence

  1. Systematic reviews / meta-analyses of RCTs
  2. Individual RCTs
  3. Cohort studies
  4. Case-control studies
  5. Case series / case reports
  6. Expert opinion

Study Types

TypeDescriptionMeasures
RCT Random assignment to intervention/control RR, ARR, NNT
Cohort Exposed vs unexposed followed over time Relative Risk (RR)
Case-Control Cases vs controls, look back at exposure Odds Ratio (OR)
Cross-Sectional Snapshot in time Prevalence

Key Statistics

  • Relative Risk (RR): Risk in exposed / Risk in unexposed
  • Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR): Risk in control - Risk in treatment
  • Number Needed to Treat (NNT): 1 / ARR — patients to treat to prevent one event
  • Number Needed to Harm (NNH): 1 / ARI — patients treated for one adverse event

Bias

TypeDescription
Selection biasNon-random selection of subjects
Recall biasDifferential recall of exposures (case-control)
Observer biasObserver interpretation affected by knowledge
Lead-time biasEarlier detection appears to prolong survival
Length-time biasScreening preferentially detects slower-growing disease
ConfoundingThird variable associated with both exposure and outcome

20. Professionalism

▼

Core Elements

  • Primacy of patient welfare
  • Patient autonomy
  • Social justice
  • Honesty and integrity
  • Confidentiality
  • Commitment to competence

Conflicts of Interest

  • Financial relationships with industry
  • Self-referral
  • Gifts from pharmaceutical companies
  • Disclosure is key — transparency to patients and institutions

Impaired Physicians

  • Obligation to report colleagues who may be impaired
  • Most states have physician health programs
  • Balance patient safety with support for colleague

Sexual Boundaries

  • Sexual contact with current patients is always unethical
  • Sexual contact with former patients may be unethical (depends on nature of prior relationship, power differential, time elapsed)
  • Sexual harassment: zero tolerance

Social Media

  • Never post PHI or patient-identifiable information
  • Maintain professional boundaries
  • Be aware of institutional policies

21. High-Yield Board Pearls

▼

Preventive Care Pearls

  • 💎 Grade D = Do not offer: PSA routine screening, ovarian cancer screening, thyroid cancer screening, carotid ultrasound in asymptomatic
  • 💎 Aspirin primary prevention: Do NOT start in patients ≥60 (Grade D)
  • 💎 AAA screening: Men 65-75 who ever smoked — ONE TIME ultrasound
  • 💎 Stop screening: When life expectancy <10 years (cancer screening unlikely to benefit)

Perioperative Pearls

  • 💎 Functional capacity ≥4 METs (climb a flight of stairs) = low cardiac risk, no testing needed
  • 💎 Continue beta-blockers perioperatively; do NOT start de novo (POISE)
  • 💎 Bridging anticoagulation: Generally do NOT bridge Afib unless mechanical valve or very high risk
  • 💎 Hold SGLT2i 3-4 days before surgery (euglycemic DKA risk)
  • 💎 PFTs NOT routine before non-thoracic surgery

Ethics Pearls

  • 💎 Capacity is decision-specific — assess 4 elements: Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, Choice
  • 💎 Disagreeing with physician ≠ lack of capacity
  • 💎 Withholding = Withdrawing treatment ethically and legally
  • 💎 Break confidentiality: Imminent harm to identifiable third party, mandatory reporting (abuse, certain diseases)

Quality & Safety Pearls

  • 💎 Always disclose harmful errors to patients
  • 💎 PDSA cycle: Plan-Do-Study-Act for QI
  • 💎 Root cause analysis: Focus on systems, not individuals
  • 💎 SnNOut / SpPIn: Sensitive test + negative = rule out; Specific test + positive = rule in

Statistics Pearls

  • 💎 NNT = 1/ARR
  • 💎 Sensitivity/Specificity don't change with prevalence; PPV/NPV do
  • 💎 Lead-time bias: Earlier detection appears to increase survival without actually prolonging life
  • 💎 Intention-to-treat analysis preserves randomization, prevents attrition bias

🏥 General Internal Medicine Study Guide

USPSTF • ACC/AHA • ACP Guidelines

📚 All Study Guides 💎 Board Pearls 💰 High Value Care
↑ Back to top

🌸 Allergy & Immunology

Anaphylaxis • Drug Allergy • Immunodeficiency • Angioedema • Urticaria

v1.0 BUILD #8

Anaphylaxis Drug Allergy Urticaria Angioedema Immunodeficiency Rhinitis Board Pearls

1. Anaphylaxis — Recognition

▼

Definition

Severe, potentially life-threatening systemic allergic reaction with rapid onset (usually minutes to hours after exposure).

Diagnostic Criteria (Any ONE of the following)

🚨 Anaphylaxis Criteria
  1. Acute onset with skin/mucosal involvement (hives, pruritus, flushing, lip/tongue swelling) PLUS respiratory compromise OR hypotension/end-organ dysfunction
  2. Two or more rapidly after exposure to likely allergen:
    • Skin-mucosal involvement
    • Respiratory compromise
    • Hypotension or symptoms of end-organ dysfunction
    • GI symptoms (cramping, vomiting)
  3. Hypotension after exposure to known allergen (SBP <90 or >30% decrease)

Common Triggers

CategoryExamples
FoodsPeanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, milk, eggs, wheat, soy
MedicationsPenicillins, NSAIDs, contrast media, anesthetics
Insect stingsHymenoptera (bees, wasps, hornets, fire ants)
LatexHealthcare workers, spina bifida patients
ExerciseMay be food-dependent
IdiopathicNo identifiable cause (~20%)

Clinical Features by System

SystemManifestationsFrequency
SkinUrticaria, angioedema, flushing, pruritus80-90%
RespiratoryDyspnea, wheezing, stridor, throat tightness70%
CardiovascularHypotension, tachycardia, syncope45%
GINausea, vomiting, diarrhea, cramping45%
CNSAnxiety, confusion, dizzinessVariable
⚠️ Key Point

Skin findings may be ABSENT in 10-20% of anaphylaxis cases. Don't exclude anaphylaxis based solely on absence of hives.

2. Anaphylaxis — Treatment

▼

Immediate Management

✓ First-Line Treatment: EPINEPHRINE
  • Route: IM (anterolateral thigh) — NOT subcutaneous
  • Dose: 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1000 concentration = 1 mg/mL) = 0.3-0.5 mL
  • Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg)
  • May repeat every 5-15 minutes if inadequate response
  • No absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis

Additional Measures

  1. Position: Supine with legs elevated (if hypotensive); sitting if respiratory distress
  2. Remove trigger if possible (stop infusion, remove stinger)
  3. Call for help — activate emergency response
  4. Oxygen: High-flow
  5. IV fluids: 1-2 L NS bolus for hypotension
  6. Airway management: Early intubation if airway compromise

Adjunctive Medications (NOT First-Line)

MedicationRoleNotes
H1 antihistamines Skin symptoms (hives, pruritus) Diphenhydramine 25-50mg; does NOT treat hypotension or airway
H2 antihistamines May add to H1 blockers Famotidine 20mg IV
Glucocorticoids May prevent biphasic reaction Methylprednisolone 125mg IV; NOT immediate benefit
Bronchodilators Bronchospasm Albuterol nebulizer
Glucagon Beta-blocker refractory 1-5mg IV; for patients on beta-blockers not responding to epinephrine
🚫 Common Errors
  • Giving antihistamines INSTEAD of epinephrine
  • Delaying epinephrine
  • Using subcutaneous instead of IM route
  • Using IV epinephrine inappropriately (can cause arrhythmias)

Observation & Biphasic Reactions

  • Observe 4-6 hours minimum (longer if severe or history of biphasic)
  • Biphasic reaction: Recurrence of symptoms after initial resolution (1-20% of cases)
  • Usually within 8-12 hours; can occur up to 72 hours
  • Risk factors: Severe initial reaction, delayed epinephrine, need for multiple doses

Discharge Planning

  • Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen, Auvi-Q)
  • Educate on use and when to use
  • Referral to allergist for trigger identification
  • Medic-Alert bracelet
  • Anaphylaxis action plan

3. Drug Allergy — Overview

▼

Types of Drug Reactions

TypeMechanismTimingExamples
Type I (IgE) IgE-mediated mast cell activation Minutes to hours Anaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema
Type II (Cytotoxic) IgG/IgM antibodies against cell surface Days Hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia
Type III (Immune complex) Antigen-antibody complexes 1-3 weeks Serum sickness, vasculitis
Type IV (Delayed/T-cell) T-cell mediated Days to weeks Contact dermatitis, DRESS, SJS/TEN

Important Distinctions

  • Drug allergy: Immune-mediated (requires prior sensitization)
  • Drug intolerance: Non-immune (e.g., GI upset from erythromycin)
  • Drug toxicity: Predictable based on pharmacology
  • Drug idiosyncrasy: Unpredictable, non-immune (e.g., G6PD hemolysis)

Drugs Most Commonly Causing Allergic Reactions

  • Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins)
  • Sulfonamides
  • NSAIDs
  • Radiocontrast media
  • Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine)
  • Allopurinol
  • Chemotherapy agents

4. Penicillin Allergy

▼

Epidemiology

  • ~10% of patients report penicillin allergy
  • <1% have true IgE-mediated allergy when tested
  • IgE sensitization wanes over time (~50% lose sensitivity after 5 years, ~80% after 10 years)

History Assessment

📋 Key Questions
  • What was the reaction? (rash, hives, anaphylaxis, GI symptoms)
  • How long ago?
  • How long after taking the drug?
  • Were other drugs given simultaneously?
  • Has patient tolerated penicillins since?

Risk Stratification

Risk LevelHistoryAction
Low Non-specific rash, GI symptoms, unknown/family history, remote (>10 years) Consider direct challenge or penicillin skin testing
Moderate Urticaria, pruritic rash within hours-days Penicillin skin testing before use
High Anaphylaxis, angioedema, bronchospasm, SJS/TEN, DRESS Avoid penicillins; use alternative; allergist referral

Cross-Reactivity

⚠️ Penicillin-Cephalosporin Cross-Reactivity
  • Overall cross-reactivity is ~2% (lower than historically thought)
  • Higher with similar R-group side chains (1st gen cephalosporins)
  • Very low cross-reactivity with 3rd/4th generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, aztreonam
  • Aztreonam: Safe in penicillin allergy EXCEPT cross-reacts with ceftazidime (identical side chain)

Penicillin Skin Testing

  • Negative predictive value ~99% for IgE-mediated reactions
  • If negative → safe to give penicillin (with initial observation)
  • If positive → avoid or consider desensitization
  • Does NOT predict non-IgE reactions (SJS, DRESS)

Penicillin Allergy Delabeling

✓ Benefits of Delabeling
  • Access to first-line antibiotics
  • Reduced use of broad-spectrum alternatives
  • Lower cost
  • Reduced antimicrobial resistance
  • Better outcomes (e.g., surgical prophylaxis)

5. Severe Cutaneous Drug Reactions

▼

Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)

🚨 Medical Emergency

Severe mucocutaneous reaction with epidermal necrosis and detachment

Definition by BSA Involvement

  • SJS: <10% body surface area detachment
  • SJS/TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA
  • TEN: >30% BSA

Clinical Features

  • Prodrome: Fever, malaise, upper respiratory symptoms
  • Painful skin with erythematous/purpuric macules
  • Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites): oral, ocular, genital
  • Nikolsky sign positive (epidermis separates with lateral pressure)
  • Atypical targets with necrotic centers

Common Culprits

  • Allopurinol (highest risk)
  • Sulfonamides (TMP-SMX)
  • Anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbital)
  • NSAIDs (piroxicam)
  • Nevirapine

Management

  • Immediately discontinue causative drug
  • Supportive care in burn unit or ICU
  • Fluid/electrolyte management
  • Wound care
  • Ophthalmology consult (ocular complications)
  • Role of systemic steroids, IVIG, cyclosporine controversial

DRESS Syndrome

Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms

Clinical Features

  • Onset: 2-8 weeks after drug initiation
  • Fever, diffuse morbilliform rash, facial edema
  • Lymphadenopathy
  • Eosinophilia and/or atypical lymphocytes
  • Internal organ involvement: Liver (most common), kidney, lung, heart

Common Causes

  • Anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine)
  • Allopurinol
  • Sulfonamides
  • Vancomycin
  • Minocycline

Management

  • Stop offending drug
  • Systemic corticosteroids for severe cases
  • Monitor for organ involvement (LFTs, renal function, cardiac)
  • Prolonged course; may flare during steroid taper
⚠️ HLA Associations
  • HLA-B*5701: Abacavir hypersensitivity — screen before use
  • HLA-B*1502: Carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN in Asian populations — screen before use
  • HLA-B*5801: Allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN/DRESS (Asians, African Americans)

6. NSAID & Aspirin Reactions

▼

Types of NSAID Reactions

TypeMechanismFeaturesCross-Reactivity
NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) COX-1 inhibition → leukotriene overproduction Asthma + nasal polyps + ASA/NSAID sensitivity All COX-1 inhibitors
NSAID-induced urticaria/angioedema COX-1 inhibition Urticaria/angioedema in patients with chronic urticaria All COX-1 inhibitors
Single NSAID-induced IgE-mediated (true allergy) Anaphylaxis, urticaria to specific NSAID Same drug or structurally similar only

AERD (Samter's Triad)

📋 Classic Triad
  1. Asthma (often severe)
  2. Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps
  3. Respiratory reaction to aspirin/NSAIDs

Management

  • Avoid all COX-1 inhibitors
  • COX-2 selective inhibitors (celecoxib) are usually safe
  • Acetaminophen safe at low doses (<1000mg)
  • Aspirin desensitization if aspirin needed (e.g., CAD)
  • Leukotriene modifiers (montelukast)
  • Intranasal steroids
  • Consider biologic therapy for severe asthma

Contrast Media Reactions

Types

  • Immediate: Within 1 hour; urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension
  • Delayed: 1 hour to 1 week; usually maculopapular rash

Premedication for Previous Immediate Reaction

✓ Premedication Regimen
  • Prednisone 50mg PO at 13h, 7h, and 1h before procedure
  • Diphenhydramine 50mg 1h before
  • Use low-osmolar or iso-osmolar contrast

Note: Premedication reduces but doesn't eliminate risk (~10% breakthrough)

7. Urticaria

▼

Definition

Wheals (hives): Raised, erythematous, pruritic plaques that blanch with pressure and resolve within 24 hours

Classification

TypeDurationCause
Acute <6 weeks Often identifiable: infection, food, drug, insect sting
Chronic spontaneous ≥6 weeks Usually idiopathic; rarely autoimmune
Chronic inducible ≥6 weeks Physical trigger: cold, pressure, vibration, cholinergic, solar

Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria

  • Most common form of chronic urticaria
  • Cause identified in <10%
  • Not typically allergic; extensive allergy testing not indicated
  • May be associated with autoimmune thyroid disease
  • Consider checking: CBC, TSH, CRP/ESR

Treatment Algorithm

✓ Stepwise Approach
  1. Step 1: Second-generation H1 antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) — standard dose daily
  2. Step 2: Increase to 2-4× standard dose (off-label but guideline-recommended)
  3. Step 3: Add H2 blocker OR leukotriene antagonist OR switch antihistamine
  4. Step 4: Add omalizumab (anti-IgE; FDA-approved for chronic urticaria)
  5. Step 5: Add cyclosporine (refractory cases)

Key Points

  • Avoid first-generation antihistamines (sedation, anticholinergic effects) — especially in elderly
  • Corticosteroids for short-term flares only; NOT for chronic management
  • Avoid NSAIDs if they exacerbate urticaria

8. Angioedema

▼

Definition

Deep swelling of dermis, subcutaneous, or submucosal tissue. Often affects face, lips, tongue, throat, extremities, genitalia, bowel.

Types

TypeMechanismKey Features
Allergic/Mast cell-mediated Histamine release Usually with urticaria; responds to antihistamines/epinephrine
ACE inhibitor-induced Bradykinin accumulation NO urticaria; doesn't respond to antihistamines/steroids
Hereditary (HAE) C1-INH deficiency/dysfunction NO urticaria; recurrent; family history; can be life-threatening
Acquired C1-INH deficiency Autoantibodies or consumption Associated with lymphoproliferative disorders; older onset

ACE Inhibitor Angioedema

🚨 Important Facts
  • Occurs in 0.1-0.7% of patients on ACEi
  • 5× more common in Black patients
  • Can occur at any time (even after years of use)
  • Usually affects head/neck; can be life-threatening
  • NO urticaria
  • Does NOT respond to epinephrine, antihistamines, or steroids

Management

  • Stop ACE inhibitor permanently
  • Supportive care, airway management
  • Consider icatibant or C1-INH concentrate for severe cases
  • Do NOT substitute ARB (small cross-reactivity risk; avoid if severe reaction)

Hereditary Angioedema (HAE)

Types

  • Type 1 (85%): Low C1-INH level and function
  • Type 2 (15%): Normal level, low function
  • Type 3 (rare): Normal C1-INH; often Factor XII mutation

Diagnosis

📋 Laboratory Findings
  • Low C4 (screening test — low during and between attacks)
  • Low C1-INH level (Type 1) or function (Type 2)
  • C3 is normal (unlike acquired deficiency)

Triggers

  • Trauma, surgery, dental procedures
  • Stress, infections
  • Estrogen (OCPs, pregnancy)
  • ACE inhibitors (contraindicated)

Treatment

SettingTreatment
Acute attack C1-INH concentrate (Berinert, Cinryze), icatibant (bradykinin B2 antagonist), ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor), fresh frozen plasma
Prophylaxis (long-term) C1-INH concentrate, lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein), berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor)
Short-term prophylaxis C1-INH concentrate before procedures
🚫 Ineffective in HAE/Bradykinin-Mediated

Epinephrine, antihistamines, and corticosteroids do NOT work for HAE or ACEi-induced angioedema

9. Primary Immunodeficiency — Overview

▼

Warning Signs (Adults)

⚠️ When to Suspect Immunodeficiency
  • ≥2 serious bacterial infections (sepsis, meningitis, osteomyelitis)
  • ≥3 sinus/ear/pneumonia infections per year requiring antibiotics
  • Infections with unusual organisms
  • Recurrent deep abscesses
  • Infections requiring prolonged or IV antibiotics
  • Family history of immunodeficiency
  • Autoimmune disease with infections

Categories of Primary Immunodeficiency

DefectTypical InfectionsExamples
B-cell (antibody) Encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae), Giardia CVID, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, IgA deficiency
T-cell Viruses, fungi, Pneumocystis, mycobacteria DiGeorge, SCID
Phagocyte Bacteria (Staph, Serratia, Aspergillus) Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD)
Complement Neisseria (meningococcus), encapsulated bacteria, autoimmune disease Terminal complement deficiency

Initial Workup

  • CBC with differential (lymphopenia, neutropenia)
  • Quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM)
  • Specific antibody responses (tetanus, pneumococcal titers)
  • Complement: CH50 (total), C3, C4
  • HIV testing

10. Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)

▼

Overview

  • Most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults
  • Typically diagnosed in 2nd-4th decade
  • Heterogeneous group of disorders

Clinical Features

  • Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (pneumonia, sinusitis, bronchiectasis)
  • GI infections (Giardia, C. difficile)
  • Autoimmune manifestations (ITP, AIHA, thyroiditis)
  • Granulomatous disease (lungs, liver, spleen)
  • Increased risk of lymphoma and gastric cancer
  • Splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy

Diagnosis

📋 Diagnostic Criteria
  • Low IgG (at least 2 SD below mean)
  • Low IgA and/or IgM
  • Poor vaccine responses (to protein and/or polysaccharide antigens)
  • Exclusion of other causes of hypogammaglobulinemia

Treatment

  • Immunoglobulin replacement (IV or subcutaneous) — lifelong
  • Goal: trough IgG >500-800 mg/dL
  • Reduces frequency and severity of infections
  • Prompt antibiotic treatment for infections
  • Avoid live vaccines

11. Selective IgA Deficiency

▼

Overview

  • Most common primary immunodeficiency (~1:500 in Caucasians)
  • Most patients are asymptomatic

Definition

IgA <7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM in patient ≥4 years old, after exclusion of other causes

Clinical Features

  • Many asymptomatic
  • Increased sinopulmonary and GI infections
  • Associated with autoimmune diseases (celiac, thyroiditis, SLE, RA)
  • Increased risk of allergies

Important Considerations

🚨 Transfusion Reactions

Some IgA-deficient patients develop anti-IgA antibodies and can have anaphylactic reactions to blood products containing IgA.

  • Use IgA-depleted blood products or washed RBCs
  • Not all patients develop anti-IgA antibodies

Management

  • Most don't require specific treatment
  • Antibiotics for infections
  • IgG replacement NOT indicated (unless concurrent IgG subclass deficiency with infections)
  • Can progress to CVID in some patients

12. Complement Deficiency

▼

Complement Pathways

PathwayActivationComponents
ClassicalAntigen-antibody complexesC1, C2, C4
AlternativeMicrobial surfaces (direct)Factor B, D, properdin
LectinMannose on microbial surfacesMBL, MASP
Terminal (MAC)Common final pathwayC5, C6, C7, C8, C9

Clinical Associations

DeficiencyClinical Features
Early classical (C1, C2, C4) SLE-like syndrome, autoimmune disease; increased encapsulated bacteria
C3 Severe recurrent infections (encapsulated bacteria)
Terminal (C5-C9) Recurrent Neisseria infections (meningococcus, gonococcus)
C1-INH Hereditary angioedema (see Section 8)
⚠️ Board Pearl

Recurrent Neisseria meningitidis infections → Check terminal complement (CH50). Vaccinate against meningococcus (MenACWY + MenB).

Laboratory Testing

  • CH50: Screens entire classical + terminal pathway; absent if any component missing
  • AH50: Alternative pathway screen
  • C3, C4: Individual components

13. Allergic Rhinitis

▼

Classification

  • Seasonal: Pollens (trees, grasses, weeds)
  • Perennial: Dust mites, mold, animal dander, cockroach
  • Intermittent: <4 days/week or <4 weeks
  • Persistent: ≥4 days/week and ≥4 weeks

Symptoms

  • Nasal congestion, rhinorrhea (clear)
  • Sneezing, nasal pruritus
  • Postnasal drip
  • Ocular symptoms (itching, tearing, redness)
  • Allergic shiners (dark circles under eyes)
  • Allergic salute (transverse nasal crease)

Treatment

✓ Stepwise Approach
  1. Allergen avoidance (if feasible)
  2. Intranasal corticosteroids — MOST EFFECTIVE for moderate-severe
    • Fluticasone, mometasone, budesonide
  3. Second-generation antihistamines — for mild or PRN
    • Cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine
  4. Combination: Intranasal steroid + antihistamine
  5. Add-ons: Leukotriene antagonist, intranasal antihistamine (azelastine)
  6. Allergen immunotherapy — for refractory cases

Allergen Immunotherapy

  • Subcutaneous (SCIT): Weekly buildup → monthly maintenance × 3-5 years
  • Sublingual (SLIT): Daily tablets; FDA-approved for grass, ragweed, dust mite
  • Only treatment that modifies disease course
  • Contraindicated in severe/uncontrolled asthma

Non-Allergic Rhinitis

TypeFeatures
VasomotorTriggered by irritants, temperature changes; no allergic component
Drug-induced (rhinitis medicamentosa)Rebound congestion from decongestant overuse (>3-5 days)
GustatoryClear rhinorrhea with eating
HormonalPregnancy, hypothyroidism

14. Asthma — Allergy Connection

▼

Allergic Asthma

  • Most common asthma phenotype
  • IgE-mediated response to aeroallergens
  • Often associated with allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis
  • Elevated total IgE, specific IgE to allergens

Allergy Testing in Asthma

  • Skin prick testing: Preferred; rapid, sensitive
  • Specific IgE (ImmunoCAP): When skin testing not possible
  • Identify triggers for avoidance
  • Assess candidacy for immunotherapy

Biologic Therapies for Severe Asthma

DrugTargetIndication
Omalizumab Anti-IgE Allergic asthma; elevated IgE; positive aeroallergen testing
Mepolizumab, Reslizumab, Benralizumab Anti-IL-5 / IL-5R Eosinophilic asthma
Dupilumab Anti-IL-4/IL-13 Eosinophilic or oral steroid-dependent asthma
Tezepelumab Anti-TSLP Severe asthma regardless of phenotype

15. Food Allergy

▼

Types of Food Reactions

TypeMechanismTimingSymptoms
IgE-mediated Type I hypersensitivity Minutes to 2 hours Urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis, vomiting
Mixed IgE/non-IgE IgE + cell-mediated Hours Eosinophilic esophagitis, atopic dermatitis
Non-IgE-mediated Cell-mediated Hours to days FPIES, enterocolitis
Food intolerance Non-immune Variable Lactose intolerance (GI symptoms only)

Common Food Allergens ("Big 9")

  • Peanut
  • Tree nuts
  • Milk
  • Egg
  • Wheat
  • Soy
  • Fish
  • Shellfish
  • Sesame

Diagnosis

  • History is most important
  • Skin prick testing: High NPV; positive test doesn't confirm clinical allergy
  • Specific IgE: Same limitations as skin testing
  • Oral food challenge: Gold standard; performed under supervision

Management

  • Strict avoidance of allergen
  • Epinephrine auto-injector — all patients with IgE-mediated food allergy
  • Education on label reading
  • Oral immunotherapy (OIT): FDA-approved for peanut (Palforzia) in children/adolescents
⚠️ Food-Dependent Exercise-Induced Anaphylaxis

Anaphylaxis occurs when exercise follows ingestion of specific food (often wheat, shellfish). Either alone doesn't cause symptoms.

16. Insect Sting Allergy

▼

Hymenoptera

  • Honeybees
  • Wasps, hornets, yellow jackets
  • Fire ants

Types of Reactions

ReactionFeaturesRisk of Future Systemic
Local Redness, swelling at sting site (<10cm) Low (~5%)
Large local Swelling >10cm, peaks 24-48h, lasts days ~10%
Systemic/Anaphylaxis Urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, hypotension 30-60% (if untreated)

Management

Systemic Reaction

  • Epinephrine for acute anaphylaxis
  • Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector
  • Referral to allergist for testing and consideration of venom immunotherapy

Venom Immunotherapy (VIT)

✓ Highly Effective
  • Reduces risk of future anaphylaxis from 30-60% to <5%
  • Indicated for systemic reactions + positive testing
  • 5 years of maintenance; lifelong for severe reactions or mast cell disease

Large Local Reactions

  • Ice, elevation, antihistamines
  • Short course oral steroids if severe
  • Venom immunotherapy NOT routinely recommended (but can be considered)

17. Eosinophilic Disorders

▼

Causes of Eosinophilia

📋 "NAACP" Mnemonic
  • Neoplasm (Hodgkin lymphoma, other malignancies)
  • Allergy/Atopy
  • Adison's disease (adrenal insufficiency)
  • Connective tissue disease (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
  • Parasites (helminths)

Also: Drug reactions (DRESS), hypereosinophilic syndrome, eosinophilic GI disorders

Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE)

  • Chronic immune-mediated esophageal disease
  • Symptoms: Dysphagia, food impaction (adults); feeding difficulties, vomiting (children)
  • Diagnosis: ≥15 eosinophils/hpf on esophageal biopsy (after PPI trial)
  • Treatment: PPI, topical steroids (swallowed fluticasone or budesonide), elimination diets, dilation

Hypereosinophilic Syndrome (HES)

  • Persistent eosinophilia >1500/μL + organ damage
  • Affects heart, lungs, skin, nervous system, GI tract
  • Rule out secondary causes
  • Treatment: Corticosteroids, hydroxyurea, imatinib (if FIP1L1-PDGFRA+)

18. High-Yield Board Pearls

▼

Anaphylaxis Pearls

  • 💎 Epinephrine IM is FIRST-LINE — no absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis
  • 💎 Skin findings may be absent in 10-20% of anaphylaxis
  • 💎 Antihistamines treat hives, NOT hypotension or airway compromise
  • 💎 Biphasic reactions can occur up to 72 hours later; observe 4-6+ hours

Drug Allergy Pearls

  • 💎 <1% of reported penicillin allergies are true IgE-mediated
  • 💎 Penicillin-cephalosporin cross-reactivity is ~2% (not 10%)
  • 💎 Aztreonam is safe in penicillin allergy (except cross-reacts with ceftazidime)
  • 💎 SJS/TEN: Allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants; Nikolsky sign positive
  • 💎 DRESS: 2-8 weeks after drug start; eosinophilia + liver involvement
  • 💎 Screen HLA-B*5701 before abacavir

Angioedema Pearls

  • 💎 ACEi angioedema: No urticaria; doesn't respond to epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids
  • 💎 ACEi angioedema 5× more common in Black patients
  • 💎 HAE: Low C4 (even between attacks); treat with C1-INH concentrate or icatibant
  • 💎 Recurrent angioedema WITHOUT urticaria → think bradykinin-mediated (HAE or ACEi)

Immunodeficiency Pearls

  • 💎 CVID: Most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults; low IgG + poor vaccine responses
  • 💎 Selective IgA deficiency: Most common overall; usually asymptomatic; risk of transfusion reactions
  • 💎 Recurrent Neisseria infections → check terminal complement (CH50)
  • 💎 Encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. flu) → antibody or complement deficiency

Other Pearls

  • 💎 Intranasal corticosteroids are most effective treatment for allergic rhinitis
  • 💎 Chronic urticaria: Up-dose antihistamines 2-4× before adding other agents; omalizumab for refractory
  • 💎 AERD (Samter's triad): Asthma + nasal polyps + ASA/NSAID sensitivity; COX-2 inhibitors usually safe
  • 💎 Venom immunotherapy reduces anaphylaxis risk from 30-60% to <5%
  • 💎 All patients with IgE-mediated food allergy should have epinephrine auto-injector

🌸 Allergy & Immunology Study Guide

AAAAI • ACAAI • WAO Guidelines

📚 All Study Guides 💎 Board Pearls 💰 High Value Care
↑ Back to top

👩‍⚕️ Women's Health

ABIM Board Review — ACOG/USPSTF Guidelines • 4% of Exam

🎯 Core Women's Health Principles

💊LARCs (IUD, implant) are most effective contraception — <1% failure
🤰Gestational diabetes: screen all at 24–28 weeks with 1-hour GCT
🔥Menopausal HRT: use lowest effective dose for shortest duration
🔍Cervical cancer: Pap q3y (21–29) or co-test q5y (30–65)
🧬PCOS: diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria
🦷Osteoporosis: DEXA at 65 or earlier with FRAX-based risk
💊 Contraception 🤰 Pregnancy 🔥 Menopause 🔍 Screening 🧬 PCOS 🦷 Osteoporosis 💎 Pearls

💊 Contraception

▼

Methods by Effectiveness

Tier Method Failure Rate
Tier 1
Most Effective
(<1%)		 Copper IUD (Paragard) 0.8%
Hormonal IUD (Mirena, Kyleena) 0.2%
Implant (Nexplanon) 0.05%
Tier 2
(4-9%)		 Injectable (Depo-Provera) 6%
Pills, Patch, Ring 9%
Tier 3
(12-28%)		 Male condom 18%
Withdrawal, fertility awareness 20-28%
💎 LARC (Long-Acting Reversible Contraception) = IUDs + Implant. First-line for most women due to highest efficacy and no adherence issues.

Combined Hormonal Contraceptives (Estrogen + Progestin)

Forms: Pills (COCs), Patch (Xulane), Vaginal Ring (NuvaRing)

Absolute Contraindications (Category 4)

  • Age ≥35 + smoking ≥15 cigs/day
  • History of VTE, PE, DVT
  • Known thrombogenic mutations (Factor V Leiden, etc.)
  • History of stroke or CAD
  • Migraine with aura (at any age)
  • Breast cancer (current)
  • Severe cirrhosis, hepatocellular adenoma, or hepatoma
  • Hypertension (≥160/100 or with vascular disease)
  • <21 days postpartum (VTE risk)
  • Major surgery with prolonged immobilization

Relative Contraindications (Category 3)

  • Age ≥35 + smoking <15 cigs/day
  • HTN (140-159/90-99) controlled on meds
  • Migraine without aura (age ≥35)
  • History of breast cancer (>5 years, no recurrence)
  • Symptomatic gallbladder disease
  • 21-42 days postpartum + additional VTE risk
✅ Can use estrogen-containing contraceptives: Obesity (alone), diabetes (no vascular complications), well-controlled HTN (<140/90), family history VTE, varicose veins, depression, fibroids, dysmenorrhea.

Progestin-Only Methods

Forms: Mini-pill, Depo-Provera (DMPA), Hormonal IUD, Implant

Advantages over combined methods:
  • Safe with migraine with aura
  • Safe with history of VTE
  • Safe immediately postpartum (including breastfeeding)
  • Safe with hypertension

Depo-Provera (DMPA) Considerations

⚠️ Bone Density Loss:
  • FDA black box: ↓BMD with prolonged use
  • Limit to 2 years unless no alternatives
  • Reversible after discontinuation
Other effects: Weight gain (~5 lbs), irregular bleeding, delay return to fertility (up to 18 months)

IUD Specifics

Type Duration Mechanism Effects on Menses
Copper (Paragard) 10-12 years Spermicidal, prevents fertilization ↑Heavier, longer periods
Mirena (52mg LNG) 8 years Thickens mucus, thins endometrium ↓Lighter → amenorrhea (20%)
Kyleena (19.5mg) 5 years Same as Mirena ↓Lighter periods
Skyla (13.5mg) 3 years Same as Mirena ↓Lighter periods
✅ IUDs are safe in: Nulliparous women, adolescents, history of ectopic pregnancy, immediately post-abortion, and postpartum (can place within 10 min of placental delivery).
❌ IUD Contraindications:
  • Current PID or purulent cervicitis
  • Current pregnancy
  • Unexplained vaginal bleeding (until evaluated)
  • Cervical or endometrial cancer
  • Uterine anomalies distorting cavity
  • Wilson's disease (copper IUD only)

Emergency Contraception

Method Timing Efficacy Notes
Copper IUD ≤5 days >99% MOST effective; provides ongoing contraception
Ulipristal (Ella) ≤5 days ~85% Rx only; ↓efficacy with BMI >26
Levonorgestrel (Plan B) ≤3 days (best) ~75-89% OTC; ↓↓efficacy with BMI >25
💎 Board Pearl: Copper IUD is the MOST EFFECTIVE emergency contraception and works up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse. Also provides ongoing contraception for 10+ years.

Drug Interactions

⚠️ Drugs that ↓ efficacy of hormonal contraceptives:
  • Rifampin (most significant - use backup method)
  • Anticonvulsants: phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, topiramate (>200mg)
  • Some antiretrovirals
  • St. John's Wort
Note: Antibiotics (other than rifampin) do NOT significantly affect efficacy

🤰 Pregnancy Complications

▼

Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy

Disorder Definition Onset
Gestational HTN BP ≥140/90 after 20 wks, NO proteinuria, resolves by 12 wks postpartum >20 weeks
Preeclampsia BP ≥140/90 + proteinuria (≥300mg/24h) OR severe features >20 weeks (typically)
Eclampsia Preeclampsia + new-onset seizures >20 weeks
Chronic HTN HTN before pregnancy or <20 weeks <20 weeks
Superimposed Preeclampsia Chronic HTN + new proteinuria or severe features >20 weeks

Preeclampsia with Severe Features

Diagnosis (any one of):
  • BP ≥160/110 on two occasions ≥4 hours apart
  • Thrombocytopenia (<100,000)
  • Creatinine >1.1 mg/dL or doubling of baseline
  • LFTs >2× upper limit normal
  • Pulmonary edema
  • New-onset headache (unresponsive to meds)
  • Visual disturbances
✅ Management:
