  • Definitive treatment = Delivery
  • Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
  • Antihypertensives: labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine
  • Betamethasone (fetal lung maturity if <34 weeks)
❌ Avoid in pregnancy:
  • ACE inhibitors / ARBs — teratogenic (renal dysgenesis)
  • Nitroprusside — fetal cyanide toxicity
  • Atenolol — IUGR

HELLP Syndrome

Hemolysis (↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, schistocytes)
Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >2× normal)
Low Platelets (<100,000)
Key Points:
  • Variant of severe preeclampsia
  • Can occur without significant HTN or proteinuria
  • Treatment = delivery (regardless of gestational age if severe)
  • Can present with RUQ/epigastric pain (hepatic capsule distension)
  • Complication: hepatic rupture (rare but catastrophic)
💎 Board Pearl: HELLP can mimic TTP/HUS — both have MAHA and thrombocytopenia. Check ADAMTS13 if unclear. In HELLP, delivery resolves the syndrome.

Gestational Diabetes (GDM)

Screening

When Test Positive Result
24-28 weeks (all women) 1-hour 50g glucose challenge ≥130-140 mg/dL → proceed to 3-hour
If 1-hour positive 3-hour 100g OGTT ≥2 abnormal values = GDM
3-hour OGTT Thresholds (Carpenter-Coustan):
  • Fasting: ≥95
  • 1 hour: ≥180
  • 2 hour: ≥155
  • 3 hour: ≥140

Management

  • First-line: Diet and exercise (70-80% achieve control)
  • If inadequate: Insulin (preferred) or metformin/glyburide
  • Targets: Fasting <95, 1-hr postprandial <140, 2-hr <120

Postpartum

⚠️ GDM → Future Type 2 DM Risk:
  • 50% lifetime risk of developing T2DM
  • Screen with 75g OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum
  • Screen every 1-3 years thereafter

Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy

Risk factors: Pregnancy is hypercoagulable state (↑factors, ↓protein S, stasis)
  • VTE risk 5-10× higher than non-pregnant
  • Highest risk: postpartum (especially first 6 weeks)
  • Left leg > right (uterine compression of left iliac vein)

Treatment

Anticoagulant Use in Pregnancy
LMWH (enoxaparin) ✅ Preferred — does not cross placenta
UFH ✅ Safe — does not cross placenta
Fondaparinux ⚠️ Limited data, may consider if heparin allergy
Warfarin ❌ Teratogenic (1st trimester) — warfarin embryopathy
DOACs ❌ Contraindicated — cross placenta, no safety data
💎 Board Pearl: Warfarin is safe during breastfeeding. Can transition postpartum.

Medications in Pregnancy

Category Safe Avoid
Antibiotics Penicillins, cephalosporins, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (not at term) Tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX (1st tri)
Antiemetics Ondansetron, doxylamine-B6, metoclopramide —
Pain Acetaminophen NSAIDs (3rd tri — premature DA closure, oligohydramnios)
Psych SSRIs (some risk), SNRIs Valproate, lithium (1st tri), paroxetine (1st tri)
Seizures Levetiracetam, lamotrigine Valproate (neural tube defects), phenytoin
Anticoagulation LMWH, UFH Warfarin (1st tri), DOACs
Antihypertensives Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa ACE-I, ARBs, atenolol

Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy (AFLP)

Swansea Criteria (≥6 features):
  • Vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria
  • Encephalopathy, hypoglycemia
  • ↑Bilirubin, ↑uric acid, ↑creatinine
  • ↑WBC, ↑ammonia, ↑AST/ALT
  • Coagulopathy, ascites
  • Bright liver on US, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy
AFLP vs HELLP:
  • AFLP: Liver failure predominates (↑↑bilirubin, ↓glucose, coagulopathy, encephalopathy)
  • HELLP: Hemolysis/platelets predominate
  • Both require delivery, but AFLP has higher mortality

🌡️ Menopause & HRT

▼

Definitions

Term Definition
Menopause 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea (average age 51)
Perimenopause Transition period with irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms
Premature ovarian insufficiency Menopause before age 40
Early menopause Menopause between ages 40-45
Diagnosis: Clinical (12 months amenorrhea in woman >45). Labs NOT routinely needed. If uncertain: ↑FSH (>30), ↓estradiol.

Vasomotor Symptoms (Hot Flashes)

Characteristics:
  • Sudden heat sensation, flushing, sweating
  • Lasts 1-5 minutes, occurs multiple times daily
  • More common at night → sleep disruption
  • Average duration: 7 years (can persist >10 years)

Treatment Options

Treatment Efficacy Notes
Hormone Therapy (HT) +++ (best) First-line if no contraindications
Fezolinetant (NK3 antagonist) ++ Non-hormonal, FDA-approved for VMS
SSRIs/SNRIs (paroxetine, venlafaxine) ++ Paroxetine 7.5mg only FDA-approved
Gabapentin + Useful for nighttime symptoms
Clonidine + Modest effect, useful if HTN

Hormone Therapy (HT)

Indications

✅ Appropriate candidates:
  • Bothersome vasomotor symptoms
  • Age <60 or within 10 years of menopause onset
  • No contraindications
  • Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (low-dose vaginal preferred)
  • Premature ovarian insufficiency (HT recommended until age 51)

Regimens

Uterus Status Regimen Rationale
Intact uterus Estrogen + Progestogen Progestogen prevents endometrial hyperplasia/cancer
Hysterectomy Estrogen alone No need for endometrial protection

Absolute Contraindications to Systemic HT

  • Unexplained vaginal bleeding
  • Known or suspected breast cancer
  • Known or suspected estrogen-dependent cancer
  • History of VTE or thrombophilia
  • History of stroke or MI
  • Active liver disease
  • Known or suspected pregnancy

WHI Trial Key Points

E+P (Women's Health Initiative):
  • ↑ Breast cancer (↑26% after 5+ years)
  • ↑ VTE, stroke, CHD (in older women)
  • ↓ Hip fractures, ↓ colon cancer
Estrogen alone (in women post-hysterectomy):
  • ↓ Breast cancer (in E-alone arm)
  • ↑ VTE, stroke
  • No ↑ CHD
💎 "Timing Hypothesis": HT is safer and may provide cardiovascular benefit when initiated in early menopause (<60 yo or <10 years post-menopause). Risk outweighs benefit if started later.

Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause (GSM)

Symptoms: Vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary urgency/frequency, recurrent UTIs

Treatment

  • First-line: Vaginal moisturizers, lubricants
  • If inadequate: Low-dose vaginal estrogen (cream, tablet, ring)
  • Vaginal estrogen is safe even with history of breast cancer (minimal systemic absorption)
  • Ospemifene (oral SERM) — alternative if vaginal application not tolerated

🔬 Cancer Screening

▼

Breast Cancer Screening

Organization Recommendation
USPSTF (2024) Mammography every 2 years, ages 40-74
ACS Annual mammography starting at 45; can start at 40. Every 2 years at age 55+.
ACR Annual mammography starting at 40
⚠️ High-Risk Patients (add MRI):
  • BRCA1/2 mutation carriers
  • First-degree relative with BRCA mutation
  • Lifetime risk ≥20-25% (based on models)
  • History of chest radiation before age 30 (Hodgkin's)
  • Li-Fraumeni, Cowden syndrome
✅ Dense Breasts: ~50% of women. Reduces mammogram sensitivity. Some states mandate notification. Supplemental screening (US, MRI) may be considered.

Cervical Cancer Screening

Age Recommendation (USPSTF/ACS 2020)
<21 No screening (regardless of sexual activity)
21-29 Cytology (Pap) alone every 3 years
30-65 Options: (1) HPV testing alone every 5 years (preferred), (2) Cotesting (Pap + HPV) every 5 years, (3) Cytology alone every 3 years
>65 Stop if adequate prior screening and no history of CIN2+
Post-hysterectomy Stop if no history of CIN2+ and cervix removed
⚠️ Continue screening beyond guidelines if:
  • History of CIN2, CIN3, or AIS → screen for 25 years after treatment
  • HIV-positive or immunocompromised
  • DES exposure in utero
💎 Board Pearl: HPV primary testing (without cytology) is now preferred for ages 30-65 per ACS guidelines. Most cervical cancers are caused by HPV 16 and 18.

Ovarian Cancer Screening

❌ NOT recommended for average-risk women:
  • Neither CA-125 nor transvaginal US improves mortality
  • High false-positive rate leads to unnecessary surgery
  • PLCO trial: No benefit, harm from unnecessary procedures
High-risk women (BRCA1/2): Consider risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy by age 35-40 (BRCA1) or 40-45 (BRCA2). No proven screening strategy.

Endometrial Cancer

  • No routine screening for average-risk women
  • Evaluate abnormal uterine bleeding promptly (especially postmenopausal)
  • Lynch syndrome: Offer annual endometrial biopsy starting age 30-35

🔄 PCOS

▼

Diagnosis (Rotterdam Criteria)

Need 2 of 3:
  1. Oligo/anovulation (irregular or absent menses)
  2. Hyperandrogenism (clinical: hirsutism, acne, alopecia; OR biochemical: ↑testosterone)
  3. Polycystic ovaries on US (≥12 follicles 2-9mm or ovarian volume >10mL)
After excluding other causes (thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, CAH, androgen-secreting tumor)
⚠️ Rule out other causes:
  • TSH (thyroid disease)
  • Prolactin (hyperprolactinemia)
  • 17-OH progesterone (late-onset CAH)
  • Consider DHEA-S if virilization (tumor)

Associated Conditions

  • Metabolic syndrome — screen with lipids, fasting glucose/HbA1c
  • Type 2 DM — 40% will develop impaired glucose tolerance by age 40
  • Obstructive sleep apnea — screen if symptomatic
  • Endometrial hyperplasia/cancer — due to unopposed estrogen
  • Infertility — anovulation
  • Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
  • Depression and anxiety

Management

Lifestyle (First-Line for All)

  • Weight loss (5-10% improves metabolic and reproductive parameters)
  • Exercise
  • Dietary modification

Pharmacologic Treatment

Goal Treatment
Menstrual regulation / Endometrial protection Combined oral contraceptives (first-line) OR cyclic progestins
Hirsutism COCs (first-line) + spironolactone (antiandrogen). Also: hair removal, eflornithine cream
Acne COCs ± topical/oral acne medications
Metabolic (glucose intolerance, DM prevention) Metformin (especially if not on COCs or if prediabetes)
Infertility Letrozole (first-line for ovulation induction) > clomiphene
💎 Board Pearl: Metformin is NOT first-line for menstrual irregularity (use COCs). Metformin's role is for metabolic features and may help with ovulation induction.

🦴 Osteoporosis in Women

▼

Screening

Population Recommendation
All women ≥65 DXA screening
Postmenopausal women <65 Screen if 10-year fracture risk ≥ that of 65 yo white woman (use FRAX)
Men ≥70 DXA screening
T-score interpretation:
  • Normal: T-score ≥ -1.0
  • Osteopenia: T-score -1.0 to -2.5
  • Osteoporosis: T-score ≤ -2.5

When to Treat

Treat if ANY of:
  • Hip or vertebral fracture (clinical or morphometric)
  • T-score ≤ -2.5 at spine, femoral neck, or total hip
  • T-score -1.0 to -2.5 (osteopenia) AND:
    • 10-year hip fracture probability ≥3% (FRAX), OR
    • 10-year major osteoporotic fracture probability ≥20%

Treatment Options

Class Examples Notes
Bisphosphonates Alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid First-line. Duration 3-5 years, then reassess ("drug holiday")
Denosumab Prolia (60mg SC q6mo) Alternative to bisphosphonates. Rebound vertebral fractures if stopped
SERMs Raloxifene ↓Vertebral fractures only. ↑VTE risk. Also ↓breast cancer
Anabolic Teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab For very high-risk (severe OP, prior fractures). Limited duration (2 years)
⚠️ Bisphosphonate Adverse Effects:
  • GI upset, esophagitis (take upright with water)
  • Atypical femur fractures (rare, with prolonged use)
  • Osteonecrosis of jaw (rare, higher risk with IV, dental procedures)
  • Avoid if CrCl <30-35
💎 Board Pearl: After 5 years of bisphosphonates (3 years if IV zoledronic acid), consider "drug holiday" in lower-risk patients. Reassess in 2-3 years. Transition to antiresorptive after anabolic agents.

💎 High-Yield Board Pearls

▼
Contraception:
  1. Copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception
  2. Migraine WITH AURA = absolute contraindication to estrogen-containing contraceptives
  3. IUDs are safe in nulliparous women and immediately postpartum
  4. Only rifampin significantly reduces OCP efficacy (not other antibiotics)
  5. Depo-Provera causes reversible bone loss — limit to 2 years if possible
Pregnancy:
  1. ACE-I and ARBs are teratogenic — contraindicated in all trimesters
  2. HELLP = Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — variant of severe preeclampsia
  3. Magnesium sulfate is used for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia (NOT for BP control)
  4. LMWH is the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy (warfarin is teratogenic)
  5. Gestational diabetes → 50% lifetime risk of type 2 DM
  6. Left leg DVT is more common in pregnancy (uterine compression of left iliac vein)
Menopause:
  1. HRT is most beneficial when started <60 yo or <10 years post-menopause ("timing hypothesis")
  2. Women with intact uterus need estrogen + progestogen (endometrial protection)
  3. Low-dose vaginal estrogen is safe even with history of breast cancer
  4. Paroxetine 7.5mg is the only non-hormonal FDA-approved treatment for hot flashes
Screening:
  1. Cervical cancer screening starts at 21 (regardless of sexual activity)
  2. HPV primary testing is now preferred for ages 30-65
  3. Ovarian cancer screening is NOT recommended (even CA-125 + TVUS)
  4. BRCA carriers: Consider risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy by 35-45
  5. USPSTF 2024: Mammography for all women 40-74 every 2 years
PCOS:
  1. Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3 (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries)
  2. COCs are first-line for menstrual regulation AND hirsutism
  3. Metformin is for metabolic features, NOT first-line for menstrual irregularity
  4. Letrozole > clomiphene for ovulation induction (infertility)
  5. Screen for metabolic syndrome, OSA, and depression
Osteoporosis:
  1. Screen all women ≥65 with DXA; younger if high-risk
  2. Bisphosphonate drug holiday after 5 years (3 years for IV)
  3. Denosumab: Rebound vertebral fractures if stopped — must transition to another agent
  4. Raloxifene reduces vertebral (not hip) fractures AND reduces breast cancer risk
↑ Back to top

👴 Geriatric Medicine

Frailty • Falls • Delirium • Polypharmacy • Goals of Care

v1.0 BUILD #8

Assessment Falls Delirium Dementia Polypharmacy Goals of Care Board Pearls

1. Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment

▼

Components of CGA

DomainAssessment Tools
Functional StatusADLs, IADLs, gait speed
CognitionMMSE, MoCA, Mini-Cog
MoodPHQ-2, PHQ-9, GDS
NutritionMNA, weight loss, BMI
PolypharmacyMedication review, Beers Criteria
Social SupportLiving situation, caregivers, safety
Goals of CareAdvance directives, prognosis discussion

Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)

📋 Basic ADLs — "DEATH"
  • Dressing
  • Eating
  • Ambulating/Transferring
  • Toileting
  • Hygiene (bathing, grooming)

Also: Continence

Instrumental ADLs (IADLs)

📋 IADLs — "SHAFT"
  • Shopping
  • Housekeeping
  • Accounting (finances)
  • Food preparation
  • Transportation

Also: Medications, telephone use, laundry

Frailty

Fried Frailty Phenotype (≥3 = frail, 1-2 = pre-frail):

  1. Unintentional weight loss (>10 lbs or 5% in past year)
  2. Exhaustion (self-reported)
  3. Weakness (low grip strength)
  4. Slow walking speed
  5. Low physical activity
⚠️ Clinical Implications of Frailty
  • Increased mortality
  • Increased hospitalization
  • Increased falls
  • Higher surgical risk
  • Consider when making treatment decisions

2. Falls — Risk Assessment

▼

Epidemiology

  • 1 in 4 adults ≥65 falls each year
  • Leading cause of injury-related death in older adults
  • Falls cause 95% of hip fractures

Risk Factors

IntrinsicExtrinsic
Age ≥65Environmental hazards (rugs, poor lighting)
Prior fallsInappropriate footwear
Gait/balance impairmentLack of assistive devices
Visual impairmentMedications (sedatives, antihypertensives)
Cognitive impairmentAlcohol use
Lower extremity weakness
Vitamin D deficiency
Orthostatic hypotension
Peripheral neuropathy

Screening

✓ Annual Falls Screening (CDC STEADI)
  1. Have you fallen in the past year?
  2. Do you feel unsteady when standing or walking?
  3. Do you worry about falling?

"Yes" to any → Full fall risk assessment

Fall Risk Assessment

  • Timed Up and Go (TUG): Rise from chair, walk 3m, return, sit. ≥12 seconds = increased risk
  • 30-Second Chair Stand: Number of stands in 30 sec. <8 = increased risk
  • 4-Stage Balance Test: Tandem, semi-tandem, side-by-side, one-leg stand
  • Orthostatic vitals
  • Vision screening
  • Medication review
  • Footwear assessment

3. Falls — Prevention & Management

▼

Evidence-Based Interventions

✓ Proven Fall Prevention Strategies
  • Exercise: Balance and strength training (Tai Chi, physical therapy)
  • Vitamin D: 800-1000 IU daily if deficient or at risk
  • Medication review: Reduce/stop fall-risk medications (see Beers)
  • Vision correction
  • Home safety assessment: Remove hazards, improve lighting, grab bars
  • Footwear: Non-slip, low-heeled, well-fitting
  • Assistive devices: Cane, walker as appropriate

High-Risk Medications for Falls

ClassExamplesMechanism
BenzodiazepinesLorazepam, diazepamSedation, impaired balance
Non-benzo hypnoticsZolpidem, eszopicloneSedation
AnticholinergicsDiphenhydramine, oxybutyninSedation, confusion
OpioidsAllSedation, dizziness
AntipsychoticsQuetiapine, haloperidolSedation, orthostasis, EPS
AntihypertensivesAlpha-blockers, diureticsOrthostatic hypotension
AntidepressantsTCAs, SSRIsSedation, hyponatremia
⚠️ Board Pearl

Multifactorial interventions are most effective for fall prevention. Single interventions (except exercise) are less effective than combined approaches.

4. Delirium — Diagnosis

▼

Definition

Acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention and awareness, with cognitive change, not explained by preexisting dementia.

Types

TypeFeaturesNotes
HyperactiveAgitation, restlessness, hallucinationsEasily recognized
HypoactiveLethargy, decreased activity, withdrawalOften missed; worse prognosis
MixedAlternating featuresMost common

Screening Tools

📋 Confusion Assessment Method (CAM)

Delirium = 1 + 2 + (3 or 4)

  1. Acute onset and fluctuating course
  2. Inattention (difficulty focusing, easily distracted)
  3. Disorganized thinking OR
  4. Altered level of consciousness

CAM-ICU

Modified version for intubated/non-verbal patients

Delirium vs Dementia

FeatureDeliriumDementia
OnsetAcute (hours to days)Insidious (months to years)
CourseFluctuatingProgressive, stable day-to-day
AttentionImpaired (hallmark)Usually intact until late
ConsciousnessAlteredUsually normal
ReversibilityUsually reversibleUsually irreversible
🚨 Key Point

Dementia is a major risk factor for delirium. Delirium superimposed on dementia is common and associated with worse outcomes.

5. Delirium — Causes & Management

▼

Common Causes — "I WATCH DEATH"

🔍 Mnemonic
  • Infection (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis)
  • Withdrawal (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids)
  • Acute metabolic (electrolytes, glucose, renal/hepatic failure)
  • Trauma (head injury, pain, surgery)
  • CNS pathology (stroke, seizure, meningitis)
  • Hypoxia
  • Deficiencies (thiamine, B12)
  • Endocrine (thyroid, adrenal)
  • Acute vascular (MI, PE, CHF)
  • Toxins/drugs (anticholinergics, opioids, benzos, steroids)
  • Heavy metals (rarely)

High-Risk Medications for Delirium

  • Anticholinergics: Diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs, antipsychotics
  • Benzodiazepines
  • Opioids (especially meperidine)
  • Corticosteroids
  • H2 blockers (cimetidine, ranitidine)
  • Fluoroquinolones

Workup

  • Complete metabolic panel, glucose
  • CBC
  • Urinalysis, urine culture
  • Chest X-ray
  • Blood cultures if febrile
  • Medication review
  • Consider: TSH, B12, ammonia, drug levels, CT head, LP if indicated

Non-Pharmacologic Management (FIRST LINE)

✓ HELP Protocol / Multicomponent Interventions
  • Reorientation: Clocks, calendars, familiar objects, family presence
  • Sleep promotion: Reduce nighttime interruptions, daytime activity
  • Sensory optimization: Glasses, hearing aids
  • Early mobilization
  • Hydration and nutrition
  • Avoid physical restraints (worsen agitation)
  • Minimize lines, catheters
  • Treat pain

Pharmacologic Management

🚫 Medications Are Second-Line

Use only when patient is a danger to self or others and non-pharmacologic measures have failed.

  • Haloperidol: 0.5-1mg PO/IM; avoid if QTc prolonged, Parkinson's, DLB
  • Atypical antipsychotics: Quetiapine 12.5-25mg, risperidone 0.25-0.5mg
  • Avoid benzodiazepines except for alcohol/benzo withdrawal or seizures

Prevention

Up to 40% of delirium is preventable with multicomponent interventions (HELP program)

6. Dementia — Overview

▼

Definition (Major Neurocognitive Disorder)

Significant cognitive decline in ≥1 domain (memory, executive function, language, visuospatial, social cognition) that interferes with independence in daily activities.

Cognitive Screening Tools

ToolTimeScoring
Mini-Cog3 min3-word recall + clock draw
MMSE10 min0-30; <24 abnormal
MoCA10 min0-30; <26 abnormal; better for MCI

Dementia Types

Type% of CasesKey Features
Alzheimer's 60-80% Gradual onset, memory loss early, hippocampal atrophy
Vascular 10-20% Stepwise decline, focal deficits, CVD risk factors
Lewy Body (DLB) 5-10% Visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations, REM sleep disorder
Frontotemporal 5-10% Personality/behavior changes, language problems, younger onset
Parkinson's dementia — Motor symptoms precede dementia by ≥1 year

Reversible Causes — Rule Out

⚠️ "DEMENTIA" Mnemonic
  • Drugs (anticholinergics, sedatives)
  • Emotional (depression = pseudodementia)
  • Metabolic (thyroid, B12, folate, electrolytes)
  • Eyes/Ears (sensory impairment)
  • Normal pressure hydrocephalus
  • Tumor/Trauma/Toxins
  • Infection (syphilis, HIV, prion)
  • Alcohol

Workup

  • CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, folate
  • Consider: HIV, syphilis, heavy metals if indicated
  • Brain imaging (CT or MRI) — at least once to rule out structural causes
  • Depression screening

7. Dementia — Management

▼

Pharmacologic Treatment — Cognitive Symptoms

ClassAgentsIndicationSide Effects
Cholinesterase Inhibitors Donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine Mild-moderate Alzheimer's, DLB Nausea, diarrhea, bradycardia
NMDA Antagonist Memantine Moderate-severe Alzheimer's Dizziness, confusion, headache
⚠️ Realistic Expectations

Medications provide modest symptomatic benefit at best. They do not cure or significantly slow disease progression. Benefits typically last 6-12 months.

Behavioral & Psychological Symptoms (BPSD)

  • Agitation, aggression
  • Psychosis (hallucinations, delusions)
  • Depression, anxiety
  • Wandering, sleep disturbance

Non-Pharmacologic Approaches (FIRST LINE)

✓ Behavioral Interventions
  • Identify and address triggers (pain, infection, constipation)
  • Structured routine
  • Music therapy, aromatherapy
  • Redirection, validation
  • Caregiver education
  • Safe environment

Pharmacologic — Use Sparingly

🚫 Black Box Warning

Antipsychotics increase mortality in elderly with dementia (1.6-1.7× risk). Use only for severe symptoms when non-pharmacologic approaches fail.

  • Depression: SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram) — avoid TCAs
  • Agitation: Attempt non-pharmacologic first; low-dose antipsychotic if danger to self/others
  • Sleep: Sleep hygiene; melatonin; avoid benzodiazepines

Driving

  • Mild dementia does not automatically preclude driving
  • Assess individually; consider driving evaluation
  • Document discussions and recommendations
  • Know state reporting requirements

Caregiver Support

  • Assess caregiver burnout
  • Respite care
  • Support groups (Alzheimer's Association)
  • Community resources

8. Polypharmacy & Beers Criteria

▼

Definition

  • Polypharmacy: Usually ≥5 medications
  • Hyperpolypharmacy: ≥10 medications

Consequences of Polypharmacy

  • Adverse drug reactions
  • Drug-drug interactions
  • Falls
  • Cognitive impairment
  • Non-adherence
  • Increased healthcare costs

AGS Beers Criteria 2023 — Key Categories

🚫 Potentially Inappropriate Medications in Elderly

Avoid in Most Older Adults

Drug ClassExamplesConcern
First-gen antihistamines Diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine Anticholinergic; confusion, falls
Benzodiazepines All Falls, fractures, cognitive impairment
Non-benzo hypnotics Zolpidem, eszopiclone Falls, fractures, minimal benefit
Muscle relaxants Cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol Anticholinergic; poorly tolerated
Antipsychotics All (for dementia) Increased mortality, stroke
Proton pump inhibitors All (>8 weeks without indication) C. diff, fractures, hypomagnesemia
Sulfonylureas (long-acting) Glyburide, glimepiride Prolonged hypoglycemia
NSAIDs All (chronic use) GI bleed, AKI, CV events
Alpha-blockers Prazosin, doxazosin Orthostatic hypotension

Anticholinergic Burden

High anticholinergic burden = cognitive impairment, falls, delirium

Common anticholinergic medications:

  • Diphenhydramine
  • Oxybutynin, tolterodine
  • TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
  • First-generation antipsychotics
  • Paroxetine (more than other SSRIs)

Deprescribing

✓ Deprescribing Approach
  1. Review all medications (including OTC, supplements)
  2. Identify potentially inappropriate medications
  3. Assess each medication: Is there still an indication?
  4. Prioritize which to stop based on harm/benefit
  5. Taper when appropriate (don't abruptly stop)
  6. Monitor for withdrawal or recurrence

9. Pressure Injuries

▼

Staging

StageDescription
Stage 1Non-blanchable erythema of intact skin
Stage 2Partial-thickness loss (dermis); blister or shallow ulcer
Stage 3Full-thickness loss; subcutaneous fat visible; no bone/tendon
Stage 4Full-thickness with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle
UnstageableObscured by slough or eschar
Deep Tissue InjuryPurple/maroon discoloration; suspected deep tissue damage

Risk Factors

  • Immobility
  • Malnutrition
  • Incontinence
  • Decreased sensation
  • Vascular disease

Prevention

  • Reposition every 2 hours
  • Pressure-redistributing surfaces
  • Keep skin clean and dry
  • Optimize nutrition
  • Use Braden Scale for risk assessment

Treatment

  • Offload pressure
  • Debride necrotic tissue (except stable heel eschar)
  • Keep wound moist (hydrocolloid, foam, alginate dressings)
  • Treat infection if present
  • Optimize nutrition (protein, vitamin C, zinc)
  • Consider negative pressure wound therapy for deep wounds

10. Urinary Incontinence

▼

Types

TypeMechanismFeatures
Stress Weak pelvic floor/sphincter Leakage with cough, sneeze, exertion; more common in women
Urge Detrusor overactivity Sudden urge, unable to delay; frequency, nocturia
Overflow Bladder outlet obstruction or detrusor weakness Dribbling, incomplete emptying; elevated PVR; more common in men (BPH)
Functional Physical or cognitive barriers Can't get to toilet in time; dementia, immobility
Mixed Combination Usually stress + urge

Transient Causes — "DIAPPERS"

  • Delirium
  • Infection (UTI)
  • Atrophic vaginitis
  • Pharmaceuticals (diuretics, anticholinergics, sedatives)
  • Psychological (depression)
  • Excessive urine output (hyperglycemia, CHF, hypercalcemia)
  • Restricted mobility
  • Stool impaction

Treatment by Type

TypeFirst-LineOther Options
Stress Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) Pessary, surgery (sling)
Urge Bladder training, pelvic floor exercises Antimuscarinics (use cautiously in elderly)
Overflow Treat obstruction (BPH meds, TURP) Intermittent catheterization if detrusor failure
Functional Scheduled toileting, improve access Treat underlying condition
🚫 Avoid in Elderly

Anticholinergic medications (oxybutynin, tolterodine) — high risk of confusion, falls. If needed, use lower doses or consider mirabegron (beta-3 agonist).

11. Goals of Care & Advance Care Planning

▼

Advance Care Planning Components

  • Advance directive: Written document of healthcare preferences
  • Healthcare proxy/POA: Designated decision-maker
  • POLST/MOLST: Actionable physician orders for seriously ill
  • Goals of care discussion: Ongoing conversation about values and preferences

Prognostic Indicators for Life-Limiting Illness

  • Multiple hospitalizations in past year
  • Functional decline
  • Unintentional weight loss >10%
  • "Would you be surprised if this patient died in the next year?"
  • Increasing symptom burden

Goals of Care Conversation Framework

📋 SPIKES Protocol (for serious news)
  1. Setting — private, uninterrupted
  2. Perception — assess what patient/family understands
  3. Invitation — ask how much they want to know
  4. Knowledge — share information clearly
  5. Empathy — acknowledge emotions
  6. Strategy — discuss plan, offer support

Key Questions to Ask

  • "What is your understanding of your illness?"
  • "What matters most to you?"
  • "What are you hoping for?"
  • "What are you worried about?"
  • "If you became sicker, what would be most important to you?"

12. Palliative Care & Hospice

▼

Palliative Care vs Hospice

FeaturePalliative CareHospice
PrognosisAny serious illness<6 months life expectancy
Curative treatmentMay continueForegone (comfort focus)
SettingAny (hospital, clinic, home)Usually home or facility
InsuranceStandard billingMedicare Hospice Benefit
EligibilityAnyone with serious illnessCertified terminal illness

Common Symptoms in End-of-Life

SymptomManagement
Pain Opioids (morphine, hydromorphone); follow WHO ladder; no ceiling dose for cancer pain
Dyspnea Low-dose opioids (morphine); fan, positioning; supplemental O2 if hypoxic
Nausea Ondansetron, metoclopramide, haloperidol (based on cause)
Secretions ("death rattle") Glycopyrrolate, atropine (reduces new secretions; suctioning often ineffective)
Agitation/delirium Treat reversible causes; haloperidol, lorazepam
Constipation Prophylactic laxatives with opioids (senna, polyethylene glycol)
✓ Key Principle

Morphine for dyspnea is appropriate and does NOT hasten death when used properly. Opioids reduce sensation of breathlessness.

13. Elder Abuse

▼

Types

  • Physical: Bruises, fractures, burns, restraint marks
  • Emotional/Psychological: Humiliation, threats, isolation
  • Sexual: Non-consensual contact
  • Financial: Exploitation, theft, coercion
  • Neglect: Failure to provide care, nutrition, hygiene
  • Self-neglect: Patient fails to care for self

Risk Factors

  • Cognitive impairment
  • Functional dependence
  • Social isolation
  • Caregiver stress/burnout
  • Caregiver substance abuse or mental illness
  • Shared living situation

Red Flags on Exam

  • Unexplained injuries
  • Injuries in various stages of healing
  • Delay in seeking care
  • Inconsistent history
  • Poor hygiene, malnutrition
  • Fearful behavior around caregiver
  • Caregiver speaks for patient

Management

🚨 Mandatory Reporting

Most states require mandatory reporting of suspected elder abuse to Adult Protective Services (APS). Document carefully and report even if not certain.

  • Interview patient alone
  • Document injuries with photographs
  • Assess safety — is patient safe to go home?
  • Report to APS
  • Involve social work

14. Nutrition & Malnutrition

▼

Screening

  • MNA (Mini Nutritional Assessment): Validated for older adults
  • MUST (Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool)
  • Weight loss >5% in 1 month or >10% in 6 months = significant

Causes of Malnutrition — "MEALS ON WHEELS"

  • Medications
  • Emotional (depression)
  • Anorexia (late-life)
  • Late-life paranoia
  • Swallowing problems
  • Oral/dental problems
  • No money (poverty)
  • Wandering (dementia)
  • Hyperthyroidism/disease
  • Entry problems (malabsorption)
  • Eating problems (inability to self-feed)
  • Low-salt, low-cholesterol diets
  • Shopping/food preparation difficulties

Management

  • Treat underlying cause
  • Liberalize diet (avoid overly restrictive diets in elderly)
  • Fortify foods
  • Oral nutritional supplements
  • Address dental issues
  • Social eating, pleasant environment
⚠️ Tube Feeding in Advanced Dementia

Tube feeding does NOT improve survival or quality of life in advanced dementia. Associated with aspiration, infections, restraints. Careful hand-feeding is preferred.

15. Sensory Impairment

▼

Vision

ConditionFeaturesManagement
Cataracts Gradual, painless vision loss; glare Surgery when impairs function
Macular degeneration Central vision loss; drusen AREDS vitamins (intermediate AMD); anti-VEGF (wet AMD)
Glaucoma Peripheral vision loss; elevated IOP Eye drops, laser, surgery
Diabetic retinopathy Microaneurysms, hemorrhages Glycemic control; laser; anti-VEGF

Hearing

  • Presbycusis: Age-related hearing loss; high-frequency loss first
  • Screening: Whispered voice test, finger rub, audiometry
  • Treatment: Hearing aids (underutilized), cochlear implants for profound loss

Consequences of Sensory Impairment

  • Falls
  • Depression
  • Social isolation
  • Cognitive decline (especially hearing loss)
  • Functional impairment

16. High-Yield Board Pearls

▼

Geriatric Assessment Pearls

  • 💎 Functional status (ADLs/IADLs) is the best predictor of outcomes in elderly
  • 💎 Frailty = weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slow gait, low activity (≥3 of 5)
  • 💎 Stop cancer screening when life expectancy <10 years

Falls Pearls

  • 💎 Exercise (balance + strength) is the most effective fall prevention intervention
  • 💎 Vitamin D 800-1000 IU reduces falls in deficient/at-risk patients
  • 💎 Timed Up and Go ≥12 seconds = increased fall risk
  • 💎 Multifactorial interventions are most effective

Delirium Pearls

  • 💎 CAM criteria: Acute + fluctuating + inattention + (disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness)
  • 💎 Hypoactive delirium is often missed and has worse prognosis
  • 💎 Non-pharmacologic interventions are FIRST-LINE
  • 💎 Avoid benzodiazepines except for alcohol/benzo withdrawal
  • 💎 Dementia is the #1 risk factor for delirium

Dementia Pearls

  • 💎 Rule out reversible causes: B12, TSH, depression, medications
  • 💎 DLB: Visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations, RBD, sensitive to antipsychotics
  • 💎 NPH: "Wet, wacky, wobbly" — gait first, then dementia, then incontinence
  • 💎 Antipsychotics increase mortality in dementia patients (black box warning)

Polypharmacy Pearls

  • 💎 Beers Criteria: Avoid benzos, anticholinergics, long-acting sulfonylureas, chronic NSAIDs, PPIs without indication
  • 💎 Diphenhydramine is strongly anticholinergic — avoid in elderly
  • 💎 Deprescribe when indication no longer exists or harm outweighs benefit

End-of-Life Pearls

  • 💎 Morphine for dyspnea is appropriate and effective in terminal illness
  • 💎 Tube feeding in advanced dementia does NOT improve outcomes
  • 💎 Hospice eligibility: Prognosis <6 months if disease runs its usual course
  • 💎 Palliative care ≠ hospice — palliative care can be concurrent with curative treatment

Other Pearls

  • 💎 Elder abuse: Mandatory reporting to Adult Protective Services in most states
  • 💎 Stress incontinence: Pelvic floor exercises first
  • 💎 Pressure injury prevention: Reposition every 2 hours
  • 💎 Hearing loss is associated with cognitive decline

👴 Geriatric Medicine Study Guide

AGS • Beers Criteria • USPSTF Guidelines

📚 All Study Guides 💎 Board Pearls 💰 High Value Care
↑ Back to top

🩹 Dermatology

ABIM Board Review — AAD Guidelines • 3% of Exam

🎯 Core Dermatology Principles

💊Drug eruptions: morbilliform rash + timing = most common pattern
🚨SJS/TEN is a dermatologic emergency — stop offending drug immediately
☀️Melanoma ABCDE rule: Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution
🔬Skin findings often signal systemic disease — think endocrine, rheum, malignancy
🧫Cellulitis is clinical — culture only if abscess, unusual presentation, or immunocompromised
🩺Psoriasis associated with metabolic syndrome, PsA, and cardiovascular risk
💊 Drug Reactions ☀️ Skin Cancer 🦠 Infections 🔥 Inflammatory 🔬 Systemic Signs 💎 Pearls
Systemic Signs Pearls

💊 Drug Eruptions

▼

Types of Drug Eruptions

Type Onset Appearance Common Causes
Morbilliform (Exanthematous) 4-14 days Maculopapular, confluent, starts trunk Penicillins, sulfonamides, allopurinol, anticonvulsants
Urticarial Minutes-hours Wheals, pruritic, migratory PCN, NSAIDs, opioids, radiocontrast
Fixed Drug Eruption 30 min - 8 hours Round, violaceous plaques; recur same site TMP-SMX, NSAIDs, tetracyclines, phenytoin
Photosensitivity Hours-days Exaggerated sunburn in sun-exposed areas Tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, thiazides, amiodarone
Lichenoid Weeks-months Violaceous, flat-topped papules (like lichen planus) ACE-I, thiazides, gold, antimalarials
💎 Board Pearl: Morbilliform eruptions are the MOST COMMON drug eruption. They are usually self-limited. Fixed drug eruptions recur at the SAME SITE with each exposure.

Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)

Spectrum of Severity:
  • SJS: <10% body surface area (BSA) detachment
  • SJS/TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA
  • TEN: >30% BSA (mortality 30-50%)

Clinical Features

  • Prodrome: fever, malaise, URI symptoms 1-3 days before rash
  • Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites): oral, ocular, genital (90%+)
  • Skin: atypical targetoid lesions, bullae, positive Nikolsky sign
  • Full-thickness epidermal necrosis → "sheet-like" skin sloughing

High-Risk Medications

  • Sulfonamides (TMP-SMX) — most common
  • Allopurinol
  • Anticonvulsants: carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbital
  • NSAIDs (oxicam type)
  • Antibiotics: penicillins, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones
  • Nevirapine

Management

✅ Treatment:
  • STOP the offending drug immediately
  • Burn unit or ICU admission for wound care
  • Fluid/electrolyte management (massive insensible losses)
  • Wound care: non-adherent dressings, minimize debridement
  • Ophthalmology consult (prevent corneal scarring)
  • Systemic steroids: controversial (no proven benefit, may harm)
  • IVIG: conflicting evidence
  • Cyclosporine: some evidence of benefit
💎 HLA-B*58:01: Strongly associated with allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN. Screen in high-risk populations (Han Chinese, Thai, Korean) BEFORE starting allopurinol.

DRESS Syndrome

Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms
  • Onset: 2-8 weeks after drug initiation (LATER than other reactions)
  • Fever, facial edema, lymphadenopathy
  • Morbilliform rash → can become edematous, purpuric
  • Eosinophilia (≥700) and/or atypical lymphocytosis
  • Organ involvement: hepatitis (most common), nephritis, pneumonitis, myocarditis

Causative Drugs (SPADE)

  • Sulfonamides
  • Phenytoin / Phenobarbital
  • Allopurinol
  • Dapsone
  • Enticonvulsants (carbamazepine, lamotrigine)
  • Also: vancomycin, minocycline, abacavir
⚠️ HHV-6 reactivation occurs in ~60% of DRESS cases and may contribute to severity. Associated with prolonged course and may cause "flares" even after drug discontinuation.
✅ Management: Stop drug, supportive care, systemic corticosteroids (unlike SJS/TEN, steroids DO help in DRESS)

Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis (AGEP)

  • Onset: Rapid (within 48 hours)
  • Hundreds of small, non-follicular sterile pustules on erythematous base
  • Starts in flexures (axillae, groin)
  • Fever, neutrophilia
  • Resolves quickly (days) after drug cessation with superficial desquamation

Common Causes

  • Antibiotics: aminopenicillins (most common), macrolides, quinolones
  • Calcium channel blockers
  • Hydroxychloroquine
💎 Board Pearl: AGEP has the most RAPID onset of severe drug reactions (48 hours). Unlike pustular psoriasis, it resolves quickly once the drug is stopped.

Drug Eruption Summary Table

Reaction Onset Key Feature Labs Prognosis
SJS/TEN 1-3 weeks Mucosal + skin detachment Usually normal Mortality up to 30-50%
DRESS 2-8 weeks Organ involvement + facial edema Eosinophilia, ↑LFTs Mortality ~10%
AGEP <48 hours Sterile pustules, flexures Neutrophilia Excellent (self-limited)

🔬 Skin Cancer

▼

Melanoma

ABCDEs of Melanoma

  • Asymmetry — one half unlike the other
  • Border — irregular, ragged, blurred
  • Color — varied shades of brown, black, red, white, blue
  • Diameter — >6 mm (pencil eraser), though can be smaller
  • Evolving — changing in size, shape, color, or symptoms

Types of Melanoma

Type Frequency Features
Superficial spreading ~70% Most common; horizontal growth, then vertical invasion
Nodular ~15% Most aggressive; vertical growth from onset; raised, dark nodule
Lentigo maligna ~10% Sun-damaged skin of elderly; slow growing; face/neck
Acral lentiginous ~5% Palms, soles, nail beds; most common type in dark-skinned individuals

Staging (Breslow Thickness)

  • Breslow thickness (depth in mm) is the most important prognostic factor
  • Ulceration worsens prognosis
  • Sentinel lymph node biopsy if thickness ≥1 mm (or 0.8 mm with ulceration)

Surveillance After Treatment

✅ Follow-up:
  • Full skin exams every 3-12 months (based on stage)
  • Patient self-exams monthly
  • Imaging not routine for early-stage; consider for stage IIB+
💎 Board Pearl: Acral lentiginous melanoma is the most common melanoma in African Americans, Asians, and Hispanics. Look on palms, soles, and under nails. "Hutchinson sign" (periungual pigmentation) suggests subungual melanoma.

Non-Melanoma Skin Cancers

Feature Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
Frequency Most common skin cancer (80%) Second most common (20%)
Appearance Pearly, translucent papule with telangiectasias; "rolled" borders; rodent ulcer Scaly, erythematous plaque or nodule; may ulcerate
Location Sun-exposed areas (face, especially nose) Sun-exposed areas; also lips, ears, hands
Metastatic potential Very rare (<0.1%) Low but present (2-6%); higher in immunocompromised
Treatment Excision, Mohs for high-risk; vismodegib for advanced Excision, Mohs; radiation for inoperable; immunotherapy for advanced

Actinic Keratosis

  • Precursor to SCC — "precancer"
  • Rough, scaly, erythematous macules on sun-exposed skin
  • "Sandpaper" texture
  • 1-10% progress to SCC
  • Treatment: cryotherapy, topical 5-FU, imiquimod, photodynamic therapy
⚠️ High-risk SCC features:
  • Size >2 cm
  • Poor differentiation
  • Perineural invasion
  • Location: lip, ear, scar, chronic wound
  • Immunocompromised patient

🦠 Skin Infections

▼

Bacterial Skin Infections

Condition Depth Features Treatment
Impetigo Superficial epidermis Honey-crusted lesions; S. aureus or GAS Topical mupirocin; oral abx if extensive
Erysipelas Upper dermis/superficial lymphatics Well-demarcated, raised, bright red; GAS Penicillin, cephalexin
Cellulitis Deeper dermis/subcutaneous Poorly demarcated, warm, tender; GAS or S. aureus Cephalexin; add MRSA coverage if purulent
Necrotizing fasciitis Deep fascia Pain out of proportion, rapid spread, crepitus, bullae, necrosis Emergent surgical debridement + broad-spectrum abx
Necrotizing Fasciitis Red Flags:
  • Pain out of proportion to exam
  • Rapid progression
  • Systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension)
  • Crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis
  • Failure to respond to antibiotics
Type I (polymicrobial) vs Type II (GAS/"flesh-eating")

Herpes Zoster (Shingles)

  • Reactivation of VZV in dorsal root ganglion
  • Dermatomal distribution — does not cross midline
  • Prodrome: pain, burning, tingling 1-5 days before rash
  • Vesicles on erythematous base → pustules → crusts

Treatment

✅ Antivirals (within 72 hours of rash onset):
  • Valacyclovir 1g TID × 7 days (preferred)
  • Acyclovir 800mg 5×/day × 7 days
  • Famciclovir 500mg TID × 7 days

Complications

  • Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) — pain persisting >90 days; risk ↑ with age
  • Herpes zoster ophthalmicus — V1 involvement; Hutchinson sign (nose tip) = ↑risk of ocular involvement
  • Ramsay Hunt syndrome — facial palsy + ear vesicles (CN VII + VIII involvement)
  • Disseminated zoster (>2 dermatomes) — immunocompromised
💎 Shingrix vaccine: Recombinant (not live) — safe in immunocompromised. Give at age ≥50. Two doses, 2-6 months apart. >90% effective at preventing zoster and PHN.

Fungal Infections

Condition Features Treatment
Tinea corporis Annular, scaly plaque with central clearing ("ringworm") Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, terbinafine)
Tinea cruris Pruritic, erythematous plaques in groin; spares scrotum Topical antifungals; keep area dry
Tinea pedis Interdigital maceration, scaling; "moccasin" pattern Topical antifungals; oral if extensive
Onychomycosis Thickened, discolored, dystrophic nails Oral terbinafine (most effective); itraconazole; topical for mild
Tinea versicolor Hypo/hyperpigmented macules with fine scale; upper trunk Topical selenium sulfide, ketoconazole; oral fluconazole if extensive
💎 Board Pearl: Tinea versicolor (caused by Malassezia) produces hypo- or hyperpigmented macules. "Spaghetti and meatballs" appearance on KOH prep. Recurrence common.

🔥 Inflammatory Conditions

▼

Psoriasis

Classic features:
  • Well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scale
  • Extensor surfaces: elbows, knees, scalp, sacrum
  • Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis, oil spots
  • Auspitz sign: pinpoint bleeding when scale removed
  • Koebner phenomenon: lesions at sites of trauma

Types

Type Features
Plaque (vulgaris) Most common (90%); chronic plaques
Guttate Small "drop-like" lesions; often triggered by strep pharyngitis
Inverse Intertriginous areas; smooth, erythematous (less scale)
Pustular Sterile pustules; localized (palms/soles) or generalized (von Zumbusch — life-threatening)
Erythrodermic >90% BSA involvement; medical emergency

Treatment

  • Mild: Topical steroids, vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene), tazarotene
  • Moderate-severe: Phototherapy (UVB), methotrexate, cyclosporine
  • Biologics: TNF-α inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab), IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab)
⚠️ Associated conditions:
  • Psoriatic arthritis (30%)
  • Metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease
  • Depression

Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)

Features:
  • Pruritic, erythematous, weeping/crusting patches
  • Flexural involvement in older children/adults (antecubital, popliteal fossae)
  • Associated with "atopic triad": asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema
  • Chronic: lichenification (thickened, leathery skin)

Treatment

  • Emollients: Foundation of treatment — apply immediately after bathing
  • Topical steroids: For flares; low-potency for face/flexures
  • Topical calcineurin inhibitors: Tacrolimus, pimecrolimus (steroid-sparing)
  • Severe: Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor), phototherapy, cyclosporine
  • Avoid triggers: harsh soaps, fragrances, irritants
💎 Board Pearl: Eczema herpeticum = disseminated HSV in atopic dermatitis. Presents with "punched-out" erosions, fever, lymphadenopathy. Treat with IV acyclovir.

Urticaria and Angioedema

Urticaria (hives):
  • Transient, pruritic wheals
  • Individual lesions last <24 hours
  • Acute (<6 weeks) vs chronic (>6 weeks)
Angioedema:
  • Deep dermal/subcutaneous swelling
  • Lips, eyelids, tongue, larynx
  • Lasts 24-72 hours

Types of Angioedema

Type Mechanism Features
Allergic/Mast cell Histamine-mediated + urticaria, + pruritus; responds to antihistamines/epinephrine
ACE-I induced Bradykinin-mediated NO urticaria; lips/tongue; can occur years after starting; does NOT respond to antihistamines
Hereditary (HAE) C1-INH deficiency (bradykinin) NO urticaria; recurrent episodes; abdominal pain; family history; low C4
❌ ACE-I angioedema:
  • Most common cause of drug-induced angioedema
  • Can occur after years of use
  • More common in African Americans
  • Does NOT respond to epinephrine/antihistamines (bradykinin-mediated)
  • Treat with FFP, icatibant (bradykinin antagonist), C1-INH concentrate
  • Discontinue ACE-I permanently; ARBs generally safe but small cross-reactivity risk

🩺 Skin Signs of Systemic Disease

▼

Autoimmune/Rheumatologic

Finding Associated Condition
Malar (butterfly) rash SLE (spares nasolabial folds)
Discoid lupus SLE or isolated cutaneous lupus
Heliotrope rash Dermatomyositis (violaceous eyelid discoloration)
Gottron papules Dermatomyositis (over knuckles)
Shawl sign, V-sign Dermatomyositis (photodistributed)
Mechanic's hands Antisynthetase syndrome (cracked, rough fingers)
Sclerodactyly Systemic sclerosis (tight, shiny skin on fingers)
Digital pitting scars Systemic sclerosis
Calcinosis cutis Limited scleroderma (CREST), dermatomyositis
Livedo reticularis Antiphospholipid syndrome, SLE, vasculitis
Palpable purpura Vasculitis (leukocytoclastic, IgA)

Internal Malignancy

Sign Associated Cancer
Acanthosis nigricans GI adenocarcinoma (especially gastric) if sudden/severe; also insulin resistance
Dermatomyositis Ovarian, lung, GI, breast, lymphoma (screen at diagnosis)
Sign of Leser-Trélat Sudden eruption of seborrheic keratoses → GI malignancy
Sweet syndrome AML and other hematologic malignancies
Pyoderma gangrenosum IBD, hematologic malignancy, RA
Erythema gyratum repens Lung cancer (most paraneoplastic)
Necrolytic migratory erythema Glucagonoma
Trousseau syndrome Migratory thrombophlebitis → pancreatic cancer
💎 Board Pearl: New-onset dermatomyositis in adults (especially >40) requires malignancy workup: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, age-appropriate screening, consider PET. Risk is highest in the first 3 years.

Endocrine

Finding Condition
Pretibial myxedema Graves' disease (not hypothyroidism)
Necrobiosis lipoidica Diabetes mellitus (shiny, yellow-brown plaques on shins)
Diabetic dermopathy DM (brown, atrophic macules on shins; "shin spots")
Eruptive xanthomas Hypertriglyceridemia (>1000 mg/dL)
Xanthelasma Hypercholesterolemia (yellow plaques on eyelids)
Bronze skin Hemochromatosis, Addison's disease

Nail Findings

Finding Association
Clubbing Lung cancer, ILD, IE, IBD, cirrhosis
Koilonychia (spooning) Iron deficiency anemia
Terry nails (white with pink tip) Cirrhosis, CHF, DM
Half-and-half nails CKD/uremia
Mees' lines (transverse white bands) Arsenic poisoning, chemo
Muehrcke lines (paired white bands) Hypoalbuminemia
Splinter hemorrhages Endocarditis, trauma, vasculitis
Pitting Psoriasis, alopecia areata

💎 High-Yield Board Pearls

▼
Drug Reactions:
  1. SJS/TEN: Mucosal involvement + skin detachment; mortality 30-50% in TEN
  2. DRESS: Late onset (2-8 weeks), eosinophilia, organ involvement; HHV-6 reactivation
  3. AGEP: Most rapid (48h), pustules in flexures, self-limited
  4. High-risk drugs: Allopurinol, TMP-SMX, anticonvulsants
  5. HLA-B*58:01 screening before allopurinol in Asian populations
Skin Cancer:
  1. Melanoma ABCDEs: Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolving
  2. Breslow thickness = most important prognostic factor
  3. Acral lentiginous melanoma most common in dark-skinned individuals
  4. BCC: pearly papule with telangiectasias; rarely metastasizes
  5. SCC: can metastasize; higher risk if immunocompromised
Infections:
  1. Necrotizing fasciitis: Pain out of proportion, rapid spread, crepitus → emergent surgery
  2. Herpes zoster: Dermatomal, Hutchinson sign (nose tip) = eye involvement
  3. Shingrix vaccine: Safe in immunocompromised (recombinant, not live)
  4. Tinea versicolor: "Spaghetti and meatballs" on KOH; Malassezia
Inflammatory Conditions:
  1. Psoriasis: Silvery scale, extensor surfaces, Auspitz sign, nail pitting
  2. Guttate psoriasis: Triggered by strep pharyngitis
  3. Eczema herpeticum: Disseminated HSV in atopic dermatitis — IV acyclovir
  4. ACE-I angioedema: NO urticaria, bradykinin-mediated, doesn't respond to epinephrine
  5. HAE: Low C4, recurrent episodes, family history
Skin Signs of Systemic Disease:
  1. Dermatomyositis: Heliotrope rash + Gottron papules; screen for malignancy
  2. Acanthosis nigricans (sudden onset): Think GI malignancy
  3. Sweet syndrome: Think AML
  4. Necrolytic migratory erythema: Think glucagonoma
  5. Koilonychia: Iron deficiency; Terry nails: Cirrhosis
↑ Back to top

🕊️ Palliative Care

ABIM Board Review — AAHPM Guidelines • 2% of Exam

🎯 Core Palliative Care Principles

🤝Goals of care conversations — explore values, not just preferences
💊Opioids are first-line for cancer pain — titrate to effect, not a ceiling
🏠Hospice: prognosis ≤6 months + comfort-focused goals only
🧠Withholding and withdrawing treatment are ethically equivalent
📋POLST/MOLST documents actual medical orders — different from advance directives
💙Palliative care improves QoL and may extend survival — not just end of life
🎯 Goals of Care 💊 Pain Management 🩺 Symptom Control 🏠 Hospice ⚖️ Ethics & Legal 💎 Pearls

🎯 Goals of Care Discussions

▼

When to Have Goals of Care Conversations

  • New diagnosis of serious illness
  • Disease progression or treatment failure
  • Hospital admission for serious illness
  • Functional decline
  • When patient asks "What should I expect?"
  • Before high-risk procedures
  • When current treatment is no longer aligned with goals

Framework for Goals of Care Discussion

SPIKES Protocol for Breaking Bad News:
  • Setting — Private space, sit down, minimize interruptions
  • Perception — "What do you understand about your illness?"
  • Invitation — "How much information would you like?"
  • Knowledge — Share information in small chunks
  • Empathy — Acknowledge and validate emotions
  • Summary/Strategy — Plan next steps together

Key Questions to Explore

✅ Understanding values and preferences:
  • "What matters most to you right now?"
  • "What are you hoping for?"
  • "What are you worried about?"
  • "What gives your life meaning?"
  • "What would be unacceptable to you?"
  • "If time is limited, how would you want to spend it?"
💎 Board Pearl: Ask about understanding BEFORE giving information. This allows you to meet the patient where they are and correct misconceptions.

Responding to Emotion

NURSE Statements:
  • Name the emotion: "It sounds like you're feeling scared."
  • Understand: "I can understand why you would feel that way."
  • Respect: "I can see how hard you've been fighting this."
  • Support: "I will be with you through this."
  • Explore: "Tell me more about what you're worried about."
⚠️ When emotions arise: Pause and respond to the emotion BEFORE continuing with medical information. Patients cannot process information when emotionally overwhelmed.

Code Status Discussions

Framing code status in context of goals:
  • Discuss code status as part of larger goals conversation, not in isolation
  • Provide realistic information about outcomes of CPR
  • Frame recommendations in terms of what you WOULD do, not just what you wouldn't

CPR Outcomes by Setting

Population Survival to Discharge
In-hospital cardiac arrest (all) ~25%
Metastatic cancer ~6%
Sepsis ~10%
Renal failure on dialysis ~10-15%
Nursing home residents ~5%
✅ Better framing: "Given what we know about your illness, if your heart were to stop, I would not recommend CPR because it would not help you achieve your goal of [stated goal]. Instead, I recommend we focus on [comfort, time with family, etc.]."

💊 Pain Management

▼

WHO Pain Ladder

Step Pain Level Treatment
Step 1 Mild (1-3) Non-opioid ± adjuvant (acetaminophen, NSAIDs)
Step 2 Moderate (4-6) Weak opioid ± non-opioid ± adjuvant (tramadol, codeine)
Step 3 Severe (7-10) Strong opioid ± non-opioid ± adjuvant (morphine, oxycodone, hydromorphone, fentanyl)
💎 Board Pearl: In cancer pain or severe pain, it's appropriate to start at Step 3 if clinically indicated. The ladder is a guide, not a requirement to climb sequentially.

Opioid Equianalgesic Dosing

Opioid PO Dose (mg) IV Dose (mg) PO:IV Ratio
Morphine 30 10 3:1
Hydromorphone 6 1.5 4:1
Oxycodone 20 N/A —
Hydrocodone 30 N/A —
Fentanyl N/A 0.1 (100 mcg) —
⚠️ Conversion safety: When rotating opioids, reduce calculated dose by 25-50% due to incomplete cross-tolerance. Exception: methadone (requires greater reduction).

Fentanyl Patch Conversion

  • Fentanyl patch 25 mcg/hr ≈ Morphine 60-90 mg PO/day
  • Takes 12-24 hours to reach steady state
  • Continue short-acting opioid for first 12-24 hours when starting patch
  • Avoid in opioid-naive patients
  • Heat increases absorption (fever, heating pads)

Breakthrough Pain Dosing

✅ PRN breakthrough dose:
  • 10-20% of total daily opioid dose
  • Give same opioid as scheduled if possible
  • Can give q1-2h PRN for IV, q3-4h for PO
Example: Patient on morphine ER 60mg BID (120mg/day)
  • Breakthrough = 10-20% of 120mg = 12-24mg
  • Order: Morphine IR 15mg PO q4h PRN

Opioid Selection in Special Populations

Condition Preferred Avoid
Renal failure Fentanyl, hydromorphone, methadone Morphine (active metabolites accumulate → toxicity)
Liver failure Hydromorphone, fentanyl (reduce dose) Codeine, tramadol (prodrugs); reduce all doses
Elderly Start low, go slow; hydromorphone Meperidine (neurotoxicity); high starting doses
Opioid-naive Immediate-release, low dose Fentanyl patch, methadone, high starting doses
❌ Always avoid meperidine for chronic pain — metabolite normeperidine causes seizures, especially in renal impairment.

Adjuvant Analgesics

Pain Type Adjuvant Options
Neuropathic pain Gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
Bone pain NSAIDs, bisphosphonates, denosumab, radiation, corticosteroids
Visceral pain / obstruction Corticosteroids, octreotide
Muscle spasm Baclofen, tizanidine, cyclobenzaprine
Inflammatory pain NSAIDs, corticosteroids
💎 Board Pearl: Gabapentinoids and SNRIs are first-line for neuropathic pain. Opioids alone are often insufficient for neuropathic pain.

Opioid Side Effects & Management

Side Effect Tolerance? Management
Constipation NO Prophylactic bowel regimen: senna + docusate; methylnaltrexone if refractory
Nausea Yes (3-5 days) Ondansetron, prochlorperazine, metoclopramide; often resolves
Sedation Yes (3-5 days) Wait for tolerance; consider methylphenidate if persistent
Pruritus Yes Antihistamines, opioid rotation; nalbuphine in severe cases
Respiratory depression Yes Hold opioid; naloxone 0.04-0.4mg IV if severe
Myoclonus No Dose reduction, opioid rotation, clonazepam
❌ Constipation does NOT develop tolerance. ALWAYS prescribe a bowel regimen with opioids. Stimulant laxative (senna) is preferred.

🩺 Non-Pain Symptom Management

▼

Dyspnea

Causes to evaluate: Pleural effusion, pulmonary embolism, anemia, pneumonia, CHF exacerbation, airway obstruction

Management

✅ Pharmacologic:
  • Opioids — First-line for refractory dyspnea
    • Opioid-naive: Morphine 2.5-5mg PO q4h or 1-2mg IV q4h
    • On opioids: Increase dose by 25%
  • Benzodiazepines — For anxiety component (lorazepam 0.5-1mg)
  • Corticosteroids — If lymphangitic spread, airway obstruction, COPD
✅ Non-pharmacologic:
  • Fan blowing on face (stimulates trigeminal nerve)
  • Positioning (upright, leaning forward)
  • Oxygen if hypoxic (less benefit if not hypoxic)
  • Cool environment
💎 Board Pearl: Opioids reduce the sensation of dyspnea without significantly suppressing respiratory drive at appropriate doses. They are first-line for refractory dyspnea in palliative care.

Nausea and Vomiting

Treatment by Mechanism

Cause Mechanism Treatment
Chemotherapy, opioids CTZ (5-HT3, D2) Ondansetron, prochlorperazine, haloperidol
Gastric stasis Vagal afferents Metoclopramide (prokinetic)
Bowel obstruction Mechanical Octreotide, dexamethasone, avoid prokinetics
Increased ICP Vestibular/central Dexamethasone
Vestibular Motion/position Meclizine, scopolamine
Anticipatory Cortical Lorazepam
⚠️ Bowel obstruction: Avoid metoclopramide (prokinetic can worsen colic). Use octreotide to decrease secretions, dexamethasone to reduce edema, and antiemetics.

Delirium at End of Life

Common causes:
  • Medications (opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics)
  • Metabolic (hypercalcemia, uremia, hepatic encephalopathy)
  • Infection
  • Hypoxia
  • Brain metastases
  • Terminal delirium (part of dying process)

Management

✅ Non-pharmacologic (first):
  • Reorientation, familiar objects, family presence
  • Minimize unnecessary interventions
  • Optimize sleep-wake cycle
  • Correct reversible causes if consistent with goals
✅ Pharmacologic (for agitation):
  • Haloperidol 0.5-2mg PO/IV/SC q4-6h PRN — first-line
  • Olanzapine 2.5-5mg PO/SL daily
  • Quetiapine 12.5-25mg PO BID
  • Avoid benzodiazepines (worsen delirium) unless alcohol withdrawal or terminal sedation
💎 Terminal delirium: Common in last days of life. Goals shift to patient comfort. May require palliative sedation if refractory and distressing.

Death Rattle (Terminal Secretions)

Noisy respirations from secretions in airways in dying patient
  • Does NOT cause dyspnea or discomfort to patient
  • Distressing to family — education is key

Management

  • Position patient on side
  • Gentle suctioning (avoid deep suctioning — causes discomfort)
  • Anticholinergics:
    • Glycopyrrolate 0.2-0.4mg IV/SC q4-6h (does not cross BBB)
    • Scopolamine patch 1.5mg q72h
    • Hyoscine (scopolamine) 0.3-0.6mg SC q4h
    • Atropine 1% drops SL q4h
⚠️ Anticholinergics work best prophylactically — they prevent NEW secretions but don't dry existing secretions.

Anorexia-Cachexia Syndrome

  • Metabolic syndrome with loss of muscle/fat despite nutrition
  • Driven by inflammation and tumor factors
  • Artificial nutrition does NOT reverse cancer cachexia

Management

  • Education: eating won't reverse weight loss in advanced cancer
  • Appetite stimulants (modest benefit):
    • Dexamethasone 2-4mg daily (short-term)
    • Megestrol acetate 400-800mg daily (VTE risk)
    • Dronabinol 2.5mg BID
  • Focus on pleasure of eating, small portions, favorite foods

🏠 Hospice Care

▼

Hospice Eligibility Criteria

Medicare Hospice Benefit Requirements:
  • Prognosis of ≤6 months if disease runs its expected course
  • Two physicians certify (attending + hospice medical director)
  • Patient elects comfort-focused care
  • Patient agrees to forgo curative treatment for terminal diagnosis
Key points:
  • Patients can continue treatments for OTHER diagnoses
  • Can revoke hospice at any time and return to curative care
  • Can re-enroll in hospice if still eligible
  • Hospice covers: medications, equipment, nursing, aide, social work, chaplain, bereavement

Disease-Specific Prognostic Criteria

Cancer

  • Metastatic or locally advanced disease
  • Declining performance status (KPS ≤50 or PPS ≤50%)
  • Declining despite or refusing further treatment

Heart Failure

  • NYHA Class IV despite optimal therapy
  • EF ≤20% or severe diastolic dysfunction
  • ≥1 hospitalization in past 6 months
  • Not a transplant/VAD candidate
  • Refractory symptoms, rising creatinine/BUN

COPD

  • Disabling dyspnea at rest despite optimal treatment
  • FEV1 <30% predicted
  • pO2 ≤55 or O2 sat ≤88% on room air
  • pCO2 ≥50
  • Progressive weight loss, cor pulmonale, ER visits/hospitalizations

Dementia

  • FAST Stage 7C or beyond (non-ambulatory, minimal speech, incontinent)
  • Unable to ambulate, dress, bathe independently
  • Urinary and fecal incontinence
  • ≤6 intelligible words per day
  • Complication in past 12 months: aspiration pneumonia, pyelonephritis, sepsis, decubitus ulcer, recurrent fever
💎 Board Pearl: Prognostication is difficult. If you would "not be surprised" if the patient died within 6 months, hospice referral is appropriate.

Palliative Care vs. Hospice

Feature Palliative Care Hospice
Prognosis Any stage of serious illness ≤6 months
Curative treatment Can continue Forgone for terminal diagnosis
Setting Hospital, clinic, home Primarily home (also facility, inpatient)
Insurance Billed as medical services Medicare Hospice Benefit (comprehensive)
Focus Quality of life + disease treatment Comfort, dignity, family support

⚖️ Ethics & Legal Issues

▼

Advance Directives

Document Purpose
Living Will Specifies treatments patient does/doesn't want if incapacitated
Healthcare Power of Attorney (HCPOA) Designates surrogate decision-maker; broader than living will
POLST/MOLST Physician orders for specific treatments (CPR, intubation, etc.); portable across settings
POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment):
  • Signed by physician and patient/surrogate
  • Actionable medical orders (unlike advance directive)
  • Intended for seriously ill patients
  • Addresses: CPR, intubation, hospitalization, artificial nutrition
  • Bright color (usually pink) for easy identification

Surrogate Decision-Making Hierarchy

When patient lacks capacity (typical order):
  1. Healthcare Power of Attorney / Healthcare Proxy
  2. Court-appointed guardian
  3. Spouse/domestic partner
  4. Adult children
  5. Parents
  6. Adult siblings
  7. Close friend (varies by state)
Order varies by state law

Standards for Surrogate Decision-Making

  1. Substituted judgment (preferred): What would THIS patient want?
  2. Best interest (if wishes unknown): What would reasonable person want?

Withdrawing vs. Withholding Treatment

✅ Ethically and legally equivalent:
  • Withdrawing treatment already started = withholding treatment never started
  • Both are permissible when treatment no longer benefits patient or aligns with goals
  • Psychological difference exists but not ethical distinction
⚠️ Artificial nutrition/hydration:
  • Legally considered medical treatment (can be withdrawn)
  • In dying patients, forced hydration may increase discomfort (edema, secretions)
  • Dehydration in dying is NOT uncomfortable — may even be comfortable

Principle of Double Effect

Four conditions for ethical permissibility:
  1. Action itself is morally good or neutral (giving medication for symptom relief)
  2. Good effect is intended (relief of suffering)
  3. Bad effect is foreseen but NOT intended (possible hastening of death)
  4. Good effect is not achieved BY means of the bad effect
Example: Giving morphine to relieve dyspnea in a dying patient is ethical even if it might hasten death, as long as the intent is comfort and the dose is titrated to symptom relief.
💎 Board Pearl: Appropriate opioid dosing for symptom control does NOT hasten death in most cases. Fear of hastening death should not prevent adequate pain/dyspnea treatment.

Palliative Sedation

Definition: Intentionally reducing consciousness to relieve refractory symptoms at end of life
Criteria for palliative sedation:
  • Patient is imminently dying (hours to days)
  • Symptom is refractory to standard treatments
  • Symptom is intolerable to patient
  • Consent obtained (patient or surrogate)
  • Intent is symptom relief, not hastening death
✅ Medications used: Midazolam, propofol, pentobarbital, phenobarbital

Medical Aid in Dying (MAID)

Legal in some US states: Oregon, Washington, California, Colorado, Vermont, Hawaii, New Jersey, Maine, New Mexico, and DC (as of 2024)
  • Patient must have terminal illness with ≤6 month prognosis
  • Must be mentally competent adult
  • Must make multiple requests
  • Patient self-administers lethal medication
Different from euthanasia (physician administers)

💎 High-Yield Board Pearls

▼
Goals of Care:
  1. Ask about understanding BEFORE giving information (SPIKES)
  2. Respond to emotion before continuing with information (NURSE)
  3. Frame recommendations positively: what you WILL do, not just what you won't
  4. Code status should be discussed in context of overall goals, not in isolation
Pain Management:
  1. Morphine PO:IV ratio = 3:1; Hydromorphone PO:IV = 4:1
  2. Breakthrough dose = 10-20% of total daily opioid dose
  3. AVOID morphine in renal failure (active metabolites accumulate)
  4. AVOID meperidine for chronic pain (neurotoxic metabolite)
  5. Constipation does NOT develop tolerance — ALWAYS prescribe bowel regimen
  6. When rotating opioids, reduce dose 25-50% for incomplete cross-tolerance
Symptom Management:
  1. Opioids are first-line for refractory dyspnea (safe at appropriate doses)
  2. Fan blowing on face helps dyspnea (trigeminal nerve stimulation)
  3. Death rattle: Anticholinergics work best prophylactically
  4. Terminal delirium: Haloperidol first-line; AVOID benzodiazepines
  5. Bowel obstruction: AVOID prokinetics (metoclopramide); use octreotide
Hospice & Ethics:
  1. Hospice eligibility: Prognosis ≤6 months + comfort-focused goals
  2. Withdrawing = withholding treatment (ethically equivalent)
  3. Artificial nutrition is medical treatment (can be withdrawn)
  4. Double effect: Intent matters — comfort care is ethical even if death hastened
  5. Surrogate decision: Substituted judgment > best interest
  6. POLST = actionable physician orders; Advance directive = patient wishes document
↑ Back to top

🏥 Perioperative Medicine

ABIM Board Review — ACC/AHA Guidelines • 4% of Exam

🎯 Core Perioperative Principles

🚶Functional capacity ≥4 METs (climb 2 flights) = low cardiac risk
🚫No routine preoperative testing in healthy patients for low-risk surgery
💊Continue chronic beta-blockers — never start de novo before surgery (POISE)
🩸Bridge anticoagulation only for mechanical valves and very high VTE risk
🩺Cardiology consult = risk optimization, not "clearance" — anesthesia clears
⏱️Antibiotic prophylaxis: within 60 min of incision, stop within 24h
❤️ Cardiac Risk 🫁 Pulmonary 💊 Medications 🩸 Anticoagulation 🩺 Glycemic 🔴 VTE 💎 Pearls

❤️ Cardiac Risk Assessment

▼

Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI / Lee Index)

1 point each (6 total possible):
  1. High-risk surgery (intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, suprainguinal vascular)
  2. History of ischemic heart disease (MI, positive stress test, angina, nitrate use, Q waves)
  3. History of heart failure
  4. History of cerebrovascular disease (stroke or TIA)
  5. Diabetes requiring insulin
  6. Creatinine >2.0 mg/dL
RCRI Score Risk of MACE Risk Category
0 ~0.4% Low
1 ~1% Low
2 ~2.4% Elevated
≥3 ~5.4% Elevated
💎 Board Pearl: RCRI is most useful for risk stratification in INTERMEDIATE-risk surgery. Low-risk surgery (e.g., cataract, endoscopy) doesn't need formal assessment. Very high-risk patients may need cardiac evaluation regardless.

Surgery Risk Categories

Low Risk (<1% MACE) Intermediate Risk (1-5%) High Risk (>5%)
  • Cataract
  • Breast biopsy
  • Ambulatory surgery
  • Endoscopy
  • Superficial procedures
  • Orthopedic (hip/knee)
  • Intra-abdominal
  • Intrathoracic
  • Prostate
  • Head & neck
  • Aortic surgery
  • Peripheral vascular
  • Open heart
  • Transplant
  • Emergency major surgery

ACC/AHA Stepwise Approach

Step 1: Is surgery emergent? → Proceed to surgery, optimize perioperatively

Step 2: Does patient have ACS? → Delay surgery, treat ACS

Step 3: Estimate risk using RCRI + surgery type

Step 4: Assess functional capacity (METs)

Functional Capacity (METs)

METs Activity Implication
1-4 Self-care, walking indoors, light housework POOR — may need further testing
4-10 Climbing 1-2 flights of stairs, walking up hill, yard work MODERATE — proceed if asymptomatic
>10 Swimming, running, vigorous sports EXCELLENT — proceed
✅ Key threshold: If functional capacity ≥4 METs AND asymptomatic → proceed to surgery without further cardiac testing.

Preoperative Testing

When to Order ECG

  • Known CAD, arrhythmia, PAD, CVD, structural heart disease
  • Intermediate/high-risk surgery with ≥1 RCRI risk factors
  • NOT routinely needed for low-risk surgery

When to Order Stress Testing

⚠️ Consider stress testing ONLY if:
  • Elevated risk (RCRI ≥2)
  • Poor functional capacity (<4 METs)
  • High-risk surgery
  • Result will change management
If stress testing within past 2 years was negative with no new symptoms → no need to repeat
❌ Do NOT order cardiac catheterization just because of upcoming surgery. Revascularization is indicated only if independently warranted (ACS, left main disease, etc.)

Echocardiogram

Consider if:
  • New/worsening dyspnea of unknown cause
  • Known HF with worsening symptoms
  • Known severe valvular disease (reassess if >1 year)
NOT routinely indicated for noncardiac surgery

Special Considerations: Recent PCI

Stent Type Minimum Delay Before Elective Surgery DAPT Duration
Bare-metal stent (BMS) ≥30 days 4-6 weeks minimum
Drug-eluting stent (DES) ≥6 months (ideal); ≥3 months if urgent 6-12 months
Balloon angioplasty alone ≥2 weeks 2-4 weeks
❌ High Risk of Stent Thrombosis: Stopping DAPT prematurely is a major risk. Stent thrombosis has ~30% mortality.
✅ If surgery cannot wait:
  • Continue aspirin if at all possible
  • Hold P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel 5 days, ticagrelor 3-5 days, prasugrel 7 days)
  • Resume DAPT as soon as bleeding risk allows

Perioperative Beta-Blockers

✅ CONTINUE beta-blockers if:
  • Already taking chronically
  • For any indication (HTN, CAD, HF, arrhythmia)
❌ Do NOT START high-dose beta-blockers on day of surgery. POISE trial: ↓MI but ↑stroke and ↑mortality with day-of initiation.
If starting beta-blockers:
  • Begin >1 week before surgery
  • Titrate to HR 60-80 without hypotension
  • Consider in high-risk patients with ≥3 RCRI factors undergoing vascular surgery

🫁 Pulmonary Risk Assessment

▼

Risk Factors for Postoperative Pulmonary Complications

Patient Factors Surgery Factors
  • Age >60
  • COPD (strongest patient factor)
  • Current smoking
  • CHF
  • Functional dependence
  • ASA class ≥2
  • Obstructive sleep apnea
  • Pulmonary hypertension
  • Surgical site (most predictive)
  • Thoracic surgery
  • Upper abdominal surgery
  • Aortic aneurysm repair
  • Duration >3 hours
  • Emergency surgery
  • General anesthesia
💎 Board Pearl: Surgical site is the most important predictor of pulmonary complications. Thoracic and upper abdominal surgeries carry highest risk.

Preoperative Pulmonary Testing

⚠️ Routine preoperative PFTs are NOT recommended
  • Do not predict postoperative complications better than clinical assessment
  • No absolute PFT value precludes surgery
When PFTs may be helpful:
  • Undiagnosed dyspnea or cough
  • Known COPD with unclear baseline
  • Lung resection surgery (need to predict post-op lung function)
❌ Chest X-ray: Not routinely indicated. Consider if new cardiopulmonary symptoms or known cardiopulmonary disease requiring reassessment.

Risk Reduction Strategies

✅ Evidence-based interventions:
  • Smoking cessation — ideally ≥8 weeks preop; even shorter duration helpful
  • Lung expansion techniques — incentive spirometry, CPAP, deep breathing exercises
  • Optimize COPD/asthma — continue inhalers perioperatively
  • Nasogastric decompression — only with symptomatic distension (not prophylactic)
  • Neuraxial anesthesia — consider when appropriate (lower risk than GA)
⚠️ Smoking cessation: Some concern about increased sputum production in first weeks after quitting. However, benefits outweigh risks at any time point.

Obstructive Sleep Apnea

STOP-BANG Screening:
  • Snoring loudly
  • Tired during day
  • Observed apneas
  • Pressure (HTN)
  • BMI >35
  • Age >50
  • Neck circumference >40 cm
  • Gender male
Score ≥3 = intermediate risk; ≥5 = high risk
✅ Perioperative management:
  • Continue CPAP perioperatively (bring from home)
  • Minimize opioids, use multimodal analgesia
  • Avoid supine position if possible
  • Continuous pulse oximetry postoperatively
  • May require ICU monitoring after major surgery

💊 Perioperative Medication Management

▼

Medications to CONTINUE

Drug Class Guidance Rationale
Beta-blockers ✅ Continue Rebound tachycardia, HTN, ischemia if stopped
Statins ✅ Continue Pleiotropic effects, may reduce perioperative MI
Aspirin (cardiac indication) ✅ Continue (usually) Exception: intracranial, spinal surgery
Thyroid hormone ✅ Continue Long half-life, risk of myxedema
Inhaled beta-agonists, anticholinergics ✅ Continue Prevent bronchospasm
Anticonvulsants ✅ Continue Seizure risk
Immunosuppressants (transplant) ✅ Continue Rejection risk
Psychiatric medications ✅ Continue (most) See exceptions below

Medications to HOLD

Drug Class When to Hold Rationale
ACE-I / ARBs Hold morning of surgery (controversial) Refractory intraoperative hypotension. Can continue if stable HF.
Diuretics Hold morning of surgery Hypovolemia, electrolyte abnormalities
Metformin Hold day of surgery Risk of lactic acidosis with contrast/hypoperfusion
SGLT2 inhibitors Hold 3-4 days before Risk of euglycemic DKA
Sulfonylureas Hold morning of surgery Hypoglycemia risk
GLP-1 agonists Hold day of (daily) or 1 week (weekly) Delayed gastric emptying → aspiration risk
MAOIs Continue (usually) — coordinate with anesthesia Avoid meperidine, indirect sympathomimetics
NSAIDs Hold 2-3 days before Bleeding risk, AKI
Herbal supplements Hold 1-2 weeks before Garlic, ginkgo, ginseng ↑ bleeding

Perioperative Steroid Coverage

At risk for adrenal suppression:
  • Prednisone ≥20 mg/day (or equivalent) for ≥3 weeks
  • Cushingoid appearance
  • Current or recent steroid use (within 1 year)
Surgery Type Stress Dose Recommendation
Minor (local, <1 hour) Usual daily dose only
Moderate (orthopedic, abdominal) Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV preop + 25 mg q8h × 24h
Major (cardiac, Whipple) Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV preop + 50 mg q8h × 48-72h, then taper
💎 Board Pearl: Most patients on chronic steroids can tolerate surgery with their usual dose only. Reserve stress-dose steroids for truly high-risk patients undergoing major surgery.

🩸 Anticoagulation Management

▼

Warfarin: Hold and Bridge Decision

Thromboembolic Risk Indication Examples Bridging?
HIGH (>10%/year)
  • Mechanical mitral valve
  • Older mechanical aortic valve
  • Recent stroke/TIA (<3 months)
  • CHADS₂-VASc ≥7
  • Recent VTE (<3 months)
 ✅ Bridge with LMWH
MODERATE
  • Newer mechanical aortic valve
  • AFib with CHADS₂-VASc 5-6
  • VTE 3-12 months ago
 ⚠️ Consider bridging (individualize)
LOW
  • AFib with CHADS₂-VASc ≤4
  • Bioprosthetic valve
  • VTE >12 months ago
 ❌ No bridging (BRIDGE trial)
💎 BRIDGE Trial (2015): In AFib patients with low-moderate risk, forgoing bridging was noninferior for thromboembolism and REDUCED major bleeding. Bridging now reserved for truly high-risk patients.

Warfarin Timing

  • Hold: 5 days before surgery (goal INR <1.5)
  • If bridging: Start LMWH when INR subtherapeutic, hold 24h before surgery
  • Resume: 12-24h postop when hemostasis assured

Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs)

DOAC Normal Renal (CrCl ≥50) CrCl 30-50 CrCl <30
Dabigatran Hold 2-3 days Hold 3-4 days Hold 4-5 days
Rivaroxaban Hold 2-3 days Hold 3-4 days Hold 4-5 days
Apixaban Hold 2-3 days Hold 3-4 days Hold 4-5 days
Edoxaban Hold 2-3 days Hold 3-4 days Hold 4-5 days
✅ DOACs do NOT require bridging — short half-life allows perioperative interruption without LMWH overlap.
For procedures with minimal bleeding risk (dental, cataract, minor dermatologic): Consider performing on DOAC with timing at trough (skip morning dose).

Antiplatelet Management

Agent Hold Duration Notes
Aspirin (primary prevention) Hold 7 days Can often discontinue if no cardiac indication
Aspirin (CAD, stent) Continue if possible Hold only for intracranial, spinal surgery
Clopidogrel Hold 5 days —
Prasugrel Hold 7 days Most potent, longest to clear
Ticagrelor Hold 3-5 days Reversible binding
⚠️ Recent coronary stent: See Cardiac Risk section. Balance stent thrombosis risk vs. bleeding risk. Aspirin should continue if at all possible.

📊 Glycemic Management

▼

Diabetes Medication Management

Drug Class Preoperative Day of Surgery
Metformin Continue until surgery Hold; resume when stable and eating
SGLT2 inhibitors Hold 3-4 days before Held — risk of euglycemic DKA
Sulfonylureas Continue until day before Hold — hypoglycemia risk
GLP-1 agonists Daily: hold day of
Weekly: hold 1 week before		 Held — aspiration risk
DPP-4 inhibitors May continue May continue or hold
Basal insulin Reduce by 20-25% night before Give reduced dose (50-80% of usual)
Bolus/prandial insulin Continue with meals Hold (NPO)
Insulin pump Continue basal Continue basal rate; coordinate with anesthesia

Perioperative Glucose Targets

✅ Target glucose: 140-180 mg/dL
  • Tight control (<140) → ↑hypoglycemia without benefit (NICE-SUGAR)
  • Avoid hyperglycemia >180 → infection, poor wound healing
  • More liberal targets (180-250) acceptable in some patients
Intraoperative management:
  • Check glucose on arrival, then q1-2h
  • D5W + insulin drip for major surgery
  • Sliding scale for minor procedures

Euglycemic DKA with SGLT2 Inhibitors

⚠️ Key features:
  • DKA with normal or only mildly elevated glucose (<250)
  • Precipitated by surgery, fasting, dehydration, illness
  • SGLT2 inhibitors promote ketogenesis
  • May be missed because glucose not very high
Hold SGLT2i 3-4 days before surgery

🦵 VTE Prophylaxis

▼

Risk Assessment (Caprini Score Simplified)

Risk Level Caprini Score VTE Risk Prophylaxis
Very Low 0 <0.5% Ambulation only
