Low 1-2 0.5-1.5% Mechanical (SCDs) ± pharmacologic
Moderate 3-4 1.5-3% Pharmacologic + mechanical
High ≥5 >3% Pharmacologic + mechanical; extended prophylaxis

Pharmacologic Prophylaxis Options

Agent Dose Notes
Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily
or 30 mg SC q12h (high-risk)		 Reduce to 30 mg daily if CrCl <30
Heparin 5000 units SC q8h or q12h Use if CrCl <30 or high bleeding risk
Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily Avoid if CrCl <30; no HIT risk

Special Populations

Orthopedic Surgery (Hip/Knee)

✅ Extended prophylaxis: 10-35 days total
  • Options: LMWH, fondaparinux, rivaroxaban, apixaban, warfarin, aspirin (per CHEST 2012)
  • Start 12-24h postop (if using LMWH/fondaparinux)

Abdominal/Pelvic Cancer Surgery

✅ Extended prophylaxis: 4 weeks total (high VTE risk)

High Bleeding Risk

⚠️ If pharmacologic contraindicated:
  • Use mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs)
  • Start pharmacologic as soon as bleeding risk acceptable
  • Dual therapy (mechanical + pharmacologic) when able

IVC Filter Indications

  • Acute VTE with absolute contraindication to anticoagulation
  • Recurrent VTE despite adequate anticoagulation
  • NOT indicated for primary prophylaxis (even high-risk surgery)
  • Retrievable filters should be removed when anticoagulation can resume

💎 High-Yield Board Pearls

▼
Cardiac Risk:
  1. RCRI: 6 factors (high-risk surgery, CAD, CHF, CVD, insulin DM, Cr >2.0)
  2. Functional capacity ≥4 METs → proceed without testing (if asymptomatic)
  3. Do NOT start high-dose beta-blockers on day of surgery (POISE: ↑stroke, ↑death)
  4. Continue beta-blockers if already on them
  5. Recent DES: delay elective surgery ≥6 months (≥3 months if urgent)
Pulmonary Risk:
  1. Surgical site is the strongest predictor of pulmonary complications
  2. Routine preoperative PFTs are NOT recommended
  3. Smoking cessation at any time before surgery is beneficial
  4. Continue inhalers perioperatively (COPD, asthma)
  5. OSA patients: continue CPAP, minimize opioids, monitor postoperatively
Medications:
  1. CONTINUE: beta-blockers, statins, thyroid hormones, anticonvulsants
  2. HOLD: ACE-I/ARBs (controversial), diuretics morning of surgery
  3. HOLD SGLT2 inhibitors 3-4 days before (euglycemic DKA)
  4. HOLD GLP-1 agonists (aspiration risk from delayed gastric emptying)
  5. Stress-dose steroids only for major surgery in high-risk patients
Anticoagulation:
  1. BRIDGE trial: Bridging NOT needed for most AFib patients on warfarin
  2. Bridge only for HIGH-risk: mechanical mitral valve, recent stroke, recent VTE
  3. DOACs: No bridging needed, hold 2-5 days based on renal function
  4. Continue aspirin for CAD/stents if possible (except intracranial surgery)
  5. Stop clopidogrel 5 days, ticagrelor 3-5 days, prasugrel 7 days before
Glycemic Control & VTE:
  1. Target glucose 140-180 (not tighter — NICE-SUGAR)
  2. Reduce basal insulin by 20-25% night before surgery
  3. Orthopedic surgery: Extended VTE prophylaxis 10-35 days
  4. Abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery: 4 weeks VTE prophylaxis
  5. IVC filters NOT for primary prophylaxis, only acute VTE with contraindication to anticoagulation
↑ Back to top

📖 Glossary & Abbreviations

Respiratory Failure & Mechanical Ventilation

▼

Core Concepts to Master

Types of Respiratory Failure

  • Type I (Hypoxemic): PaO2 <60 mmHg on room air
    • A-a gradient elevated
    • Causes: pneumonia, ARDS, pulmonary edema, PE
  • Type II (Hypercapnic): PaCO2 >50 mmHg
    • A-a gradient normal or elevated
    • Causes: COPD, neuromuscular disease, CNS depression, chest wall disorders

Indications for Intubation

  • Airway protection (GCS <8)
  • Hypoxemia refractory to NIV/high-flow O2
  • Severe hypercapnia with altered mental status
  • Impending respiratory arrest
  • Need for procedural sedation

Mechanical Ventilation Basics

Initial Ventilator Settings

  • Mode: Volume control (AC) vs. Pressure control
  • Tidal volume: 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight (ARDS: 6 mL/kg)
  • Respiratory rate: 12-16 (adjust for pH)
  • FiO2: Start 100%, then titrate to SpO2 88-95%
  • PEEP: Start 5 cm H2O, titrate based on oxygenation
Ideal Body Weight (IBW) Calculation
Males: 50 kg + 2.3 kg per inch over 5 feet
Females: 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg per inch over 5 feet

Ventilator Modes

  • AC (Assist Control): Set rate + tidal volume, patient can trigger additional breaths
  • SIMV: Set breaths + spontaneous breathing
  • Pressure Support: Patient-triggered, pressure-targeted
  • APRV: Two levels of CPAP, used in severe ARDS

Key Ventilator Parameters

  • Plateau pressure (Pplat): Should be <30 cm H2O (lung-protective)
  • Driving pressure (Pplat - PEEP): Should be <15 cm H2O
  • Static compliance: Tidal volume / (Pplat - PEEP)

ARDS Management

Berlin Definition

  • Timing: Within 1 week of known insult
  • Imaging: Bilateral opacities not explained by effusions/nodules
  • Origin: Not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload
  • Oxygenation (on PEEP ≥5):
    • Mild: PaO2/FiO2 200-300
    • Moderate: PaO2/FiO2 100-200
    • Severe: PaO2/FiO2 <100

Evidence-Based ARDS Management

  1. Lung-protective ventilation ARMA
    • Tidal volume: 6 mL/kg IBW
    • Plateau pressure: <30 cm H2O
  2. Higher PEEP ALVEOLI LOVS
    • Use PEEP/FiO2 tables
    • Consider recruitment maneuvers
  3. Prone positioning PROSEVA
    • For PaO2/FiO2 <150
    • 16+ hours per day
    • 50% mortality reduction
  4. Neuromuscular blockade ACURASYS
    • For PaO2/FiO2 <150 in first 48 hours
    • Cisatracurium infusion for 48 hours
    • Re-evaluated by ROSE trial (less clear benefit with lighter sedation)
  5. Conservative fluid strategy FACTT
    • After initial resuscitation
    • Target CVP <4 or negative fluid balance
  6. Consider ECMO for refractory hypoxemia
    • PaO2/FiO2 <80 or pH <7.25 with PaCO2 >60

Ventilator Liberation

Daily Assessment for SBT Readiness

  • Adequate oxygenation (FiO2 ≤50%, PEEP ≤8)
  • Hemodynamic stability (no/minimal vasopressors)
  • Able to initiate inspiratory effort
  • No planned procedures requiring sedation

Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT)

  • Method: T-piece or PS 5-7 cm H2O for 30-120 minutes
  • Pass criteria:
    • RR <35 breaths/min
    • SpO2 >90%
    • HR <140 or <20% change
    • SBP 90-180 mmHg
    • No distress/diaphoresis
Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI)
RSBI = RR / Tidal volume (in liters)
<105 predicts successful extubation

Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)

Strong Indications

  • COPD exacerbations with respiratory acidosis (pH 7.25-7.35)
  • Cardiogenic pulmonary edema
  • Immunocompromised patients with respiratory failure
  • Post-extubation respiratory failure (in select patients)

Contraindications

  • Inability to protect airway
  • Hemodynamic instability
  • Facial trauma/burns
  • Recent upper GI surgery
  • Copious secretions/emesis

Settings

  • IPAP: Start 8-10, titrate to 12-20 cm H2O
  • EPAP: Start 4-5 cm H2O
  • Monitor for 1-2 hours: if no improvement → intubate

High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC)

  • Flow rates up to 60 L/min
  • FiO2 up to 100%
  • Generates low-level PEEP (3-5 cm H2O)
  • Washes out dead space

FLORALI trial: Reduced intubation in hypoxemic respiratory failure vs. standard O2

Shock & Hemodynamics

▼

Four Types of Shock

1. Distributive Shock

  • Causes: Sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic
  • Findings: Warm extremities, wide pulse pressure, low SVR
  • Treatment: Fluids → vasopressors (norepinephrine)

2. Cardiogenic Shock

  • Causes: MI, acute valvular disease, myocarditis, post-cardiac arrest
  • Findings: Cold/clammy, elevated JVP, pulmonary edema
  • Markers: Cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m², PCWP >18 mmHg
  • Treatment: Identify cause, inotropes (dobutamine), mechanical support (IABP, Impella)

3. Hypovolemic Shock

  • Causes: Hemorrhage, GI losses, burns, third-spacing
  • Findings: Flat JVP, dry mucous membranes, oliguria
  • Treatment: Fluid resuscitation, blood products if hemorrhagic

4. Obstructive Shock

  • Causes: PE, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, massive RV infarction
  • Findings: Elevated JVP, hypotension, narrow pulse pressure
  • Treatment: Relieve obstruction

Hemodynamic Monitoring

Laboratory Markers

  • Lactate: >2 suggests tissue hypoperfusion (but many causes)
  • ScvO2 (central venous O2 saturation):
    • Normal 70-75%
    • <70% suggests inadequate O2 delivery
    • >75% may suggest sepsis or inability to extract O2

Central Venous Pressure (CVP)

  • Not reliable for predicting fluid responsiveness
  • CVP <8 suggests hypovolemia (but poor sensitivity)
  • Trends more useful than absolute values

Passive Leg Raise (PLR) Test

  • Auto-transfuse ~300 mL from lower extremities
  • Increase in CO/BP by >10% predicts fluid responsiveness
  • More reliable than static measurements

Fluid Resuscitation

Crystalloids

  • Normal saline (0.9% NS): Risk of hyperchloremic acidosis
  • Lactated Ringer's (LR): Balanced, contains potassium
  • Plasmalyte: Balanced alternative

SMART SALT-ED trials: Balanced crystalloids reduced mortality/AKI vs. saline

Recommendation: Use balanced crystalloids as first-line

Colloids

  • Albumin: Consider in cirrhosis with SBP, hepatorenal syndrome
  • SAFE trial: No difference vs. saline except harm in TBI
  • ALBIOS trial: No benefit in septic shock
  • Starches/gelatins: Associated with AKI, avoid

Vasopressors & Inotropes

Norepinephrine (First-Line)

  • Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) >> beta-1 (inotropy)
  • Dose: 0.05-3 mcg/kg/min
  • Increases MAP with minimal effect on HR

Vasopressin

  • V1 receptor agonist → vasoconstriction
  • Dose: 0.03-0.04 units/min (fixed dose)
  • VASST trial: Add to norepinephrine in septic shock
  • May reduce norepi requirements, no mortality benefit

Epinephrine

  • Alpha + beta agonist
  • Dose: 0.05-2 mcg/kg/min
  • Use: Anaphylaxis, refractory shock, cardiac arrest
  • Concerns: Tachycardia, arrhythmias, splanchnic ischemia, lactic acidosis

Dobutamine (Inotrope)

  • Beta-1 >> beta-2 agonist
  • Increases contractility and CO
  • Dose: 2.5-20 mcg/kg/min
  • Use: Cardiogenic shock with reduced CO
  • Concerns: Tachycardia, arrhythmias, hypotension

Milrinone (Inotrope)

  • Phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor
  • Increases contractility, reduces afterload
  • Dose: 0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min
  • Use: Cardiogenic shock, especially with beta-blocker use
  • Concerns: Long half-life, hypotension

MAP Goals

Standard Target: MAP ≥65 mmHg

SEPSISPAM trial: No benefit to MAP 80-85 vs. 65-70 mmHg

Exception: Chronic hypertension → may need higher MAP (75-85)

Individualize based on end-organ perfusion (UOP, mentation, lactate clearance)

Sepsis & Septic Shock

▼

Definitions (Sepsis-3)

Sepsis: Life-threatening organ dysfunction due to dysregulated host response to infection

  • Organ dysfunction: SOFA score increase ≥2 points

Septic Shock: Sepsis + persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors (MAP ≥65) + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation

qSOFA (Quick SOFA - screening tool, NOT diagnostic)

  • RR ≥22
  • Altered mentation
  • SBP ≤100
  • ≥2 points → higher risk of poor outcomes

Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines (2021)

Hour-1 Bundle

  1. Measure lactate (remeasure if >2 mmol/L)
  2. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics
  3. Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics
  4. Begin rapid fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension/lactate ≥4)
  5. Administer vasopressors if hypotensive during/after fluid resuscitation (target MAP ≥65)

Initial Resuscitation

Fluid Resuscitation

  • 30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours for hypotension or lactate ≥4
  • Use balanced crystalloids (SMART/SALT-ED trials)
  • Reassess after each bolus

Vasopressor Initiation

  • Don't delay for "adequate" fluid resuscitation
  • Can start during fluid administration if severely hypotensive
  • First-line: Norepinephrine
  • Add vasopressin (0.03-0.04 units/min) if needs escalating norepi
  • Add epinephrine for refractory shock
  • Consider hydrocortisone (200 mg/day) if refractory to fluids + vasopressors

Antibiotic Management

Timing

  • Within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
  • Each hour delay associated with increased mortality

Common Regimens by Source

Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP):

  • Ceftriaxone + azithromycin OR fluoroquinolone
  • If MRSA risk: Add vancomycin

Hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated pneumonia (HAP/VAP):

  • Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (pip-tazo, cefepime, meropenem) + vancomycin or linezolid
  • Consider double pseudomonal coverage if high resistance

Complicated UTI/Urosepsis:

  • Ceftriaxone OR fluoroquinolone
  • If ESBL risk: Carbapenem (meropenem, ertapenem)

Intra-abdominal:

  • Pip-tazo OR ceftriaxone + metronidazole
  • If high resistance: Meropenem + vancomycin

Neutropenic fever:

  • Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam + vancomycin (if catheter-associated or skin/soft tissue)

De-escalation

  • Narrow based on cultures by 48-72 hours
  • Consider shorter courses (7-8 days) if source controlled and improving

Source Control

  • Identify and control infection source within 6-12 hours
  • Examples: Drain abscess, debride necrotic tissue, remove infected devices
  • Surgical consultation if indicated

Corticosteroids

ADRENAL & APROCCHSS Trials (2018):

  • Hydrocortisone did NOT reduce 90-day mortality
  • May reduce time on vasopressors
  • Small increase in hyperglycemia, hypernatremia

Recommendation: Consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg q6h or 100 mg q12h) if refractory shock despite fluids + vasopressors. Continue for ~5-7 days, then taper.

Additional Considerations

Lactate Clearance

  • Target ≥10% decrease in first 2 hours (not absolute normalization)
  • But don't "chase" lactate at expense of over-resuscitation

Blood Products

  • Transfuse RBCs if Hgb <7 g/dL (unless active hemorrhage or coronary ischemia)
  • Platelet transfusion for active bleeding or procedures if <50,000
  • No routine FFP unless coagulopathy with bleeding/procedures

Neurological Critical Care

▼

Altered Mental Status

Differential Diagnosis (Mnemonic: AEIOU TIPS)

  • A: Alcohol, Acidosis
  • E: Encephalopathy (hepatic, uremic, hypoxic), Electrolytes, Endocrine
  • I: Infection (meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis)
  • O: Overdose, Opioids
  • U: Uremia
  • T: Trauma, Temperature
  • I: Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia)
  • P: Psychosis, Poisoning
  • S: Stroke, Seizure, Syncope, Shock

Workup

  • Glucose (bedside)
  • BMP, calcium, magnesium, phosphate
  • CBC, liver function tests
  • Ammonia (if hepatic encephalopathy suspected)
  • ABG (hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis)
  • Toxicology screen, acetaminophen, salicylate levels
  • Blood cultures, urinalysis
  • CT head (if focal findings, trauma, concern for bleed/mass)
  • LP (if concern for meningitis/encephalitis)
  • EEG (if concern for non-convulsive status epilepticus)

Delirium

CAM-ICU (Confusion Assessment Method for ICU)

  • Feature 1: Acute change or fluctuating course
  • Feature 2: Inattention
  • Feature 3: Altered level of consciousness
  • Feature 4: Disorganized thinking
  • Delirium present if Features 1 + 2 + (3 or 4)

Types

  • Hyperactive: Agitation, restlessness
  • Hypoactive: Lethargy, decreased responsiveness (more common, often missed)
  • Mixed

Prevention & Management (Most Important)

  • ABCDEF Bundle (see Sedation section)
  • Reorient frequently (calendar, clock, family)
  • Minimize sedation, avoid benzodiazepines
  • Early mobilization
  • Hearing aids/glasses
  • Sleep hygiene (reduce nighttime disruptions)
  • Treat underlying causes

Pharmacologic Treatment (only if safety concern)

  • Haloperidol 0.5-2 mg IV/PO
  • Quetiapine 25-50 mg PO
  • Not recommended routinely for prevention

Post-Cardiac Arrest Care

Targeted Temperature Management (TTM)

  • Target: 32-36°C for at least 24 hours
  • TTM-1 trial: 33°C vs. 36°C → no difference
  • TTM-2 trial: 33°C vs. normothermia (avoid fever) → no difference
  • Recommendation: Maintain normothermia, avoid hyperthermia (>37.5°C)

Avoid Hypotension

  • Maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg (consider higher if pre-arrest hypertension)

Neuroprognostication

  • Wait ≥72 hours after ROSC (longer if cooled or sedated)
  • Multimodal assessment:
    • Exam: Pupillary/corneal reflexes, motor response
    • EEG: Continuous background, reactivity
    • SSEP: Bilateral cortical N20 responses
    • Imaging: Brain MRI (DWI)
    • Biomarkers: NSE, S100B (adjunctive)

Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP)

Normal ICP: 5-15 mmHg
Treatment threshold: >20-22 mmHg

Clinical Signs

  • Cushing's triad: Hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations
  • Headache, vomiting, papilledema
  • Altered mental status → coma
  • Pupillary changes, posturing

Tier 1 Interventions

  • Head of bed elevation to 30°
  • Sedation/analgesia (reduce metabolic demand)
  • Maintain normothermia (treat fever)
  • Maintain euvolemia, avoid hypotension (CPP = MAP - ICP; target CPP >60 mmHg)
  • Maintain normal PaCO2 (35-40 mmHg)
  • Treat seizures

Tier 2 Interventions (if ICP remains elevated)

  • Osmotic therapy:
    • Mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg IV (check serum osm, avoid if >320 mOsm/kg)
    • Hypertonic saline 3% or 23.4% (target Na 145-155)
  • Brief hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35) as temporizing measure
  • Neuromuscular blockade

Tier 3 Interventions (refractory)

  • Barbiturate coma (pentobarbital; requires continuous EEG)
  • Decompressive craniectomy
  • Hypothermia (33-35°C)

Status Epilepticus

Definition: Continuous seizure >5 minutes OR recurrent seizures without return to baseline

Management Algorithm

0-5 minutes:

  • Assess ABCs, supplemental O2
  • Check glucose, correct if hypoglycemic
  • Obtain IV access

5-20 minutes:

  • Benzodiazepine:
    • Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (4 mg, may repeat once)
    • Diazepam 0.15 mg/kg IV (10 mg)
    • Midazolam 10 mg IM (if no IV access)

20-40 minutes (Established Status):

  • Second-line AED:
    • Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) OR
    • Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV (max 1500 mg PE) OR
    • Valproic acid 40 mg/kg IV (max 3000 mg)
  • ESETT trial: All three equally effective

>40 minutes (Refractory Status):

  • Continuous infusion + intubation:
    • Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg bolus, then 0.1-2 mg/kg/hr OR
    • Propofol 1-2 mg/kg bolus, then 2-10 mg/kg/hr OR
    • Pentobarbital 5-15 mg/kg bolus, then 0.5-5 mg/kg/hr
  • Continuous EEG monitoring
  • Consult neurology

Renal & Acid-Base

▼

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)

KDIGO Criteria (any of the following)

  • SCr increase ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours OR
  • SCr increase ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days OR
  • UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6 hours

Stages

  • Stage 1: SCr 1.5-1.9x baseline or ↑≥0.3 mg/dL; UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr × 6-12 hr
  • Stage 2: SCr 2.0-2.9x baseline; UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr × ≥12 hr
  • Stage 3: SCr ≥3x baseline or ≥4 mg/dL or initiation of RRT; UOP <0.3 mL/kg/hr × ≥24 hr or anuria × 12 hr

Etiology

  • Prerenal: Hypovolemia, hypotension, reduced effective arterial volume
  • Intrinsic: ATN (ischemic/nephrotoxic), AIN, glomerulonephritis, vascular
  • Postrenal: Obstruction

Workup

  • Urinalysis: WBCs, RBCs, casts, protein
  • Urine electrolytes: FENa, FEUrea
    • FENa <1% suggests prerenal (if not on diuretics)
    • FENa >2% suggests ATN
    • FEUrea <35% suggests prerenal (more reliable if on diuretics)
  • Renal ultrasound: Assess for obstruction, kidney size

Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT)

Indications (AEIOU)

  • A: Acidosis (severe metabolic acidosis, pH <7.1)
  • E: Electrolytes (hyperkalemia refractory to medical management)
  • I: Intoxication (dialyzable toxins: lithium, methanol, ethylene glycol, salicylates)
  • O: Overload (volume overload, refractory pulmonary edema)
  • U: Uremia (symptomatic: encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)

Modalities

Intermittent Hemodialysis (IHD):

  • Duration: 3-4 hours
  • Pros: Rapid correction of electrolytes/volume
  • Cons: Hemodynamic instability, risk of hypotension
  • Use: Stable patients, hyperkalemia, toxin removal

Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT):

  • Duration: 24 hours continuous
  • Pros: Hemodynamic stability, gradual fluid removal
  • Cons: Requires anticoagulation, immobility
  • Use: Hemodynamically unstable patients, cerebral edema

STARRT-AKI Trial (2020):

  • Accelerated (early) vs. standard (delayed) RRT initiation
  • No mortality benefit with early initiation
  • More patients in standard group recovered without RRT

Acid-Base Disorders

Systematic Approach

  1. Check pH: Acidemia (<7.35) or alkalemia (>7.45)?
  2. Determine primary disorder: Respiratory (PaCO2) or metabolic (HCO3)?
  3. Calculate anion gap (if metabolic acidosis)
  4. Check for compensation
  5. Identify additional disorders (delta-delta)
Anion Gap
AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
Normal: 10-12 mEq/L
Adjust for albumin: AG increases ~2.5 for each 1 g/dL decrease in albumin

High Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (MUDPILES)

  • M: Methanol
  • U: Uremia
  • D: DKA
  • P: Propylene glycol, Paracetamol (late)
  • I: Iron, Isoniazid
  • L: Lactic acidosis
  • E: Ethylene glycol
  • S: Salicylates

Non-Anion Gap (Hyperchloremic) Metabolic Acidosis

  • GI losses: Diarrhea, fistula
  • Renal: RTA, early AKI
  • Iatrogenic: Saline infusion, TPN
Compensation Formulas

Metabolic Acidosis:
Expected PaCO2 = 1.5 × (HCO3) + 8 ± 2 (Winter's formula)

Metabolic Alkalosis:
Expected PaCO2 = 0.7 × (HCO3) + 20 ± 5

Respiratory Acidosis:
Acute: HCO3 ↑ by 1 for every 10 ↑ in PaCO2
Chronic: HCO3 ↑ by 3.5 for every 10 ↑ in PaCO2

Respiratory Alkalosis:
Acute: HCO3 ↓ by 2 for every 10 ↓ in PaCO2
Chronic: HCO3 ↓ by 5 for every 10 ↓ in PaCO2

Electrolyte Emergencies

Hyperkalemia

ECG Changes (progressive):

  • Peaked T waves
  • Widened QRS
  • Prolonged PR
  • Loss of P wave
  • Sine wave → VF/asystole

Treatment:

  1. Stabilize cardiac membrane (if ECG changes):
    • Calcium gluconate 1-2 g IV over 2-5 min
  2. Shift K+ intracellularly:
    • Insulin 10 units IV + dextrose 25 g (D50 50 mL)
    • Albuterol 10-20 mg nebulized
    • Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV (if acidotic)
  3. Remove K+ from body:
    • Loop diuretics (furosemide 40-80 mg IV) if UOP adequate
    • Patiromer 8.4 g or Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO
    • Hemodialysis (most effective, for severe/refractory)

Severe Hyponatremia

  • Symptoms: Nausea, headache, confusion, seizures, coma
  • Treatment:
    • 3% NaCl 100 mL bolus, repeat PRN
    • Target: Increase Na by 4-6 mEq/L in first few hours
    • Caution: Avoid correcting >8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours (risk of osmotic demyelination)
Na Increase Formula:
Na increase = (Infusate Na - Serum Na) / (TBW + 1)
TBW = 0.6 × weight (men), 0.5 × weight (women)

Severe Hypernatremia

  • Correct slowly: ≤10-12 mEq/L per 24 hours
  • Use D5W or 0.45% NaCl
  • Caution: Rapid correction → cerebral edema
Free Water Deficit:
Free water deficit = TBW × [(Na/140) - 1]

Sedation, Analgesia & Delirium

▼

ABCDEF Bundle (PAD Guidelines)

  • A: Assess, prevent, and manage pain
  • B: Both spontaneous awakening trials (SAT) and spontaneous breathing trials (SBT)
  • C: Choice of analgesia and sedation
  • D: Delirium - assess, prevent, and manage
  • E: Early mobility and exercise
  • F: Family engagement and empowerment

Pain Assessment

Scales

  • CPOT (Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool): For intubated/non-communicative
  • BPS (Behavioral Pain Scale)
  • Numeric Rating Scale (0-10) for communicative patients

First-Line Analgesics

  • Fentanyl: 25-100 mcg IV q30min-1hr PRN; infusion 25-200 mcg/hr
  • Hydromorphone: 0.2-0.6 mg IV q1-2hr PRN
  • Morphine: Less preferred in ICU (histamine release, active metabolites in renal failure)

Non-Opioid Adjuncts

  • Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h
  • Gabapentin, pregabalin for neuropathic pain
  • Avoid NSAIDs (risk of AKI, bleeding)

Sedation

RASS Scale (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale)

  • +4: Combative
  • +3: Very agitated
  • +2: Agitated
  • +1: Restless
  • 0: Alert and calm
  • -1: Drowsy
  • -2: Light sedation
  • -3: Moderate sedation
  • -4: Deep sedation
  • -5: Unarousable

Target: RASS 0 to -2 (light sedation) for most patients

Sedative Agents

Propofol:

  • Dose: 5-50 mcg/kg/min
  • Pros: Rapid on/off, short context-sensitive half-life
  • Cons: Hypotension, propofol infusion syndrome, hypertriglyceridemia
  • Use: Short-term sedation, neuro patients (lowers ICP)

Dexmedetomidine:

  • Dose: 0.2-1.5 mcg/kg/hr (no bolus)
  • Pros: Less respiratory depression, easier communication, less delirium
  • Cons: Bradycardia, hypotension, expensive
  • Use: Weaning sedation, extubated but agitated, ARDS
  • MENDS/SEDCOM trials: Reduced delirium vs. benzodiazepines

Benzodiazepines (Midazolam, Lorazepam):

  • Cons: Delirium, prolonged awakening, propylene glycol toxicity
  • Avoid as first-line per PAD guidelines
  • Use: Alcohol withdrawal, seizures, neuromuscular blockade

Daily Sedation Interruption (SAT)

  • Turn off sedation daily until patient awakens
  • Assess readiness for extubation
  • Resume at half dose if needed
  • Reduces duration of mechanical ventilation, ICU length of stay

Delirium Management

Screening: CAM-ICU or ICDSC at least daily

Prevention (Most Important)

  • Minimize sedation (target RASS 0 to -1)
  • Avoid benzodiazepines
  • Treat pain adequately
  • Early mobilization
  • Sleep hygiene
  • Reorient frequently
  • Sensory aids (glasses, hearing aids)

Treatment

  • Identify and treat underlying cause
  • Non-pharmacologic measures (above)
  • Pharmacologic (only if safety threat):
    • Haloperidol 0.5-2 mg IV/PO q4-6h PRN
    • Quetiapine 25-50 mg PO BID
  • Do NOT use for prevention

Critical Care Procedures

▼

Central Venous Catheter (CVC)

Indications

  • Vasopressor administration
  • Inadequate peripheral access
  • Monitoring (CVP, ScvO2)
  • Hemodialysis
  • TPN administration

Sites

  • Internal jugular (IJ): Preferred (lower pneumothorax risk)
  • Subclavian: Higher success, lower infection, but higher pneumothorax risk
  • Femoral: Higher infection risk, avoid if ambulating

Technique

  • Ultrasound guidance (reduces complications)
  • Maximal sterile barrier precautions
  • Chlorhexidine skin prep
  • Seldinger technique
  • Confirm placement with CXR (tip at cavoatrial junction)
  • Check for pneumothorax

Complications

  • Arterial puncture
  • Pneumothorax/hemothorax
  • Infection (CLABSI)
  • Thrombosis
  • Air embolism
  • Arrhythmia

Arterial Line

Indications

  • Continuous BP monitoring (vasopressor use)
  • Frequent ABGs
  • Hemodynamic instability

Sites

  • Radial (most common): Perform Allen test first
  • Femoral
  • Brachial (higher complication rate)
  • Dorsalis pedis

Endotracheal Intubation

Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI)

Pre-oxygenation:

  • 100% O2 for 3-5 minutes

Induction Agents:

  • Etomidate 0.3 mg/kg: Hemodynamically stable
  • Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg: Good for asthma, maintains BP
  • Propofol 1-2 mg/kg: Rapid onset, but hypotension
  • Midazolam 0.1-0.3 mg/kg: Slower onset, hemodynamically stable

Neuromuscular Blockade:

  • Succinylcholine 1-1.5 mg/kg: Rapid onset (45 sec), short duration
    • Contraindications: Hyperkalemia, neuromuscular disease, malignant hyperthermia, burns/crush >24 hrs
  • Rocuronium 1-1.2 mg/kg: Onset 60 sec, duration 45-60 min

Confirmation:

  • Chest rise, breath sounds bilateral, EtCO2, CXR

Post-Intubation Hypotension

  • Common! Loss of sympathetic tone + positive pressure ventilation
  • Treat: Fluid bolus, vasopressors

Chest Tube Placement

Indications

  • Pneumothorax (especially tension PTX, hemodynamically significant)
  • Hemothorax
  • Pleural effusion (large, symptomatic, empyema)

Technique

  • Site: 4th-5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line ("safe triangle")
  • Blunt dissection over rib (avoid neurovascular bundle below rib)
  • Direct tube posteriorly and apically
  • Connect to water seal/suction (-20 cm H2O)
  • CXR to confirm

ICU Emergencies

▼

Tension Pneumothorax

Clinical Features

  • Sudden respiratory distress, hypoxia, hypotension
  • Tracheal deviation (away from affected side)
  • Absent breath sounds
  • Distended neck veins
  • Hyperresonance to percussion

Management

  • DO NOT WAIT for imaging
  • Needle decompression: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line (or 5th ICS, anterior axillary line)
  • Immediate chest tube placement

Massive Hemoptysis

Definition: >200-600 mL blood in 24 hours or life-threatening

Management

  1. Airway protection: Consider early intubation
  2. Position: Bleeding side down (protect non-bleeding lung)
  3. Resuscitate: Large-bore IV, type and cross
  4. Bronchoscopy: Identify source, temporize
  5. Interventional radiology: Bronchial artery embolization
  6. Surgery: If refractory

Acute Upper GI Bleeding

Initial Resuscitation

  • Two large-bore IVs
  • Type and cross
  • Correct coagulopathy: FFP (INR >2), platelets (<50k)
  • Transfusion target: Hgb 7-9 g/dL (TRIGGER trial)

Medications

  • PPI: 80 mg bolus, then 8 mg/hr infusion (before endoscopy)
  • Octreotide: 50 mcg bolus, then 50 mcg/hr (if variceal bleed suspected)
  • Erythromycin: 250 mg IV (prokinetic, improves visualization)
  • Antibiotics: Ceftriaxone if cirrhosis

Endoscopy

  • Within 24 hours (12 hours if high risk)
  • Therapies: Clips, cautery, epinephrine injection, banding (varices)

Anaphylaxis

Management

  1. Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1000) in anterolateral thigh - FIRST-LINE
    • Repeat q5-15min if needed
    • IV infusion if refractory: 2-10 mcg/min
  2. Remove trigger
  3. Airway management: Prepare for intubation if angioedema/stridor
  4. IV fluids: 1-2 L bolus
  5. Adjunctive:
    • H1 blocker: Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV
    • H2 blocker: Ranitidine 50 mg IV
    • Glucocorticoids: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
    • Albuterol for bronchospasm
  6. Observe: 4-8 hours minimum

Cardiac Arrest (ACLS)

Immediate Actions

  1. Recognize arrest: Unresponsive, no pulse, apnea/gasping
  2. Call for help, get defibrillator
  3. High-quality CPR:
    • Rate: 100-120/min
    • Depth: ≥2 inches (5 cm)
    • Allow full recoil
    • Minimize interruptions (<10 sec)

Shockable Rhythms (VF/pVT)

  • Defibrillate: 200 J biphasic
  • Resume CPR immediately × 2 min
  • Epinephrine: 1 mg IV q3-5min (after 2nd shock)
  • Amiodarone: 300 mg IV after 3rd shock (then 150 mg)

Non-Shockable Rhythms (Asystole/PEA)

  • CPR × 2 min
  • Epinephrine: 1 mg IV q3-5min (ASAP)
  • Treat reversible causes (H's & T's)

Reversible Causes (H's & T's)

  • H: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia
  • T: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (MI, PE)

Preventive Care & Complications

▼

Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) Prophylaxis

Pharmacologic Prophylaxis

  • Enoxaparin: 40 mg SC daily (30 mg daily if CrCl <30)
  • Heparin: 5000 units SC q8-12h
  • If CrCl <30: Unfractionated heparin preferred
  • Contraindications: Active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia (<50k), high bleeding risk

Mechanical Prophylaxis

  • Sequential compression devices (SCDs): Use if pharmacologic contraindicated
  • Combine with pharmacologic when possible

Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis

Indications (ASHP Guidelines)

  • Mechanical ventilation >48 hours
  • Coagulopathy (platelets <50k, INR >1.5, PTT >2× normal)
  • Other risk factors: Burns >35% BSA, hepatic failure, multiple trauma, TBI

Agents

  • Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs): Pantoprazole 40 mg IV/PO daily (preferred)
  • H2 blockers: Famotidine 20 mg IV/PO BID

Catheter-Associated Infections

CLABSI Prevention

  • Maximal sterile barriers during insertion
  • Chlorhexidine skin prep
  • Avoid femoral site if possible
  • Daily review: Remove if not needed
  • Dressing changes per protocol

CAUTI Prevention

  • Remove Foley ASAP (most important)
  • Use only when necessary
  • Sterile insertion technique
  • Maintain closed drainage system

Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP) Prevention

VAP Bundle

  • Head of bed elevation (30-45°)
  • Daily sedation interruption + spontaneous breathing trials
  • Oral care with chlorhexidine
  • Peptic ulcer prophylaxis
  • DVT prophylaxis
  • Subglottic suctioning (if available)

ICU-Acquired Weakness

Risk Factors

  • Prolonged mechanical ventilation
  • Sepsis, MODS
  • Hyperglycemia
  • Corticosteroids
  • Neuromuscular blocking agents

Prevention

  • Early mobilization: Out of bed by ICU day 2-3 if able
  • Minimize sedation
  • Glycemic control
  • Physical therapy/occupational therapy consults

Landmark Trials & Key Papers

▼

Respiratory/ARDS

ARMA (NEJM 2000) - Low tidal volume ventilation in ARDS

  • 6 mL/kg IBW vs. 12 mL/kg IBW
  • 22% relative mortality reduction
  • Established lung-protective ventilation

PROSEVA (NEJM 2013) - Prone positioning in severe ARDS

  • 16+ hours/day prone vs. supine
  • 50% relative mortality reduction in PaO2/FiO2 <150

ACURASYS (NEJM 2010) - Neuromuscular blockade in early ARDS

  • 48 hours cisatracurium vs. placebo in PaO2/FiO2 <150
  • Improved 90-day survival (but see ROSE trial)

ROSE (NEJM 2019) - Reevaluation of neuromuscular blockade

  • No benefit when using lighter sedation strategy
  • Current practice: Consider for severe ARDS with difficult oxygenation

FACTT (NEJM 2006) - Conservative vs. liberal fluid strategy in ARDS

  • Conservative strategy improved ventilator-free days
  • No mortality difference

Sepsis

SMART (NEJM 2018) - Balanced crystalloids vs. saline

  • Reduced mortality, AKI, need for RRT
  • Use balanced crystalloids in ICU

VASST (NEJM 2008) - Vasopressin in septic shock

  • No mortality benefit vs. norepinephrine alone
  • May reduce norepinephrine requirements

ADRENAL & APROCCHSS (NEJM 2018) - Hydrocortisone in septic shock

  • No clear 90-day mortality benefit
  • May reduce vasopressor duration

Sedation

MENDS (JAMA 2007), SEDCOM (Lancet 2009) - Dexmedetomidine vs. benzodiazepines

  • Reduced delirium, more days alive without coma/delirium
  • Established dex as preferred agent

Transfusion

TRICC (NEJM 1999) - Restrictive vs. liberal transfusion

  • Hgb 7-9 g/dL vs. 10-12 g/dL
  • Restrictive strategy safe, possibly superior

Post-Cardiac Arrest

TTM-1 (NEJM 2013) - Targeted temperature 33°C vs. 36°C

  • No difference in mortality or neurologic outcomes

TTM-2 (NEJM 2021) - Hypothermia (33°C) vs. normothermia

  • No mortality benefit to hypothermia
  • Avoid hyperthermia (>37.5°C)

Renal

STARRT-AKI (NEJM 2020) - Early vs. standard RRT initiation

  • No benefit to early initiation
  • More patients in standard group recovered without RRT

Daily ICU Routine & Practical Tips

▼

Daily Rounds Structure (Systems-Based)

Neuro

  • Mental status (CAM-ICU)
  • Sedation/analgesia goals (RASS)
  • Delirium management
  • Neuro exam if applicable

Cardiovascular

  • Hemodynamics: BP, HR, MAP
  • Vasopressor/inotrope needs
  • Fluid status (I/O, edema)
  • DVT prophylaxis

Respiratory

  • Vent settings, mode
  • ABG, SpO2, FiO2, PEEP
  • Secretions, CXR findings
  • SBT readiness
  • VAP prevention bundle

Renal

  • UOP, creatinine trend
  • Fluid balance (24-hr, cumulative)
  • Electrolytes
  • RRT if applicable

GI/Nutrition

  • Bowel function
  • Feeding (enteral vs. parenteral, goal rate)
  • LFTs
  • Stress ulcer prophylaxis

Heme/ID

  • CBC, coagulation studies
  • Antibiotic spectrum/duration
  • Cultures pending
  • Fever workup if applicable

Disposition

  • Goals of care
  • Family meeting needs
  • Discharge planning

Ventilator Troubleshooting

High Peak Pressure

  • Check: Biting ETT, secretions, bronchospasm, PTX, mainstem intubation, kinked circuit
  • Differentiate from high plateau pressure (parenchymal issue)

Low Tidal Volumes

  • Check: Cuff leak, circuit disconnect, worsening lung compliance

Hypoxemia

  • Increase FiO2, PEEP
  • Recruitment maneuver
  • Check position (proning)
  • Rule out: PTX, mucus plug, atelectasis, PE

Hypercapnia

  • Increase respiratory rate
  • Increase tidal volume (if plateau pressure safe)
  • Reduce dead space

Common ICU Drips

Norepinephrine
Concentration: 16 mg in 250 mL (64 mcg/mL)
Dose: 0.05-3 mcg/kg/min

Vasopressin
Concentration: 20 units in 100 mL (0.2 units/mL)
Dose: 0.03-0.04 units/min (fixed dose)

Epinephrine
Concentration: 4 mg in 250 mL (16 mcg/mL)
Dose: 0.05-2 mcg/kg/min

Propofol
Concentration: 10 mg/mL (premixed)
Dose: 5-50 mcg/kg/min

Fentanyl
Concentration: 2 mg in 100 mL (20 mcg/mL)
Dose: 25-200 mcg/hr

Dexmedetomidine
Concentration: 200 mcg in 50 mL (4 mcg/mL)
Dose: 0.2-1.5 mcg/kg/hr

Goals of Care Discussions

When to Initiate

  • Patient/family request
  • Lack of improvement despite maximal therapy
  • Terminal illness
  • Poor anticipated quality of life

Approach

  • Assess understanding: "What have the doctors told you?"
  • Provide information: Honest, clear, avoid jargon
  • Explore values: "What's most important to your loved one?"
  • Make recommendations: Based on evidence + patient values
  • Summarize and plan: Ensure agreement on next steps

Key Phrases

  • "I wish things were different..."
  • "Given what we know, I'm worried that..."
  • "Would it be okay if I shared what I've seen in similar situations?"
  • Avoid: "There's nothing more we can do" (reframe as focus on comfort)

Study Plan Recommendations

▼

Week-by-Week Preparation

Week 1-2: Foundation

  • UpToDate: Read overview articles on respiratory failure, mechanical ventilation, shock
  • Review ARDS Berlin criteria + management (ARMA, PROSEVA trials)
  • Practice ventilator calculations (IBW, tidal volumes, plateau pressures)

Week 3: Sepsis & Hemodynamics

  • Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines (2021)
  • Understand vasopressor mechanisms
  • Practice MAP calculations, fluid responsiveness assessment

Week 4: Neuro, Renal, Sedation

  • Post-cardiac arrest care
  • KDIGO criteria for AKI
  • PAD Guidelines for sedation (ABCDEF bundle)
  • Practice acid-base problems

Week 5: Procedures & Emergencies

  • Review procedural techniques (CVC, arterial line)
  • ACLS algorithms
  • ICU emergency management

Final Week: Integration

  • Daily ICU rounds structure
  • Landmark trials review
  • Mock cases (ARDS, septic shock, post-arrest)

Key Resources

  • UpToDate: Primary reference for pathophysiology + management
  • NEJM/JAMA: Landmark trials (ARMA, PROSEVA, SMART, TTM)
  • Guidelines: Surviving Sepsis, PAD (sedation), KDIGO (AKI)
  • SCCM Website: Society of Critical Care Medicine - guidelines + education

Final Tips for Success

  1. Be systematic: Use a structured approach for rounds and assessments
  2. Think in terms of organ systems: Neuro, CV, Resp, Renal, etc.
  3. Know your numbers: IBW, tidal volumes, PEEP, MAP goals
  4. Don't delay treatment: Sepsis antibiotics, shock resuscitation
  5. Daily checklist: SBT readiness, line necessity, delirium assessment
  6. Practice procedures: Get supervised experience early
  7. Communicate with family: ICU is stressful; frequent updates help
  8. Take care of yourself: ICU is intense; debrief with team, maintain wellness

Good luck on your rotation! Every intensivist was once overwhelmed on their first ICU rotation. Focus on the fundamentals, ask questions, and learn from every patient encounter.

📖 Glossary & Abbreviations

▼

Quick reference for common critical care terms and abbreviations. Click any term for detailed definition. Throughout the guide, underlined blue terms are clickable for instant definitions.

Critical Care Medicine Study Guide | Internal Medicine Residency

Evidence-based references: UpToDate, NEJM, JAMA, Surviving Sepsis Campaign, PAD Guidelines, KDIGO

High-Yield Clinical Pearls from All Specialty Study Guides

9
Specialties
150+
Pearls
50+
Categories
🦠 ID 🫀 Cardio 🟠 GI 🦴 Rheum 🔬 Endo 💧 Nephro 🫁 Pulm 🩸 Heme/Onc 🧠 Neuro
🦠 Infectious Disease
18 pearls ▼
Pneumonia
💎CAP empiric therapy: β-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone. Add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage if risk factors.
💎CURB-65 predicts CAP mortality — Confusion, Uremia, RR ≥30, BP low, Age ≥65. Score ≥2 = consider admission.
💎Aspiration pneumonia: Treat with ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin for anaerobic coverage.
Meningitis
💎Don't delay antibiotics for LP in suspected bacterial meningitis — give empiric therapy immediately.
💎Dexamethasone before/with first dose of antibiotics reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis.
💎HSV encephalitis: Temporal lobe involvement, start acyclovir empirically. PCR confirms.
Endocarditis
💎Modified Duke criteria: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor = definite IE.
💎Culture-negative endocarditis: Think HACEK, Coxiella, Bartonella, Brucella, fungi.
💎Surgery indications: Heart failure, uncontrolled infection, large vegetations, prosthetic valve.
HIV/AIDS
💎CD4 <200: Start PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX). Stop when CD4 >200 for 3 months.
💎CD4 <50: Start MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin weekly).
💎IRIS: Inflammatory response after starting ART. Treat underlying infection, continue ART.
Tuberculosis
💎RIPE therapy: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol × 2 months, then RI × 4 months.
💎Add pyridoxine (B6) with INH to prevent peripheral neuropathy.
💎Check baseline LFTs — rifampin and INH are hepatotoxic.
Antibiotics
💎Ceftriaxone contraindicated with calcium-containing IV solutions in neonates (precipitation).
💎Fluoroquinolones: QT prolongation, tendon rupture, C. diff. Avoid in myasthenia gravis.
💎Vancomycin trough: Target 15-20 for serious infections. Monitor renal function.
🫀 Cardiology
20 pearls ▼
Acute Coronary Syndrome
💎STEMI: Door-to-balloon <90 min. If no PCI available, fibrinolytics within 30 min.
💎NSTEMI/UA: DAPT + anticoagulation. Risk stratify with TIMI or GRACE score.
💎Post-MI discharge: DAPT, statin, beta-blocker, ACEi (if EF reduced), lifestyle.
Heart Failure
💎HFrEF GDMT: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i = "Fantastic Four"
💎Don't start beta-blocker in acute decompensated HF. Okay to continue if already on.
💎ICD indicated if EF ≤35% despite GDMT × 3 months (primary prevention).
Arrhythmias
💎Afib stroke risk: CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) = anticoagulate.
💎Afib + WPW: NO AV nodal blockers! Use procainamide or cardioversion.
💎Torsades de pointes: IV magnesium first, even if Mg is normal.
Valvular Disease
💎Severe AS: Valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, Vmax >4 m/s.
💎Symptoms in severe AS (angina, syncope, HF) = intervention needed.
💎Mitral stenosis: Think rheumatic heart disease. A-fib common, anticoagulate.
Hypertension
💎HTN urgency vs emergency: Emergency = end-organ damage. Reduce BP gradually.
💎First-line: Thiazide, CCB, ACEi/ARB. Combine if needed.
💎Resistant HTN workup: Check adherence, consider secondary causes (OSA, aldosteronism).
Pericardial Disease
💎Acute pericarditis: NSAIDs + colchicine × 3 months. Reduces recurrence.
💎Cardiac tamponade: Beck's triad, pulsus paradoxus. Emergent pericardiocentesis.
💎Constrictive pericarditis: Kussmaul's sign, calcified pericardium, "square root" sign.
Lipids
💎High-intensity statin for all ASCVD patients regardless of baseline LDL.
💎PCSK9 inhibitors: For familial hypercholesterolemia or ASCVD not at goal on max statin.
🟠 Gastroenterology & Hepatology
18 pearls ▼
Upper GI
💎Variceal bleed: Octreotide + PPI + ceftriaxone + emergent EGD within 12h.
💎H. pylori treatment: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14 days. Confirm eradication.
💎Barrett's esophagus: Intestinal metaplasia. Surveillance EGD per dysplasia grade.
Liver Disease
💎Acute liver failure: INR >1.5 + encephalopathy. N-acetylcysteine for all causes helps.
💎Hepatorenal syndrome: Albumin + midodrine + octreotide. Or norepinephrine if ICU.
💎SBP prophylaxis: Fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX if ascites protein <1.5 + risk factors.
Inflammatory Bowel Disease
💎UC vs Crohn's: UC = bloody diarrhea, continuous, rectum always involved. Crohn's = skip lesions, transmural.
💎Toxic megacolon: NPO, IV steroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics. Surgery if no improvement.
💎Screen for TB and Hep B before starting biologics (anti-TNF, etc.).
Pancreatic Disease
💎Acute pancreatitis: Aggressive IVF, pain control, NPO initially. Lipase >3× ULN.
💎Gallstone pancreatitis: ERCP within 24h if cholangitis or persistent obstruction. Cholecystectomy before discharge.
💎Chronic pancreatitis: Pain, malabsorption, diabetes. CT shows calcifications.
Colon
💎C. diff first episode: Vancomycin PO (not IV). Fidaxomicin for recurrence.
💎Diverticulitis: Uncomplicated = antibiotics + diet. Complicated = abscess, perforation, fistula.
💎Colon cancer screening: Start age 45. Colonoscopy q10y or alternatives.
Biliary
💎Acute cholangitis (Charcot triad): Fever, RUQ pain, jaundice. Reynolds pentad adds shock + AMS.
💎PSC: Associated with UC. "Beading" on MRCP. Increases cholangiocarcinoma risk.
💎PBC: AMA positive, elevated ALP, pruritus. Ursodiol is treatment.
🦴 Rheumatology
21 pearls ▼
Diagnostic Pearls
💎Negative ANA essentially rules out SLE — sensitivity >95%.
💎CRP usually normal in SLE flares — if elevated, think infection.
💎Uric acid can be normal during gout attack — don't use to rule out.
💎DIP involvement: OA or PsA, NOT RA.
💎Morning stiffness >1 hour: inflammatory arthritis. <30 min: mechanical.
💎Normal CK: PMR, NOT myositis.
💎Jaw claudication: highly specific for GCA.
Treatment Pearls
💎Start steroids immediately for suspected GCA — don't wait for biopsy.
💎ACE inhibitors for scleroderma renal crisis — even if Cr rises.
💎Don't start/stop ULT during acute gout attack.
💎HCQ for everyone with SLE — reduces flares and mortality.
💎Warfarin (not DOACs) for APS — TRAPS trial.
💎MTX doesn't work for axial SpA — use biologics.
💎Rituximab = preferred for relapsing AAV — RAVE trial.
Safety Pearls
💎Screen for TB before starting biologics.
💎HLA-B*5801 testing before allopurinol in high-risk populations (Asian, African descent).
💎Eye exam for HCQ — baseline, then annually after 5 years.
💎Check TPMT before azathioprine.
💎Avoid steroids >15mg in diffuse SSc — precipitates renal crisis.
💎C-spine imaging before intubating severe RA patients (atlantoaxial instability).
💎Triple-positive APS (LA + aCL + anti-β2GP1) = highest thrombosis risk.
🔬 Endocrinology
22 pearls ▼
Diabetes
💎SGLT2i and GLP-1 RA have cardiorenal benefits independent of glucose control.
💎Never use sliding scale alone — always add basal insulin.
💎Check K+ before starting insulin in DKA — insulin drives K+ intracellularly.
💎Low C-peptide with hypoglycemia = exogenous insulin.
💎DKA in T2DM: Think SGLT2i-induced euglycemic DKA. Check ketones even if glucose normal.
Thyroid
💎Low RAIU = thyroiditis or exogenous hormone. High RAIU = Graves' or toxic nodule.
💎Always check prolactin before pituitary surgery — prolactinomas are treated medically.
💎PTU before iodine in thyroid storm.
💎PTU in 1st trimester, methimazole in 2nd/3rd (PTU = hepatotoxic, MMI = teratogenic in T1).
💎Subclinical hypothyroidism: Treat if TSH >10, or symptomatic, or pregnant.
Adrenal
💎IPSS = gold standard for distinguishing pituitary vs ectopic Cushing's.
💎Alpha-blocker before beta-blocker in pheochromocytoma — prevent unopposed α-stimulation.
💎Give stress-dose steroids in adrenal crisis — don't wait for labs. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV.
💎Hyperpigmentation = primary adrenal insufficiency (high ACTH stimulates melanocytes).
Pituitary
💎Prolactinoma is the ONLY pituitary tumor treated primarily with medication (dopamine agonists).
💎Large mass + mildly elevated prolactin = stalk effect, not prolactinoma.
💎Replace cortisol BEFORE thyroid to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.
Calcium/Bone
💎First step in hypercalcemia workup: check PTH.
💎Correct magnesium before treating hypocalcemia — PTH won't work if Mg is low.
💎Bisphosphonate drug holiday after 5 years oral or 3 years IV (fracture risk/benefit).
MEN Syndromes
💎MEN1: "3 P's" — Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreatic neuroendocrine.
💎MEN2: Always rule out pheo before thyroidectomy for MTC.
💧 Nephrology
18 pearls ▼
Acute Kidney Injury
💎FENa <1% = prerenal (unless on diuretics — use FEUrea <35%).
💎Muddy brown casts = ATN. RBC casts = glomerulonephritis. WBC casts = pyelonephritis/AIN.
💎Stop ACEi/ARB in AKI but restart when stable — they're renoprotective long-term.
Chronic Kidney Disease
💎SGLT2 inhibitors slow CKD progression — use unless eGFR <20 (varies by agent).
💎Finerenone reduces CV events and CKD progression in diabetic kidney disease.
💎Target BP <120/80 in CKD with proteinuria (SPRINT trial).
Electrolytes
💎Correct sodium slowly: ≤8-10 mEq/L per 24h to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.
💎Hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis + HTN: Think hyperaldosteronism.
💎Hyperkalemia ECG changes: Peaked T waves → prolonged PR → wide QRS → sine wave → arrest.
Acid-Base
💎MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, INH/Iron, Lactic acid, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.
💎Winter's formula: Expected PaCO2 = 1.5 × [HCO3] + 8 ± 2 (for metabolic acidosis compensation).
💎RTA Type 1: Distal, low K+, can't acidify urine. Type 4: Low K+, low aldosterone effect.
Glomerular Disease
💎Nephrotic: Proteinuria >3.5g/day, edema, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia.
💎Nephritic: RBC casts, HTN, oliguria, mild-moderate proteinuria.
💎Anti-GBM disease: Linear IgG on IF. May have pulmonary hemorrhage (Goodpasture).
Dialysis
💎Urgent dialysis indications: A-E-I-O-U — Acidosis, Electrolytes (K+), Ingestion, Overload, Uremia symptoms.
💎Dialysis disequilibrium: Cerebral edema from rapid urea removal. Start slow in new HD patients.
🫁 Pulmonology
18 pearls ▼
Obstructive Disease
💎FEV1/FVC <0.70 = obstruction. Reversibility (≥12% + 200mL) suggests asthma.
💎Normal or rising PaCO2 in acute asthma = impending respiratory failure.
💎GOLD 2024: ICS in COPD if eosinophils ≥300; avoid if <100.
💎Only smoking cessation and O2 (if hypoxemic) proven to improve COPD survival.
Interstitial Lung Disease
💎UIP pattern = honeycombing, basal/peripheral, traction bronchiectasis.
💎NO steroids in IPF — PANTHER trial showed harm.
💎Chronic HP can mimic IPF — look for upper lobe involvement, exposure history.
💎Pleural plaques = asbestos; eggshell nodes = silicosis.
Pulmonary Vascular
💎V/Q scan is screening test for CTEPH (CTPA may miss it).
💎Right heart cath required for PH diagnosis and treatment decisions.
💎Massive PE = hemodynamic instability → thrombolysis.
Critical Care
💎ARDS: Low Vt (6 mL/kg IBW), plateau <30, prone if P/F <150.
💎BiPAP in AECOPD reduces intubation and mortality.
💎Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD (avoid suppressing hypoxic drive).
Pleural Disease
💎Light's criteria: any 1 of 3 = exudate.
💎pH <7.2 in parapneumonic effusion = drain (complicated).
Lung Cancer
💎Screening: Age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current or quit <15 years → annual LDCT.
💎SCLC: Central, aggressive, paraneoplastic (SIADH, Cushing's, Lambert-Eaton).
🩸 Hematology/Oncology
21 pearls ▼
Anemia
💎Low ferritin = iron deficiency. Normal ferritin with inflammation doesn't rule it out.
💎B12 deficiency can cause neuro symptoms WITHOUT anemia. Check MMA if borderline.
💎Spherocytes + positive DAT = warm AIHA. Spherocytes + negative DAT = hereditary spherocytosis.
💎G6PD assay is falsely normal during acute hemolysis — retest later.
Bleeding Disorders
💎Prolonged PTT + mixing corrects = factor deficiency. Doesn't correct = inhibitor.
💎vWD: Most common inherited bleeding disorder. DDAVP for Type 1.
💎DIC: Treat the underlying cause. Replace products only if bleeding.
Thrombosis
💎HIT = prothrombotic! Stop heparin AND start non-heparin anticoagulant.
💎TTP: Don't wait for ADAMTS13 — start plasma exchange immediately.
💎Warfarin skin necrosis = protein C/S deficiency. Bridge with heparin.
Leukemia
💎APL [t(15;17)] = DIC. Start ATRA immediately on suspicion.
💎CML = Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22), BCR-ABL1. TKIs are curative.
💎CLL = CD5+/CD23+ B-cells. Watch and wait if asymptomatic.
💎Auer rods = AML (not ALL).
Lymphoma
💎Reed-Sternberg cells = Hodgkin. CD15+/CD30+.
💎"Starry sky" pattern = Burkitt lymphoma. t(8;14) c-MYC.
💎Gastric MALT lymphoma: Treat H. pylori first — 70% remission with antibiotics alone.
Myeloma
💎CRAB criteria: Calcium, Renal, Anemia, Bone lesions.
💎Lytic lesions on skeletal survey (not bone scan — no blastic activity).
Transfusion
💎TRALI = noncardiogenic pulmonary edema. TACO = volume overload (diuretics help).
💎Febrile non-hemolytic = most common reaction. Cytokines in product.
🧠 Neurology
21 pearls ▼
Stroke
💎tPA window 4.5 hours; thrombectomy up to 24h with favorable imaging.
💎Dual antiplatelet (ASA + clopidogrel) × 21 days after minor stroke/TIA.
💎Nimodipine for ALL SAH patients × 21 days (vasospasm prevention).
Seizures
💎Lorazepam IV first-line for status epilepticus.
💎Valproate teratogenic — avoid in women of childbearing age.
💎Check HLA-B*1502 before carbamazepine (SJS risk in Asians).
Headache
💎Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise.
💎Cluster headache: High-flow O2 + sumatriptan SC; verapamil for prevention.
💎New headache >50 years = consider temporal arteritis.
Multiple Sclerosis
💎INO (impaired adduction with nystagmus in abducting eye) = MLF lesion, classic for MS.
💎Oligoclonal bands in CSF in >90% of MS.
💎Natalizumab: Check JCV antibody (PML risk).
Movement Disorders
💎PD tremor: Rest, 4-6 Hz, asymmetric, pill-rolling.
💎Essential tremor: Action, higher frequency, bilateral, improves with alcohol.
💎Early falls + vertical gaze palsy = PSP.
Neuromuscular
💎GBS: Ascending weakness + areflexia + albuminocytologic dissociation. Steroids DON'T work.
💎Myasthenia: Fatigable weakness, AChR antibodies, thymoma association.
💎Aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones worsen MG.
Dementia & CNS Infections
💎NPH triad: Wet, wobbly, wacky (incontinence, gait, dementia) — treatable!
💎DLB: Visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations; sensitive to antipsychotics.
💎HSV encephalitis: Temporal lobe, start acyclovir empirically.

💎 ABIM Board Pearls — Compiled from all specialty study guides

📚 Study Guides 🩺 IM Guide 💊 Drug Guide

Evidence-based • ABIM-focused • Quick reference

↑ Back to top

💰 High Value Care

ACP/ABIM Board Review — Evidence-Based, Cost-Conscious Medicine

🎯 Core Principles of High Value Care

🚫 Avoid unnecessary tests & treatments
💡 Choose cost-effective alternatives
📊 Use evidence-based screening
⚖️ Balance benefits vs harms
🤝 Shared decision-making
💊 Deprescribe when appropriate
🔍 Screening 📷 Imaging 🧪 Laboratory 🫀 Cardiology 🫁 Pulmonology 🟠 GI 🦠 Infectious Disease 🧠 Neurology 🦴 Rheumatology 🔬 Endocrine 💧 Nephrology 🩸 Hematology 👴 Geriatrics 🏥 Perioperative 🛏️ Hospital Medicine
🔍 Cancer Screening & Prevention
▼
✅ Evidence-Based Screening (USPSTF Grade A/B)
✓
Lung Cancer USPSTF A
Annual low-dose CT: Age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current smoker or quit <15 years. Stop if quit >15 years or limited life expectancy.
✓
Colorectal Cancer USPSTF A
Start age 45, stop at 75 (individualize 76-85). Options: Colonoscopy q10y, FIT annually, or Cologuard q3y. Colonoscopy NOT superior to FIT for average-risk screening.
✓
Breast Cancer USPSTF B
Mammography every 2 years, ages 40-74. Annual mammography not proven superior to biennial.
✓
Cervical Cancer USPSTF A
Age 21-29: Pap q3y. Age 30-65: Pap + HPV q5y (preferred) or Pap alone q3y. Stop at 65 if adequate prior screening.
🚫 Low-Value Screening (Avoid)
✗
PSA Screening for Prostate Cancer USPSTF C
Do not routinely screen. Shared decision-making for men 55-69. Do NOT screen men ≥70 or with <10-year life expectancy.
✗
Ovarian Cancer Screening USPSTF D
Do not screen with CA-125 or transvaginal ultrasound in average-risk women. Causes more harm than benefit.
✗
Thyroid Cancer Screening USPSTF D
Do not screen asymptomatic adults with neck palpation or ultrasound.
✗
Pancreatic Cancer Screening
Do not screen average-risk adults. No proven effective screening method.
✗
Whole-Body CT Screening
Never recommended. High false positive rate, radiation exposure, incidentalomas, unnecessary procedures.
📌 When to STOP Screening
  • Life expectancy <10 years (cancer screening unlikely to provide benefit)
  • Colonoscopy: After age 75 (or 85 max)
  • Pap smear: After age 65 with adequate prior screening
  • Mammography: Individualize after 75; stop if limited life expectancy
📷 Diagnostic Imaging
▼
🚫 Avoid Unnecessary Imaging
✗
Acute Low Back Pain (<6 weeks) Choosing Wisely
No imaging without red flags (trauma, neurologic deficits, cancer history, fever, IV drug use, osteoporosis, age >50 with new symptoms, prolonged steroid use).
✗
Uncomplicated Headache Choosing Wisely
No imaging for stable headache patterns without red flags. Red flags: thunderclap, neurologic signs, papilledema, fever, immunocompromise, cancer, age >50 new onset.
✗
Syncope Without High-Risk Features Choosing Wisely
No head CT or carotid imaging for typical vasovagal syncope. Focus on cardiac workup if concerning features.
✗
Minor Head Injury Choosing Wisely
Use Canadian CT Head Rule to avoid unnecessary CT. Low-risk patients (GCS 15, no LOC, no amnesia, no vomiting) do not need imaging.
✗
Preoperative CXR Choosing Wisely
Not routine. Only if acute cardiopulmonary symptoms or needed for specific surgical planning.
✗
Routine Follow-up Imaging After Incidental Findings
Follow Fleischner (pulmonary nodules), Bosniak (renal cysts), ACR (adrenal incidentaloma) guidelines. Many small findings need no follow-up.
✅ Use Clinical Decision Rules
Condition Decision Rule Purpose
Pulmonary Embolism Wells + D-dimer (PERC if very low risk) Avoid CT in low-risk patients
Ankle/Foot Injury Ottawa Ankle Rules Reduce unnecessary X-rays by 30-40%
Knee Injury Ottawa Knee Rules Rule out fracture clinically
C-Spine Injury NEXUS or Canadian C-Spine Rule Avoid CT in low-risk trauma
Head Injury Canadian CT Head Rule Identify who needs CT
📌 Imaging Pearls
  • MRI is NOT always "better" than CT — choose based on clinical question
  • Contrast is often unnecessary and adds risk (nephropathy, allergy)
  • PET scans should not be used for cancer screening
  • Repeat imaging rarely needed if prior study was normal and symptoms unchanged
🧪 Laboratory Testing
▼
🚫 Low-Value Laboratory Tests
✗
Routine Daily Labs in Stable Hospitalized Patients Choosing Wisely
Order labs only when results will change management. Daily BMP/CBC not needed in stable patients.
✗
Routine Preoperative Testing Choosing Wisely
No routine CBC, BMP, coags, or UA before low-risk surgery in healthy patients. Test only if indicated by history/exam.
✗
BNP for Routine Dyspnea Evaluation
BNP is most useful when HF probability is intermediate. Don't use in clear-cut cases (obvious HF or clearly non-cardiac).
✗
D-dimer in High-Risk PE Patients
Skip D-dimer if Wells score high — go directly to imaging. D-dimer only useful in low/moderate pretest probability.
✗
ESR/CRP for Non-specific Symptoms
Don't order as "fishing expeditions." Useful for specific inflammatory conditions (PMR, GCA, RA monitoring), not general workup.
✗
ANA Without Clinical Suspicion for CTD Choosing Wisely
ANA is positive in many healthy people. Only order if clinical features suggest SLE, scleroderma, or other CTD.
✗
Lyme Serology Without Appropriate Exposure/Symptoms Choosing Wisely
High false positive rate. Only test with compatible clinical syndrome AND endemic exposure.
✅ When Testing Adds Value
✓
HbA1c for Diabetes Screening
Screen adults 35-70 who are overweight/obese. More convenient than fasting glucose, one-time test.
✓
Lipid Panel for CV Risk Assessment
Non-fasting lipids acceptable for initial screening. Repeat fasting only if triglycerides very high.
✓
High-Sensitivity Troponin
Enables faster rule-out of MI with 0/1-hour or 0/3-hour protocols. Reduces length of stay.
🫀 Cardiology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Cardiac Testing
✗
Stress Testing in Asymptomatic Low-Risk Patients Choosing Wisely
Do not perform routine stress testing in patients without symptoms. Does not improve outcomes.
✗
Routine Preoperative Stress Testing ACC/AHA
Only if it will change management. Most patients can proceed to surgery with medical optimization.
✗
Annual ECGs in Asymptomatic Adults Choosing Wisely
Not recommended for routine screening. Leads to false positives and unnecessary testing.
✗
Echocardiogram for Uncomplicated Hypertension Choosing Wisely
Not routine. Reserve for suspected HF, valvular disease, or hypertensive emergency with LV dysfunction concern.
✗
Coronary Calcium Score in Known CAD
No added value if CAD already established. Useful only for risk stratification in intermediate-risk asymptomatic patients.
✗
Routine Follow-up Imaging After Stable Stent
No routine stress testing after PCI if asymptomatic. Only if new or worsening symptoms.
✅ High-Value Cardiology Practices
✓
Exercise ECG Before Imaging
If patient can exercise and has interpretable ECG, start with exercise ECG (not imaging stress test). Cheaper, no radiation.
✓
Generic Statins
Atorvastatin and rosuvastatin are generic, high-potency, and first-line for ASCVD prevention.
✓
Rate Control First for Afib
Rate control is non-inferior to rhythm control for most patients (AFFIRM trial). Less costly, fewer side effects.
✓
DOACs Over Warfarin for Afib
Non-inferior or superior efficacy, less monitoring, lower ICH risk. Exception: Mechanical valve or severe MS — use warfarin.
📌 Stent Duration — Current Guidelines
  • Stable CAD with DES: DAPT × 6 months (can shorten to 1-3 months if high bleeding risk)
  • ACS with DES: DAPT × 12 months (can extend if high ischemic, low bleeding risk)
  • After DAPT: Single antiplatelet indefinitely (aspirin or clopidogrel alone)
🫁 Pulmonology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Pulmonary Practices
✗
CT Pulmonary Angiography in Low-Risk PE Choosing Wisely
Use PERC rule first. If PERC negative, no testing needed. If Wells low + D-dimer negative, no CTPA.
✗
Routine Preoperative PFTs ATS
Not routine before non-thoracic surgery. Clinical assessment is sufficient for most patients.
✗
Antibiotics for Acute Bronchitis Choosing Wisely
No antibiotics for acute bronchitis in healthy adults. Viral in >90% of cases. Offer supportive care.
✗
Chest X-ray for Every COPD Exacerbation
Not routine for typical exacerbations. Only if pneumonia, pneumothorax, or other complication suspected.
✗
Continuous Pulse Oximetry in Stable Patients Choosing Wisely
Discontinue when stable. Leads to alarm fatigue and unnecessary interventions.
✗
Aggressive Follow-up of Tiny Pulmonary Nodules
Per Fleischner 2017: Solid nodules <6mm in low-risk patients need no follow-up CT.
✅ High-Value Pulmonary Practices
✓
NIV for AECOPD
BiPAP reduces intubation and mortality in AECOPD with respiratory acidosis. Cost-effective.
✓
Smoking Cessation Counseling
Most cost-effective intervention in medicine. Pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT) doubles quit rates.
✓
Inhaler Technique Assessment
Check technique before escalating therapy. Up to 90% of patients use inhalers incorrectly.
📌 Supplemental O2 — When It's Needed
  • LTOT criteria: PaO2 ≤55 or SpO2 ≤88% at rest (or 56-59 with cor pulmonale)
  • Ambulatory O2: Only if documented exertional desaturation AND improved with O2
  • Don't prescribe O2 for dyspnea without hypoxemia — no benefit
🟠 Gastroenterology
▼
🚫 Low-Value GI Practices
✗
Repeat Colonoscopy Too Soon AGA
Follow guidelines: Normal = 10 years. 1-2 small adenomas = 7-10 years. 3-4 small adenomas = 3-5 years. Advanced adenoma = 3 years.
✗
EGD for Uncomplicated GERD Choosing Wisely
No EGD for typical GERD symptoms responding to PPI. Only for alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, age >60 new onset).
✗
Long-term PPI Without Indication Choosing Wisely
Re-evaluate PPI need annually. Step down or discontinue if no clear indication. Use lowest effective dose.
✗
CT Abdomen for Typical IBS Symptoms
Clinical diagnosis. Rome IV criteria. Only image if red flags (weight loss, rectal bleeding, anemia, age >50 new onset).
✗
Routine Stool Testing for All Diarrhea
Most acute diarrhea is self-limited and viral. Test only if: >7 days, bloody, severe, immunocompromised, recent antibiotics, hospitalized.
✅ High-Value GI Practices
✓
FIT for CRC Screening
Annual FIT is an acceptable alternative to colonoscopy for average-risk screening. Patient preference matters.
✓
Treat H. pylori to Prevent Ulcer Recurrence
Eradication eliminates need for long-term PPI in H. pylori-related PUD. Cost-effective.
✓
Non-invasive Fibrosis Assessment
FIB-4, NAFLD fibrosis score, or elastography before liver biopsy for NAFLD/MASLD staging.
🦠 Infectious Disease
▼
🚫 Antibiotic Stewardship — Avoid
✗
Antibiotics for Viral URI Choosing Wisely
No antibiotics for common cold, viral pharyngitis (if Strep rapid test/culture negative), or acute sinusitis <10 days.
✗
Antibiotics for Asymptomatic Bacteriuria Choosing Wisely
Do NOT treat except in pregnancy or before urologic procedures. Treating ASB increases antibiotic resistance.
✗
Prolonged Antibiotic Courses
Shorter courses are often equivalent: CAP 5 days, cellulitis 5-6 days, UTI 3-5 days. Use shortest effective duration.
✗
Fluoroquinolones as First-line for Uncomplicated UTI Choosing Wisely
Reserve for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis. Use nitrofurantoin or TMP-SMX for uncomplicated cystitis.
✗
Urine Culture for Typical Cystitis in Young Women
Treat empirically. Culture only if recurrent, complicated, or treatment failure.
✗
IV Antibiotics When Oral Equivalent Exists
Oral step-down early. Many infections (including osteomyelitis, endocarditis in select cases) can complete therapy PO.
✅ High-Value ID Practices
✓
Procalcitonin-Guided Antibiotic Duration
Can safely shorten antibiotic courses in pneumonia and sepsis. Stop when procalcitonin <0.25 or drops >80%.
✓
Generic Antibiotics
Most antibiotics are generic. Brand-name rarely justified. Doxycycline, amoxicillin, TMP-SMX are highly cost-effective.
✓
Hepatitis C Screening and Treatment
Universal screening once for adults. Cure prevents cirrhosis and HCC — highly cost-effective despite drug cost.
📌 Antibiotic Duration — Evidence-Based Short Courses
  • Uncomplicated UTI: 3 days (nitrofurantoin 5 days)
  • Community-acquired pneumonia: 5 days (if afebrile 48-72h and stable)
  • Uncomplicated cellulitis: 5-6 days
  • Intra-abdominal infection (source controlled): 4 days
  • VAP: 7 days
🧠 Neurology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Neurology Practices
✗
Imaging for Primary Headache Choosing Wisely
No MRI/CT for stable migraine or tension headache without red flags.
✗
EEG for Syncope Without Seizure Features Choosing Wisely
Low yield. Only order if clinical features suggest seizure (witnessed tonic-clonic activity, tongue bite, prolonged confusion).
✗
Carotid Imaging for Non-hemispheric Symptoms
Carotid disease doesn't cause dizziness, syncope, or confusion. Only image for hemispheric stroke/TIA symptoms.
✗
Routine Seizure Prophylaxis After Stroke
Not recommended. Only treat if seizure occurs. Prophylaxis does not prevent late epilepsy.
✗
Brand-Name ASMs When Generic Available
Levetiracetam, lamotrigine, carbamazepine, valproate, phenytoin — all generic and effective.
✅ High-Value Neurology Practices
✓
HINTS Exam for Acute Vestibular Syndrome
More sensitive than early MRI for posterior stroke. Skilled exam can avoid unnecessary imaging.
✓
Epley Maneuver for BPPV
Highly effective, costs nothing. Avoids unnecessary medications, imaging, and specialist referrals.
✓
Generic Triptans
Sumatriptan is generic and effective. Reserve branded or newer agents for treatment failures.
🦴 Rheumatology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Rheumatology Practices
✗
ANA Testing Without Clinical Suspicion Choosing Wisely
ANA is positive in 15-20% of healthy people. Only test with clinical features of CTD.
✗
Lyme Testing for Non-specific Symptoms Choosing Wisely
High false positive rate leads to unnecessary treatment. Test only with compatible syndrome + endemic exposure.
✗
X-rays for Acute Low Back Pain <6 Weeks
Clinical assessment only unless red flags. Most acute LBP resolves without imaging.
✗
MRI for Non-specific Back Pain Without Red Flags
MRI finds "abnormalities" in most asymptomatic adults. Leads to unnecessary interventions.
✗
DEXA Before Age 65 in Average-Risk Women
Screen at 65, or younger if risk factors (FRAX). Not routine in younger women.
✅ High-Value Rheumatology Practices
✓
Methotrexate as First-Line DMARD
Effective, inexpensive, well-studied. Use before biologics in RA. Anchor drug in many regimens.
✓
Generic Allopurinol for Gout
First-line ULT, generic. Febuxostat and pegloticase reserved for allopurinol failures/intolerance.
✓
Acetaminophen and Topical NSAIDs for OA
Start with safest options. Topical diclofenac effective for knee OA with fewer systemic effects.
🔬 Endocrinology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Endocrine Practices
✗
Routine Thyroid Screening in Asymptomatic Adults USPSTF I
Insufficient evidence for routine TSH screening. Test if symptomatic or high-risk.
✗
Multiple Daily Glucose Checks in Stable T2DM on Oral Agents
Minimal benefit in non-insulin-treated T2DM. HbA1c is sufficient for monitoring.
✗
Sliding Scale Insulin Alone Choosing Wisely
Reactive, not proactive. Always add basal insulin for hospitalized patients with diabetes.
✗
Vitamin D Screening in Low-Risk Adults
Not recommended routinely. Supplement empirically if high-risk (malabsorption, limited sun, elderly).
✗
Testosterone Testing for Fatigue Without Other Signs
Low testosterone common with aging. Only test if clinical hypogonadism suspected (specific symptoms + signs).
✅ High-Value Endocrine Practices
✓
Metformin as First-Line for T2DM
Generic, effective, weight-neutral, CV benefits. First-line unless contraindicated.
✓
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for Cardiorenal Protection
Add early if ASCVD, HF, or CKD — benefit beyond glucose lowering. Now considered high-value despite cost.
✓
Levothyroxine (Generic)
Brand-name not superior. Generic levothyroxine is effective and far less expensive.
✓
Observation for Small Thyroid Nodules
Per ATA guidelines: Nodules <1 cm (or <1.5 cm without suspicious features) — observation, not biopsy.
💧 Nephrology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Nephrology Practices
✗
Renal Ultrasound for Isolated AKI Without Obstruction Concern
Low yield if no clinical suspicion for obstruction (anuria, known stones, pelvic malignancy). History determines need.
✗
Stopping ACEi/ARB Permanently for Small Cr Rise
Up to 30% Cr increase is acceptable when starting ACEi/ARB. Long-term renoprotection outweighs short-term rise.
✗
Routine 24-Hour Urine for Proteinuria Quantification
Spot urine protein/Cr ratio is sufficient for most clinical decisions. 24-hour collection cumbersome and often inaccurate.
✗
NSAIDs in CKD
Avoid in CKD stage 3+. Nephrotoxic, worsen hypertension, cause hyperkalemia. Use alternatives.
✅ High-Value Nephrology Practices
✓
SGLT2 Inhibitors for CKD
Slow CKD progression regardless of diabetes status (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY). Now standard of care.
✓
ACEi/ARB for Proteinuric CKD
Proven to slow progression. Continue unless hyperkalemia or Cr rise >30%.
✓
Avoid Contrast Nephropathy Overtreatment
Risk often overstated. Volume expansion helps, but don't delay necessary imaging. Hold metformin only if eGFR <30.
🩸 Hematology
▼
🚫 Low-Value Hematology Practices
✗
Liberal Blood Transfusion Choosing Wisely
Restrictive strategy (Hgb <7) is safe and non-inferior in most patients. Liberal (Hgb <8) for ACS, symptomatic anemia.
✗
Hypercoagulability Workup After Provoked VTE
Not indicated for VTE with clear provoking factor (surgery, immobility). Won't change management.
✗
Routine Preoperative Coagulation Testing Choosing Wisely
PT/INR/PTT not needed unless history of bleeding, liver disease, or anticoagulant use.
✗
Iron Studies for Mild Anemia Without Symptoms
Check MCV first. If microcytic, then iron studies. If normocytic, consider other causes before extensive workup.
✅ High-Value Hematology Practices
✓
IV Iron for Iron Deficiency in HF/CKD
Improves symptoms and reduces hospitalizations even without anemia. More effective than oral iron.
✓
DOACs for VTE Treatment
No monitoring needed, fewer interactions than warfarin. Exception: mechanical valve, antiphospholipid syndrome.
✓
Observation for Low-Grade Lymphomas
"Watch and wait" for asymptomatic follicular lymphoma or CLL. Treatment doesn't prolong survival if asymptomatic.
👴 Geriatrics & Deprescribing
▼
🚫 Medications to Avoid in Elderly (Beers Criteria)
✗
Benzodiazepines & Non-Benzo Hypnotics Choosing Wisely
Avoid for insomnia. Falls, fractures, cognitive impairment. Use sleep hygiene, CBT-I instead.
✗
Anticholinergics (Diphenhydramine, Oxybutynin, TCAs)
High anticholinergic burden → delirium, falls, cognitive decline. Cumulative effect of multiple agents.
✗
Proton Pump Inhibitors Long-Term
Re-evaluate annually. Associated with C. diff, fractures, hypomagnesemia. Use lowest dose, shortest duration.
✗
Tight Glycemic Control in Frail Elderly
HbA1c target 7.5-8.5% in limited life expectancy. Hypoglycemia risk outweighs benefit of tight control.
✗
Aggressive BP Lowering in Frail Elderly
SPRINT enrolled robust elders. Individualize targets — orthostatic hypotension and falls are real risks.
✅ Deprescribing Opportunities
✓
Stop Statins if Life Expectancy <1-2 Years
No benefit in terminal illness. Reduces pill burden, interactions, and costs.
✓
Stop Bisphosphonates After 5 Years (Oral) or 3 Years (IV)
"Drug holiday" — benefits persist, reduces atypical fracture risk.
✓
Reassess Aspirin for Primary Prevention
Not recommended for most adults >60 for primary prevention (ASPREE, ARRIVE). Bleeding risk increases with age.
✓
Review and Stop Unnecessary Vitamins/Supplements
Most have no proven benefit. Reduce polypharmacy and costs.
📌 Deprescribing Questions to Ask
  • Is there still a valid indication for this medication?
  • Is the benefit likely to be realized given life expectancy?
  • Are there potential harms that outweigh benefits?
  • What are the patient's goals and preferences?
🏥 Perioperative Medicine
▼
🚫 Low-Value Perioperative Testing
✗
Routine Preoperative Testing in Healthy Patients Choosing Wisely
No routine CBC, BMP, PT/INR, UA, CXR, ECG for low-risk surgery in healthy patients. Test only if indicated.
✗
Stress Testing Before Low/Intermediate-Risk Surgery ACC/AHA
Rarely changes management. For elevated-risk surgery: test only if result will change care (cancel or revascularize).
✗
PFTs Before Non-Thoracic Surgery
Clinical assessment is sufficient. PFTs rarely change management. Optimize asthma/COPD clinically.
✗
Cardiology "Clearance"
Not a thing. Risk assessment and optimization is the goal, not "clearance." Anesthesia clears for surgery.
✅ High-Value Perioperative Practices
✓
Use Functional Capacity Assessment
Can patient climb 2 flights of stairs? If yes (≥4 METs), low cardiac risk regardless of comorbidities.
✓
Continue Beta-Blockers Perioperatively
Don't stop chronic beta-blockers (withdrawal risk). Don't start de novo before surgery (POISE harm).
✓
Appropriate Antibiotic Prophylaxis Timing
Within 60 min of incision. Redose if surgery prolonged. Stop within 24h (48h for cardiac surgery).
📌 When Preoperative ECG IS Indicated
  • Known CAD, arrhythmia, or structural heart disease
  • New cardiac symptoms
  • High-risk surgery (vascular, thoracic) in patients with risk factors
  • NOT routine for low-risk surgery regardless of age
🛏️ Hospital Medicine
▼
🚫 Low-Value Inpatient Practices
✗
Routine Daily Labs Choosing Wisely
Order only when results will change management. Daily phlebotomy causes iatrogenic anemia.
✗
Telemetry for Low-Risk Patients Choosing Wisely
Use AHA guidelines. Most patients don't need continuous monitoring. Causes alarm fatigue.
✗
Foley Catheters Without Indication Choosing Wisely
Remove as soon as possible. CAUTI is preventable. "Patient convenience" is not an indication.
✗
Strict Bed Rest for Most Conditions
Early mobilization improves outcomes. Bed rest causes deconditioning, VTE, delirium, pressure ulcers.
✗
Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis in Low-Risk Patients Choosing Wisely
Not routine. Reserve for ICU with risk factors (coagulopathy, mechanical ventilation, burns, TBI). PPI overuse causes C. diff.
✅ High-Value Inpatient Practices
✓
VTE Prophylaxis for ALL Hospitalized Patients
Pharmacologic or mechanical depending on bleeding risk. One of most effective preventive measures.
✓
Glycemic Control Without Hypoglycemia
Target 140-180 mg/dL in most hospitalized patients. Hypoglycemia is harmful. Avoid sliding scale alone.
✓
Discharge Planning Starting Day 1
Reduces length of stay, readmissions. Engage case management early for complex patients.
✓
Medication Reconciliation
At admission and discharge. Prevents errors, inappropriate continuation of inpatient-only meds.

💰 High Value Care — ACP/ABIM Board Review

💎 Board Pearls 📚 Study Guides 🩺 IM Guide

Choosing Wisely • USPSTF • Evidence-Based Guidelines

↑ Back to top
